also_known_as: Tongyi Qianwen / Qwen robotics; Qwen embodied AI team
one_liner: Internal Tongyi/Qwen task force extending Alibaba's multimodal foundation models into vision-language-action and embodied "robot brain" systems (Qwen-VLA, RynnBrain) for generalist robot control across platforms.
notes: Internal division of Alibaba (NYSE/HKEX listed; parent founded 1999), not a standalone funded startup, so funding/round/team-size are not separately disclosed. Robotics/embodied-AI task force announced Oct 2025 by Lin Junyang (head of Qwen/Tongyi), described as a small team extending Qwen multimodal foundation models from virtual to physical world. Outputs include Qwen-VLA (vision-language-action model, announced May 2026) and DAMO Academy's open-source RynnBrain embodied foundation model (based on Qwen3-VL; variants RynnBrain-Plan/Nav/CoP) plus RynnVLA. Genuinely applies learning to physical robot control/manipulation/navigation, so real_robotics_ai=true. Sources: SCMP, Bloomberg, TechNode, Pandaily, Alibaba Cloud blog, GitHub (alibaba-damo-academy/RynnBrain). HQ city/country reflects Alibaba's Hangzhou, China headquarters.
business_model: Not a standalone revenue line: the Qwen/Tongyi robotics group releases its VLA "robot brain" models (Qwen-VLA, RynnBrain, built on Qwen3-VL) as open-source/free downloads to drive adoption, then monetizes indirectly by funneling embodied-AI workloads onto paid Alibaba Cloud (Model Studio/Bailian) GPU, inference, and API infrastructure, and by improving automation in Alibaba's own logistics (Cainiao warehouses) and e-commerce operations. Parent Alibaba's revenue comes from cloud, e-commerce, and logistics, not robot sales.
customers: Primarily internal/affiliate to date: Alibaba Cloud and logistics arm Cainiao (automated warehouse robot fleets) are the named deployment targets, plus open-source developers consuming the models via Hugging Face/GitHub. No external paying robotics OEM customers are publicly disclosed as of mid-2026; broader "ecosystem of deployment partners" is stated but unnamed.
revenue_status: N/A (internal R&D unit of profitable parent Alibaba; the robotics models themselves are pre-revenue/open-source, while parent group revenue is multi-billion and profitable)
technical_differentiation: Positions Qwen-VLA/RynnBrain as a single unified, open-weight generalist VLA controlling diverse hardware (single-arm, dual-arm, humanoid) without platform-specific retraining, leveraging Qwen3-VL multimodal backbone plus proprietary Taobao/Cainiao real-world data and Alibaba Cloud scale. Closest named competitors are Nvidia (GR00T/Isaac, also foundation-model-for-robots but tied to Nvidia hardware/closed stack) and Google DeepMind (Gemini Robotics / RT-2, more closed); Alibaba's differentiation is the open-source, China-domestic, cloud-distribution play rather than a clear architectural moat.

one_liner: Open-source robot foundation model (MolmoAct) that reasons in 3D space to plan and execute real-world manipulation tasks, released fully open with weights, data, and code.
notes: Ai2 is a nonprofit AI research institute founded by Microsoft co-founder Paul Allen in 2014, HQ on Seattle's Lake Union. MolmoAct is its robot foundation model line; MolmoAct 2 released May 5, 2026 as a fully open robotic foundation model that reasons in 3D before acting, achieving ~87.1% success across manipulation tasks, ~37x faster than the original, with bimanual capability and no per-task fine-tuning. Ai2 also released a 720+ hour open bimanual manipulation dataset, training code, eval data, and an open action tokenizer. Piloted with Stanford School of Medicine (Cong Lab / CRISPR self-driving wetlab). As a nonprofit it does not raise VC rounds; the $152M is a 2025 NVIDIA + NSF grant for open science models (GeekWire). Total funding undisclosed (Paul Allen endowment plus grants). Assigned Layer 1 Robot Foundation Models; secondary L3 due to large open robotics dataset release.
business_model: N/A (research/open-source). As a 501(c)(3) nonprofit founded by Paul Allen, Ai2 is funded by the Paul G. Allen estate/endowment plus grants and partnerships - notably a $152M public-private OMAI grant (~$75M NSF, ~$77M Nvidia); it releases MolmoAct models, weights, and datasets fully open rather than selling them, with annual revenue/expenses around $110M.
customers: Stanford School of Medicine researchers are piloting MolmoAct 2 to automate repetitive lab manipulation in CRISPR gene-editing workflows. As open-source models, broader adopters are the research/robotics developer community; no paying customers (nonprofit, free release).
revenue_status: N/A (nonprofit; ~$110M annual budget funded by endowment and grants, not product revenue).
technical_differentiation: MolmoAct is an "Action Reasoning Model" that explicitly reasons in 3D space to plan before acting, and is released fully open (weights, training code, and a 720-hour bimanual YAM dataset). Versus Physical Intelligence's pi0.5 (closed weights) it reports higher LIBERO/real-world success and ~37x faster inference; versus Nvidia GR00T N1.7 (humanoid-focused, partially open) it differentiates on full openness and 3D spatial-reasoning planning.

also_known_as: Amazon Science Robotics; DeepFleet; Kiva Systems (predecessor)
one_liner: Builds AI foundation models (DeepFleet) that coordinate and route Amazon's 1M+ warehouse mobile robots, predicting traffic patterns to cut travel time and boost fleet efficiency ~10%.
notes: Division of Amazon.com (NASDAQ: AMZN), not an independently funded startup; funding/round/team-size fields are not applicable/undisclosed. Originated from Kiva Systems (founded 2003), acquired by Amazon in March 2012 for ~$775M and rebranded Amazon Robotics. HQ in North Reading, MA with major facilities in Westborough, MA. DeepFleet unveiled July 2025 alongside Amazon's 1 millionth deployed robot; research paper "DeepFleet: Multi-Agent Foundation Models for Mobile Robots" published arXiv 2508.08574 (Aug 2025). Four model architectures: robot-centric (autoregressive decision transformer), robot-floor (cross-attention), image-floor (convolutional), graph-floor (temporal attention + GNN). Reported ~10% improvement in robot travel efficiency across 300+ facilities. Also does manipulation FM research (Amazon Science Robotics). Genuinely applies learning to physical warehouse robots, so real_robotics_ai=true. Layer 1 Robot Foundation Models is appropriate primary layer.
business_model: Internal cost-reduction division of Amazon, not a standalone revenue generator: DeepFleet foundation models coordinate Amazon's 1M+ warehouse mobile robots across 300+ fulfillment centers to cut robot travel time ~10%, lowering fulfillment operating costs and speeding delivery. Robotics spend is absorbed as fulfillment infrastructure capex/opex; the models are not licensed or sold externally.
customers: Sole "customer" is Amazon's own fulfillment/logistics operations (300+ facilities worldwide). No external paying or piloting customers; predecessor Kiva Systems' third-party clients were phased out after the 2012 acquisition.
revenue_status: N/A (cost-center within Amazon; no standalone robotics revenue disclosed). Amazon parent is profitable, but DeepFleet generates savings rather than direct revenue.
technical_differentiation: Claims to be the first multi-agent foundation model for fleet-scale robot coordination (DeepFleet's RC/RF/IF/GF architectures, trained on real movement data from hundreds of thousands of robots), versus rule-based/optimization traffic systems from warehouse-automation peers like Symbotic and Zebra/Fetch Robotics. Key moat is proprietary fleet-scale operational data (1M+ robots) that named competitors cannot match.

also_known_as: Advanced Machine Intelligence; AMI; LeWM / LeWorldModel; JEPA
one_liner: Builds JEPA-based world models that learn structured representations of physical environments and predict their evolution, enabling reliable planning for robotics, industrial automation, and other physical systems.
last_round_lead: Cathay Innovation, Greycroft, Hiro Capital, HV Capital, Bezos Expeditions (co-leads)
notes: Founded by Yann LeCun (ex-Meta Chief AI Scientist) after leaving Meta. $1.03B seed at ~$3.5B pre-money valuation, among largest AI seed rounds. Co-leads: Cathay Innovation, Greycroft, Hiro Capital, HV Capital, Bezos Expeditions; angels include Berners-Lee, Mark Cuban, Eric Schmidt, Xavier Niel, Jim Breyer. Teams planned in Paris, New York, Montreal, Singapore. LeWM (LeWorldModel) paper (arXiv 2603.19312, Mar 2026, with Mila/NYU/Samsung SAIL) introduces SIGReg-regularized JEPA that matches/beats baselines on Push-T (96% success), Reacher, and 3D pick-and-place robot manipulation benchmarks at ~15M params on a single GPU. Targets robotics, industrial process control, automation, wearables, healthcare. Round labeled "seed" by most coverage; one source called it Series A. Team size undisclosed.
business_model: Frontier research lab building JEPA-based world models; currently no product/revenue, planning to monetize via industry partnerships and licensing of world-model AI to sectors like industrial process control, robotics, wearables and healthcare, while open-sourcing much research/code. CEO LeBrun explicitly says it is "not your typical applied AI startup that can release a product in three months." Funded by a $1.03B seed (largest in European history) at a ~$3.5B valuation from Nvidia, Samsung, Sea, Temasek, Toyota Ventures, and French backers (Mulliez, Dassault, Publicis).
customers: First disclosed partner is Nabla, the digital-health startup run by AMI CEO Alexandre LeBrun, which plans to deploy AMI's early models. Other industrial investors/partners (Samsung, Toyota Ventures, Publicis) signal strategic interest; broader paying customers undisclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no revenue plans yet; raised $1.03B seed at ~$3.5B valuation, March 2026)
technical_differentiation: Bets on JEPA (Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture) operating in latent/embedding space rather than pixel-generation or token-prediction, plus efficient research like LeWorldModel/LeWM (~15M-param, single-GPU, ~48x faster planning). Closest named competitors are Fei-Fei Li's World Labs (also ~$1B raised, pixel/3D generative "spatial intelligence") and SpAItial ($13M seed); AMI differentiates on LeCun-led non-generative, energy-based world-model approach over generative rendering.

company_name: BAAI / Zhiyuan Research Institute (Beijing Academy of Artificial Intelligence)
also_known_as: 智源研究院; Beijing Academy of AI; BAAI; FlagOpen; RoboBrain
one_liner: Non-profit AI lab building open-source RoboBrain embodied foundation models that give robots unified perception, 3D spatial reasoning, and manipulation planning for general-purpose and humanoid robotics.
notes: Non-profit AI research institute founded Nov 14, 2018, backed by Beijing Municipal Science and Technology Commission and Zhongguancun Science Park; funding members include Baidu, ByteDance, Megvii, Meituan, Xiaomi, Tsinghua, Peking University. Not a venture-funded startup, so funding is government/grant-based and undisclosed; "round" fields apply loosely. RoboBrain is a robotics program within BAAI (hence division). RoboBrain 2.0 launched June 2025 (annual conference); RoboBrain 2.5 ("Depth in Sight, Time in Mind") released with arXiv 2601.14352, adding 3D spatial reasoning and dense temporal value estimation for fine-grained manipulation. Open-source on Hugging Face (BAAI/RoboBrain2.5-8B) and GitHub (FlagOpen/RoboBrain2.5). Qualifies as real robotics AI: applies learning to physical/humanoid robot manipulation and spatial reasoning.
business_model: Non-profit research institute funded primarily by the Beijing Municipal Government (Beijing Municipal Science & Technology Commission, Zhongguancun Science Park) and China's Ministry of Science and Technology, supplemented by contributions from founding member companies (Baidu, Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance, Xiaomi, Megvii) and academic partners (Tsinghua, Peking University, CAS). It does not sell products; RoboBrain/FlagOpen models are released open-source, with value captured indirectly via spin-outs it incubates (e.g., XYZ Embodied AI) and an ecosystem of 20+ robotics partners.
customers: Not a paying-customer model. Adopters/ecosystem partners of RoboBrain and RoboOS include 20+ Chinese robotics firms; BAAI incubated XYZ Embodied AI (founded Aug 2025, raised ~1B yuan). Government sponsors (Beijing municipality, MOST) are the de facto funders rather than customers.
revenue_status: N/A (nonprofit; annual operating budget reported in the multi-hundred-million-yuan range, unverified)
technical_differentiation: RoboBrain 2.0/2.5 positions as an open-source embodied vision-language "brain" emphasizing unified perception, 3D spatial reasoning, and task planning (7B and 32B variants), paired with the RoboOS cloud orchestration platform - distinct from NVIDIA Isaac GR00T (an open VLA action model for humanoids, Apache-2.0) and Physical Intelligence's pi0 (closed-weight flow-matching VLA action policy). BAAI differentiates on being a fully open, China-sovereign, hardware-agnostic reasoning/planning layer plus fleet orchestration rather than a single end-to-end action policy.

one_liner: Chinese tech giant applying its ERNIE multimodal foundation models toward embodied AI and robotics, partnering with robot makers (e.g. AgiBot) to power perception and robot action.
notes: Baidu is a publicly traded company (NASDAQ: BIDU, HKEX: 9888) founded Jan 18, 2000 by Robin Li and Eric Xu, HQ in Beijing Haidian District. Not VC-funded; "total funding/round" fields are N/A for a public giant. Core business is search, AI cloud, ERNIE foundation models, and Apollo Go robotaxi. The robotics/embodied-AI effort is a division leveraging the ERNIE multimodal stack (ERNIE 5.0 is natively omni-modal across text/image/audio/video). Baidu AI Cloud has partnered with humanoid maker AgiBot, integrating its large vision model platform and EdgeBoard edge computing for robotics. Qualifies as real_robotics_ai=true because the ERNIE stack is being applied to physical robot perception/action via partnerships, though Baidu is primarily a foundation-model/platform provider rather than a hardware robotics company. Layer 1 (Robot Foundation Models) is appropriate; vertical robot products (Tiantian Home Robot) also exist. Funding/team-size for the specific robotics unit not separately disclosed.
business_model: Baidu monetizes its ERNIE foundation models for robotics indirectly: it licenses/serves the multimodal ERNIE (Wenxin) models to robot makers and sells the underlying AI Cloud compute and Qianfan MaaS platform (subscription + usage-based) that powers their embodied-AI development, rather than selling robots itself. Core revenue still comes from online marketing/search plus the growing AI Cloud (RMB 6.2B in Q3 2025), within which a humanoid-robotics cloud vertical is an emerging line.
customers: Robot makers integrating ERNIE/Wenxin: UBTECH (Wenxin LLM integrated into its Walker S humanoid for perception, intent understanding and task planning) and AgiBot (cited as an embodied-AI partner). Broader AI Cloud/Qianfan enterprise customers are undisclosed by name in the robotics context.
revenue_status: profitable (Baidu group; Q3 2025 AI Cloud revenue ~RMB 6.2B, +21% YoY; robotics/humanoid cloud vertical revenue reportedly doubled QoQ in Q4 but remains early-stage)
technical_differentiation: Unlike pure embodied-AI model labs, Baidu pairs a frontier omni-modal LLM (ERNIE 5.0, jointly modeling text/image/audio/video) with at-scale AI Cloud infrastructure, positioning it as the model-plus-compute backbone for Chinese robot OEMs rather than a competing robot/VLA builder. Closest named rivals supplying brains to the same robots are DeepSeek (partnered with UBTECH on multimodal embodied reasoning) and AgiBot's own in-house GO-2 embodied foundation model; Baidu's edge is its integrated cloud/MaaS distribution and breadth of multimodal capability versus those model-only or vertically-integrated competitors.

also_known_as: X-Humanoid; Beijing Innovation Center of Humanoid Robotics; National-Local Co-built Embodied AI Robot Innovation Center (国家地方共建具身智能机器人创新中心); 北京人形
one_liner: Builds embodied-AI foundation models and humanoid robots; its Pelican-VL "brain" handles perception-to-execution reasoning and action across diverse robot bodies, plus Tiangong hardware platform.
last_round_lead: Syndicate incl. Beijing AI Industry Investment Fund, Yizhuang State-owned Investment, Beijing High-end Fund, Huakong Fund; Baidu strategic (no single lead disclosed)
notes: Founded 2023-11-02 in Beijing (Yizhuang/E-Town). Upgraded Oct 2024 to national-local co-built Embodied AI Robot Innovation Center. Registered capital grew from 300M to 460M RMB (May 2025). First market-based funding round >700M RMB (~$98M USD) announced Feb 2026; investors include Beijing AI Industry Investment Fund, Yizhuang State-owned Investment, Beijing High-end Fund, Huakong Fund, with strategic investment from Baidu and Dongtu Technology — no single lead named. R&D staff >70% of workforce. Pelican-VL 1.0 is an open-source embodied "brain" model family (7B-72B params) developed by its WFM System Group; also released XR-1 VLA model and Tiangong humanoid hardware. Total funding undisclosed beyond this round; ~$98M is the disclosed market round, registered capital separate. Sources: x-humanoid.com/about.html, beijing.gov.cn funding announcement, GitHub/arxiv for Pelican-VL.
business_model: As a Beijing government-backed nonprofit innovation center (founded Nov 2023), it is primarily funded by state-linked sources: a ~700M yuan (~$100M) first round led by the Beijing Robotics Industry Development Investment Fund, E-Town Capital, Beijing Gaojingjian fund and TH Capital, plus strategic investors Baidu/Alibaba and Kyland. It also generates revenue by selling/deploying Tien Kung humanoid robots and the Tian Yi platform into factories and warehouses and licensing toolchains, while open-sourcing models (Pelican-VL) and the Tien Kung 3.0 platform to build an ecosystem.
customers: Named industrial deployments include Foton Cummins (Tien Kung 2.0 and Tian Yi 2.0 running autonomous bin pickup/transport/placement on an engine production line), plus pilots reported with Cummins and Bayer. Strategic/investor partners include Baidu, Alibaba and Kyland Technology. A ~9,700 sqm pilot manufacturing line targets 5,000 units/year capacity.
revenue_status: early-revenue (nonprofit; raised ~$100M / ~700M yuan first round Feb 2026; specific product revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a fully open-sourced full-stack platform (Tien Kung 3.0 hardware designs, motion-control frameworks, training datasets, and the Pelican-VL/Pelican-Unify 1.0 embodied "brain," which ranked No. 1 on WorldArena), contrasting with closed/hardware-led peers like Unitree and the Nvidia-GR00T-dependent Fourier GR-1. Its claimed edge is whole-body high-dynamic motion control fused with tactile interaction plus a unified perception-to-action model, vs Nvidia Isaac GR00T N1 which is a model others (including X-Humanoid) adapt rather than a vertically integrated robot.

one_liner: Builds advanced mobile robots (Atlas humanoid, Spot quadruped, Stretch), now using a Large Behavior Model with TRI for learned full-body humanoid control of manipulation and locomotion.
notes: Founded 1992 (MIT spin-off, Marc Raibert). Owned by Hyundai Motor Group: acquired ~80% stake from SoftBank for ~$880M, announced Dec 2020, completed June 2021 (Hyundai valued the deal at ~$1.1B incl. Hyundai/affiliate stakes). As a Hyundai division it has no independent VC funding (prior owners: Google/Alphabet 2013, SoftBank 2017). Layer 1 fit: Aug 2025 demo of a single Large Behavior Model (LBM) directly controlling full-body Atlas, co-developed with Toyota Research Institute (partnership announced Oct 2024), treating hands and feet near-identically for whole-body manipulation+locomotion. Team size ~1,440 (third-party estimate, Dec 2025). HQ ISO country US. [GATE2 scope-flag] Transitioning from traditional control to AI-learned policies (LBM with TRI). Historical product predates AI; kept because LBM is a genuine new capability category, not just enhancement.
business_model: Sells and leases mobile robots plus software/support subscriptions: Spot quadrupeds for industrial inspection (energy, construction, utilities), Stretch for warehouse case-unloading, and the electric Atlas humanoid for factory assembly/material handling. Owned by Hyundai Motor Group; Atlas production is contracted out to anchor customers under multi-year industrial deployment agreements.
customers: Spot: 1,000+ enterprise customers / 2,000+ units across 40+ countries. Stretch: DHL Supply Chain, Maersk, H&M, Gap, Otto Group/Hermes. Atlas: 2026 production fully allocated to Hyundai's Robotics Metaplant Application Center (RMAC) and Google DeepMind; third-party Atlas pilots not expected until ~2027.
revenue_status: scaling (unverified ~$130M-$300M estimated 2025 revenue per third-party estimates; Spot and Stretch are commercially shipping, Atlas is pre-commercial/early-customer). Owned by Hyundai; standalone profitability not publicly disclosed.
technical_differentiation: Versus Tesla Optimus and Figure AI, Atlas leads on dynamic whole-body mobility (electric high-torque actuation, ~56 DoF, agile locomotion) and uniquely uses a single Large Behavior Model (with Toyota Research Institute) that controls hands and feet jointly for full-body learned humanoid control, plus Google DeepMind foundation models. Figure (BMW) and Tesla (Fremont/Austin) are further along in at-scale commercial humanoid deployment, where Atlas trails.

one_liner: Robot foundation model team building GR-3/GR-RL large-scale VLA models for long-horizon dexterous bi-manual manipulation, deployed on the in-house ByteMini two-arm mobile robot.
notes: Internal robotics research effort within ByteDance's Seed AI team (Seed founded 2023, HQ Beijing, with labs also in Singapore and Palo Alto). Not an independent funded startup, so funding/round data is not applicable; ByteDance is a privately held company. GR-3 is a 4B mixture-of-transformers VLA with a diffusion-transformer action head (flow-matching), co-trained on web-scale vision-language data plus VR-collected human trajectories and robot imitation data. ByteMini is the in-house 22-DoF bi-manual mobile robot (sphere-wrist design, multiple wrist/head cameras) used as the embodiment. GR-3 technical report: arXiv:2507.15493 (July 2025). Confirmed via official ByteDance Seed blog and arXiv. founded_year reflects the Seed team's establishment, not a standalone robotics entity.
business_model: An internal R&D team within ByteDance's Seed AI lab (not an independent revenue entity); funded by ByteDance's core advertising/short-video profits (TikTok, Douyin) to build robot foundation models (GR-3, GR-RL/GR-Dexter) and prototype hardware (ByteMini). No standalone product sales; intended future model is embodied-AI/robotics commercialization (home and service robots) layered onto ByteDance's platform business.
customers: Undisclosed - no external paying or piloting customers are public. GR-3 is deployed only internally on ByteDance's in-house ByteMini bi-manual mobile robot for lab-stage research demonstrations (table cleaning, clothes folding/hanging, shoelace lacing).
revenue_status: N/A (corporate research lab; not separately monetized). Parent ByteDance is profitable with ~$150B+ annual revenue (2024, unverified for the robotics unit specifically), but the Seed Robotics team itself is pre-revenue research.
technical_differentiation: vs Google DeepMind (Gemini Robotics/RT-2) and Physical Intelligence (pi-0): GR-3 differentiates via efficient fine-tuning from minimal VR-collected human trajectory data plus co-training on web-scale vision-language data, reducing dependence on costly robot trajectory collection, and via tight co-design with its own 22-DoF sphere-wrist ByteMini hardware. GR-RL adds an RL framework for long-horizon precise dexterity (e.g., first learned policy to autonomously lace shoes, 83.3% success).

one_liner: Builds AI robotic foundation models (RFM-1) enabling warehouse robots to perceive, reason, and manipulate diverse objects via the Covariant Brain for pick-and-place automation.
notes: Founded 2017 as Embodied Intelligence by Pieter Abbeel, Peter Chen, Rocky Duan, Tianhao Zhang. RFM-1 (8B params) robotics foundation model announced 2024-03, trained on text, images, video, robot actions and sensor readings. Total funding $222M; last priced round Series C $75M (April 2023, led by Radical Ventures and Index Ventures, valuation ~$625M). In Aug 2024 Amazon executed a reverse-acqui-hire: non-exclusive license to Covariant's models plus hiring of founders Abbeel/Chen/Duan and ~25% of staff (reported ~$380M+$20M). Company nominally continues but core leadership and tech moved to Amazon, so largely subsumed. Current team size post-deal unknown.
business_model: Pre-acquihire, Covariant licensed its "Covariant Brain" AI software (and later RFM-1 foundation model) to warehouse/logistics operators and sold integrated AI-powered robotic picking systems, typically delivered through robot-arm integrator partners (ABB, KNAPP) on commercial deployment contracts with ongoing support. In Aug 2024 it pivoted to a licensing/IP model when Amazon paid ~$400M ($380M up front + $20M to investors) for a non-exclusive license to its models and hired its three co-founders.
customers: System integrators and operators including ABB, KNAPP, Bastian, Fortna, Radial, and Otto Group; end markets named include fulfillment/distribution, apparel, grocery, health and beauty, and pharmaceuticals. Amazon became the marquee licensee in 2024.
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercial deployments via integrators; a third-party source claims "$100M+ ARR" but this is unverified and inconsistent with its acqui-hire outcome). One verified transaction: ~$400M from Amazon for a non-exclusive model license in 2024.
technical_differentiation: Built a single "universal" robot foundation model (RFM-1), trained on its own multimodal warehouse manipulation data, claiming generalist pick-and-place across diverse unseen SKUs rather than per-task programming. Versus closest Layer 1 peers Physical Intelligence (pi-0) and Skild AI, Covariant's edge was real-world warehouse-scale operational data and live integrator deployments, though that advantage eroded after its founders/IP largely moved to Amazon.

company_name: Cross-Dimensional Intelligence (跨维智能 / DexForce Technology Co., Ltd.)
also_known_as: DexForce; Kuawei/Kuangwei Intelligence; 跨维（深圳）智能数字科技有限公司
one_liner: Builds DexVerse engine-driven Sim2Real VLA models and general grasping/manipulation policies (PickWiz, X-Wiz brain, W1 humanoid) for industrial robots across 50+ industries.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (multiple rounds totaling several hundred million RMB: ~$10M angel + ~100M RMB Pre-A + several hundred million RMB A1&A2)
last_round_lead: Chengdu Kehuang Innovation Investment (成都科创投) and Hongtai Capital (洪泰基金)
notes: IMPORTANT ALIAS CORRECTION: The task's alias "Dexmal" is WRONG. Dexmal (原力灵机) is a SEPARATE company founded March 2025 by ex-Megvii founders (Fan Haoqiang, Zhou Erjin), registered in Chongqing/Beijing, that raised ~1B RMB from NIO Capital + Alibaba. Cross-Dimensional Intelligence / 跨维智能 is DexForce, founded June 15 2021 in Shenzhen (Nanshan) by Jia Kui, focused on Sim2Real embodied AI / general grasping for industrial robots. Do not conflate them. DexForce funding history: angel ~$10M (Songhe Capital + ZhenFund, 2022-03), Pre-A ~100M RMB (Lianchuang Capital, 2022-10), A1&A2 several hundred million RMB (2025-07, China Renaissance advisor). Products: DexVerse engine, PickWiz manipulation, X-Wiz embodied brain, DexForce W1 Pro humanoid, DexSense/KINGFISHER vision sensors. Claims #1 on WorldArena benchmark.
business_model: Sells DexVerse-engine-driven Sim2Real vision/VLA manipulation systems (3D vision sensors, grasping/insertion/assembly policy software like PickWiz, and the X-Wiz embodied "brain") to industrial manufacturers, deployed via integration, configuration and training services on robot arms; also commercializing the DexForce W1/W1 Pro humanoid for factory scenarios. Effectively project-based industrial automation deployments plus hardware-software product sales rather than off-the-shelf SKUs.
customers: Named end customers include GAC Aion (automaker), CRRC/Zhong Che (rail), and appliance makers Midea, Haier and Hisense; broadly serves leading automakers, new-energy battery manufacturers, 3C electronics, semiconductor, photovoltaic and aerospace firms. Company claims 100+ cumulative customers across 50+ industry segments.
revenue_status: scaling (publicly reported revenue at the hundred-million-yuan / ~100M+ RMB annual level; raised several hundred million yuan A1&A2 round in July 2025 led by Chengdu Kechuang Investment and Hongtai, with Lenovo Capital as existing investor)
technical_differentiation: Built on its proprietary DexVerse Sim2Real engine that auto-generates large-scale synthetic 3D data to train and deploy manipulation policies (claimed >99.9% grasp success at millimeter accuracy in semi-structured industrial scenes), giving it deeper real-world industrial deployment depth than research-stage US foundation-model peers like Physical Intelligence and Skild AI. Versus Chinese embodied-AI rivals such as Galbot, its differentiation is the established industrial revenue base and Sim2Real-first data pipeline rather than teleoperation/real-data collection.

one_liner: Builds real-time foundational world models (Oasis) and ultra-optimized AI infrastructure, generating interactive simulated 3D environments usable as synthetic training data for embodied/Physical AI agents.
notes: Israeli AI research lab founded 2023, registered Sep 2023. Funding history: ~$21M seed (Aug 2024, Sequoia); $32M Series A (Dec 2024, Benchmark, ~$500M valuation); $100M Series B (Aug 2025, $3.1B valuation); $300M (announced May 2026, Radical Ventures lead, $4B valuation, with NVIDIA, Amazon/Adobe/Toyota/eBay Ventures, Valor, Atreides, Zeev). Total reported >$450M. Core products: world models (Oasis, including Oasis-for-Physical-AI positioning), Lucy video model, and DOS/optimization infrastructure. Robotics relevance is via Oasis world models generating simulated environments and synthetic data for robotic training (e.g., simulated warehouse pick-and-place); Decart does not build physical robot hardware itself, so real_robotics_ai is true on the world-model/simulation-for-embodied-AI basis rather than direct robot manufacturing. Team size not disclosed.
business_model: Dual revenue: licenses its "DOS" GPU-optimized inference/training stack to cloud providers, hyperscalers, and AI labs to cut their compute costs, while also offering its real-time world/video models (Oasis for physical-AI/robotics simulation and synthetic training data; Lucy/Mirage for interactive media) as products. Says it has been profitable nearly from day one, funding compute via revenue.
customers: Amazon (AWS, including a chips/Trainium-related deal) and NVIDIA are named strategic customers/partners; several Series B investors (e.g., Adobe, Toyota, eBay ventures) are described as both customers and investors. Specific robotics/automaker users of Oasis are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (reportedly already generating "millions" and described as profitable from day one; raised ~$446-450M total, ~$4B valuation, having spent under $10M of investor capital - exact revenue figure not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Focuses on real-time, low-latency interactive world models generated frame-by-frame (Oasis went viral as a real-time playable Minecraft-style demo), differentiating on speed/latency and a vertically integrated optimized inference stack (DOS) versus offline-render video/world models from OpenAI (Sora) and Google (Veo 3). For robotics simulation, competes with NVIDIA's Omniverse/Cosmos world-model approach but emphasizes generative real-time environments rather than physics-engine-based simulation.

one_liner: Builds Physical AI foundation models that give industrial and warehouse robots human-like dexterity to perceive, reason, and manipulate objects in unstructured logistics and manufacturing environments.
last_round_type: Venture round (reported $1.65B valuation; some sources label Series B)
last_round_lead: Lightspeed Venture Partners and Sumitomo Corporation
notes: Founded 2017 by Samir Menon (ex-Stanford PhD), HQ Redwood City, CA. Develops Physical AI for dexterous manipulation in logistics/manufacturing; customers include FedEx, UPS, Maersk. Mar 2025 raised $95M at $1.65B post-money valuation (led by Lightspeed Venture Partners and Sumitomo Corp), bringing total funding to ~$291M (TechCrunch: "nearly $300M"). Round labeled "Series B" by some aggregators (Tracxn) but reported as a venture round by TechCrunch/Bloomberg. Earlier backers include Kleiner Perkins. Primary layer L1 Robot Foundation Models fits their Physical AI model claim; also vertical (warehouse/industrial) deployment. Sources: dexterity.ai/about; techcrunch.com/2025/03/11; Bloomberg 2025-03-11.
business_model: Sells AI-powered industrial manipulation robots (e.g., the DexR/Mech two-armed roving robots) for warehouse and logistics tasks such as truck loading/unloading, palletizing, depalletizing, singulation, and sortation, primarily via a Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS) subscription model rather than one-time hardware sales. It also partners with OEMs (e.g., Kawasaki Heavy Industries for intelligent robot arms; ASRock Rack for edge AI supercomputers) to embed its Physical AI software stack.
customers: Named customers/partners include FedEx (co-developed the DexR trailer-loading robot), UPS, GXO Logistics, and Sumitomo Corp. (2022 contract to deploy ~1,500 robots across Japanese warehouses by 2026; also a strategic investor). Kawasaki and ASRock Rack are technology/OEM partners.
revenue_status: scaling (revenue figures not publicly disclosed; multi-year commercial deployments with FedEx, UPS, GXO and a 1,500-robot Sumitomo contract; raised $95M Series B at a $1.65B valuation in March 2025, ~$291-300M total funding)
technical_differentiation: Unlike pure foundation-model players such as Physical Intelligence (embodiment-agnostic manipulation models, no full robot) and Skild AI (general-purpose "robot brain" software), Dexterity ships a full vertically integrated stack: its modular Physical AI architecture (foundation-model decision engines plus specialized manipulation agents, including a state-of-the-art 3D Box Packing Agent) runs on its own purpose-built dual-arm Mech/DexR hardware deployed in live enterprise logistics operations. Its edge is industrial-grade, production-deployed manipulation tied to owned hardware rather than a hardware-agnostic model API.

one_liner: Builds robot foundation models (DYNA-1) enabling low-cost robotic arms to perform fully autonomous, round-the-clock dexterous manipulation tasks for commercial deployment toward physical AGI.
notes: Founded 2024 by repeat founders Lindon Gao and York Yang (sold Caper AI for $350M) and former DeepMind research scientist Jason (Hwei Jen) Ma. $23.5M seed (co-led CRV + First Round Capital, closed March 2025) plus $120M Series A (led Robostrategy, CRV, First Round; participation from Salesforce Ventures, NVentures/NVIDIA, Amazon Industrial Innovation Fund, Samsung Next, LG Technology Ventures; closed ~Sept 15 2025). DYNA-1 claimed first commercial-ready robot foundation model with 99+% success in 24-hour non-stop dexterous manipulation. Layer 1 robot foundation model is core focus; also vertical deployments and low-cost hardware. Team size not disclosed in public sources.
business_model: Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS): deploys low-cost robotic arms running its DYNA-1 foundation model at customer sites and charges a recurring monthly per-robot subscription bundling hardware, software, maintenance, and continuous model updates, replacing capex with opex for service businesses.
customers: No specific named accounts publicly disclosed; company states robots are deployed and running ~16-24 hrs/day at hotels, restaurants, laundromats, and gyms. Third-party commentary speculates facilities-management firms (Compass, Sodexo) as potential customers, but these are unverified.
revenue_status: early-revenue (specific figures not publicly disclosed; company claims proven commercial viability via paying deployments and 99.4% success in 24/7 operation; raised $143.5M total funding at ~$600M valuation, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Unlike Physical Intelligence (pi0/pi0.5), which positions as a model provider partnering with third parties for deployment, Dyna verticalizes — it builds and deploys its own commercial-grade robots and optimizes for customer ROI rather than benchmark success rates, using a single-weight generalist model plus a proprietary reward model enabling nightly self-improvement from production data. Versus Skild AI, similarly more deployment/commercial-traction focused than research-stage.

one_liner: Builds "physics-first" Field Foundation Models (robot brains) enabling risk-aware autonomous navigation across robot embodiments—humanoid, legged, wheeled, flying—without prior maps or GPS.
notes: Assigned primary layer: L1_foundation_model. Founded 2023 (PitchBook/TechCrunch); Tracxn lists 2016 reflecting founder Ali Agha's earlier autonomy research (NASA JPL/Caltech/MIT). HQ Irvine, CA (some sources Mission Viejo). Total $405M across two consecutive rounds announced Aug 2025; latest tranche $314M at ~$2B valuation. Backers: NVIDIA (NVentures), Khosla Ventures, Intel Capital, Canaan, Gates Frontier, Samsung, BHP Ventures, Emerson Collective. CEO/founder Ali Agha. Genuine robotics AI—learning-based foundation models deployed on physical robots across embodiments for autonomous navigation in unstructured environments. [GATE2 scope-flag] Foundation model is cross-embodiment (humanoid, legged, wheeled, flying). Product is the robot brain, not the vehicle. Kept.
business_model: Licenses its hardware-agnostic "Field Foundation Models" (robot brain software) to enterprises that deploy them on their own/third-party robot fleets across industrial sites; revenue comes from customer contracts plus multiple expansion contracts, with large customers running thousands of robots across hundreds of construction, energy, mining, and inspection sites.
customers: Named/identified piloting and paying customers include SAP (physical-AI robotics pilots announced Nov 2025) and BHP (strategic investor via BHP Ventures, mining use); FieldAI also states it works with three to five of the top construction companies in Japan and the U.S. (specific firms not named), plus energy, manufacturing, urban delivery, and federal deployments.
revenue_status: early-revenue (revenue figure not publicly disclosed; signals include oversubscribed round, "rapid customer adoption," and multiple expansion contracts across hundreds of real-world deployments)
technical_differentiation: Versus Physical Intelligence and Skild AI (both pursuing data-scaled VLA generalist "brains"), FieldAI takes a "physics-first" approach: its Field Foundation Models incorporate a Belief World Model that natively reasons about physics, risk, and uncertainty for map-free, risk-aware navigation in unstructured environments across embodiments, rather than retrofitting vision/language models or relying chiefly on massive sim/crowd-sourced data scale.

one_liner: Builds robot foundation models (Vision-Language-Action and World Action Models) plus full-stack hardware to enable general-purpose embodied manipulation by real-world and humanoid robots.
last_round_lead: Lens Technology (among ~20 investors; round also incl. Financial Street Capital, CICC Capital, GL Ventures)
notes: Beijing-based embodied/robotics AI startup, founded 2023. Raised ~$290.4M Series B+ in April 2026 (~2B yuan) valuing it ~$29B (note: some sources report valuation above 20B yuan/~$2.9B vs $29B headline discrepancy). Followed a ~$140M Series B (1B yuan) in Feb 2026 and a $100M round in 2025 (~$700M valuation). Develops VLA and World Action Models, open-sourced three VLA models. Builds full-stack: robot hardware (arms, humanoids), perception, and foundation models. Lens Technology is hardware/mass-production partner and investor. Total funding approximate. Suzhou also referenced as a location.
business_model: Sells full-stack embodied-AI products: wheeled dual-arm and humanoid robot platforms (R1, R1 Pro, R1 Lite) plus its VLA/World-Action foundation models, sold into automotive and logistics customers for manipulation tasks, and licensed/provided to research labs and AI firms for algorithm training and real-world embodied-data collection. No recorded revenue in 2024; projected tens of millions of yuan in 2025 with profitability targeted for 2026.
customers: Reported 40+ clients using its robots/data for training, deployment and data collection, including Huawei Cloud, Volkswagen, Haier, Samsung, ByteDance, Physical Intelligence, Stanford University, and MIT; wheeled dual-arm platform entered large-scale deployment with automotive and logistics customers in H2 2025 (specific buyers undisclosed).
revenue_status: early-revenue (no revenue in 2024; projected tens of millions of yuan in 2025 sales, targeting profitability in 2026; raised ~$290M Series B+ in April 2026 at ~$29B/CNY200B valuation; ~$596M total raised - valuation figures unverified across sources)
technical_differentiation: Builds a real-world embodied data engine paired with co-developed VLA and World-Action Models and its own hardware, versus competitor Galbot which relies primarily on simulation-generated training data; also distinct from Physical Intelligence (a customer/partner) by coupling models tightly with self-built full-stack robot bodies rather than being model-only. Trade-off is higher data-collection cost and slower scaling than simulation-first peers.

one_liner: Builds embodied foundation models (GEN-1) giving general-purpose robots of any form factor dexterous, reliable real-world manipulation skills that adapt across tasks and environments.
notes: Founded 2024 by Pete Florence (CEO), Andy Zeng (Chief Scientist), Andrew Barry (CTO); team from OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Boston Dynamics. June 2026 $400M round at ~$2B valuation led by Radical Ventures; new investors 8VC, Union Square Ventures, Hanabi Capital, Norwest; existing NVIDIA NVentures, Boldstart, Spark Capital, Bezos Expeditions, NFDG; angels Eric Yuan, Bin Lin, Fei-Fei Li, Naval Ravikant. Total funding >$500M. GEN-1 embodied foundation model reports 99% task success vs prior 64%, ~3x faster on dexterous manipulation. Building physical data engine + large-scale compute. Team size not disclosed.
business_model: Sells its GEN-1 embodied foundation model plus deployment to industrial operators, manufacturers, and logistics firms via forward-deployed pilot fees and integration/deployment contracts (resembling applied-AI engagements more than SaaS subscriptions) rather than selling robot hardware. Targets dexterous workflows like auto-parts kitting, box folding, and electronics packing.
customers: Undisclosed - Sacra reports early customers are manufacturers and logistics operators with repetitive-but-variable manual workflows, but no specific paying/piloting organizations are named in public sources.
revenue_status: early-revenue (revenue figures not publicly disclosed; reportedly has early customers using GEN-1 on deployment/pilot contracts; raised $400M at $2B valuation in June 2026, >$500M total)
technical_differentiation: Versus Physical Intelligence and Skild AI (the closest hardware-agnostic robot-brain labs), Generalist pretrains GEN-1 entirely on ~500K hours of human data and only touches real robot hardware in a final ~1 hour of task-specific adaptation, claiming 99% task reliability and ~3x faster execution; Physical Intelligence instead relies on staged imitation plus autonomous RL on real robots, and Skild emphasizes a robot-agnostic "Skild Brain" with OEM partnerships.

one_liner: Builds a universal robotics foundation model (GENE-26.5) and full-stack platform for general-purpose physical AI, using a proprietary physics engine to generate synthetic training data for dexterous manipulation.
notes: Founded December 2024 by Zhou Xian (CMU robotics PhD) and Theophile Gervet (ex-Mistral). Dual HQ in Palo Alto/Silicon Valley and Paris; also expanded to London. Additional seed backers: Bpifrance, HongShan (HSG), Eric Schmidt, Xavier Niel, Daniela Rus, Vladlen Koltun. Unveiled GENE-26.5 foundation model in May 2026 with a dexterous robotic hand (built with Wuji Tech) demoing cooking, Rubik's cube, piano. Went full-stack into hardware including a 1:1 sensor data glove for low-cost data collection. Primary layer L1 robot foundation models; also touches simulation (physics engine), data infra (synthetic data), and hardware.
business_model: Plans to monetize a full-stack robotics foundation model (GENE-26.5) plus proprietary hardware: licensing the AI model and human-scale dexterous robotic hand to robotics/automation customers on multi-year engagements (3-5 years), and selling/licensing its data-collection glove to pharma, manufacturing, and lab operators. Commercialization terms are still being finalized ("haven't nailed the details yet").
customers: Undisclosed. Founders say they are "signing customers" and "in talks with a lot of customers" for the data-collection glove (pharma, manufacturing, lab settings), but no paying or piloting organizations are named publicly.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (raised $105M seed co-led by Eclipse and Khosla Ventures; no revenue disclosed, commercialization terms not yet finalized as of May 2026)
technical_differentiation: Vs. Physical Intelligence and Skild AI (both building robot foundation models), Genesis AI differentiates via a data-centric, fully in-house physics-simulation/data engine combining high-fidelity synthetic data with real-robot data, plus a vertically integrated "full-stack" approach pairing the model with a purpose-built human-scale dexterous robotic hand for direct human-to-robot skill transfer, where Physical Intelligence and Skild focus primarily on model software.

one_liner: Builds robot foundation models (RT-1/2/X, Open X-Embodiment, Gemini Robotics VLA) that let robots see, reason, and act on general manipulation tasks across embodiments.
notes: DeepMind founded Nov 2010 by Demis Hassabis, Shane Legg, Mustafa Suleyman; acquired by Google in 2014 (reported ~$400-650M); merged with Google Brain to form Google DeepMind in April 2023. Now a division of Alphabet, so no standalone VC funding rounds apply. HQ at 6 Pancras Square, King's Cross, London, with major US operations (Mountain View). Robotics work: RT-1 (2022), RT-2 (2023), RT-X/Open X-Embodiment (2023), Gemini Robotics + Gemini Robotics-ER (Mar 2025), Gemini Robotics On-Device and 1.5 (2025). [Merged duplicate: Google DeepMind (MuJoCo)] Open-source contact-rich physics engine simulating articulated structures fast and accurately, enabling robot reinforcement learning, control research, and sim-to-real transfer.
business_model: Licenses robot foundation models (Gemini Robotics VLA, Gemini Robotics-ER embodied-reasoning) to robot makers, with the ER model exposed via the paid Gemini Developer API (token-based, e.g. ~$0.10/M input tokens for Flash-Lite tier; free in Google AI Studio). Robotics is a strategic AI program inside Alphabet rather than a standalone P&L; Google also takes equity stakes in partner robot makers (e.g., participating in Apptronik's $350M round and subsequent Series A extension).
customers: Hardware partners and trusted testers integrating Gemini Robotics: Apptronik (Apollo humanoid, also a Google investment), Apptronik plus Boston Dynamics (next-gen Atlas), Agility Robotics, Agile Robots (announced March 2026), Enchanted Tools, and Franka/ALOHA platforms. These are integration/co-development partners; explicit per-deal licensing fees are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: undisclosed (no standalone robotics revenue broken out; ER model monetized via Gemini API token pricing, but robotics-specific figures not public)
technical_differentiation: vs. Physical Intelligence (pi-0/pi-0.5) and Skild AI: DeepMind's edge is building the VLA directly on the frontier Gemini multimodal model (broad world knowledge, language reasoning, embodied-reasoning spatial grounding) plus the Open X-Embodiment cross-robot dataset, enabling one model to transfer across many embodiments. Backed by Alphabet's compute and equity stakes in the hardware makers it serves, competing largely on model quality and capital rather than owning robot hardware.

company_name: Huawei Robotics / Wireless Technology Lab (Huawei CloudRobo)
also_known_as: Huawei CloudRobo; Huawei Cloud Embodied AI Platform
one_liner: Cloud-based embodied-AI platform (Pangu-powered) providing cross-robot foundation models for perception, planning and manipulation execution, plus synthetic-data, simulation and edge-cloud deployment for partner robots.
notes: CloudRobo is a Huawei Cloud platform unveiled at Huawei Developer Conference 2025 on 2025-06-23; Huawei Cloud explicitly will NOT build robot hardware ("leave hardware to partners") and instead supplies cloud foundation models (embodied multimodal generation, planning, execution), synthetic data, simulation and edge-cloud deployment via R2C protocol, claiming >90% task-execution accuracy. Separately, Huawei Wireless Technology Lab (authors Guangjian Wang, Wen Tong et al.) publishes VLA/robot-foundation-model research tied to 6G and the MELISAC dual-arm prototype. Funding/round/team-size are N/A: Huawei is a privately held employee-owned company founded 1987 (Shenzhen), so CloudRobo is an internal division with no separate funding. Layer 1 (robot foundation models) confirmed as primary; secondary layers cover its simulation, data-infra and middleware (R2C) offerings. Corroborating: huaweicloud.com CloudRobo/Pangu 5.5 announcements and huawei.com Wireless Technology Lab paper "Robots Empowered by AI Foundation Models and the Opportunities for 6G".
business_model: Consumption-based cloud service: monetizes Pangu-powered embodied-AI foundation models (multimodal generation, planning, execution) and an end-to-end toolchain (synthetic data, labeling, simulation, cloud-edge deployment) sold to robot OEMs via Huawei Cloud compute/API subscriptions, with the Robot-to-Cloud (R2C) protocol locking partners into Huawei's Ascend cloud. Revenue is bundled into Huawei Cloud's broader AI/compute (CloudMatrix, Pangu) and robotics-ecosystem business rather than standalone hardware sales.
customers: Robotics OEM partners/pilots include UBTECH, Leju Robotics, Flexiv, Yijiahe, Hualong, Avant Robotics, Topstar, and Zhaowei (first 20 R2C-protocol partners plus 16 MOU signatories); deployed via the Shenzhen Bao'an Embodied AI Industry Innovation Center across 100+ ecosystem enterprises. Specific paying-vs-pilot breakdown not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: undisclosed (not broken out; folded into Huawei Cloud revenue, which exceeded ~RMB 68.7B/~USD 9.5B in 2024 unverified-allocation)
technical_differentiation: Positions on a cloud-centric "brain-in-the-cloud" architecture (R2C protocol offloading perception/planning to Pangu models on Ascend, claiming 90%+ task accuracy and sub-100-micron arm precision), versus NVIDIA Isaac GR00T's on-device, Apache-2.0 open VLA foundation model running on Jetson Thor edge hardware. Unlike GR00T's open-weight/edge-inference approach, Huawei differentiates via vertical China-stack integration (Ascend silicon, Huawei Cloud, Pangu world model for synthetic data) and a closed ecosystem protocol.

one_liner: Open-source AI platform whose LeRobot division builds robot-learning tools and open vision-language-action models (e.g. SmolVLA) that run on consumer hardware to make robotics AI accessible.
notes: Founded 2016 in NYC by Clement Delangue, Julien Chaumond, Thomas Wolf; began as teen chatbot then pivoted to ML. Series D Aug 2023, $235M, led by Salesforce at $4.5B valuation; total ~$400M over 8 rounds (no later round publicly reported as of mid-2026). Robotics arm = LeRobot, launched 2024 led by ex-Tesla Remi Cadene; released SmolVLA (450M open VLA, June 2025, runs on a MacBook) and acquired French robotics maker Pollen Robotics (2025) to sell open hardware. Robotics is a division of the broader open-source ML company. Sources: Wikipedia, TechCrunch (2025-06-04 SmolVLA; 2023-08-24 Series D), HF blog (Pollen acquisition).
business_model: Hugging Face monetizes its open-source AI Hub via Pro ($9/mo) and Enterprise ($20/user/mo) subscriptions, pay-per-use Inference Providers/API fees, and cloud-partner referral revenue (AWS, Azure); the LeRobot/Pollen Robotics division adds robot hardware sales such as the ~$100 SO-100/SO-101 arms, a ~$2,500 LeRobot humanoid kit, and the Reachy 2 robot at ~$70,000, while the models (e.g. SmolVLA) themselves are free and open.
customers: Hub Enterprise has 2,000+ paying organizations (e.g. Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, Intel, Grammarly are public Hugging Face customers/partners). On the robotics side, Reachy 2 units are deployed in research labs including Cornell and Carnegie Mellon.
revenue_status: scaling (company-wide ~$130M revenue in 2024, up from ~$70M in 2023; LeRobot/robotics hardware is an early-stage line within this, revenue not broken out)
technical_differentiation: vs Physical Intelligence (pi0/pi0.5) and NVIDIA (GR00T N1.5): LeRobot differentiates on a fully open, community-data-driven stack and extreme efficiency, with its 450M-parameter SmolVLA running on a single consumer GPU and reportedly outperforming much larger VLAs, plus uniquely low-cost open hardware (~$100 SO-100 arm) tying models to accessible robots rather than proprietary fleets.

one_liner: Builds embodied-AI 'eye-brain-hand' systems combining 3D vision cameras, deep learning, and motion planning so industrial robots can perceive, plan, and manipulate objects like bin-picking and palletizing.
notes: Founded 2016, HQ Beijing, China. Last publicly reported round was a $155M Series C+ in Sep 2021 led by Meituan and IDG Capital, with prior investors including Sequoia Capital China, Source Code Capital, Qiming Venture Partners, and Intel Capital. Team size ~600 (PitchBook); Tracxn lists 501-1,000 as of July 2024. Total funding not consistently disclosed across sources (some aggregate ~$314M). Products: industrial 3D cameras, Mech-Vision/Mech-Viz software, deep learning tools for bin picking, palletizing, assembly. Genuine AI-for-physical-robot manipulation; qualifies for L1 robot foundation models via embodied-AI manipulation models, with strong hardware (L4), middleware (L5), and industrial vertical (L6) components.
business_model: Sells industrial 3D vision hardware (Mech-Eye cameras) bundled with a software suite (Mech-Vision, Mech-DLK deep learning, Mech-Viz motion planning) to robot system integrators and manufacturers for applications like bin-picking, depalletizing, and machine tending; layers on installation, training, customization, and maintenance services. Venture-backed (over $314M raised, including a ~$155M Series C led by IDG Capital and Meituan).
customers: Named customers/partners include BMW, DHL, Honeywell, Siemens, and Yaskawa, spanning automotive OEMs, logistics, home appliances, food/beverage, and pharmacy; reports 1,500+ clients and 10,000+ cameras shipped across 50+ countries.
revenue_status: scaling (specific revenue not publicly disclosed; private, ~500 employees, 1,500+ clients, no IPO)
technical_differentiation: Versus pure 3D-camera vendors Photoneo (parallel structured-light, fast motion capture) and Zivid (HDR full-color point clouds for reflective/transparent parts), Mech-Mind differentiates as a full 'eye-brain-hand' stack — pairing its own cameras with integrated deep-learning segmentation/pose-estimation and motion-planning software rather than selling sensors alone — reportedly achieving 99%+ pick success in production.

also_known_as: Meta AI FAIR; Fundamental AI Research; Facebook AI Research
one_liner: Research division building embodied-AI foundation models (visual cortex VC-1, tactile Sparsh, Motivo behavior model, Digit Plexus tactile hardware) to give robots perception, touch, and dexterous manipulation.
notes: FAIR is Meta Platforms' internal research lab (no standalone funding/rounds; funded by Meta). Founded 2013 as Facebook AI Research (FAIR), now Fundamental AI Research. Embodied-AI/robotics outputs include VC-1 visual cortex (2023), Sparsh tactile encoder, Digit 360/Digit Plexus tactile hardware (with GelSight and Wonik Robotics), and Motivo behavior foundation model. HQ Menlo Park, CA; additional labs in Paris, London, NYC, Pittsburgh, Montreal, etc. Note 2025 reports of FAIR restructuring/cuts amid Meta Superintelligence Labs reorganization. Funding fields undisclosed because it is a corporate division, not a VC-backed company.
business_model: Internal research division of Meta Platforms; generates no direct revenue. It open-sources its embodied-AI artifacts (VC-1, Sparsh, Motivo, Digit 360, Digit Plexus) to seed an ecosystem, while Meta's actual revenue comes from advertising on its consumer apps; the robotics research is a long-term strategic bet feeding into AR/VR, wearables, and world models rather than a standalone product line.
customers: No paying customers for the robotics research itself. Distribution/manufacturing partners named publicly are GelSight Inc. (commercializing Digit 360 tactile sensor) and Wonik Robotics (manufacturing the next-gen Allegro Hand integrating Digit Plexus); academic collaborators include University of Washington and Carnegie Mellon (Sparsh). End "customers" are the open research community, not paying clients.
revenue_status: N/A (research division of for-profit Meta Platforms; the embodied-AI/robotics unit is pre-revenue and not separately monetized; Meta corporate revenue ~$160B+/yr from advertising, unverified attribution to FAIR)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via deep multimodal tactile/touch perception (Digit 360 human-level fingertip, Sparsh tactile foundation encoders, Digit Plexus standardized sensor-integration platform) released fully open-source, versus Google DeepMind (RT-2/Gemini Robotics, largely closed VLA models) and NVIDIA (GR00T/Isaac, vendor-locked simulation+hardware stack). Meta's edge is open hardware-software co-design for touch and dexterity rather than proprietary end-to-end robot platforms.

also_known_as: MSR Autonomous Systems; Microsoft Research Asia embodied AI; MSR Asia / MSR Asia Tokyo embodied AI
hq_city: Redmond, WA (embodied AI groups in Beijing and Tokyo)
one_liner: Corporate research division developing large action and vision-language-action foundation models so robots can perform embodied perception-reasoning-action, including dexterous manipulation, from internet-scale video and 3D data.
notes: Microsoft Research is an internal R&D division of Microsoft Corporation, founded 1991 by Richard Rashid, Bill Gates and Nathan Myhrvold; HQ at Redmond, WA. It is not a standalone funded startup, so funding/round/team-size fields are not applicable (undisclosed/unknown). Embodied AI and large robotics-model work is concentrated in Microsoft Research Asia (Beijing) and the new MSR Asia Tokyo lab (announced Nov 2024), which lists embodied AI as a core focus. Research covers VLA models, latent/large action models, internet-scale video pretraining, 3D vision-language datasets, RL-based dexterous robotic hands, and the IGOR (Image-GOal Representations) project. This is genuine learning applied to physical robots (manipulation/dexterous hands), so real_robotics_ai=true. Founded year, name, and one-liner all sourced from microsoft.com and Wikipedia.
business_model: No direct revenue; it is a cost-center corporate research division of Microsoft, funded by parent-company R&D budget. Outputs (e.g., the Rho-alpha VLA model, derived from Microsoft's Phi vision-language series) are intended to feed Microsoft's commercial products (Azure cloud/AI, simulation tooling) and are offered to partners via a Research Early Access Program rather than sold standalone.
customers: No paying customers; research collaborators and early-access partners only. Named: NVIDIA (Isaac Sim synthetic data), University of Washington (Prof. Abhishek Gupta), and Johns Hopkins APL (multi-robot AI agent demo). Microsoft is recruiting robotics manufacturers, integrators, and end users into a Research Early Access Program; specific commercial customers are undisclosed.
revenue_status: N/A (corporate research division; parent Microsoft revenue ~$245B FY2024, but this unit generates no direct/attributable revenue)
technical_differentiation: Rho-alpha is derived from Microsoft's in-house Phi small-language-model family and adds tactile/force sensing to the standard vision-language-action stack (most rivals are vision+language only), trained heavily on NVIDIA Isaac Sim synthetic data for bimanual manipulation. Versus Google DeepMind (Gemini Robotics/RT-2) and Physical Intelligence (pi-0), it leans on a lightweight efficient-model lineage and multimodal tactile fusion rather than scaling the largest VLM backbone; still pre-product and under evaluation, so differentiation is largely research-stage.

also_known_as: Project GR00T, Isaac GR00T, NVIDIA Robotics
one_liner: Builds GR00T open vision-language-action foundation models and Cosmos world models that let generalist humanoid robots learn dexterous manipulation from human video and simulation.
notes: GR00T is a robotics program/division within NVIDIA, a publicly traded company (NASDAQ: NVDA), not a separately funded startup, so funding/round fields are N/A. GR00T N1 announced March 2025 as the world's first open humanoid robot foundation model; N1.7 (Open Reasoning VLA, released ~late 2025/2026) pre-trained on 20,854 hours of EgoScale human egocentric video plus robot demos, with finger-level dexterous control and commercial licensing on Jetson/Ampere/Hopper/Lovelace/Blackwell. Accompanied by Cosmos world model platform and Isaac/Omniverse simulation. Assigned primary layer L1 Robot Foundation Models is correct; NVIDIA also spans simulation (Isaac Sim/Omniverse), data infra (synthetic data, Omniverse Blueprints), hardware (Jetson/Thor compute, reference humanoid design), and middleware (Isaac ROS). Founded April 5, 1993 by Jensen Huang, Chris Malachowsky, Curtis Priem; HQ Santa Clara, California, US. [Merged duplicate: NVIDIA Isaac Sim / Isaac Lab] GPU-accelerated robotics simulation platform and learning framework (RL/IL) built on Omniverse PhysX/Newton for high-fidelity, photorealistic sim-to-real robot policy training. [Merged duplicate: NVIDIA Omniverse] OpenUSD-based simulation and digital-twin platform plus Cosmos world foundation models that generate physically accurate synthetic data to train and sim-to-real transfer robot policies.
business_model: NVIDIA gives away the GR00T VLA and Cosmos world models as open weights (Apache 2.0) to seed demand for the paid stack underneath: Jetson Thor edge inference modules, RTX/Blackwell GPUs and DGX systems for training, and enterprise licenses for Isaac Sim/Lab and Omniverse simulation. Robotics is a razor-and-blades play to sell silicon and full-stack software, not to monetize the models directly.
customers: Named GR00T/Isaac adopters and early-access partners include 1X Technologies, Agility Robotics, Boston Dynamics, NEURA Robotics (4NE1 humanoid), Mentee Robotics, Figure AI, Apptronik, Franka Robotics, ABB, FANUC, KUKA, Universal Robots and Yaskawa; collaborators include Google DeepMind, Disney Research and Hugging Face (LeRobot).
revenue_status: scaling (NVIDIA total FY revenue ~$130B; robotics/automotive segment is a small but fast-growing line, roughly $1.7B in automotive+robotics for FY2025 - models themselves are free, revenue flows through GPU/Jetson hardware)
technical_differentiation: Versus Physical Intelligence (pi-0/pi-0.5) and Skild AI, NVIDIA differentiates by owning the entire physical-AI stack rather than just a model: open commercially-licensable weights plus the Cosmos world-model/synthetic-data pipeline, Isaac Sim/Lab simulation, and Jetson Thor hardware for on-robot inference. Its dual-system Action Cascade (Cosmos-Reason VLM + diffusion-transformer motor policy) is hardware-agnostic across many humanoid OEMs, whereas Physical Intelligence and Skild ship models tied to fewer embodiments and lack the chip/sim flywheel.

one_liner: Builds autoregressive, general-purpose interactive world models that stream explorable 3D video frame-by-frame, usable for robotics, AV, gaming, and simulation training.
notes: Founded by self-driving pioneers Oliver Cameron (ex-Cruise VP, ex-Voyage) and Jeff Hawke (ex-Wayve). Seed: $9M led by GV (Jul 2024); Series A: $18M led by EQT Ventures (Nov 2024); total ~$27M before the Feb 2026 NVentures/Samsung Next strategic investment (size undisclosed; some reports of a larger raise unverified). Backers include GV, EQT, Air Street Capital, DCVC, Elad Gil, Jeff Dean, Garry Tan, Kyle Vogt, Ed Catmull. Product: Odyssey-2 Pro general-purpose world model targeting robotics, AV, gaming, education, defense. HQ Santa Clara, CA per Tracxn/Crunchbase. Founding year reported as 2023 (Tracxn); one source said "founded two years ago" implying ~2024. Team recruited from DeepMind, OpenAI, Tesla, Waymo, Wayve, Luma. Qualifies as real robotics AI via world-model simulation/data for robot and AV training, though it is a world-model/sim infrastructure play rather than a robot hardware maker.
business_model: Pre-revenue / early commercialization: builds proprietary real-time interactive world models (Odyssey-2 Pro, Starchild-1, Agora-1) and is monetizing via a developer API ("coming soon") that lets companies in robotics, gaming, AV, education, and defense integrate streaming explorable 3D worlds and generate synthetic training environments (e.g., integration with NVIDIA Isaac Sim). Currently offers free web research previews (experience.odyssey.ml) rather than paid product tiers.
customers: No named paying customers publicly disclosed; relationships are investor/ecosystem partnerships (NVIDIA via NVentures, Samsung Next) and a technical integration with NVIDIA Isaac Sim for robotics training. Paying/piloting customers: undisclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (API not yet generally available; ~$27M+ total funding raised including $18M Series A led by EQT Ventures, plus investment from NVentures/NVIDIA, Samsung Next, GV, Air Street Capital, and angels including Jeff Dean, Elad Gil, Garry Tan, Kyle Vogt, Guillermo Rauch)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on autoregressive, real-time interactive video that streams explorable frames (~40ms/frame, 720p/22fps) with multimodal synchronized audio (Starchild-1) and multi-agent shared-world coherence via separating world state from rendering (Agora-1) — vs. World Labs' Marble, which produces persistent/downloadable static 3D scenes rather than live-controllable streams, and vs. Google DeepMind's Genie 3, a comparable real-time interactive world model but closed/not API-accessible. Also differentiated by purpose-built backpack multi-sensor (6 cameras, 2 lidars, IMU) real-world data capture rig.

one_liner: Builds general-purpose robot foundation models (π0/π0.5 flow-matching VLA) that let diverse robots perform real-world manipulation tasks like folding laundry and bagging groceries.
notes: Founded 2024 by Sergey Levine, Karol Hausman, Chelsea Finn, Brian Ichter, Quan Vuong, Lachy Groom, Adnan Esmail (ex-DeepMind/Stanford/Berkeley). Seed $70M (Mar 2024); Series A $400M Nov 2024 at ~$2.4B (OpenAI, Bezos, Sequoia, Khosla, Thrive, Lux); Series B $600M Nov 2025 at $5.6B led by CapitalG. As of 2026, reportedly in talks for ~$1B raise at $11B valuation (Founders Fund/Lightspeed). Genuine learning applied to physical robot manipulation via π0/π0.5 cross-embodiment VLA foundation models.
business_model: Licenses/provides its general-purpose robot foundation models (π0/π0.5/π0.6 VLA models) as a reusable "physical intelligence layer" that robotics partners integrate into their own hardware and fine-tune with proprietary data; reportedly moving toward a per-robot subscription (one third-party source cites ~$300/month per connected robot, unverified). Still research-first with no fully released commercial product as of early 2026.
customers: Named deployment partners include Ultra (warehouse/e-commerce order packaging robots deployed across the US) and Weave (laundry-folding robots in a live San Francisco laundromat and Bay Area businesses). Whether these are paying licensees vs. co-development partners is not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (multiple sources describe PI as having "no commercial products or revenue" as of early 2026; ~$1.07B-$1.1B raised across seed/Series A/Series B, latest reported valuation ~$5.6B-$11B, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Hardware-agnostic, cross-embodiment foundation models using flow-matching action generation atop a VLM backbone, positioned as a shared "robot brain" layer reusable across many robot types — versus Skild AI (also building a general-purpose robot foundation model/"robot brain" but less publicly benchmarked on dexterous tasks like laundry folding) and Google DeepMind's RT-2/Gemini Robotics (tied more closely to Google's own platforms). PI's edge is open research output (open-sourced π0 weights/openpi) plus demonstrated generalist dexterous manipulation.

one_liner: Builds a force-centered embodied 'brain' large model combining multimodal models with force-feedback data, enabling general robots to learn manipulation skills like grasping at low cost.
notes: Founded Nov 2023, strategically incubated by Flexiv (非夕科技). Co-founded by Lu Cewu (卢策吾), deputy dean of SJTU School of AI, and Wang Shiquan (Stanford PhD, Flexiv founder). Flagship product Noematrix Brain (now 2.0) is a force-centered embodied brain spanning data collection, pre-training, deployment. Funding rounds: Angel (led by Xiaomiao Langcheng), Pre-A (Prosperity7 + GF Xinde, Sep 2024), Pre-A+ (Sequoia China), Oct 2025 round led by Alibaba, Series A Feb 2026 led by C Capital (with Sea Limited, Puhua Capital, Prosperity7 follow-on). Total funding reported to exceed RMB 1B (~$137M). Team size not disclosed publicly. Additional sources: https://eu.36kr.com/en/p/3065605732541830 ; https://en.tmtpost.com/post/7735640 ; https://finance.sina.cn/stock/jdts/2026-02-10/detail-inhmhvwr8650447.d.html
business_model: Sells its "Noematrix Brain" force-centered embodied-AI software stack (large models, atomic skill library, software framework and developer toolchain) plus integrated robot solutions to industrial and service-robot deployers, monetized via per-scenario deployment orders and software/platform licensing; tightly coupled to robot hardware from strategic incubator/affiliate Flexiv (非夕科技). Early commercial traction reported as single-scenario orders exceeding 100 units.
customers: Largely undisclosed by name. Reported deployments/pilots span manufacturing (automotive seat processing, paint grinding via Flexiv adaptive robots), retail operations, logistics picking, and pharmaceutical sorting; specific paying customer organizations are not publicly named beyond the Flexiv ecosystem relationship.
revenue_status: early-revenue (single-scenario orders reported >100 units; no revenue figure publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a "force-centered" foundation model that fuses multimodal perception with force-feedback/tactile data (leveraging Flexiv's force-control adaptive robots) plus a dual-model architecture (physical-world model + robot-behavior model), targeting contact-rich manipulation. Versus vision-action-centric Chinese peers like X Square Robot (WALL-A) and Galbot, the force/tactile emphasis is the main claimed edge; co-founder Lu Cewu's academic pedigree adds credibility but the manipulation-foundation-model space is crowded, making execution and force-data moat the key bet (some specifics unverified).

one_liner: Builds robot foundation models that pre-train on internet-scale video, then use closed-loop video-predictive control (Direct Video Action) to run commercial/industrial robots in real-world manufacturing and logistics.
notes: Exited 18 months of stealth March 2026 with $450M Series A at $1.7B valuation. Lead: Premji Invest (Managing Partner Sandesh Patnam). Other investors: Khosla Ventures, Temasek, Capricorn, Leitmotif, Matter Venture Partners, Mayfield, Prelude Ventures, Xora, and John Doerr. Co-founders: CEO Jagdeep Singh (serial deep-tech founder) and CSO Eric Ryan Chan (ex-World Labs/Stanford). Foundation model branded "FutureVision"; architecture "Direct Video Action (DVA)". Pre-trained on 1M+ videos; learns new tasks with ~10 hours teleoperation data. HQ reported as Palo Alto by most sources; PitchBook lists San Jose. Founding year 2024 corroborated by PitchBook/Crunchbase summaries.
business_model: Sells robotic automation systems powered by its FutureVision foundation model to industrial customers (manufacturing, logistics, automotive, ecommerce), with a stated plan to also license the model as an "intelligence layer" to third-party robot hardware/software makers over time. Currently focused on deploying its own robot systems for production workflows.
customers: Undisclosed - the company cites "leading industrial partners" across automotive, manufacturing, logistics, and ecommerce, and one high-volume manufacturing evaluation (component-processing in under two minutes/cycle exceeding customer KPIs), but no customer organizations are named publicly as of the March 2026 stealth exit.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no figures public; running paid production evaluations/deployments with industrial customers after March 2026 stealth exit; raised $450M Series A at a $1.7B valuation led by Premji Invest)
technical_differentiation: Vs. Physical Intelligence (pi0/VLA action models) and Skild AI, Rhoda's "Direct Video Action" (DVA) architecture skips an explicit language/VLA layer and instead pre-trains on internet-scale video, predicts future states directly as video, and converts those into actions in a closed loop every few hundred milliseconds for physics-aware control. This video-predictive, closed-loop approach contrasts with the more open-loop, language-conditioned VLA policies of its peers, though the differentiation is unverified beyond company claims.

one_liner: Builds dexterity-first robotics foundation models (RLDX-1) that let high-DoF robot hands and humanoids grasp, pour, and use tools autonomously across diverse industrial embodiments.
notes: Founded 2024 in Seoul by serial entrepreneur Jung-hee (Joong-hee) Ryu; KAIST professor Jinwoo Shin is Chief Scientist. Seed 1 raised $14.8M (Apr 2025, led by Hashed); Seed 2 raised $26M (announced Feb 2026), total ~$41M. NVIDIA-, LG-, and AWS-backed; strategic investors include SK Telecom, LG Electronics, CJ Logistics, Lotte, KDDI, ANA Holdings, Kakao. Unveiled RLDX-1 dexterity-first foundation model in May 2026 for robot hands/humanoids. Announced relocation of HQ to the US (Feb 2026) to become a global physical-AI company; primary R&D/operations and origin remain Seoul, with a Tokyo office. Last_round_lead reflects Seed 2 financial investors. Founded_year, company_name, and one-liner each sourced. Sources: GlobeNewswire (funding), TechCrunch (Apr 2025 seed), The Robot Report (RLDX-1, $26M), Dealroom, Seoul Economic Daily (HQ relocation).
business_model: Develops the RLDX-1 dexterity-first robotics foundation model (8.1B params) and monetizes via enterprise "Robotics Transformation (RX)" engagements - joint benchmark development, proof-of-concept deployments, and licensing/deploying its policies onto partners' robot embodiments (e.g. WIRobotics Allex humanoid, Franka). Open checkpoints are published on Hugging Face for adoption, with revenue intended from industrial/logistics/hospitality automation projects rather than from selling the open weights themselves.
customers: Running PoC/RX and benchmark projects with 10+ large enterprise partners; named: CJ Logistics (Nov 2025 MOU + equity), Lotte Hotel (lead hospitality operator in Korea's K-Humanoid Alliance, back-of-house humanoid deployment targeted 2029), plus strategic backers/partners LG Electronics, SK Telecom, KDDI, ANA Holdings, Mitsui Chemicals, and Shimadzu. Hardware partners include WIRobotics, Enactic, Origami Robotics, Proception AI, and NVIDIA.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no revenue disclosed; RLDX-1 unveiled May 2026 and engagements are PoC/MOU stage. ~$41M total seed funding raised: $15M Seed 1 in Apr 2025 + $26M Seed 2 in Feb 2026)
technical_differentiation: Positions RLDX-1 as "dexterity-first" - optimized for high-DoF robot hands (grasping, pouring, tool use) rather than whole-body locomotion, and reports beating Physical Intelligence's pi-0/pi-0.5 and NVIDIA's GR00T N1.5/N1.6 on manipulation benchmarks, its two closest named competitors in the robot foundation model layer. Differentiation is the hand-manipulation focus plus human-technique training data (body-cam-instrumented workers) and multi-embodiment portability; durability vs better-capitalized PI and NVIDIA is unproven.

also_known_as: RAI Institute; The AI Institute; formerly Boston Dynamics AI Institute
one_liner: Research institute developing reinforcement-learning and generalizable mobile-manipulation policies for humanoid and quadruped robots to make machines smarter, more capable, and useful in human environments.
last_round_lead: Hyundai Motor Group and Boston Dynamics
notes: Founded August 2022 as Boston Dynamics AI Institute; rebranded to The AI Institute then Robotics & AI Institute (RAI Institute). Led by Marc Raibert (Boston Dynamics founder), serving as Executive Director. Initial investment of more than $400M from Hyundai Motor Group and Boston Dynamics. HQ at 145 Broadway, Kendall Square, Cambridge, MA, with a second office in Zurich, Switzerland. Research spans cognitive AI, athletic AI, organic hardware design, and robot ethics. Partners with Boston Dynamics on accelerating Atlas humanoid learning via RL. Known projects include Roadrunner and Ultra Mobility Vehicle quadruped. Funding is strategic/corporate rather than venture VC rounds; no subsequent disclosed external round found.
business_model: N/A (research/open-source) - it is a research institute, not a revenue-generating business. It is funded by a single corporate sponsor, Hyundai Motor Group, which committed an initial investment of over $400 million to launch it in 2022; Hyundai (which also owns Boston Dynamics) bankrolls long-horizon, pre-commercial R&D in cognitive AI, athletic AI, and robot hardware rather than monetizing products.
customers: No paying customers; it operates as a sponsored research lab. Its primary external counterpart is Boston Dynamics (a sister Hyundai-owned company), with which it partnered in Feb 2025 to apply reinforcement learning to the Atlas humanoid. It also has a research framework agreement with ETH Zurich (Marco Hutter's group, new Zurich office) and buys Unitree quadrupeds for testing. No commercial buyers disclosed.
revenue_status: N/A (corporate-funded research institute; ~$400M+ initial Hyundai funding, no product revenue)
