technical_differentiation: Differentiates via deep legged-locomotion and reinforcement-learning expertise under Marc Raibert (Boston Dynamics founder) and Marco Hutter (ANYmal/ETH), focusing on generalizable RL policies for humanoids and quadrupeds rather than shipping commercial robots. Unlike Layer-1 RFM peers such as Boston Dynamics (its own commercialization-focused sister company) and Physical Intelligence/Skild AI, it competes on long-horizon, non-commercial research freedom backed by patient single-sponsor capital rather than near-term product or paying customers.

also_known_as: RunwayML; Runway AI, Inc.; GWM-1; GWM Robotics
one_liner: Generative video-AI lab whose GWM-1 general world model serves robotics: action-conditioned video rollouts act as a simulator, synthetic-data generator, and policy prior for training and evaluating robot policies.
notes: Core business is generative video/image foundation models (Gen-4.5); robotics is a product line (GWM Robotics) under the GWM-1 general world model family, an autoregressive model built on Gen-4.5 that generates action-conditioned, multi-view video rollouts used as a robot simulator, synthetic training-data generator, and visual representation/policy prior. Robotics qualifies it for Layer 1. Founded 2018, HQ New York (US). Total funding ~$1.05B per PitchBook/Sacra; latest Series E $315M at $5.3B valuation announced 2026-02-10 led by General Atlantic (also NVIDIA, AMD Ventures, Nvidia, Felicis, etc.); prior Series D $308M April 2025 also led by General Atlantic. Team size not reliably found (older getLatka figures outdated); listed unknown. Sources: runwayml.com/product/robotics, runwayml.com/research/introducing-runway-gwm-1, runwayml.com/news/runway-series-e-funding, TechCrunch 2026-02-10, en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Runway_(company).
business_model: Sells AI video/world-model generation via tiered creator subscriptions (up to ~$76/mo) and enterprise/studio contracts (~$3K-$30K/yr), plus API access and custom model licensing partnerships (Getty Images, Lionsgate, Adobe). For robotics specifically, GWM-1 / GWM Robotics is offered via a Python SDK as a simulator and synthetic-data generator, monetized through enterprise deployment deals with robotics firms (early/pre-revenue in this segment).
customers: Core video customers include enterprise studios and named partners Lionsgate, Getty Images, and Adobe (preferred API creativity partner). For GWM Robotics, no specific paying robotics customers are publicly named - Runway states it is "in discussions with robotics firms for enterprise deployment," so robotics customers are undisclosed.
revenue_status: scaling (company-wide ~$300M ARR 2025 per third-party getLatka/unverified, up from ~$121.6M in 2024; raised $315M at $5.3B valuation Feb 2026). Robotics/GWM line is pre-revenue to early-revenue.
technical_differentiation: vs NVIDIA Cosmos and Google DeepMind Genie 3: GWM-1 is an autoregressive, real-time interactive world model built on its production-grade Gen-4.5 video stack, action-conditioned (camera pose, robot commands, audio) with strong sim-to-real validation (0.95 Pearson correlation between GWM-1 and real-world policy success). Differentiation is thinner than it appears - Runway joined NVIDIA's Cosmos Coalition and is co-developing a base model with NVIDIA, so it partly competes on its frontier video-generation lineage and execution rather than a fully distinct robotics moat.

one_liner: Builds Fury, a camera-only Vision-Language-Action foundation model that gives defense and field robots autonomous perception and motor control in GPS- and comms-denied environments.
notes: Founded 2024 by Colby Adcock and Collin Otis. Emerged from stealth April 2025 with $15M seed and 2 DoD contracts, unveiling Fury foundation model. Raised $100M Series A April 2026 (co-led by Align Ventures and Draper Associates; participants include Booz Allen Ventures, Decisive Point, BVVC, Neman Ventures, Evolution VC Partners, Heraclitus Capital, Sigmas Group, Disruptive Founders Fund, Vaughn Capital). Total ~$115M. Awarded U.S. Army UxS autonomy contract integrating Fury onto Infantry Squad Vehicles. Fury is a defense-specific VLA model running on a single COTS camera and low-power inference chip. Genuinely physical robotics AI (land/air/sea unmanned systems with onboard autonomy), not RPA or pure-software. Team size not disclosed.
business_model: Sells/licenses its camera-only VLA foundation model "Fury" (plus the "Ox" C2 autonomy orchestrator) to the U.S. military via DoD/Department of War contracts, and embeds Fury into its own G01 ground and A01 aerial vehicles as well as partner robot platforms. Revenue comes from defense autonomy contracts and prime-integration deals rather than commodity robot sales.
customers: U.S. Department of War/DoD (named customer), including programs xTechOverwatch and a 1st Cavalry Division integration on the Polaris mRZR for logistics/CASEVAC. Partnered exclusively with Textron Systems on the Army UxS autonomy contract; Booz Allen Ventures is a strategic investor.
revenue_status: early-revenue ($11M in DoD contract bookings in first year; $115M total funding raised - $15M seed plus $100M Series A)
technical_differentiation: Fury is a defense-specific, camera-only VLA model that runs fully on-edge on a single COTS camera and low-power inference chip in GPS/comms-denied environments, versus Anduril's Lattice (sensor-fusion C2 software tied to its own hardware ecosystem) and Physical Intelligence (general-purpose commercial VLA not hardened for defense). Differentiation rests on a hardware-agnostic, no-cloud, defense-purpose-built model rather than a broad robotics or general C2 platform.

one_liner: Builds Cortex robotic-brain AI software (a VLA model fused with a world model) giving warehouse and industrial robots general-purpose perception and manipulation skills.
notes: German robotics AI startup, HQ Stuttgart, founded 2021 by Dr. Ralf Gulde (CEO). Cortex 2.0 combines a vision-language-action (VLA) model with a learned world model that simulates candidate trajectories in visual latent space, trained on 1B+ real production picks. Customers include BMW and PepsiCo. $110M Series B (Apr 2026) led by Headline; participants Bullhound Capital, Daphni, Felix Capital; returning Air Street Capital, Creandum, Point Nine. Prior ~$29M (EUR25M) Series A led by Creandum (Jan 2025). Total raised over $140M. Expanding into US market. Team size not disclosed publicly.
business_model: Sells its Cortex robotic-brain AI software to warehouse/logistics and industrial operators, deployed largely on a Robot-as-a-Service (RaaS) basis where customers pay recurring fees for AI-driven pick-and-place running on existing third-party robot hardware (hardware-agnostic software, not robots). Reports customers saving up to ~EUR 111,000/month per deployment; exact pricing structure (per-pick vs subscription vs license) not publicly disclosed.
customers: Named deployed/paying customers include BMW, Mercedes-Benz, Daimler Truck, PepsiCo, Austrian Post, Rohlik Group, Active Ants, bol., Monta, MS Direct, and DeltiLog. Over 200 systems live across Europe, having completed 1B+ production picks.
revenue_status: scaling (specific revenue figure not publicly disclosed; raised $110M Series B in April 2026, >$140M total; 200+ live deployments and 1B+ picks indicate commercial traction)
technical_differentiation: Vs Covariant (RFM-1, emphasizing cross-fleet learning) and Physical Intelligence (general API platform layer), Sereact's Cortex 2.0 fuses a vision-language-action model with a world model that rolls out candidate future trajectories against learned physics and scores them for stability/risk/efficiency before acting. It is fully hardware-agnostic software (any robot arm), with claimed scale leadership in deployed AI picking robots and very low human-intervention rate (~1 in 53,000 picks).

one_liner: Builds a general-purpose 'robot brain' foundation model that retrofits to diverse robot embodiments and tasks without extensive per-robot retraining.
notes: Founded 2023 by Deepak Pathak (CEO) and Abhinav Gupta (President), both former Carnegie Mellon professors. Funding trajectory: $300M Series A (Jul 2024, $1.5B valuation, led by Lightspeed/Coatue/SoftBank/Bezos), $135M Series B (~mid-2025, $4.5B valuation), $1.4B Series C (Jan 2026, $14B+ valuation, led by SoftBank with NVIDIA/NVentures, Bezos Expeditions, Macquarie, Samsung, LG, Salesforce Ventures, Sequoia, Felicis, Lightspeed, Coatue). CEO stated >$2B raised to date. Builds general-purpose robot foundation models retrofittable across embodiments. Primary layer L1 Robot Foundation Models confirmed.
business_model: Licenses its "Skild Brain" robot foundation model to robot makers and industrial operators via cloud APIs/software, charging for embodiment-agnostic robot intelligence (training + inference) rather than selling hardware; revenue comes through partnerships that retrofit the model onto third-party robots (e.g., ABB and Universal Robots arms, humanoids) for manufacturing, warehousing, inspection, and delivery deployments.
customers: Foxconn (deploying Skild Brain on robotic assembly lines building NVIDIA Blackwell GPU servers in Houston, TX), plus integration partnerships with ABB Robotics and Universal Robots; NVIDIA is both an investor and deployment partner. Strategic investors Samsung, LG, Schneider Electric, CommonSpirit, and Salesforce Ventures are likely pilot/commercial channels.
revenue_status: early-revenue (reported ~$30M revenue in 2025, up from zero; described as growing exponentially)
technical_differentiation: Versus closest competitor Physical Intelligence (pi-0, VLA flow-matching trained on real multi-robot teleoperation data), Skild bets on massive physics-based simulation (a "universe" of ~100,000 robot bodies) plus internet human video to produce an "omni-bodied" brain that controls unseen embodiments (quadrupeds, humanoids, arms) and self-recovers from damage (lost limb, jammed wheel) without per-robot retraining. Both are CMU/Berkeley-pedigree foundation-model labs; Skild's edge is simulation-scale embodiment generalization and resilience versus PI's real-data dexterity focus.

one_liner: Open-source 7B vision-language-action foundation model that maps camera images and language instructions to robot actions, enabling generalist manipulation across multiple robot embodiments.
notes: Not a company but an academic open-source research project. OpenVLA released June 2024 (arXiv 2406.09246), led by Stanford University researchers (Moo Jin Kim, Siddharth Karamcheti) with UC Berkeley, Toyota Research Institute, Google DeepMind, Physical Intelligence, and MIT. 7B VLA built on Llama 2 with fused DINOv2+SigLIP encoder, trained on 970k Open X-Embodiment demonstrations across 64 A100s. MIT-licensed checkpoints on HuggingFace. No corporate entity or funding rounds; "founded_year" = model release year. HQ taken as Stanford, CA. Secondary layer L3 reflects Open X-Embodiment data contributions.
business_model: N/A (research/open-source). OpenVLA is an academic project (Stanford, UC Berkeley, Toyota Research Institute, Google DeepMind) with model checkpoints and training code released under MIT license; no commercial revenue model. Underlying work is funded by university/industry research labs (notably TRI and Google DeepMind sponsorship).
customers: N/A as a commercial entity. Adoption is by the open-source robotics research community and labs building on the model; OpenVLA weights/code are widely used as a default open starting point (Hugging Face openvla-7b, GitHub openvla/openvla). No paying customers.
revenue_status: N/A
technical_differentiation: vs Octo (Berkeley) and RT-2-X (Google): OpenVLA is a 7B Llama-2 backbone fused with DINOv2 + SigLIP visual encoders, trained on 970k Open-X Embodiment trajectories, and is fully open (MIT). It outperforms the 55B closed-source RT-2-X by ~16.5% absolute success rate across 29 tasks while being far smaller; differs from Octo (93M, diffusion action head, robot-data-only) by leveraging web-pretrained VLM knowledge and offering parameter-efficient fine-tuning to new robots.

one_liner: Tencent's robotics lab developing embodied AI, robot foundation models, and control for general-purpose robots (quadruped Max, humanoid "The Five"), plus the Tairos embodied-intelligence platform.
notes: Internal R&D lab/division of Tencent Holdings, not a standalone funded startup, so funding rounds are not applicable (undisclosed). Established Shenzhen 2018; director/chief scientist Zhang Zhengyou. Builds physical robots: quadruped "Max"/"Jamoca", wheel-legged humanoid "The Five" (Xiao Wu, 2024). Co-released the Tairos embodied-intelligence platform with Tencent Futian lab to provide foundation-model software capabilities to robot makers. Several alumni have spun out startups (e.g. Yuansheng Intelligence dexterous hands).
business_model: Operates as a "water seller" / platform provider rather than a hardware vendor: licenses its TAIROS embodied-intelligence software platform (foundation models, simulation/data tools, cloud services) to third-party robot manufacturers on a modular plug-and-play basis, monetized via Tencent Cloud compute and software/services. Its own robots (Max quadruped, "The Five"/Xiao Wu) are R&D prototypes, not sold commercially. Note: an exec stated the TAIROS platform's goal "is not to make money" but to grow the industry, so monetization runs through Tencent Cloud rather than the lab itself.
customers: TAIROS is deployed across robot platforms from Unitree (strategic partner; the Unitree G1 "Xiao Mo" guide robot runs TAIROS at the Mogao Caves exhibition center in Dunhuang), plus PaXini, Leju, Dobot, EngineAI, AgiBot, and KEENON. These are integration/deployment partners; paid contract terms are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: undisclosed (lab is a Tencent internal unit; TAIROS platform explicitly positioned as non-profit-maximizing per Tencent exec, with monetization deferred to Tencent Cloud; no standalone revenue figure public)
technical_differentiation: Unlike vertically integrated humanoid makers Unitree and AgiBot (which sell hardware plus their own onboard AI), Tencent stays hardware-neutral and sells the "brain" layer—a cross-vendor, modular foundation-model platform (TAIROS) that any manufacturer can install. Differentiation rests on Tencent Cloud-scale compute, multimodal perception-action models, and breadth of partner integrations rather than proprietary robot hardware.

one_liner: Corporate R&D lab applying generative AI (Diffusion Policy, Large Behavior Models) to teach robots dexterous manipulation skills from demonstration, plus automated driving and materials research.
notes: Wholly-owned subsidiary/division of Toyota Motor Corporation, not a venture-backed startup; launched 2016 with a $1B/5-year Toyota investment in AI and robotics. HQ Los Altos, CA with offices in Cambridge, MA. Pioneered Diffusion Policy (with Columbia) and Large Behavior Models for multitask dexterous manipulation. Total funding figure reflects initial Toyota commitment, not external rounds. Founding year and robotics work verified via Wikipedia and tri.global.
business_model: Does not sell products or earn external revenue directly; it is a wholly-owned corporate R&D subsidiary funded by parent Toyota Motor Corporation (launched 2016 with a $1B/5-year commitment) to develop AI and robotics (Large Behavior Models, Diffusion Policy) that feed into Toyota's vehicle, manufacturing, and future humanoid/home-robot products. Value accrues to the parent via technology transfer and IP, not standalone sales.
customers: No external paying customers; its primary "customer" is parent Toyota Motor Corporation. Research is conducted via partnerships, notably with Boston Dynamics (Oct 2024, applying TRI's LBMs to the electric Atlas humanoid) and university collaborators (MIT, Stanford, and others under a $100M+ joint research program).
revenue_status: N/A (corporate R&D lab; internally funded by Toyota Motor Corp, ~$1B initial 5-year budget plus ongoing parent funding, no external revenue)
technical_differentiation: Pioneered Diffusion Policy and the "Large Behavior Model" framing for learning dexterous manipulation from a few haptic/teleoperated demonstrations plus language goals, a publish-heavily academic approach distinct from product-commercialization-focused foundation-model labs like Physical Intelligence (pi-0/pi-0.5) and Google DeepMind (RT-2/Gemini Robotics). TRI's edge is deep generative-policy research depth and Toyota's manufacturing/hardware backing rather than a commercial robot product.

one_liner: Simulation platform letting robot builders spin up digital instances of their hardware with simulated sensors to close the sim-to-real gap and test/train physical AI faster.
notes: Founded May 2023 by CEO Harry Mellsop with four co-founders (alumni of Transpose/Chainalysis, Google DeepMind, Meta Reality Labs). Seed: $8.5M at $60M valuation (Apr 2026), led by A* and Category Ventures; participants MaC Venture Capital, Abstract, Box Group, Icehouse Ventures, plus angels Shyam Sankar (Palantir) and Adrian Macneil (Foxglove). Prior pre-seed $4.5M (~Dec 2025), so total ~$13M. Focus on sensor/perception simulation for AVs, farm/construction machinery, drones; building shared physics engine and world model infrastructure. Sources: TechCrunch, The Robot Report, SiliconANGLE (all 2026-04-16).
business_model: B2B simulation platform sold to robotics/physical-AI developers: customers spin up digital-twin instances of their hardware with calibrated virtual sensors/actuators to test edge cases, run reinforcement learning, and generate training data, closing the sim-to-real gap. Specific pricing/contract model (likely usage- or seat-based SaaS, "Cursor for physical AI" positioning) is not publicly disclosed.
customers: MIT CSAIL (researcher David Mayo uses the platform to evaluate LLMs and run robot-design experiments). TechCrunch notes some earliest engagements are with large multinationals with robotics divisions, but those names are undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; raised $8.5M seed at $60M valuation in April 2026, after a $4.25M pre-seed in Dec 2025)
technical_differentiation: Positions as a developer-friendly layer on top of foundational simulators (NVIDIA Isaac Sim, World Labs) rather than a competing engine, automating digital-twin creation and precision sensor/perception emulation aimed at capital-constrained robot builders. vs NVIDIA Isaac Sim (lower-level, infrastructure-heavy) and Applied Intuition (AV-focused, enterprise/expensive); Antioch differentiates on faster iteration and accessibility for smaller teams.

one_liner: Physics-based render/sensor simulation engine generating high-fidelity synthetic data with domain randomization to train and validate perception AI for automotive, robotics, defense, and Industry 4.0.
notes: Madrid, Spain HQ; founded 2018 by Victor Gonzalez. Total funding ~$3.25M (EUR 3M) in single Series A round announced 2020-04-29, led by Bullnet Capital and Inveready. Proprietary physics-grounded simulation engine simulating RGB, NIR, Radar, LiDAR, Thermal sensors. Primarily automotive (in-cabin monitoring, ADAS) but technology adopted in Robotics, Defence, and Industry 4.0 per company site. Partners include NVIDIA, Sony, Google Cloud, AWS. Founding year, name, and one-liner sourced from Crunchbase and anyverse.ai.
business_model: Sells access to its synthetic-data generation platform (Anyverse Platform plus the Anyverse ADAS web app and in-cabin monitoring workflows) on a subscription/license basis to automotive OEMs, Tier 1 suppliers, and sensor/cloud vendors, plus paid custom dataset-generation engagements. Revenue is primarily recurring software/data subscriptions rather than one-off services; specific list pricing is not publicly disclosed.
customers: Named partners/users include Tech Mahindra, Toyo Corporation, Keymotek, Sony, NVIDIA, Google Cloud, AWS, and CRONAI; academic/research users include Stanford, WMG University of Warwick, University of Galway, and Tsinghua University. Core paying base is described as top-tier automotive OEMs and Tier 1 suppliers (specific OEM names undisclosed).
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$2.9M ARR, 2024, per Latka — unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a proprietary physics-based spectral/radiometric render and sensor-simulation engine producing pixel-accurate, sensor-true synthetic data (including a hyperspectral platform and Euro NCAP-aligned ADAS scenario libraries), emphasizing sensor fidelity over scale. Closest named competitors Parallel Domain (much larger funding, ~$30M raised, scenario/data scale focus) and Cognata (full closed-loop AV simulation/validation); Anyverse competes on physical/spectral sensor accuracy and in-cabin monitoring rather than capital or scenario breadth.

one_liner: Builds simulation, data, and toolchain software for developing, validating, and deploying autonomy across vehicles and robotics, using sim-based scenario testing and policy validation.
notes: Founders Qasar Younis and Peter Ludwig. Series F at $15B valuation (June 2025); prior Series E $250M at $6B (March 2024). Positions itself as "Physical AI" spanning end-to-end autonomous driving and robotics (defense, agriculture, construction, trucking). Site has dedicated "Autonomy & robotics research" and robotics product lines; 50M+ simulations run by customers in 2025. Total funding approximate, summed from disclosed rounds. HQ Mountain View, CA (expanded to Sunnyvale in 2025). [GATE2 scope-flag] Originally AV simulation but now has explicit robotics product lines (warehouse, construction, agriculture autonomy). Selling simulation tools, not vehicles. Kept.
business_model: Licenses an autonomy software toolchain (simulation products like Simian/Spectral, scenario authoring, data management, and on-vehicle SDV software) to automotive OEMs and defense/industrial customers, primarily via multi-year subscription/SaaS licenses plus services and engineering contracts. Defense business adds government contract vehicles (e.g., DIU/Army RCV, AFWERX/AFSIM integration) worth tens to hundreds of millions.
customers: 18 of the top 20 global automotive OEMs, including Toyota, Volkswagen, GM, Porsche, Stellantis (STLA Brain), Isuzu, and the TRATON Group (Scania, MAN, International); defense/government customers include the US Army (Robotic Combat Vehicle program, ~$49M), DIU, US Air Force (AFWERX/AFSIM), and partner SNC. Also LG Innotek partnership.
revenue_status: scaling (~$830M ARR 2025, up ~2x YoY from ~$415M in 2024; $15B valuation after $600M Series F)
technical_differentiation: Versus Foretellix (narrower V&V/scenario compliance, ~$135M raised) and Cognata (smaller-scale sim/perception platform, NVIDIA-aligned), Applied Intuition offers a far broader end-to-end stack spanning simulation, perception, data management, and on-vehicle SDV/autonomy software, plus a large defense business and dramatically greater capital/scale. It is platform-agnostic rather than tied to NVIDIA DRIVE/Omniverse, and competes with NVIDIA Drive Sim while remaining independent of NVIDIA's silicon.

one_liner: Develops CoppeliaSim (formerly V-REP), a multi-physics robot simulator (MuJoCo, Bullet, ODE, Newton, Vortex) for robot algorithm development, control prototyping, and digital twins in research, education, and industry.
notes: V-REP first released commercially in March 2010; originally developed within Toshiba R&D. Company formally registered as Coppelia Robotics AG in Zurich on 2014-04-17 (Swiss commercial registry). Appears to be a bootstrapped small company with no disclosed venture funding rounds across Crunchbase/PitchBook/Tracxn; team size 2-10 per LinkedIn. Layer 2 (Simulation) fits: multi-physics robot simulator used for algorithm development and digital twins. Founded_year 2010 reflects product launch; legal entity 2014.
business_model: Sells commercial licenses for CoppeliaSim (formerly V-REP), its multi-physics robot simulator. The free "Edu" edition is given to students/universities for non-commercial use, while industrial/commercial users buy paid CoppeliaSim Pro/Player licenses (quote-based, contact-sales pricing); revenue also comes from custom development and support services.
customers: No commercial paying customers named publicly. Heavily used in academia/research (e.g., Northwestern University, Florida Atlantic University, the Poppy Project) and originally developed within Toshiba R&D; specific paying industrial licensees are undisclosed.
revenue_status: undisclosed (private ~5-employee Swiss SME, no public revenue/ARR figure; bootstrapped/unfunded per Crunchbase)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates as a long-established, distributed/scripted multi-physics simulator that bundles four+ physics engines (Bullet, ODE, Newton, Vortex, MuJoCo) with each scene object independently scriptable, versus open-source Gazebo (free, ROS-centric, less out-of-box GUI) and NVIDIA Isaac Sim (GPU/photorealistic, Omniverse-locked). Competes on maturity and ease-of-use rather than scale or capital.

one_liner: Develops Webots, an open-source robot simulator for modeling, programming and RL-based simulation of robots, enabling sim-to-real transfer for industry, research and education.
notes: EPFL spin-off founded by Olivier Michel; headquartered at EPFL Innovation Park, Lausanne. Webots has been developed since 1998 and was open-sourced in 2018 (Apache 2.0). Used by 1200+ companies, universities and research centers. Privately held, bootstrapped with grant funding; no traditional VC rounds. Last funding recorded as a grant (Jan 2020) per Crunchbase. Sources: Crunchbase, Wikipedia (Webots), cyberbotics.com, LinkedIn.
business_model: Webots was open-sourced in December 2018, so revenue comes from paid services around the free simulator rather than license sales: custom development, technical/customer support contracts, training, and consulting for industrial and academic robotics projects (priced as bespoke service/consulting engagements). Development is additionally funded through EU and Swiss research grants (e.g. OpenDR, OPTIMA, SimGait).
customers: Webots is used in 1,200+ companies, universities and research centers; named corporate/research users include Toyota, Honda, Sony, Panasonic, NTT, Samsung, NASA, SRI, BAE Systems and Vorwerk. Which of these are paying consulting/support clients vs. free open-source users is not publicly broken out; grant-funded partners include EU/Swiss research consortia.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; ~6 employees, privately held, founded 1998)
technical_differentiation: Webots is a long-established, fully open-source desktop robot simulator (modeling, programming, RL, sim-to-real) differentiated from open-source Gazebo (Open Robotics) by its integrated GUI/editor and easier out-of-the-box usability, and from commercial NVIDIA Isaac Sim by being free/open and lighter-weight rather than GPU/photorealism-first. Competes on maturity, breadth of robot models, and education/research adoption rather than capital.

one_liner: Converts industrial 3D/CAD/BIM models into SimReady digital twins and virtual training grounds for robot simulation and sim-to-real, integrating with NVIDIA Isaac Sim/Omniverse.
last_round_lead: KDDI Open Innovation Fund 3 (with Singtel Innov8)
notes: Founded 2014 in Seattle by former Microsoft employees; US HQ in Bellevue, WA, plus global HQ being established in Singapore (Tiong Bahru). FactVerse Designer is DataMesh's no-code digital-twin product; SimReady assets + FactVerse Twin Engine integrate with NVIDIA Isaac Sim/Omniverse for photorealistic synthetic data, sensor/physics simulation, and sim-to-real robot training. Total funding ~$23.51M per Crunchbase/CB Insights; investors include NVIDIA Inception, SIG Asia, Singtel Innov8, Global Brain, KDDI. Most recent round closed June 2024 led by KDDI Open Innovation Fund 3 with Singtel Innov8 — size and round type undisclosed. Team size not publicly found. Layer 2 (Simulation & Sim-to-Real) confirmed; secondary L3 (synthetic data/ground-truth labeling) and L6 (industrial/manufacturing vertical). Categorized as division since FactVerse Designer is a product line within DataMesh. Sources: datamesh.com, prnewswire, crunchbase.com/organization/datamesh, cbinsights.com/company/datamesh.
business_model: Sells the FactVerse industrial digital-twin/simulation platform (including FactVerse Designer for no-code SimReady scene authoring and SimReady asset conversion) to enterprises via SaaS subscriptions in China, Japan, and Singapore, plus on-prem/hybrid "DCSA" all-in-one server licenses for confidential deployments. Related product DataMesh Director is published at $199/mo ($1,999/yr for 1 creator + 3 viewers); larger FactVerse deployments are custom enterprise contracts.
customers: Named deployments include NIO (1:1 digital-twin Production Management Center for vehicle assembly with AGV/RFID integration) and Foxconn (3D/MR training and equipment-maintenance guides via DataMesh Director and Apple Vision Pro). Targets semiconductor fabs, aviation MRO, energy, logistics, and data centers.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; total funding ~$23.5M raised, last round June 2024)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates by converting existing industrial 3D/CAD/BIM models into SimReady (OpenUSD) assets and bridging its FactVerse Twin Engine to NVIDIA Isaac Sim/Omniverse via the FactVerse Adaptor, plus a no-code Designer letting frontline workers (not just engineers) build robot-cell training scenarios. Versus NVIDIA Omniverse/Isaac Sim (an upstream partner/platform it sits atop) and Siemens (Tecnomatix/Plant Simulation), its edge is the no-code authoring layer and automated CAD-to-SimReady asset pipeline rather than physics engine ownership.

one_liner: Builds AI-powered dexterous manipulation robots (Vega dual-armed mobile robot) using a digital-twin simulation and synthetic-data flywheel for sim-to-real learning on industrial and logistics tasks.
last_round_lead: undisclosed (recent strategic investor LG Technology Ventures / LG CNS)
notes: Founded by PhDs from MIT, UCSD, CMU. CEO Tao Chen (MIT PhD), CTO Yuzhe Qin (UCSD, simulation expert), co-founder Chongyang Wang. Flagship: Vega dual-armed mobile robot with dexterous hand claiming ~99% manipulation success across thousands of objects. NVIDIA-backed; uses digital-twin sim and data flywheel for sim-to-real (matches Layer 2 assignment). Total raised ~$41M (Tracxn/PitchBook); latest round reported as Series C dated Feb 20, 2026 with size/lead undisclosed. LG CNS/LG Technology Ventures took a strategic stake (amount undisclosed). Other investors: Epsilon Ventures, Mana Ventures, RoboStrategy, Cadenza Capital, TSVC. Founding year (2024) and HQ (Santa Clara, CA) per Tracxn/PitchBook/Korea Herald; cross-verified via company site dexmate.ai.
business_model: Hardware sales of the Vega general-purpose dual-arm mobile robot (~$89,999 per unit, $999 deposit, sub-four-month lead times) to manufacturing, logistics, and retail enterprises; for paying customers Dexmate also scans their facility, builds a digital twin, and runs Isaac Sim synthetic-data simulations to automate specific tasks (effectively a robot+sim integration/service model). A discounted Vega-U variant is placed with university labs via a research grant program.
customers: Undisclosed specific names; reported to have units in testing with unnamed public companies and pilot deployments in logistics warehouses, plus academic labs through the "Build with Vega" Vega-U research grant program. No named paying customers are publicly confirmed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; ~$41M total funding raised through a Feb 2026 Series C)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a digital-twin + synthetic-data flywheel (facility scanning to NVIDIA Isaac Sim simulation for sim-to-real) paired with a foldable, omnidirectional dual-arm mobile robot with five-finger dexterous hands (36 DOF), versus humanoid/manipulation peers like Figure AI and 1X, which pursue full bipedal humanoids, and mobile-manipulation players like Sanctuary AI; Dexmate emphasizes a lower-cost wheeled, high-payload general-purpose platform tied to its simulation pipeline rather than legged form factor.

one_liner: Builds Falcon, a photorealistic Unreal Engine digital-twin platform generating synthetic data and virtual sensors to train and validate robots and autonomous systems, closing the sim-to-real gap.
notes: Founded 2018, HQ San Mateo, CA (3 E 3rd Ave). Co-founders Apurva Shah (CEO, ex-Pixar) and Mike Taylor (Chief Robotics Officer). Falcon is a cloud-based, real-time photorealistic digital-twin simulator on Unreal Engine for testing/training AI and autonomous systems; FalconCloud launched 2023; offers EDU license for synthetic data. Total funding ~$14.8M; Series A ~$12M closed 2021-12-07 (lead investor not confirmed in sources). Other named investors: Alumni Ventures, Comeback Capital, AngelPad, Plug and Play, Google for Startups Accelerator. Note: distinct from unrelated 'Duality Technologies' (privacy/data company). Sources: cbinsights.com/company/duality, tracxn, crunchbase.com/organization/duality-robotics, therobotreport.com, siliconangle.com.
business_model: Sells access to its Falcon digital-twin simulation platform (FalconCloud browser-based sim, FalconEditor/FalconSim built on Unreal Engine) to robotics, autonomy, defense, and manufacturing teams who use it to generate synthetic sensor data and validate AI systems. Monetizes via tiered subscriptions (EDU, Site, Twin, Share tiers with metered cloud simulation hours) plus direct enterprise/government contracts (e.g., U.S. Army anti-drone XM30 program).
customers: U.S. Army (contracted for AI-based anti-drone detection on the XM30 infantry vehicle program); Honeywell and Procter & Gamble (P&G) have publicly cited results from FalconSim. Described as serving Fortune 100 customers; full customer list otherwise undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no reliable global figure publicly disclosed; third-party aggregators give a wide unverified estimate of $11M-$100M, and the India subsidiary Duality Robotics Pvt Ltd reported ~$335K revenue FY ending Mar 2025 - not the global figure)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from NVIDIA Isaac Sim/Omniverse (GPU/RTX, open-source reference framework) by building on Epic's Unreal Engine for photorealistic rendering plus native co-simulation of real control code (PX4/C/MATLAB/ROS2) and a fully browser-based, no-special-hardware cloud sim. Versus Applied Intuition (autonomy/AV-validation focused), Duality targets broader robotics/smart-system digital twins across manufacturing, warehouse, off-road autonomy, and defense rather than primarily on-road vehicles.

one_liner: Builds a sim-to-real AI autonomy stack (RL, VLA, whole-body control) giving humanoid robots a general "brain" to reason, act and adapt without scripts or teleoperation.
notes: Founded by ex-NVIDIA/ETH Zurich researchers: Nikita Rudin (CEO), David Hoeller (CTO), Julian Nubert, Fabian Tischhauser. CTO previously worked on NVIDIA Isaac Gym. Total funding $57.35M = $7.35M seed (Frst, Moonfire, redalpine) + $50M Series A (Nov 2025, led by DST Global Partners with NVentures, Prosus Ventures, redalpine, Moonfire). Legal entity Flexion Robotics AG (CHE-393.058.632) published in Swiss gazette Sept 2025; reported "founded in January" 2025, though The Robot Report lists 2024 - minor source conflict on founding year; using 2025 per registry and majority of press. Strong L2 fit: trains primarily on synthetic data from high-performance physics simulation, adding real data selectively to close sim-to-real gaps.
business_model: Licenses a horizontal humanoid-robot autonomy software stack (command/LLM reasoning layer + VLA motion layer + RL whole-body control layer) to humanoid hardware OEMs, positioning itself as the "Android/Windows" of humanoids so manufacturers skip in-house intelligence R&D. Buyer is robot OEMs; pricing/contract terms (likely per-unit licensing or platform deals) are not publicly disclosed.
customers: Says it is "already working with major OEM partners" but names none publicly; demo videos feature a Unitree-style humanoid, suggesting an OEM tie that is unconfirmed. Named paying customers: undisclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; raised $50M Series A in Nov 2025 led by DST Global Partners with NVentures, Prosus Ventures, redalpine, Moonfire, plus $7.35M seed, ~$57.35M total funding)
technical_differentiation: Unlike full-stack humanoid makers (e.g., Figure AI, Tesla Optimus) that pair their brain with their own hardware, Flexion sells a hardware-agnostic, cross-morphology autonomy layer; vs Skild AI and Physical Intelligence (also hardware-agnostic foundation-model players), Flexion differentiates on a sim-to-real RL whole-body-control stack from ex-NVIDIA researchers emphasizing synthetic-data training and rough-terrain locomotion. Edge over these named peers is execution/team-and-capital rather than a proven unique moat.

one_liner: Builds foundation models and world models trained on game video to give agents spatial-temporal reasoning that transfers to robot arms, drones, and autonomous vehicles.
notes: Spun out of gaming clip platform Medal (Dutch-origin). Founders: Pim de Witte (CEO), Eloi Alonso, Vincent Micheli, Adam Jelley (DIAMOND world-model researchers). Raine also participated in seed. Trains agents on ~2B annual game clips. World models are internal training environments, not the product (avoiding copyright/productization). Stated transfer targets: robotic arms, drones (search-and-rescue, GPS-denied navigation), AVs — qualifies as real robotics AI under Layer 2 sim-to-real. HQ New York chosen for capital/talent. OpenAI reportedly attempted ~$500M acquisition of Medal (unconfirmed). Team size not disclosed.
business_model: Pre-commercial frontier AI research lab (spun out of game-clip platform Medal) building foundation/world models trained on Medal's gaming video data. No product is sold yet; intended monetization spans gaming agents (dynamic-difficulty NPCs/bots scaling to ~50% player win-rates) and licensing spatial-temporal reasoning models for robotics/drone applications (e.g., search-and-rescue). Explicitly avoids licensing world models in competition with game developers. Pricing/contract model not publicly disclosed.
customers: Undisclosed. No paying or piloting customers named publicly; company closed its seed round in October 2025 and is pre-commercial. Key relationship is its parent/data source Medal (10M MAU, ~2B videos/year), which OpenAI reportedly tried to acquire for $500M in 2024.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no revenue/ARR figure publicly disclosed; $133.7M seed raised Oct 2025 led by Khosla Ventures and General Catalyst with Raine)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by a proprietary data moat: trains action-conditioned world models purely from Medal's first-person gameplay video across tens of thousands of games (controller inputs map naturally to robot arms/drones/AVs), claimed to surpass Twitch/YouTube data and to generalize to unseen environments. Vs Google DeepMind's Genie and World Labs' Marble (both world-model builders), its edge is the scale/diversity of human-controlled gameplay data rather than synthetically generated worlds.

one_liner: Embodied-AI startup building sim-to-real mobile manipulation robots; uses SAPIEN/ManiSkill and generative AI to synthesize infinite simulated scene/object variations from small real-world datasets for generalizable robot skill learning.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (reported ~$82.5M aggregate per third-party trackers; unconfirmed)
last_round_lead: undisclosed (investors include Cherubic Ventures, Vitalbridge Capital, Hillhouse, Unicorn Capital, Eminence, GPU Ventures, NVIDIA Inception)
notes: Co-founded by Robin Han (CEO) and Hao Su (CTO, UC San Diego professor, creator of SAPIEN/ManiSkill simulators). Hillbot Alpha (unveiled Nov 2024) is its first fully autonomous mobile-manipulation robot trained via sim-to-real; initial application is retail shelf-stocking. Primary Layer 2 (Simulation & Sim-to-Real) fit confirmed: uses generative AI in SAPIEN/ManiSkill to procedurally generate object/texture/environment variations for synthetic training data. Funding totals differ across trackers (Crunchbase/PitchBook/Tracxn) and exact seed round size/date/lead not disclosed in primary sources; left undisclosed to avoid inventing figures.
business_model: Pre-commercial: intends to monetize general-purpose mobile-manipulation robots (demoed as "Hillbot Alpha") trained via its sim-to-real pipeline, targeting industrial and eventually domestic tasks; likely robot-as-hardware-plus-skills or robotics-as-a-service deployments. Its SAPIEN and ManiSkill simulation/synthetic-data stacks are released open-source and serve as a research/ecosystem moat rather than a direct revenue line. Specific buyer and pricing/contract terms are not publicly disclosed.
customers: undisclosed (no named paying or pilot customers found; public demos shown in environments like cafes, but no commercial contracts disclosed).
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; founded 2024, ~$82.5M raised per Crunchbase/PitchBook, investors incl. Hillhouse, Cherubic Ventures, Unicorn Capital Partners, Vitalbridge, Eminence Ventures)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via its founder-built open-source simulation stack (SAPIEN + ManiSkill, from UCSD professor/co-founder Hao Su) to generate "infinite" synthetic scenes for sim-to-real training, plus mobile-manipulation focus and use of NVIDIA Cosmos for video-based training. Vs. Skild AI and Physical Intelligence (both pursuing robot foundation models with larger raises), Hillbot leans on simulation-first synthetic data generation rather than primarily large-scale real-world teleoperation data.

also_known_as: Lightwheel AI, Light Wheel Intelligence, 光轮智能
one_liner: Builds SimReady physics-accurate simulation assets, egocentric human data, and the RoboFinals evaluation platform to train and benchmark embodied-AI robots at scale.
last_round_lead: undisclosed (round included Ant Group, Dingstone/Ding Shi Asset Mgmt, New Hope Group, JIC Capital, AUX Group)
notes: Physical AI infrastructure company founded 2023 by a former NVIDIA simulation head. Products: Lightwheel SimReady System/Library (OpenUSD physics-accurate assets), EgoSuite (egocentric human data, 20,000+ hours/week), and RoboFinals (industrial-grade simulation evaluation platform). Partners/customers include NVIDIA, Google DeepMind, Figure, AgiBot, ByteDance, Geely, BYD, World Labs. March 2026 raised ~CNY 1B (~$138M, Series A++/A+++), reported as world's first embodied-data unicorn; earlier Pre-A+ led by Beijing AI Industry Investment Fund with Matrix Partners. Total funding figures cited range ~$138-146M across sources. Some US materials list a Santa Clara, CA presence, but primary HQ is Beijing. Crunchbase: light-wheel-intelligence. Dealroom and Gasgoo corroborate the round.
business_model: Sells Physical-AI infrastructure across four stages: SimReady Library (physics-accurate simulation assets/scenes), EgoSuite (egocentric human demonstration data), RoboFinals (industrial-grade simulation evaluation platform), and deployment/continuous-improvement systems—sold to robotics/AV teams as ongoing data + evaluation contracts ("continuous data infrastructure, not one-off collection") rather than one-time licensing. Specific pricing not publicly disclosed.
customers: Named partners/customers leveraging its simulation assets and synthetic/human data include Google DeepMind, Figure, AgiBot, ByteDance, Geely, BYD, Hugging Face, and PeritasAI (strategic deal targeting ~200 humanoid robots in perioperative healthcare through 2026-2027). It reports 1.5M+ hours of human data delivered.
revenue_status: scaling (~$100M in Q1 2026 orders; revenue grew ~10x in 2025, with Q1 2026 projected to exceed all of 2025)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on a full physical-AI stack (SimReady assets + egocentric human data + RoboFinals evaluation) led by a former NVIDIA simulation head, vs. point-solution competitors: it provides the industry's first industrial-grade VLA/world-model evaluation benchmark (RoboFinals) that data-collection peers like Scale AI lack, and a deeper SimReady asset library (2,000+ USD assets) versus pure simulation-engine players. Raised RMB ~1B to fund this breadth.

one_liner: Open research lab (UC San Diego, Zhiting Hu's group) building world/agent/LLM models, including SimWorld, an open-ended UE5 simulator with realistic physics and language-driven procedural generation for embodied LLM/VLM agents.
notes: Maitrix.org is not a venture-backed company but an open academic research organization associated with Prof. Zhiting Hu's group at UC San Diego (Halicioglu Data Science Institute / CSE). No funding rounds, founding date, or team size are publicly disclosed; these are genuinely not findable on the site, GitHub (maitrix-org, no public members), or Crunchbase. SimWorld (arXiv:2512.01078, NeurIPS 2025 poster, first release 2025-06) is an open-ended UE5 simulator for autonomous agents in physical/social worlds, supporting vehicle/robot/human embodiments with language-driven procedural generation -- qualifies as Layer 2 Simulation & Sim-to-Real for embodied/robotic agents. Other projects: Pandora and Genie-style world models, Voila voice-language model, LLM Reasoners, ReasonerAgent. real_robotics_ai=true: simulator explicitly targets robot embodiments and embodied AI, not RPA or pure-software. Funding/round/team fields marked undisclosed/unknown rather than invented.
business_model: N/A (research/open-source). Maitrix.org is an open academic research organization affiliated with UC San Diego (Zhiting Hu's group); it releases open-source artifacts (SimWorld UE5 simulator, LLM Reasoners, Pandora world model) rather than selling a product. Funding is via university/research channels rather than commercial revenue.
customers: Undisclosed - no paying customers. Outputs are open-source and consumed by the academic/AI research community; SimWorld is used to benchmark frontier models (GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5, Claude 3.5, DeepSeek) but these are evaluation targets, not paying clients.
revenue_status: N/A
technical_differentiation: SimWorld is an open-ended, UE5-built simulator with language-based procedural generation and native LLM/VLM agent interfaces (multimodal perception + natural-language actions) for physical and social reasoning, contrasting with NVIDIA Isaac Sim/Omniverse (physics/robotics-focused, not LLM-agent-native) and Allen Institute's AI2-THOR / Meta's Habitat (indoor embodied navigation, lower visual fidelity and less open-ended social-reasoning scope). Differentiation is research-novelty driven, not commercial.

also_known_as: QunHe / 群核科技 / Kujiale / Coohom; SpatialVerse, SpatialTwin, SpatialLM
one_liner: Spatial-intelligence platform (SpatialVerse) generating physically-consistent synthetic indoor scenes and digital twins to train robots and embodied AI for manipulation, alongside its Kujiale/Coohom interior design tools.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (multiple VC rounds; ~$2B valuation in 2020) plus ~$130M HK IPO in 2026
last_round_lead: undisclosed (IPO; prior backers include IDG Capital, GGV, Hillhouse, Coatue)
notes: Founded 2011 in Hangzhou by Huang Xiaohuang, Chen Hang, Zhu Hao (UIUC graduates, ex-NVIDIA/Microsoft/Amazon). Core business is cloud interior/architectural design (Kujiale in China, Coohom globally). Robotics relevance: SpatialVerse spatial-intelligence platform plus open-sourced SpatialLM (Mar 2025) produce physically-consistent synthetic 3D indoor scenes/digital twins at scale for embodied-AI and robot manipulation/grasping training; partnerships with embodied-intelligence firms including Silicon Valley players. Listed on HKEX April 17, 2026 (one of Hangzhou's 'Six Little Dragons'), raising ~HK$1.02B (~$130M); shares closed +144% on debut. Reached ~$2B valuation in 2020 funding round. Robotics is a strategic division/initiative rather than the core revenue line, hence core_or_division=division. Sources: PR Newswire (SpatialLM/embodied intelligence), Fortune (2026 IPO), en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manycore_Tech.
business_model: Core revenue (~96.9% of total) is subscription SaaS for its cloud-based 3D spatial/interior design CAD platform — Kujiale in China and Coohom internationally — sold on a freemium model to professional designers, furniture/home-decor brands, and retailers, plus professional-service fees and commissions on platform transactions. The emerging Layer-2 line is SpatialVerse, which licenses physically-consistent synthetic indoor scenes and digital twins (built on its open-source SpatialLM model) to embodied-AI and robotics developers for training data; this is sold via enterprise cooperation/data-licensing agreements and is still a small share of revenue.
customers: Design-platform side: 45,500+ enterprise customers across 200+ countries (furniture and home-decor brands, retailers, interior-design firms). SpatialVerse/embodied-AI side: company states it has signed spatial- and embodied-intelligence training cooperation agreements with multiple global embodied-AI firms "including leading Silicon Valley players," but specific names are undisclosed in public sources.
revenue_status: profitable (RMB 820M / ~$113M revenue FY2025, gross margin 82.2%, adjusted net profit RMB 57.1M / ~$8.4M)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via its massive proprietary library of 86M+ monthly-active-visitor real-world spatial design data feeding a synthetic-data engine that generalizes to "trillions" of physically-accurate indoor scenes, plus its open-sourced SpatialLM model (on Hugging Face/GitHub/ModelScope) that converts real scans into structured 3D layouts — an asset most pure sim-to-real players lack. Versus NVIDIA Omniverse/Isaac Sim (general physics-sim platform) it is narrower but owns vastly more real indoor-scene data; versus AI2-THOR/embodied-AI scene datasets it offers far larger scale and a commercial pipeline rather than an academic dataset.

also_known_as: OSRF, Open Robotics, Gazebo, Ignition Gazebo, Gazebo Harmonic, ROS maintainer
one_liner: Nonprofit stewarding open-source robotics software: maintains Gazebo, the standard multi-engine physics and sensor simulator, plus ROS, enabling robot development, simulation, and learning.
notes: OSRF is a 501(c)(3) California nonprofit founded May 2012 to steward ROS and Gazebo after Willow Garage dissolved; not VC-funded (funded via grants, sponsorships, contracts). In Dec 2022, Alphabet's Intrinsic acquired the for-profit commercial arms OSRC and OSRC-SG (Open-RMF), but the nonprofit OSRF retained ROS, Gazebo, and Open-RMF IP and remains independent. In 2024 OSRF launched the Open Source Robotics Alliance (OSRA) for governance, with Intrinsic as an inaugural Platinum member. HQ: 170 S Whisman Rd, Mountain View, CA 94041. Gazebo provides multi-engine physics and sensor simulation central to sim-to-real robot development (Layer 2). Funding figures genuinely undisclosed/not applicable as a nonprofit.
business_model: 501(c)(3) nonprofit funded by corporate membership dues via the Open Source Robotics Alliance (OSRA, launched 2024; tiered Platinum/Gold/Silver/Associate fees paid by firms like NVIDIA, Qualcomm, Bosch, Intrinsic), plus donations/grants (DARPA, NASA, Amazon, Toyota Research Institute's $1M+ recurring gifts) and software royalties to steward the Gazebo simulator and ROS. Its for-profit consulting/custom-engineering arm OSRC was sold to Intrinsic (Alphabet) in Dec 2022, so revenue now skews to memberships/grants rather than services.
customers: Funders/members include NVIDIA, Qualcomm, Bosch, Intrinsic (Alphabet), Toyota Research Institute, Apex.AI, and Zettascale; historic government/R&D backers include DARPA, NASA, and Amazon. Former OSRC consulting clients included Toyota Research Institute and Bosch.
revenue_status: early-revenue ($1.94M total revenue FY2023, down from $6.0M FY2022 per IRS Form 990; FY2022 inflated by ~$2.1M one-time gain on the OSRC sale to Intrinsic)
technical_differentiation: Gazebo is the de facto open-source, multi-physics-engine robotics simulator tightly co-developed with ROS, giving it ecosystem lock-in and vendor neutrality that proprietary sim-to-real platforms lack. Closest competitors are NVIDIA Isaac Sim (higher-fidelity GPU/RTX rendering and synthetic data, but proprietary and GPU-locked) and Coppelia Robotics' CoppeliaSim; Gazebo differentiates on being free, open, ROS-native, and governance-backed by an industry consortium.

one_liner: Turns ordinary video into physics-enabled hyper-realistic digital twins so robotics teams can stress-test and validate AI robots in cloud-scale simulation before factory deployment, closing the sim-to-real gap.
notes: Founded 2024 by Tanmay Agarwal (CEO, Stanford robotics under Fei-Fei Li/Jiajun Wu) and Aumkar Renavikar (CTO, UIUC robotics; prior factory automation at BMW and Michelin). Nov 2025 $4M seed led by Pear VC with GS Futures, Kakao Ventures, Lightscape Partners, Gaingels, Nova Threshold, Mana Ventures; total $4.9M incl. pre-seed. Platform converts iPhone/video footage to interactive 3D digital twins, integrates with NVIDIA Omniverse, Unreal, and Unity. Customers include Orangewood Labs, BotBuilt, Rainier Labs. Competed in 2024 RoboBusiness Pitchfire. Team size not disclosed.
business_model: Sells a video-to-digital-twin simulation platform that turns smartphone video of a facility into a physics-enabled, navigable 3D twin where robotics teams stress-test and validate AI robots against real factory conditions using their actual control software (integrates with NVIDIA Omniverse, Unreal, Unity). The website also markets an outcome-based robot-deployment offering with a "Zero Risk" model billed at a single hourly rate (no pilot fees or upfront hardware cost) until the robot hits agreed KPIs on the customer's floor.
customers: Named robotics teams using the platform include Orangewood Labs (industrial manufacturing robots; CEO Abhinav Das gave a testimonial), BotBuilt (home/construction robots), and Rainier Labs (mobile welding robots), plus undisclosed additional customers.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; $4.9M total pre-seed + seed funding raised, $4M seed led by Pear VC, Nov 2025)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates by generating high-fidelity, physics-enabled digital twins directly from ordinary captured video rather than requiring manual CAD/scene authoring, and lets teams run their real robot control software in-loop to close the sim-to-real gap. Closest named competitors are NVIDIA Isaac Sim/Omniverse and game-engine-based stacks (Unreal/Unity), which Parallax integrates with rather than fully replacing; its edge is the automated real-world-capture-to-twin pipeline.

one_liner: Synthetic data platform that procedurally generates photorealistic, sensor-accurate scenes (Data Lab API) to train and test perception models for autonomous vehicles, robotics, and drones.
notes: Founded 2017 by Kevin McNamara. HQ at 44 Montgomery St, San Francisco, CA. Series B $30M (Nov 16, 2022) led by March Capital; prior $11M Series A (return investors Costanoa Ventures, Foundry Group, Calibrate Ventures, Ubiquity Ventures). Total funding ~$45M (Series A $11M + Series B $30M plus seed); some sources cite cumulative figures varying $30M-$60M. Primarily AV/perception but explicitly serves robotics and drones via synthetic sensor data, qualifying as Layer 2 Sim-to-Real for robotics perception. Not a pure-AV company. Note: distinct from unrelated "Parallel Web Systems".
business_model: Sells access to its synthetic data platform (Data Lab self-serve API plus custom managed datasets) to perception/ML teams at automakers and AV companies, monetized via annual subscriptions and usage-based pricing; the company has cited multiple customers on seven-figure annual subscriptions.
customers: Named customers include Google, Toyota Research Institute, Woven Planet (Toyota), and Continental; customer count reported in the double digits, several on seven-figure annual contracts.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no precise figure publicly disclosed; reported 2.5x YoY revenue growth as of Series B Nov 2022, with third-party estimates of ~$5-25M annual revenue, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Vs Applied Intuition (full simulation/validation suite incl. Spectral) and Cognata, Parallel Domain is narrowly focused on procedurally generated, sensor-accurate photorealistic synthetic data for perception training rather than end-to-end AV simulation, with the Data Lab API letting ML engineers programmatically generate labeled datasets in a few lines of code.

one_liner: Open-source real-time physics engine with a Python interface (PyBullet) for rigid/soft-body dynamics, widely used to prototype robot reinforcement learning and sim-to-real research.
notes: Not a company; it is an open-source project (Zlib license), not a venture-funded entity, so funding/round/HQ fields are not applicable. Created by Erwin Coumans; first public Bullet release 2005, PyBullet Python interface introduced 2016. Developed largely while Coumans was at Sony (2003-2010), AMD (2010-2014), and Google Brain (2014-2022, Robot Locomotion team); Coumans now at NVIDIA. Coumans won a Scientific and Technical Academy Award for Bullet. GitHub: bulletphysics/bullet3. Widely used for robot RL and sim-to-real, supports URDF/SDF/MJCF and integrates with PyTorch/TensorFlow/Stable Baselines3. Layer 2 (Simulation) classification confirmed.
business_model: N/A (research/open-source). Bullet Physics SDK and its PyBullet Python bindings are free and open-source under the permissive zlib license; there is no commercial product, license fee, or paid support offering. Development has historically been volunteer- and employer-sponsored (lead author Erwin Coumans worked on it while at Google 2014-2022, later NVIDIA).
customers: No paying customers (free OSS). Adopted widely as a free tool by robotics/RL researchers and labs including OpenAI (used PyBullet-based environments), Google Brain/DeepMind robotics, and numerous universities; these are users, not payers.
revenue_status: N/A
technical_differentiation: Free, lightweight, easy-to-install real-time rigid/soft-body engine with a clean Python API, making it a default for cheap RL/robotics prototyping versus MuJoCo (formerly paid, now open-sourced by DeepMind) and NVIDIA Isaac Gym/Isaac Sim. Differentiates on simplicity, permissive zlib license, and CPU accessibility, but trails MuJoCo on contact-dynamics accuracy and Isaac on GPU-accelerated massively parallel simulation.

one_liner: Physics-based synthetic data PaaS generating labeled imagery (RGB, SAR, radar, thermal, hyperspectral) to train computer vision and robot perception models where real data is scarce.
notes: Seed round led by Space Capital with Tectonic Ventures, Congruent Ventures, Union Labs, Uncorrelated Ventures; other investors per PitchBook include In-Q-Tel, NSIN, Marlinspike. Legal entity DADOES, Inc. Omniverse-integrated synthetic data platform; serves geospatial/defense and robot perception. No newer round found post-2021; total funding remains ~$6M. Team size not reliably findable.
business_model: Sells a synthetic-data Platform-as-a-Service via tiered SaaS subscriptions (Teams and Organizations tiers, flat monthly fee for unlimited data generation, also offered through AWS Marketplace), plus custom project-based contracts for managed Synthetic Data as a Service, model development, and auto-labeling. Buyers are computer-vision/ML and remote-sensing engineering teams in defense, geospatial, medical, and robotics.
customers: Named engagements/partners include the UK's Dstl (via Faculty), Planet Labs (remote-sensing data), Esri, Carahsoft (public-sector reseller), and RIT's DIRS Laboratory; In-Q-Tel is a strategic investor/customer-aligned partner. Specific paying-customer named contracts otherwise largely undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on physics-based simulation of hard, non-visible sensor modalities (SAR/radar, thermal/IR, hyperspectral, X-ray) and geospatial/defense use cases, versus Parallel Domain (focused on photorealistic camera/lidar/radar for automotive and robotics autonomy) and Anyverse (focused on automotive in-cabin/ADAS RGB-IR sensor simulation).

one_liner: Builds AI-powered, high-performance robotics simulation and digital-twin platforms (O3DE/ROS2, GPU lidar, GenAI sim2real) to train, validate, and de-risk robots before real-world deployment.
notes: Polish software company specializing in robotics simulation and digital twins. Flagship product RoSi is an open-core digital-twin platform supporting real-time multi-robot simulation with SiL/HiL, offered as SaaS or on-premise. Serves warehousing, mining, agriculture, and automotive sectors with 3D modeling, scenario development, GPU lidar simulation, and GenAI-based sim2real. Active contributor to open-source robotics simulation (O3DE, ROS2). Public partnership with AMD/Silo AI on robotics simulation (2025). Founding year 2019 and team size confirmed via LinkedIn; HQ Warsaw confirmed via company site and Crunchbase. No funding rounds are publicly disclosed on Crunchbase/PitchBook search results, hence undisclosed/unknown. Fits Layer 2 (Simulation & Sim-to-Real).
business_model: Sells robotics simulation via its RoSi platform (high-performance physics/GPU-raytraced lidar sensor sim on O3DE/ROS2, with SiL/HiL testing, digital twins, and synthetic dataset generation) plus custom simulation engineering services delivered under per-project Statements of Work to automotive OEM/Tier 1-2, mining, warehouse, and agriculture customers. Revenue is primarily project-based contracts and engineering services rather than disclosed seat-based SaaS pricing; it also builds the open RAI agentic-AI sim2real framework.
customers: Named collaborators/partners include Tier IV and the Autoware Foundation (AWSIM autonomous-vehicle simulation), AWS Game Tech, Open Robotics, and AMD/Silo AI (digital-twin scenario reconstruction); end clients are described only as "numerous automotive OEM and Tier 1/Tier 2" companies. Specific paying-customer names beyond these partnerships are otherwise undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; ~75 employees, Warsaw, Poland)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via open-standards stack (native O3DE engine + ROS2, also NVIDIA Omniverse interop) and a proprietary CUDA GPU raytracing library for high-fidelity lidar/sensor simulation, versus closed/heavier incumbents like NVIDIA Isaac Sim (Omniverse-locked) and Applied Intuition/dSPACE; positions on open-source contribution leadership (O3DE ROS2 Gem, ros2-for-unity) rather than a single proprietary platform.

one_liner: Open-source generative digital-twin framework that auto-synthesizes diverse dual-arm manipulation data with strong domain randomization, plus a benchmark for robust sim-to-real bimanual robot policies.
notes: Not a company but an academic open-source research project from SJTU MoE AI Lab, HKU MMLab, and Shanghai AI Lab (with D-Robotics, THU, FDU, and others as contributors). RoboTwin 1.0 (arXiv 2409.02920, Sept 2024) was a CVPR 2025 highlight; RoboTwin 2.0 (arXiv 2506.18088, June 2025) adds 50 tasks, 731 objects across 147 categories, 100K expert demos, and supports Aloha-AgileX, ARX-X5, Franka, UR5 platforms. No funding/round/team-size data exists because it is a lab project, not a venture-backed firm. Founded_year reflects first public release (2024). Core lab affiliations span Shanghai/Hong Kong; HQ set to Shanghai (SJTU/Shanghai AI Lab) as primary anchor.
business_model: N/A (research/open-source)
customers: N/A as a paid product. Adopted as an open-source benchmark/data generator by the robot-learning research community; co-developed and used by its academic/lab consortium including SJTU, HKU MMLab, Shanghai AI Lab, and D-Robotics. Also served as the basis for the CVPR 2025 RoboTwin Dual-Arm Manipulation Challenge.
revenue_status: N/A
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from RoboCasa and ManiSkill2 by focusing specifically on dual-arm (bimanual) manipulation with fully automated expert-data generation (a multimodal LLM agent auto-synthesizes task programs) plus strong structured domain randomization across five axes (clutter, lighting, background, tabletop height, language). vs RoboCasa, which offers large-scale human demos but lacks automation and dual-arm focus; ships RoboTwin-OD (731 objects/147 categories), 50 tasks across 5 embodiments, and 100K+ pre-collected randomized trajectories.

also_known_as: 上海人工智能实验室; SHLAB; GRUtopia (桃源 / Taoyuan); InternUtopia; OpenRobotLab; InternRobotics; GRScenes; GRBench
one_liner: Builds GRUtopia/InternUtopia, a city-scale embodied-AI simulation platform (GRScenes, GRBench, LLM-driven NPCs) enabling scalable data collection and sim-to-real robot learning.
notes: Shanghai AI Laboratory (SHLAB) is a Chinese state-backed nonprofit national research institute, not a venture-funded company; founded/inaugurated at WAIC July 2020, HQ at 701 Yunjin Road, Shanghai. Led by Zhou Bowen. The robotics simulation work is by its OpenRobotLab group (rebranded InternRobotics); GRUtopia ("Dream General Robots in a City at Scale", arXiv 2407.10943) is a city-scale embodied-AI sim platform with GRScenes (100k interactive scenes, 89 categories), GRResidents (LLM-driven NPCs), and GRBench (loco-navigation/manipulation benchmark) for legged robots — qualifies as Layer 2 Simulation & Sim-to-Real. Now branded InternUtopia. Funding/team size for the robotics division specifically are undisclosed; as a government lab it has no conventional VC rounds.
business_model: N/A (research/open-source). As a state-backed nonprofit research lab, it is funded by Chinese central government, Shanghai municipal government, and university budgets via grant cycles rather than product sales; GRUtopia/InternUtopia and associated datasets (GRScenes, GRBench) are released free under open-source licenses on GitHub. Shanghai also runs broader AI industry subsidy programs (e.g. a ~1B yuan / ~$139M scheme) that support the ecosystem.
customers: No paying customers (open-source). Adopters/users are the global academic embodied-AI research community; InternUtopia was used as the official simulation platform for the Multimodal Robot Learning challenge at the IROS 2025 workshop. Specific named institutional partners are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: N/A
technical_differentiation: Built on NVIDIA Isaac Sim but differentiated from Meta's Habitat and SOTA city-scale platforms by GRScenes (100k finely-annotated interactive scenes across 89 categories) at city scale plus GRResidents, an LLM-driven NPC system enabling social interaction, task generation, and loco-manipulation benchmarks (GRBench). Versus Isaac Sim (a physics engine/toolkit) it adds a full scene+task+social ecosystem; versus Habitat (photorealistic navigation focus) it adds interactive manipulation and LLM-driven task generation. Closest emerging peer: TongSIM (BIGAI).

one_liner: Generative-AI synthetic data cloud that builds photorealistic digital twins of sensors, robots and environments to train and test computer vision and robotic perception models at scale.
notes: Founded 2018 by Dr. Bartek Wlodarczyk and Jakub Pietrzak; Polish roots with HQ in London. Raised $7M Series A Jan 2024 led by Cogito Capital Partners, with Edge VC, Taiwania Capital, Movens Capital, and HTGF. Total funding reported as $7M (earlier seed amount undisclosed). Platform procedurally generates fully annotated multimodal synthetic data and digital twins for automotive, robotics, medical, and industrial vision AI. Customers cited include Renault, Scania, Ericsson, Samsung, Syngenta. Robotics qualification: explicitly markets sim/digital-twin synthetic data for robotics perception training (Layer 2 simulation/sim-to-real), so real_robotics_ai=true. Team size not found.
business_model: Sells subscription/licensed access to the SKY ENGINE AI Synthetic Data Cloud, a platform that procedurally generates photorealistic, fully-annotated synthetic imagery and digital twins of sensors/robots/environments to train and validate computer-vision models. Buyers are enterprise ML/CV teams (automotive, robotics, telecom, medical); pricing spans self-serve tiers (reportedly ~$99-$399/month by data-point volume, unverified) up to custom enterprise contracts.
customers: Named customers/partners include Renault, Scania, Ericsson, Samsung, and Syngenta, with technology partnerships with NVIDIA and Microsoft.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$6.3M ARR FY2024, third-party Latka estimate, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from synthetic-data peers like Parallel Domain and Datagen by combining a physics-accurate sensor/digital-twin simulation engine with built-in domain-adaptation and at-scale CV model training in one cloud (tight NVIDIA Omniverse/TAO integration), rather than only emitting datasets. Multimodal coverage (RGB, X-ray/MRI, 5G RAN) is broader than vision-only rivals.

one_liner: Physical AI "Agentic Skill Library" letting any robot learn vision-driven manufacturing tasks; Illusion module generates synthetic data to train policies entirely in simulation and deploy sim-to-real.
notes: Spun out of Intelligent Autonomous Systems Group at TU Darmstadt; founders Suman Pal, Arjun Vir Datta, Mohammed Safiulla Dadabai, with Prof. Jan Peters as technical advisor. Earlier tech framed as "Visual Robot Programming" (robots learn from videos); later positioned as Physical AI Agentic Skill Library with NVIDIA Isaac Sim integration. Illusion = synthetic photorealistic dataset generation for vision models, trained in sim and deployed sim-to-real (qualifies Layer 2). Total raised ~$2.08M per Crunchbase/PitchBook aggregators; Techstars Pre-Seed June 2023 (~$20K accelerator check); other investors Atlantic (Berlin), Interface Capital, HIGHEST, TU Darmstadt. Round-size and team-size precise figures not disclosed. founded_year and Darmstadt HQ corroborated across Crunchbase/PitchBook/Sesamers/TU Darmstadt; company site confirms Telekinesis GmbH (Germany) and product. Crunchbase page returned 403 to fetch but data surfaced via search.
business_model: Sells a "Physical AI / Agentic Skill Library" software platform (Python-based, with the Illusion synthetic-data module) that lets factory operators deploy vision-driven robots from any vendor (ABB, KUKA, Universal Robots) for high-mix manufacturing tasks via no-code/Visual Robot Programming. Monetized through consultative enterprise deals/demos pitched on ROI (claims ~70% cost savings, 12-18 month payback); exact license/subscription pricing not publicly disclosed.
customers: Lists hardware/integration partners (ABB, KUKA, Universal Robots, OnRobot, Schunk, RobotIQ, Zivid, Lucid, NVIDIA Isaac Sim) and references industry partners Deutsche Bahn (DB) and Siemens plus backing from ex-CEOs of KUKA and Universal Robots; named paying customers are otherwise undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; total funding ~$2.08M raised, a TU Darmstadt/Techstars Berlin spin-off)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via train-entirely-in-simulation sim-to-real using its Illusion synthetic-data engine with Extreme Domain Randomization plus a broad agentic skill library (perception, planning, control), versus Micropsi Industries (MIRAI), which relies on real-world human hand-guided demonstration learning, and versus Covariant-style learned-picking stacks; Telekinesis emphasizes hardware-agnostic, no-code deployment across robot vendors.

also_known_as: Unity Software Inc., Unity Robotics, ML-Agents
one_liner: Real-time 3D engine whose Robotics Hub and ML-Agents toolkit let developers build simulation environments to train robots with reinforcement/imitation learning and run sim-to-real transfer.
notes: Public company on NYSE (ticker: U), IPO Sept 2020 raising ~$1.3B; pre-IPO VC funding raised over multiple rounds (Sequoia, Silver Lake, DFJ Growth, etc.) but consolidated total not cleanly reported, marked undisclosed. Founded as Over the Edge Entertainment in Copenhagen 2004; HQ now San Francisco, CA. Robotics is a division/use-case of the broader Unity real-time 3D engine, not the company's core business. Robotics relevance confirmed via official Unity-Technologies GitHub repos: Unity-Robotics-Hub (ROS integration, robotics simulation) and ml-agents (RL/imitation-learning training environments, PPO/SAC), with documented sim2real robotic-arm demonstrations. Assigned Layer 2 Simulation and Sim-to-Real. Team size not separately findable for the robotics division.
business_model: Sells per-seat subscriptions to its Unity real-time 3D engine (Pro ~$2,310/seat/yr; the Unity Industry plan, ~$4,000-5,000+/seat/yr custom, is mandatory for non-game/robotics use by companies over $1M revenue), plus Cloud, Vector/Grow ad monetization, and Unity AI services. The Robotics Hub and ML-Agents toolkit themselves are free/open-source funnels that drive paid engine and Industry-plan adoption among roboticists; Unity does not break out robotics as a standalone revenue line.
customers: Robotics/simulation users include Ghelia (sim-to-real soccer robots via ML-Agents) and academic/MATLAB-bridge research labs; broader Industry/enterprise simulation customers span automotive and manufacturing (e.g., Volkswagen, BMW, Hyundai use Unity for industrial/digital-twin work). Robotics-specific paying accounts are largely undisclosed.
revenue_status: profitable ($1.85B revenue FY2025, +2% YoY; Q4 2025 Adjusted EBITDA $125M at 25% margin) - company-wide; robotics segment not separately disclosed
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a mature, widely-adopted real-time rendering engine with native ROS integration (Robotics Hub) and the open-source ML-Agents RL/imitation toolkit, plus an Asset Store ecosystem. Versus NVIDIA Isaac Sim/Omniverse (GPU-accurate physics, photorealistic synthetic data, deeper sim-to-real tooling) and Epic's Unreal Engine, Unity competes on ease-of-use, accessibility, and breadth of developer community rather than physics fidelity, where NVIDIA leads.

also_known_as: 1HMX Inc.; formerly AIS Global (Advanced Input Systems)
one_liner: Builds Nexus NX1, a turnkey whole-body teleoperation rig bundling HaptX gloves, Virtuix Omni One and full-body mocap to capture sub-mm motion and tactile data for humanoid AI training.
notes: 1HMX is the rebrand (announced Aug 20, 2025) of AIS Global, an established human-machine interface (HMI) manufacturer HQ'd in Coeur d'Alene, Idaho, with nine facilities across US, UK, Germany, Taiwan, China. Portfolio company of PE firm KPS Capital Partners (privately held; no disclosed venture funding => undisclosed). Brands: Advanced Input Systems, Gamesman, HaptX, Medigenic, Duraswitch, Touch International. Census assignment driven by Nexus NX1 (launched Nov 18, 2025; pre-order, ships Q2 2026): a stationary turnkey rig integrating HaptX Gloves G1, Virtuix Omni One treadmill, Freeaim motorized shoes and 72-DoF full-body/hand mocap, capturing sub-mm kinematics plus tactile pressure, COM and locomotion data for humanoid teleoperation and AI training. Teleoperation/demonstration-data play for robot learning => in_scope=true, L3 Data Infrastructure, L4 hardware secondary. core_or_division='division' as NX1/robotics is a new business line within the broader HMI parent. Leadership: Joe Baddeley (CEO), Jake Rubin (President Immersive Technologies, HaptX founder), Joe Michaels (SVP Global). Founding year of underlying AIS business not cleanly findable; left unknown to avoid invention.
business_model: Hardware/systems sales: 1HMX is a global human-machine interface (HMI) manufacturer (formerly AIS Global) that designs and builds interface components, sub-assemblies and complete systems for OEMs across aerospace, medical, industrial, gaming and robotics; its flagship robotics product, the Nexus NX1, is a turnkey whole-body teleoperation rig sold to enterprise/industrial buyers (manufacturing, medical, defense, research) on a per-unit pre-order/purchase model (pricing undisclosed, shipping Q2 2026). Notably it vertically integrates its own owned brand HaptX (gloves) alongside third-party Virtuix Omni One and Freeaim hardware.
customers: Undisclosed for the Nexus NX1 specifically (pre-order stage as of late 2025, no named pilots public). At the parent/HMI level, customers are major OEMs and system integrators in aerospace, medical, industrial and gaming; a notable adjacent reference is Virtuix's Omni One being used by the University of Central Florida for humanoid-robot teleoperation, but no NX1 paying customer is publicly named.
revenue_status: scaling (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; privately held by KPS Capital Partners as an established multi-facility HMI manufacturer with ~200+ employees in the Advanced Input Systems unit alone, so legacy HMI business is revenue-generating; the Nexus NX1 robotics line itself is pre-revenue/pre-shipment)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on sensory density and turnkey integration: NX1 bundles 72-DoF full-body mocap with sub-mm motion and high-fidelity tactile/force feedback (via owned brand HaptX gloves) plus 360-degree locomotion (Virtuix Omni One, Freeaim shoes), capturing tactile displacement/pressure data that vision-only or lightweight inertial rigs miss. Versus closest competitors Xsens (lightweight textile mocap suits) and Unitree (lightweight strap-based teleop), 1HMX bets on data fidelity/touch over operator portability and cost, backed by in-house HMI manufacturing scale.

one_liner: Multimodal AI data-infrastructure platform (MooreData) providing collection, annotation and evaluation pipelines—including dedicated embodied-AI/robotics training data such as teleop and manipulation trajectories—for genAI, automotive and robotics builders.
notes: Multimodal AI data-infrastructure company founded 2019; global offices in Singapore, Paris, and Silicon Valley (Palo Alto/Mountain View, CA likely operational HQ). MooreData Platform processes image/video/text/audio/point-cloud data. Raised $8M Series A announced ~May 28, 2026; lead investor not disclosed in public sources. Reported ~$9.5M ARR / ~$28.4M valuation (getLatka, unverified). Serves 1,000+ customers across Generative AI, Embodied AI, and Automotive AI. Qualifies for L3 via dedicated embodied-AI/robotics data offering (build/scale robotics training data, teleoperation, manipulation trajectory). Partly a general data-labeling vendor; included for its dedicated robotics data product. Sources: Dealroom, Yahoo Finance, Crunchbase, company site. Team size not findable.
business_model: Sells AI training-data services and software via its MooreData platform—data collection, cleaning, annotation, off-the-shelf datasets, and model-in-the-loop evaluation pipelines across text/image/audio/video/3D/point-cloud, with a dedicated embodied-AI/robotics offering. Buyers are AI/ML and autonomous-driving/robotics teams; monetization is a mix of per-project annotation/dataset service contracts and platform deployment (public cloud, on-prem, or hybrid), rather than pure self-serve SaaS.
