Other investors: Lanchi VC (BlueRun), VitalBridge, 58 Strategic Investment, Oasis Capital, Shuimu Tsinghua Seed Alumni Fund. Core product: NeuroScale paradigm using surface-EMG (sEMG) signals encoded by PULSE foundation model to reconstruct hand posture, force, and tactile data, overcoming occlusion/force-capture limits of vision-only collection. Deploys data collection in home services (with 58 Group: laundry, cleaning, kitchen) and high-end manufacturing. Deliberately avoids robot hardware; sells data infrastructure only. In scope: AI-native demonstration/manipulation-data service for robot learning. L1 secondary tag because PULSE is a foundation model; core layer is L3 data infrastructure. Round size in USD undisclosed (only aggregate CNY 500M+ reported); last_round_date approximate (~May 2026). Team size not found.
business_model: Sells a proprietary "NeuroScale" data-acquisition stack: self-developed surface-EMG (sEMG) capture kits worn by human operators plus the PULSE foundation model that reconstructs hand posture, force, and tactile signals, productized as a high-fidelity training-data layer for embodied-AI/humanoid-robot developers. Monetization is via paid data-collection/partnership engagements with robot makers and manufacturers (selling embodied-manipulation datasets and the EMG hardware+model pipeline); specific pricing/contract terms are not publicly disclosed and the company is still pre-commercial.
customers: 58 Group (58同城) is a named strategic investor and data-collection partner for home-service robot skill datasets (laundry, cleaning, kitchen tasks). The company also cites unnamed "leading global high-end manufacturers" for industrial embodied-data scenarios; no other paying customers are named publicly (otherwise undisclosed).
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; founded Aug 2025, ~5 months operating, raised 500M+ yuan / ~$70M across angel/strategic/Pre-A1 rounds)
technical_differentiation: Bets on non-invasive sEMG motor-neural signals to capture continuous hand posture plus force/tactile values, vs vision/teleoperation-based data approaches; the PULSE model reconstructs fine-grained force not visible to cameras, addressing robotic force-control rigidity. Closest named peers using human muscle/neural signals are Meta's sEMG wristband (Nature 2025) and China's neural-wristband maker Vele (唯理), but those target HCI input/gesture control rather than OriginFlow's robot-manipulation training-data layer, so direct head-to-head competition is limited.

one_liner: Decentralized teleoperation and data platform that crypto-token-incentivizes operators to puppeteer robots, producing verifiable, on-chain demonstration datasets to train robotics foundation models.
notes: a16z CSX-led $11M seed announced June 2025 at CSX Demo Day (June 3); participants include Stanford Blockchain Builders Fund, Symbolic, Volt Capital, Virtuals Protocol, plus angels. Founders Bayley Wang and Chyna Qu. Three pillars: Data (protocols to validate/incentivize visual robot data), Teleoperation (uniform standards, turnkey operators ~$50/hr, payments, software), and Models (collaboration on robot policy/foundation models). Crypto-token-incentivized crowdsourcing with on-chain data provenance/revenue-share. In scope: AI-native robot training-data service — the demonstration-data/teleoperation category exists specifically to train learned robot policies and would not exist without robot learning. Team size not publicly disclosed.
business_model: Operates a decentralized teleoperation and visual-data marketplace where robotics companies pay to access a global pool of human teleoperators and verifiable demonstration datasets; coordination, payments, and dispute resolution run through smart contracts using its native $PIX utility token, which buyers use as a reservation/payment asset and which rewards operators/data contributors. Revenue is intended to come from taking a cut on teleop sessions and data transactions between robotics firms (buyers) and operators rather than fixed SaaS seats.
customers: No named paying robotics-company customers are publicly disclosed; the company is in a community/points pre-launch phase. A partnership with Sentient (data consortia) has been referenced, but paying or piloting robotics OEMs are largely undisclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; $11M seed raised June 2025 led by a16z CSX, token/network in pre-launch points stage)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from centralized teleoperation/data vendors like Scale AI and Encord by crowdsourcing teleoperators globally and using crypto-token incentives ($PIX) plus on-chain smart contracts to produce verifiable, provenance-tracked robot demonstrations with standardized teleop interfaces. Closest crypto-native peers are other decentralized physical-AI/robotics-data projects (e.g., BitRobot/FrodoBots, PrismaX competes on building a verifiable data flywheel rather than proprietary in-house labeling.

one_liner: End-to-end robotics training-data division of Cogito Tech: multimodal collection, 3D/LiDAR annotation, and human-in-the-loop teleoperation to train embodied-AI and robot perception/control systems.
notes: Division/brand of Cogito Tech LLC (parent founded ~2014 per LeadIQ; sources vary 2006-2014), a global AI training-data / data-annotation managed-service firm headquartered at Levittown, New York, US (~870 employees across 4 continents as of 2026). RoboStream is Cogito Tech's dedicated robotics/embodied-AI data division, publicly launched ~May 2026 (IssueWire press release, "RoboStream Launches a Specialized Robotics Data Division"). founded_year reflects the division's launch (2025-2026); parent firm is older. Provides end-to-end robot training-data pipelines: multimodal collection (real + simulated across RGB-D/LiDAR/IMU/telemetry), advanced 3D annotation, and teleoperation; serves logistics, manufacturing, defense, agriculture, healthcare. in_scope=true: teleoperation/demonstration-data service is AI-native (data infra for robot learning, would not exist without learned policies). No standalone funding found; parent Cogito Tech appears bootstrapped/unfunded per Crunchbase/Tracxn (no rounds disclosed). Primary layer L3 Data Infrastructure; secondary L6 (vertical robotics data services). Caution: multiple unrelated companies named "Cogito" exist (e.g. Cogito Corp emotion-AI, Cogito Capital) — distinct from Cogito Tech LLC. [Merged duplicate: Cogito Tech] RoboStream, Cogito Tech's robotics division, captures multimodal RGB/depth/LiDAR/IMU/force data via expert-in-the-loop teleoperation and annotation to train robot control and manipulation policies.
business_model: Services-based (not SaaS): RoboStream sells end-to-end robotics training-data services to robotics/embodied-AI developers via project and managed-service contracts spanning multimodal data collection (RGB-D/LiDAR/IMU/telemetry), 3D point-cloud and sensor-fusion annotation, and human-in-the-loop teleoperation/demonstration data, plus off-the-shelf datasets (e.g., robotics OTS/Ego-Exo4D-style sets). Pricing is custom/contract-based per-project or volume; specific rates are not publicly disclosed.
customers: Undisclosed. No named robotics customers are public for RoboStream; marketing cites use-case verticals (logistics, manufacturing, defense, agriculture, healthcare) rather than named buyers. Parent Cogito Tech serves enterprise/AI clients but does not name RoboStream accounts.
revenue_status: early-revenue (RoboStream is a newly launched 2025/2026 division with no separately disclosed revenue; parent Cogito Tech revenue is third-party-estimated and inconsistent, ranging from ~$5.1M (Kona Equity, unverified) to ~$144.5M (Growjo, unverified) — no audited figure)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on owning the full embodied-AI data stack in one vendor — physical multimodal collection of edge cases (glare, transparency, deformables) plus dedicated low-latency leased-line teleoperation for high-stakes domains (surgery, EOD/bomb disposal, precision manipulation) — backed by Cogito Tech's decade of HITL labeling and ISO 9001/27001/SOC 2 compliance. Versus Scale AI (broader/AV-and-LLM data, heavier teleop investment) and Encord/Labellerr (annotation-tooling-led), RoboStream competes as a services-led, robotics-specialized provider rather than a self-serve platform.

also_known_as: Silicon Valley Robotics Center, SVRC, roboticscenter.ai, DeepAware AI
one_liner: Full-stack robotics data infrastructure: a marketplace where teams buy and sell robot demonstration datasets (LeRobot/MCAP/RLDS/HDF5), plus teleop collection, custom data, and hardware rentals.
notes: Legal entity is DeepAware AI (YC S25); roboticscenter.ai / "Silicon Valley Robotics Center" (SVRC) is its operating brand. NOT to be confused with Silicon Valley Robotics (svrobo.org), the older 2010 nonprofit industry cluster — different organization. Founders: Jerry Huang (CEO) and Stela Tong. HQ Mountain View, CA (1117 Independence Ave) with a Boston, MA office. Layer 3 fit: operates a robot demonstration-data marketplace ("world's fastest-growing robotics data marketplace") with 20,000+ hours / 11M+ episodes indexed in LeRobot/MCAP/RLDS/HDF5, 42+ commercial datasets, licensed across 30+ countries; also teleop data platform, custom collection, RL environments, plus robot hardware sales/rentals (72-hr delivery). Classic "Scale AI for robotics" data marketplace — AI-native (demonstration-data service for robot learning), in_scope=true. Funding: $500K reported (standard YC investment); exact round size/date not separately disclosed on Crunchbase — date approximated to YC S25 batch (Aug 2025). Team size not found. [Merged duplicate: Silicon Valley Robotics Center] Full-stack robotics infrastructure provider offering VR-teleoperated fleets and trained operators that produce HDF5 manipulation demonstration datasets for training ACT, Diffusion Policy, and VLA models.
business_model: Operator-led, multi-stream physical-AI infrastructure: (1) a robot demonstration-data marketplace where collectors set per-episode/per-dataset prices and ML teams buy/license datasets in LeRobot/MCAP/RLDS/HDF5 (priced from ~$12K for medical datasets up to a ~$120K "Embodied AI Bundle" of 7M+ episodes), (2) hardware resale and leasing of 60+ robot platforms (e.g., Unitree G1 humanoid from ~$18,400, OpenArm arms, Go2 quadrupeds) with 72-hour US delivery, and (3) paid teleoperation/fleet-ops and custom data-collection programs plus on-platform GPU training/VLA fine-tuning.
customers: Site cites use by research teams from Stanford, MIT, and Carnegie Mellon (and 6+ universities), plus AI labs, robotics startups, industrial operators, and event producers; no named paying enterprise contracts are publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; YC Summer 2025 company)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates by bundling the full stack (hardware procurement/leasing + a priced data marketplace + teleop collection + on-platform training) rather than selling data alone, with format-agnostic LeRobot/RLDS/HDF5 support and scene-organized datasets (supermarket, home, industrial, 3C). Closest competitors: Sensei (YC, robot training-data-at-scale, more pure data-collection/teleop services) and Hugging Face's LeRobot Hub (free open-source community dataset repository); Robotics Center layers a commercial marketplace and hardware logistics on top of the LeRobot ecosystem it is built around.

one_liner: Wearable inertial mocap suits and gloves that capture human demonstrations and export (BVH/FBX/CSV) or stream to Isaac/MuJoCo/MoveIt for robot teleop, sim, and training-data creation.
notes: Founded March 2014 in Copenhagen by Jakob Balslev, Matias Sondergaard, Anders Klok. Core consumer/creative business is studio-grade inertial motion capture (Smartsuit Pro II, Smartgloves II, Coil Pro). Robotics is a dedicated division/use-case: human demonstration capture, segmenting/exporting BVH/FBX/CSV, real-time streaming to Isaac/MuJoCo/MoveIt with Linux/Ubuntu SDK for teleop, sim, and embodied-AI training datasets. Primary fit Layer 3 (data infrastructure / demonstration-data service for robot learning); secondary L4 (hardware wearables). In_scope=true: teleoperation/demonstration data service for robot learning is AI-native per scope rules, even though mocap also serves non-robotics media use. Total funding ~$13.7M (Tracxn); CB Insights reports ~$11.8M — discrepancy noted. Last disclosed round: $3M strategic led by Naver Z (Aug 2022) at ~$80M valuation, with Vaekstfonden and North-East Ventures. Team size varies by source (~119-127 per PitchBook/RocketReach 2025-2026). ai_intensity left blank (not an L6 vertical).
business_model: Hardware + freemium SaaS: sells inertial mocap hardware (Smartsuit Pro II ~$2,495-$2,745 suit-only, ~$3,500-$5,000 full body+glove+face bundle) to creators and studios, plus tiered Rokoko Studio software subscriptions (free Starter tier; Studio+/Pro paid plans roughly $20-$50/mo or ~$299/yr per seat) and a Motion Library asset marketplace. Enterprise/custom-priced contracts add local storage, dedicated support, and onboarding; the robotics/teleop use case (export BVH/FBX/CSV or stream to Isaac/MuJoCo/MoveIt) reuses the same hardware+software stack.
customers: ~50,000 users, 60-70% individual indie creators; named larger clients cited include Netflix, Sony, Disney, and Microsoft (entertainment/animation use). Specific robotics/teleop paying customers not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: scaling (~$17.5M annual revenue, third-party estimate via Growjo/Owler, unverified; total funding ~$14.9M, last round Series A $3M Aug 2022)
technical_differentiation: Competes on affordability and creator accessibility vs Xsens (Movella), the higher-precision incumbent that launched a dedicated Xsens Link + Xsens Humanoid suite with native ROS 2 / Isaac Sim / MuJoCo integration explicitly for humanoid teleop and AI training; Rokoko's IMU suits/gloves are far cheaper than Xsens and Perception Neuron but lower-accuracy, so its edge is price/ecosystem breadth rather than the robotics-grade kinematic fidelity Xsens markets to humanoid training.

one_liner: Generates tens of thousands of perfectly labeled synthetic images from a handful of real robot camera frames to train robot vision models for detection, segmentation, and 6D pose.
notes: Y Combinator W21 batch. Founders: Artem Avdacev, Ian Dewancker, Josh Kuntz. Investors include Y Combinator, Garage Capital, Olima Ventures. Total funding reported only as ~$130K (unverified third-party); treating round size/total as undisclosed. Uses film/gaming simulation tech for synthetic and hybrid (real+synthetic) training data. Reported ~$5M revenue with 5-person team in 2024 (getLatka, unverified). Sits squarely in Layer 2 sim-to-real / synthetic data for robot perception.
business_model: Sells custom synthetic training datasets (and the data-generation pipeline behind them) for robot/computer-vision models — object detection, segmentation, and 6D pose estimation — built from a handful of real camera frames using film/game rendering tech. Buyers are robotics and manufacturing/automation teams; engagements are delivery-based custom datasets (typically 10-15 business days) plus recurring data-as-a-service/SaaS subscriptions; specific list pricing not publicly disclosed.
customers: OTTO Motors (autonomous mobile robots) used SBX synthetic data to bootstrap and improve its AMR computer-vision systems. Broader customer base spans automotive, healthcare, and manufacturing for defect detection, assembly inspection, and pose estimation; most individual accounts undisclosed.
revenue_status: scaling (~$5M ARR FY2024, per getLatka; bootstrapped, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on extreme data efficiency — claims ~100 real images is enough to generate tens of thousands of perfectly labeled synthetic images reaching strong model performance — and on fast custom dataset turnaround for industrial robotics. Versus Parallel Domain and Synthesis AI (broader autonomous-driving/general synthetic-data players), SBX is a small, bootstrapped, capital-efficient niche player focused specifically on robot perception (detection/segmentation/6D pose) rather than AV scene simulation.

one_liner: Data infrastructure provider whose Data Engine for Physical AI collects and semantically labels real-world robot teleoperation and manipulation data to train robot foundation models.
notes: In_scope applies to the robotics product line only: "Data Engine for Physical AI" — a dedicated robot data-collection and annotation operation with 100,000+ production hours at an SF prototyping lab plus global contributors, using human teleoperators and autonomous data-collection robots, with semantic labeling of intent/task-structure/failure-modes for robot foundation models. Customers cited include Physical Intelligence, Generalist AI, Cobot. 2026 Universal Robots partnership embeds the Physical AI Data Engine in the UR AI Trainer (imitation-learning system, large-scale industrial robotics dataset). Scale's general business (data annotation, RLHF, LLM eval, enterprise software) is out of scope. Company-level data: founded 2016 by Alexandr Wang and Lucy Guo (YC). Series F May 2024 raised $1B at $13.8B valuation; total equity funding ~$1.6B across 7 rounds. June 2025 Meta invested ~$14.3B for a 49% non-voting stake valuing Scale at $29B; Wang departed to lead Meta Superintelligence Labs, Jason Droege became interim CEO. Scale remains an independent entity (core_or_division="division" reflects that the robotics offering is one product line within the larger company, not a separately incorporated unit). Team size not reliably findable for the robotics line specifically. Sources: scale.com/blog/physical-ai, scale.com/blog/scale-ai-universal-robots-physical-ai, en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scale_AI, builtinsf.com Meta $29B valuation article.
business_model: B2B data services: Scale sells its Data Engine for Physical AI (custom robot data collection from teleoperation/manipulation across robot embodiments, ML-assisted 3D semantic annotation, and pre-built robotics dataset "Data Streams") to robotics and foundation-model companies, typically via direct/consultative enterprise sales and custom contracts (its broader business runs on per-task/volume-based and committed enterprise data-labeling deals, e.g. Google reportedly spent ~$150M in 2024). Pricing for the Physical AI line is not publicly disclosed.
customers: Physical AI robotics partners named by Scale include Physical Intelligence, Generalist AI, and Cobot. Broader (non-physical-AI) named clients include OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and the U.S. military/government, though several large clients (Google, OpenAI, Microsoft) reportedly churned after Meta's June 2025 investment.
revenue_status: scaling (~$2B revenue/ARR 2025, up from ~$1.5B run-rate end-2024; ~$870M revenue 2024 reported, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Scale's edge is scale and integration: it claims the largest enterprise data engine, 100,000+ hours of real-robot interaction data from its own SF prototyping lab (not simulation), and tight coupling to foundation-model training workflows. Versus physical-AI data competitors like TELUS Digital and Encord/Labellerr, Scale differentiates on first-party real-world collection volume and semantic annotation depth (intent, task structure, failure modes), though it competes for the same robotics buyers and faces reputational/customer-conflict pressure post-Meta.

one_liner: Provides a combined low-cost teleoperation hardware and software platform with paid human operators to generate robotic training demonstration data at scale, addressing data scarcity in robot AI.
notes: YC Summer 2024 (S24) batch. Founders Anubhav Guha (CEO) and John Piotti (CTO), both MIT engineers, ex-Aurora Flight Sciences/Boeing DARPA team. Builds a sub-$300 sensorized exoskeleton teleoperation arm; network of paid operators generates human-demonstration data labeled and delivered to robotics clients ("Scale AI for robotics data"). CAUTION: a separate Portuguese company named "Sensei" (contactless stores) raised a ~$16M/EUR15M Series A led by BlueCrow Capital in Oct 2024 — this is a DIFFERENT company and was conflated in some aggregator data (e.g. "$23.1M total"). The robotics Sensei's confirmed round is a $500K pre-seed led by Y Combinator (2024-09). Total/round figures for the robotics entity beyond YC pre-seed are not reliably findable.
business_model: Operates a "Scale AI for robotics" two-sided data service: Sensei manufactures a low-cost (<$300) sensorized teleoperation/exoskeleton device and runs a network of trained, paid human operators who use it to fulfill bespoke robot-demonstration data requests (e.g., folding clothes, loading dishwashers). Buyers are robotics/embodied-AI companies who pay per dataset/demonstration on a project-based contract; pricing details are not publicly disclosed beyond the "~10x cheaper, 2x faster than in-house teleop" claim.
customers: Undisclosed - no specific paying robotics customers are named in public sources; marketed broadly to robotics manipulation/embodied-AI companies and researchers.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$3.9M revenue, third-party estimate, unverified). Note: only ~$500K disclosed funding (YC, Calibrate Ventures, Lotus Venture Partners, Network VC); a separate European "Sensei Robotics" with ~$23M raised is a different entity and should not be conflated.
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on an ultra-low-cost (<$300) sensorized exoskeleton/teleop hardware paired with a managed paid-operator labor network, versus Scale AI (broader data-labeling incumbent now entering robotics, but without proprietary low-cost capture hardware) and Physical Intelligence/Tesla Optimus-style in-house teleop fleets. Edge is the cost/speed of distributed real-world demonstration capture rather than proprietary models; defensibility vs. cheap open-source rigs (e.g., ALOHA/UMI) is limited, making it primarily an execution and operator-network play.

one_liner: Builds anthropomorphic dexterous robot hands and a glove-based teleoperation system, capturing rich tactile/motion demonstration data to train embodied-AI manipulation policies.
notes: London-based; began 1987 as Richard Greenhill's hobbyist group, formally incorporated 1997 (founding year sourced from Tracxn/Crunchbase). Flagship Dexterous Hand (24 movements / 20 DoF) and newer DEX-EE series; Shadow Teleoperation System uses a glove controller (historically paired with HaptX gloves) so operators remotely drive an anthropomorphic hand, capturing tactile/motion demonstration data feeding embodied-AI manipulation learning. In scope as teleoperation/demonstration-data service for robot training — the qualifying L3 product — even though the hands predate modern AI. Funding: Crunchbase reports ~9 rounds from ~43 investors, first round 2015, most recent a grant in 2020-07; sizes/lead undisclosed. Team size varies by source (Tracxn ~20, PitchBook ~40). Partners with Google DeepMind on RL manipulation research.
business_model: Hardware sales plus custom engineering and government R&D contracts: sells the Shadow Dexterous Hand (Dexterous Hand / DEX-EE series, roughly $74K to $100K+ per unit, quote-based) and a glove-based teleoperation system, primarily to research labs and to nuclear/pharma operators who need remote dexterous manipulation. Increasingly positions the hands as a source of rich tactile/motion demonstration data for training embodied-AI manipulation policies.
customers: Named users include NASA, ESA, OpenAI, GSK, Carnegie Mellon, UCL, and University of Bielefeld; the hand is a long-standing research tool across universities. In May 2025 the UK's ARIA awarded Shadow ~£11M for the OGRES and UPWARD robotic-hand projects (government-funded R&D, not commercial revenue).
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$7-7.5M annual revenue, third-party estimate, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on highly anthropomorphic, tendon-driven hands with human-equivalent degrees of freedom and rich tactile sensing plus a matched teleoperation glove for demonstration capture, a 25+ year R&D pedigree. Versus Wonik Robotics' Allegro Hand (16-DOF, ~$15K, lower fidelity/cheaper) and China's Linkerbot (explicitly aiming to match Shadow performance at a fraction of cost and mass-producing 1,000+ hands/month), Shadow is the premium, lower-volume, higher-dexterity incumbent now pressured on price and scale.

one_liner: Training-data vendor whose Physical AI service collects teleoperation demonstration data (leader-follower, VR, exoskeleton) with synced joint/force/camera logs and licenses datasets for humanoid/manipulation models.
notes: General AI training-data vendor (data collection, annotation, licensing, de-identification) for healthcare, conversational AI, computer vision, and GenAI; founded 2018 by Vatsal Ghiya and Hardik Parikh as an evolution of a prior medical-transcription business (roots in 2004), formerly Healthly.AI. Robotics relevance is its dedicated "Physical AI" service: teleoperation cells for robot-manipulation demonstration data (trajectories, joint states, force/contact dynamics, multi-camera) plus dataset licensing for humanoid/manipulation models. In scope per the teleoperation/demonstration-data-service criterion (L3 data infrastructure). Bootstrapped — no disclosed VC rounds; reported ~$39.7M revenue (2023) per getLatka. HQ Louisville, KY (offices in Silicon Valley and India). Multiple sources report acquisition by Ubiquity dated Feb 2026 — unconfirmed against a primary source, so status left active; team-size figures vary by source (265 in 2023 to 600+).
business_model: Sells project-based and licensed AI training-data services: collects, annotates, and licenses multimodal datasets (text/audio/image/video, plus its newer Physical AI service capturing teleoperation demonstration data with synced joint/force/tactile/proprioception signals) to enterprises building AI/ML and robotics/VLA models. Revenue comes from custom data-collection/annotation contracts and dataset licensing via the ShaipCloud platform; specific per-unit or contract pricing is not publicly disclosed.
customers: Fortune 100 enterprises cited by the company including Google, Microsoft, and Amazon as data customers; broader base of 100+ customers across healthcare and conversational AI. Specific named buyers of the newer Physical AI / teleoperation data service are not publicly disclosed. Acquired by Ubiquity Global Services (announced Feb 12, 2026), now operating as "Shaip, by Ubiquity."
revenue_status: scaling (~$39.7M revenue 2023 per getLatka; ~$60.5M estimated per Growjo, both unverified third-party estimates)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates vs general AI-data labeling competitors (Scale AI, iMerit) through deep regulated-domain expertise (healthcare/clinical, 60+ language conversational data) and proprietary hard-to-acquire datasets, now extended into Physical AI with operated teleop cells capturing synchronized vision/depth/tactile/force/proprioception multimodal robot demonstration data rather than lab-only or purely text/image annotation. Edge is domain access and full-stack collection-plus-annotation, not a unique technical moat.

one_liner: Automated dense annotation and quality checks for robotics training data; learned models segment video frame-by-frame into discrete labeled actions at low cost with fast turnaround.
notes: YC Spring 2026 batch. Founders: Farhan Khan (CEO), Ali Abdalla, Ali Abid, Nour Eldifrawy. Customers cited on site: Ultra, Parametric. Open-source demo at demo.shotwell.ai. NOTE: Assigned primary layer is L2 Simulation, but actual offering is data-annotation/quality infrastructure (better fit L3 data infra). Applies learned models to robot training data (manipulation video), so real_robotics_ai=true though it is a data/tooling layer rather than physical robots itself. Funding not publicly disclosed beyond YC participation.
business_model: Sells an automated robotics-data-annotation and QA service: learned models segment training videos frame-by-frame into discrete labeled actions and score them against task/SOP rubrics, sold to robotics/embodied-AI companies as a faster, cheaper alternative to manual labeling. Pricing/contract model is not publicly disclosed (likely usage- or volume-based annotation fees); buyers are robot foundation-model teams.
customers: Two named users cited on the company site: Ultra and Parametric (both robotics startups). No other paying customers publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (YC Spring 2026 batch, founded 2026, ~4 employees; no public revenue or ARR figure disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from generalist data-labeling vendors like Scale AI and Encord by being robotics-native: learned models auto-segment video into discrete actions and run automated quality/SOP-rubric scoring (dense annotation at low cost and instant turnaround) rather than relying on human-in-the-loop labeling pipelines. Founders bring Gradio (acquired by Hugging Face), Tesla self-driving, and humanoid-robotics (Sunday Robotics) experience.

also_known_as: Mifeng Technology; 蜀途科技; SynaData
one_liner: Extracts multimodal embodied training data from internet RGB videos via its SynaData pipeline (dimension enhancement, cross-domain retargeting), cutting robot data-collection cost to ~1/5000 of industry average.
last_round_lead: Orient Fortune Capital and Jiangu Capital
notes: Assigned primary layer L3 Data Infrastructure — correct: embodied-AI training-data provider (SynaData pipeline) feeding robot learning / VLA models, AI-native, in_scope=true. IMPORTANT ENTITY DISAMBIGUATION: the prompt conflates two distinct companies. (1) SHUTU TECHNOLOGY (�this entity) — Shenzhen, founded 2024, CTO Lin Xiao; raised an angel round of tens of millions RMB led by Orient Fortune Capital + Jiangu Capital (36kr exclusive, ~Oct 2025); extracts multimodal embodied data from internet videos. This row documents Shutu. (2) MIFENG / 觅蜂科技 (Maniformer) — a SEPARATE embodied-data platform under Zhiyuan Robotics (AgiBot) that raised hundreds of millions RMB led by Sequoia China, targeting 10M hours of data in 2026. These are different entities; 蜜蜂/Mifeng and 觅蜂 vs 蜀途/Shutu should not be merged. 'Shenwei China' funding mentioned in prompt not independently confirmed for Shutu. Founding year and one-liner both sourced from 36kr. Team size and total funding (USD) not disclosed.
business_model: Sells embodied-AI training data and pipeline services: its SynaData pipeline extracts sub-centimeter, multimodal robot-action trajectories from internet/human-activity RGB(D) videos (basis of its open-sourced HORA dataset, 150k+ trajectories), positioned as a "neutral data infrastructure" layer licensed/sold to robotics and embodied-AI model developers. Earlier it also built general-purpose composite robots (青山 robot, 百舸 model) for agriculture/environmental tasks; specific pricing/contract terms are not publicly disclosed.
customers: Undisclosed by name. Company claims business partnerships with several unnamed "industry-leading" enterprises with smart-agriculture and smart-environmental orders being deployed; HORA dataset is open-sourced for the wider ecosystem. Named ecosystem partner: NVIDIA (Inception program; Isaac Sim/Omniverse, Jetson AGX Orin).
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue or ARR figure disclosed; only early smart-agriculture/environmental orders reported as deploying)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on video-to-action data extraction (cross-domain retargeting and dimension enhancement converting 2D human-activity video into sub-cm 6-DoF robot trajectories with tactile signals), branding itself the "Scale AI of Robotics." Closest named competitors: ShengShu Technology (Vidar multi-view physical-AI data) and embodied-data collectors like Abaka AI / 整数智能 (manual teleop collection/annotation); Synapath's edge is mining existing internet video rather than costly teleoperated data capture.

also_known_as: 至简动力; Hangzhou Zhijian Power; Joyful Embodied
one_liner: Full-stack embodied-AI developer training robot foundation models via a high-efficiency human-hand and human-demonstration data closed loop, plus its own robot hardware.
notes: Founded 2025-07-31. Chairman Wang Kai (ex-Li Auto CTO), CEO Jia Peng (ex-Li Auto intelligent-driving R&D head), COO Wang Jiajia (ex-Li Auto). Raised ~$289.3M / RMB ~2B across 5 rounds in ~6 months; valuation >$1B — fastest embodied-AI unicorn. Investors: Tencent, Alibaba, HongShan (Sequoia China), Lanchi Ventures, Vision Capital, Legend Capital, CAS Star, Gaorong, China Merchants Ventures, CTF Capital. NOTE: orchestrator's "20B RMB" is a unit error — sources report 20亿 RMB = RMB 2 billion ≈ $289M. Full-stack: builds AI models, robot hardware and control software; first self-developed robot body in ~45 days. Distinctive Layer-3 data-loop: mass human-hand operation-data capture for pretraining plus 24/7 teleoperation/demonstration data-collection facility ("school for robots") reportedly in Fujian. in_scope=true: AI-native unified world-model+VLA and demonstration-data operation, not a pre-AI category. Team size not disclosed. Last-round individual lead/size not separately disclosed in cumulative reporting. Also-known-as "Joyful Embodied"/"Hangzhou Zhijian Power" per assignment; not independently confirmed in English sources.
business_model: Pre-commercial full-stack embodied-AI developer: builds its own humanoid/robot hardware ("self-developed body") and a proprietary robot foundation-model stack (LaST0 world-model+VLA base model, ManualVLA long-horizon task model, TwinRL). Intended monetization is selling robot systems plus the trained foundation models to enterprise and consumer buyers; first commercial deployments planned in controlled settings (factories, supermarkets, logistics) before broader rollout. No published pricing or contract model.
customers: Undisclosed. As of early 2026 the first-generation robot is only in small-batch production with proof-of-concept (PoC) validation underway; no named paying or piloting customers are public. Strategic backers Tencent and Alibaba are investors rather than disclosed deployment customers.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no revenue/ARR figure disclosed; raised ~RMB 2B / ~US$289.3M across 5 rounds in 6 months, valuation >US$1B as of March 2026)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a unified world-model + VLA "fast/slow thinking" base model (LaST0) and a high-efficiency human-hand/human-demonstration data closed loop, plus a founding team drawn from Li Auto's autonomous-driving core (data-engine pedigree). Versus closest Chinese embodied-AI/foundation-model peers Unitree and Galbot (Galaxea/Beijing), Simplexity bets on data-loop efficiency and integrated world-model reasoning rather than hardware breadth or a pure VLA approach; with hardware still in PoC, advantage is largely unproven and rests heavily on team execution and capital.

also_known_as: 松应科技; ORCA; 北京松应科技有限公司
one_liner: Builds ORCA 2.0, a physics-AI simulation platform fusing high-fidelity rendering with high-precision physics to generate synthetic data and close the embodied-AI sim-to-real training loop.
total_funding_usd: ~tens of millions USD (several hundred million RMB across Angel + Pre-A + Pre-A+ within ~10 months; exact total undisclosed)
last_round_lead: Dianshi Capital (点石资本) and E Fund Private Equity (易方达)
notes: Physical-AI simulation company. Beijing 松应科技有限公司 incorporated 2021-11-02 (legal rep 聂凯旋), wholly owned by Shanghai 松应科技; product office in Shanghai Minhang (科技绿洲, Tianlin Rd 889). Core team from Huawei, HiSilicon, Intel, Tencent. ORCA 2.0 fuses high-fidelity rendering + high-precision physics for a full embodied-AI build-collect-develop-train-validate-deploy (sim-to-real) closed loop; supports heterogeneous domestic GPUs, robot sensors, and third-party robot simulators/AI generation. Positioned as a domestic rival to NVIDIA Omniverse. Funding: Angel round led by CAS Star (中科创星) with 上海天使会/接力天使/奇绩创坛 (~Mar 2025); then consecutive Pre-A and Pre-A+ rounds led by Dianshi Capital and E Fund PE with Danbe Capital and Zhongxin Capital co-investing — several hundred million RMB within ~10 months (announced ~Feb 2026). In_scope=true: AI-native sim-to-real/synthetic-data platform purpose-built for robot learning, not AV-only. NOT to be confused with Orca AI (Israeli maritime autonomy startup). Team size and exact per-round amounts undisclosed. Sources: orca3d.cn; pedaily.cn/202602/560645; eastmoney finance; pingwest; baidu baike (北京松应科技).
business_model: Sells three products around its ORCA physics-AI simulation platform: (1) the ORCA Sim system licensed to enterprises on a per-year license fee; (2) 3D high-precision "training grounds"/scene assets priced per-scene by complexity (e.g. a mall or kitchen environment); and (3) robot training synthetic data priced per-task/per-trajectory, marketed at under 1/10 the cost of real-robot data collection. A freemium "ORCA Lab" lightweight free tier targets developers/academics. Buyers are enterprise/institutional (industrial robotics and embodied-AI players).
customers: ~20 commercial customers within ~3 months of commercializing (late 2025): tech-focused central/state-owned enterprises (SOEs), national- and provincial-level robot innovation centers and national humanoid-robot innovation center, embodied-AI/humanoid model vendors, universities and research institutes; plus strategic partnerships with domestic GPU makers (e.g. Moore Threads/Metax 沐曦). Specific named paying orgs not individually disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; commercialized late 2025 with ~20 customers in first 3 months; cumulative funding raised ~several hundred million RMB over past year, but that is financing, not revenue)
technical_differentiation: Positions explicitly as a domestic alternative to NVIDIA Omniverse / Isaac Sim ("China's software-version NVIDIA"), claiming ~1/3 the cost and a distributed heterogeneous compute design (GPU for high-concurrency low-precision physics, CPU modules for high-precision) that fuses high-fidelity rendering with high-precision physics. Runs on domestic GPUs (e.g. Metax C-series at 4096 parallel robots, claimed on par with Omniverse Isaac), unlike Omniverse's CUDA/NVIDIA lock-in; uses a 1:8:1 (demo/synthetic/real) data strategy to cut robot training cost.

one_liner: Human-data platform that, via a robotics product line, runs multimodal physical-capture logistics and temporal action annotation to produce robot-training datasets for model teams.
notes: L3 Data Infrastructure. General human-data/crowdsourcing platform (LLM training, agent testing, knowledge data) originally Yandex.Toloka, founded 2014 by Olga Megorskaya; now a unit of Nasdaq-listed Nebius Group (NBIS), which retains majority economic stake but ceded majority voting rights. Qualifies under the rule that general-purpose labelers count only with a specific robotics data product: Toloka runs a dedicated 'Human data for robotics models' line (toloka.ai/robotics) doing multimodal physical data collection (crowdsourced + controlled facility capture, participant recruitment, site/equipment logistics) and temporal action annotation (frame-level detection, temporal event boundaries, task-success scoring) for robot/VLA training; also contributes to the PhAIL physical-AI leaderboard. in_scope=true for the robotics line; flag: parent is broad LLM/agent/general-purpose data company, not robotics-native. NOT in census per task instruction. $72M round announced May 2025 led by Bezos Expeditions, with Shopify CTO Mikhail Parakhin joining board as Executive Chairman. Total funding to date undisclosed. Team-size estimates vary widely across data providers.
business_model: Sells human-data services to AI/robotics labs: enterprise project contracts where Toloka recruits and manages a crowdsourced/onsite annotator network (200k+ contributors) to deliver collected and annotated training datasets (e.g., LLM/agent RLHF data, and via its robotics line, multimodal physical-capture and temporal action annotation with RoboBILT/RoboBILT-style QA). Revenue is per-project/managed-service deals priced on data volume and labor, not pure self-serve SaaS.
customers: Named clients include Anthropic, Amazon, Microsoft, Shopify, and AI coding startup Poolside; backed by Bezos Expeditions and Shopify CTO Mikhail Parakhin.
revenue_status: scaling (no reliable standalone figure; formerly part of Nebius Group whose Q1 2025 revenue was ~$55.3M total, +385% YoY, not Toloka-specific; a $3B revenue estimate from Growjo is unverified and not credible)
technical_differentiation: Versus Scale AI and Surge AI (and robotics-data peers like Encord/Roboflow), Toloka differentiates on a 200k+ global crowd plus full-time physical-capture logistics (participant recruitment, onsite rigs, equipment, labor-law compliance) and a robotics-specific QA/eval framework (RoboBILT) that scores grasp safety and calibration drift, going beyond pure software-based annotation tooling.

one_liner: IMU-based motion capture suits whose Xsens Humanoid/Link system converts human motion into robot-ready kinematics for teleoperation, simulation and imitation-learning datasets via native ROS2/Isaac Sim/MuJoCo integration.
last_round_type: SPAC (de-SPAC via Pathfinder Acquisition); subsequently taken private 2024
last_round_lead: Movella Holdings NewCo, LP (HGGC / Industry Ventures affiliates)
notes: Xsens is a motion-capture brand/division of Movella (founded 2000 in Enschede by Casper Peeters and Per Slycke; University of Twente origins). Movella was formed by merging mCube, Xsens and Kinduct; went public via SPAC merger with Pathfinder Acquisition Corp (announced Oct 2022, ~$537M pro forma EV; NASDAQ: MVLA in 2023). Voluntarily delisted from Nasdaq April 2024 and taken private into Movella Holdings NewCo, LP; now trades thinly on OTC Pink and is effectively private/active. Reported rebrand back to Xsens in 2026. Total funding figures conflict across sources ($12.1M cited by some; legacy backers include Kleiner Perkins, GIC, Columbia Threadneedle; Francisco Partners provided a $75M senior secured notes facility for the SPAC deal) — treated as undisclosed at the operating-company level. SCOPE: core mocap predates AI (film/sports), but the Nov 12 2025 launch of "Xsens Humanoid" software + redesigned "Xsens Link" suit is an AI-native SKU purpose-built to generate scalable imitation-learning datasets and live teleoperation for humanoid robots, with native ROS2 / NVIDIA Isaac Sim / MuJoCo integration. This demonstration/teleoperation data-generation product places it at L3 Data Infrastructure (hardware as L4 secondary). team_size and funding partially uncertain.
business_model: Sells inertial (IMU) motion-capture hardware-plus-software: the MVN Awinda/Link suits (one-time perpetual or licensed configs roughly $3.5K entry to $35K+, pro multi-performer up to ~$100K) bundled with the MVN software and, since Nov 2025, the Xsens Humanoid software suite (ROS 2 / NVIDIA Isaac Sim / MuJoCo integrations) that converts human motion into robot-ready kinematics. Buyers pay per-suit plus software licenses/subscriptions; also serves film/game animation, sports and biomechanics markets.
customers: Target/announced buyers are humanoid robot manufacturers, industrial humanoid customers, AI data-collection firms, and robotics researchers/developers; legacy mocap customers span film, games, sports and clinical labs. No specific humanoid-robot org is named in public sources for the new Humanoid product - named robotics customers undisclosed.
revenue_status: scaling (~$75M annual revenue, unverified third-party estimate Aug 2025; company went private via May 2025 restructuring under FP Credit Partners and no longer files financials)
technical_differentiation: Wearable inertial (IMU) full-body capture works outdoors, in tight spaces and with no fixed camera rig, unlike optical incumbents Vicon and OptiTrack; vs lower-cost inertial rival Rokoko (Smartsuit Pro II, ~$2.5-3.5K) Xsens differentiates on higher sensor-fusion accuracy/latency and the robotics-specific Xsens Humanoid software with native ROS 2 / Isaac Sim / MuJoCo pipelines for teleoperation and training-data generation.

also_known_as: AIO Intelligence; 艾欧智能（深圳）有限公司; IO-AI
one_liner: Provides teleoperation and human-motion-capture data infrastructure (hardware plus platforms) to collect, annotate and process multimodal demonstration data for training embodied-AI robot manipulation policies.
notes: In-scope: AI-native teleoperation/demonstration data service for training robot manipulation policies (Layer 3 Data Infrastructure). Founded 2023 (May 2023 per recruiting/news sources); HQ Shenzhen, with additional offices listed in Osaka, Singapore, Sydney, San Francisco. Founder Chen Xiangyu (陈相羽), PKU Intelligent Systems, PhD at U-Tokyo JSK Lab (Inaba), DARPA DRC participant. Products: TeleXperience (robot teleoperation), SenseXperience (human mocap/egocentric collection), EmbodiFlow (multimodal annotation/management platform). Claims 500,000+ multimodal demonstration data pieces (visual/kinematic/tactile/audio with NL annotation). Cited clients include Intel, NVIDIA, ByteDance, Tencent. Funding: described as angel-stage with 1-49 employees per Liepin/Boss Zhipin; specific round size/date/lead not findable from a reliable source (one candidate funding article actually referred to a different company, xDAN-AI, so excluded). Secondary layer L4 due to proprietary mocap/teleop hardware. Sources: company site io-ai.tech/en, io-ai.tech/en/about, AWS startup case study (aws.amazon.com/cn/solutions/case-studies/startup-io-ai), Leiphone/Sina interview with founder Chen Xiangyu, Liepin company page 13569211.
business_model: Sells embodied-AI training-data infrastructure to robotics/AI labs and humanoid developers: hardware (SenseXperience inertial motion-capture suits/gloves, teleoperation rigs) plus software platforms (TeleXperience teleop, EmbodiFlow annotation, LeRobot Studio). Monetizes via hardware/system sales and one-stop data-collection-and-annotation service contracts (collect, annotate, process multimodal demonstration data); specific per-unit/per-dataset pricing not publicly disclosed.
customers: Not publicly disclosed by name; positioned as a data/scene provider to global AI and robotics research enterprises and institutions, and cited running Beijing's first humanoid robot training center (data factory). No named paying customers found in public sources.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; angel-round-stage startup, founded May 2023, 1-49 employees)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via multi-sensor inertial motion capture (vs optical mocap), enabling terrain-free in-the-wild multimodal capture (motion, vision, tactile via gloves/insoles, audio), plus a universal teleoperation platform (TeleXperience) supporting 60+ robot morphologies including humanoids and dexterous hands. Vs data-infra peers such as 群核/AgiBot data ecosystems and US-based Scale AI-style providers, IO-AI's edge is integrated capture-hardware-plus-platform and LeRobot/ROS-ecosystem openness rather than pure annotation labor.

one_liner: Builds NEO humanoid robots for home and work, using world-model-based learned policies and large-scale embodied-AI data to perform everyday manipulation tasks autonomously.
last_round_lead: undisclosed (participants incl. NVIDIA, Salesforce, Intel, Qualcomm, Brookfield)
notes: Founded 2014 as Halodi Robotics by Bernt Børnich (Norway); rebranded 1X Technologies. HQ now Palo Alto, CA with manufacturing in Hayward, CA and Moss, Norway. OpenAI Startup Fund-backed. Series B $100M (Jan 2024, led by EQT Ventures). Series C ~$1B reported Sept 2025 at ~$10B valuation; specific lead not confirmed. NEO humanoid (home robot) opened pre-orders Oct 2025. Genuinely applies learned/world-model policies to physical humanoid robots, qualifying for Layer 1 Robot Foundation Models. Team size not reliably findable. Sources: Wikipedia, 1x.tech, Sacra, TechCrunch, Bloomberg, Tech Startups.
business_model: Sells the NEO consumer humanoid robot direct to households for $20,000 outright or a $499/month robot-as-a-service subscription (both bundling required services/upgrades), collected via $200 refundable pre-order deposits; earlier it also deployed the wheeled EVE robot to enterprises for industrial tasks. Subscriptions and a planned paid "Expert Mode" teleoperation/assistance service add recurring revenue.
customers: Primarily individual US consumers via pre-orders (deliveries targeted late 2026, $200 deposits). Earlier enterprise pilots of its EVE wheeled robot were deployed for tasks like security, logistics and door-opening, but specific paying customer organizations are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (pre-order deposits collected; consumer shipments not yet started, targeted late 2026; third-party site getlatka cites ~$222.5M ARR but this is unverified and inconsistent with pre-ship status)
technical_differentiation: Vs Tesla Optimus (factory-internal, simulation-heavy, not for sale) and Figure (enterprise/industrial focus), 1X is the only humanoid actually shipping to consumer homes, with a lightweight ~30kg home-safe design and an explicit human-in-the-loop "Expert Mode" teleoperation strategy that harvests real in-home demonstration data to train its world-model-based learned policies. Differentiation is the home-safety form factor plus consumer-data flywheel rather than a clear algorithmic lead.

also_known_as: AGIBOT, Zhiyuan Robotics, Zhiyuan Robot, AGIBOT Innovation (Shanghai) Technology Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Builds general-purpose humanoid and embodied robots powered by GO-1, a generalist embodied foundation model, for industrial, logistics, and service tasks.
notes: Founded Feb 2023 by ex-Huawei engineers Deng Taihua (CEO) and Peng Zhihui "Zhihui Jun" (CTO). GO-1 generalist embodied foundation model introduced March 2025 (qualifies for L1). Backed by HongShan/Sequoia China, Hillhouse, BYD, Baidu, LG Electronics, JD Digits, Miracle Plus. Total ~$83.8M over 3+ rounds per Tracxn; some sources report 8+ rounds and >$1B unicorn valuation by mid-2025. Latest round Series B (Aug 1 2025 per Tracxn; Crunchbase also lists a Series B dated 2025-03-24); round size/lead not disclosed consistently. Mass-produced 1,000th robot Jan 2025. Total funding figure varies by source.
business_model: Sells general-purpose humanoid and embodied robots (A2 service humanoid ~$100K-$190K, G2 industrial wheeled humanoid, X2 education ~$20K, plus OmniHand dexterous hands) to enterprises for factory automation, logistics, retail and reception, with units running its GO-1 foundation model. Also runs BotShare, a robot-rental/sharing platform (akin to power-bank sharing) targeting 200+ Chinese cities by 2026.
customers: Named deployments include PepsiCo (customized A2 "Fizzbot" brand ambassador serving drinks at WAIC 2025), and automakers BYD and SAIC Motor using A2 robots for factory automation; began overseas customer deliveries in 2025.
revenue_status: scaling (~$140M / 1B+ RMB FY2025 revenue, 5,168 units shipped in 2025 per Omdia making it #1 globally by humanoid shipments; passed 10,000 cumulative units by March 2026)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated vs Unitree by an AI-first stack: GO-1, a generalist embodied foundation model using a novel Vision-Language-Latent-Action (ViLLA) framework trained on its open-sourced AgiBot World dataset (1M+ real-robot trajectories, 217 tasks), enabling zero-shot/cross-embodiment transfer and enterprise service interaction (96% claimed multimodal accuracy). Rival Unitree (e.g. H2 at ~$40,900) competes on price-to-performance and athletic locomotion/payload rather than AI-interaction sophistication; vs US peers like Figure AI, AgiBot's edge is far higher mass-production volume and a public training dataset.

also_known_as: PiPER; Cobot Magic; Tracer base; Songling Robotics
one_liner: Builds low-cost research robot arms and bases plus the Cobot Magic bimanual Mobile-ALOHA platform sold to labs for teleoperation demonstration collection and imitation-learning/VLA training.
last_round_lead: Sequoia Capital China (with 5Y Capital, Vertex Ventures China, Hong Kong X Technology Fund)
notes: Founded 2016 (also reported 2017 on some aggregators); HQ Dongguan, Guangdong, China (operates as Songling Robotics). Series A ~100M RMB (~$15.5M) July 2021, leads incl. Sequoia Capital China, 5Y Capital, Vertex Ventures China, Hong Kong X Technology Fund; total funding ~$15.5M per Crunchbase/CB Insights — no newer round confirmed. Team size not findable. Scope: AGV/Tracer base alone is not AI-native, but the bimanual leader-follower Cobot Magic learning workcell (four PiPER arms + Tracer base, open-source Mobile-ALOHA) is sold explicitly for teleop demo collection and IL/VLA training => in_scope=true. Primarily Layer 4 AI-native hardware with L6 vertical (research/robot-learning) overlap.
business_model: Sells low-cost hardware directly and via global distributors (RobotShop, Generation Robots, RobotLAB, etc.) to research and education buyers: mobile UGV/AGV chassis (Tracer, Scout, Bunker), the PiPER 6-DOF research arm (~under $1k-1.5k class), and the open-source Cobot Magic bimanual Mobile-ALOHA teleoperation/data-collection platform. Revenue is per-unit hardware sales plus accessories, with the open-source software acting as a funnel to drive arm/base purchases by AI labs.
customers: Sells to universities and research labs; company claims 1,000+ enterprises and 50+ top global universities. Named research deployments of its Tracer/mobile platforms include Georgia Tech and University of Tulsa; Cobot Magic/PiPER and chassis are widely used by embodied-AI and robot-learning labs building Mobile-ALOHA-style data collection setups.
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified; total funding ~$15.5M per PitchBook/Crunchbase, founded 2016, Dongguan CN; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on price and breadth versus Trossen Robotics (the closest competitor supplying ALOHA-class arms to Stanford/DeepMind/Berkeley): AgileX bundles its own mobile UGV/AGV bases (Tracer) with low-cost PiPER arms into a single Mobile-ALOHA stack with open-source software, whereas Trossen focuses on standalone X-Series/ViperX arms. Hardware is largely commodity (low-cost integrated joint motors, off-the-shelf depth cameras), so the edge is China-based cost/manufacturing scale and an integrated arm-plus-mobile-base catalog rather than proprietary IP.

also_known_as: LingBot; Robbyant; Ant Robotics; Shanghai Ant Lingbo Technology Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Ant Group's embodied-intelligence subsidiary building general-purpose service/humanoid robots; open-sourced LingBot-VLA, a vision-language-action foundation model giving robots a cross-embodiment, cross-task "universal brain."
notes: Wholly-owned embodied-AI subsidiary of Ant Group (Hangzhou-based fintech, Alibaba affiliate). Shanghai Ant Lingbo Technology Co. registered 2024-12-17 with RMB 100M (~USD 14M) capital, 100% held by Ant Intelligent (Hangzhou) Tech; CEO Zhu Xing (朱兴). Public English brand "Robbyant." First humanoid Robbyant-R1 unveiled Sep 2025. LingBot-VLA open-sourced late Jan 2026: Qwen2.5-VL-based VLA trained on ~20,000 hrs real dual-arm teleoperation across 9 robot embodiments; ~17.3% avg success on GM-100 benchmark, ~80 demos for task migration. No external funding round disclosed; capitalized by parent. Sources: SCMP, Pandaily, MarkTechPost, BusinessWire, stcn.com, qbitai.com.
business_model: Sells the R1 wheeled two-armed humanoid robot bundled into vertical "scenario solutions" (catering/chef, tour-guiding, healthcare med-sorting) rather than as standalone hardware, backed by parent Ant Group capital; the LingBot-VLA, LingBot-Depth foundation models are open-sourced (no direct revenue) to build an ecosystem and extend Ant's digital services into physical environments.
customers: Shanghai History Museum (R1 deployed for tour-guiding); pilots/tests in restaurants, community centers, and healthcare institutions (medicine sorting, voice consultations). Robotics manufacturers Galaxea Dynamics and AgileX Robotics have adapted its open-source LingBot-VLA model (adopters, not necessarily paying).
revenue_status: early-revenue (R1 in mass production and shipping to clients as of late 2025; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Vs. Unitree (closest CN humanoid-hardware rival): competes on AI/software via a cross-embodiment VLA "universal brain" rather than locomotion hardware. Vs. Physical Intelligence's Pi0.5 (closest VLA rival): LingBot-VLA, trained on 20,000+ hours across nine dual-arm configs, reports 15.7% vs Pi0.5's 13.0% cross-body success on the GM-100 benchmark and 1.5-2.8x faster training than StarVLA/OpenPI, plus deep Ant Group capital backing.

one_liner: Builds the ANYmal quadruped robot for autonomous industrial inspection in hazardous sites, using deep-RL learned perceptive locomotion and sim-to-real to handle rough terrain.
notes: ETH Zurich spin-off (Robotic Systems Lab, Wyss Zurich project), founded 2016; HQ Zurich with a San Francisco office. Funding: total ~$150M. Dec 2024 raised $60M (often described Series B2/C, led by Qualcomm Ventures and Supernova Invest), bringing total to ~$130M; in Sep 2025 ~$20M additional from Climate Investment pushed total to ~$150M (~EUR 127M cumulative per EU-Startups/BeBeez). Team ~200-250 (251 reported Mar 2026 per Tracxn/PitchBook). In_scope=true: legged locomotion over unstructured industrial terrain depends on learned RL policies and sim-to-real — AI-native category, not a pre-programmed industrial arm. Primary layer L4 (AI-native legged hardware/platform) with L6 industrial-inspection vertical secondary. Component/platform maker, not training its own VLA foundation model.
business_model: Sells/leases the ANYmal quadruped inspection robot to heavy-industry operators (oil & gas, power, mining, chemicals), offered both as outright purchase with a lifetime software license and as Robotics-as-a-Service (subscription/pay-as-you-go covering hardware, software, and service); adds recurring revenue via the Data Navigator inspection-analytics platform. The ANYmal X variant is marketed as the first Ex-proof (explosion-protected) legged robot for hazardous sites.
customers: Named deployments/orders include PETRONAS, Shell, BP, Equinor, Petrobras, SLB, Siemens Energy, BASF, Vale, Outokumpu, Novelis, and GE Vernova; partners include AWS, NVIDIA, SAP, and Yokogawa. Reported 500+ units reserved and 200+ shipped (2024-2025).
revenue_status: scaling (third-party getLatka estimate ~$176.5M revenue 2025, unverified; ANYmal X pre-orders/reservations reported over $150M; ~$150M total funding raised incl. 2025 round of over EUR 127M / additional $60M for U.S. expansion)
technical_differentiation: vs Boston Dynamics Spot and Ghost Robotics Vision 60, ANYmal differentiates on deep-RL learned perceptive locomotion (exteroceptive terrain anticipation enabling native omnidirectional stair/rough-terrain traversal without a dedicated stair mode, per Science Robotics work) and on the Ex-proof ANYmal X certified for explosive (ATEX/IECEx) hazardous zones that Spot's standard inspection platform does not target.

one_liner: Builds Apollo, a general-purpose humanoid robot with a VLA/foundation-model autonomy stack (with Google DeepMind) to automate physical labor in manufacturing, logistics, and warehouses.
last_round_lead: B Capital (existing backer; extension included Google, Mercedes-Benz, Peak6, AT&T Ventures, John Deere)
notes: HQ Austin, TX, founded 2016. Apollo humanoid robot; foundation-model/VLA autonomy in collaboration with Google DeepMind. Funding history: $350M Series A (Feb 2025, co-led B Capital & Capital Factory, Google participating), expanded to $415M (Mar 2025, +Mercedes-Benz, Japan Post Capital, ARK Invest), then $520M Series A extension (Feb 2026) at ~$5B valuation bringing total Series A to ~$935M and lifetime funding near $1B. Customers include Mercedes-Benz and GXO. Total funding figures vary by source (CB Insights ~$945M, PitchBook ~$976M). Primary layer L1 (robot foundation models) is appropriate given the VLA autonomy stack; also significant hardware (L4) and vertical-deployment (L6) work.
business_model: Sells/leases the Apollo general-purpose humanoid robot to manufacturers and logistics operators for physical labor automation, moving toward commercial fleet deployments (and likely Robot-as-a-Service pricing) at a targeted sub-$50K unit price; targeting commercial scale-up by 2027. Currently monetized through paid pilots and multi-phase deployment agreements with industrial OEMs.
customers: Named: Mercedes-Benz (automotive assembly pilots across European facilities, plus a low-double-digit-million-euro strategic investment), GXO Logistics (multi-phase warehouse R&D/deployment in the US), and Jabil (contract manufacturing partner and end-user in its own factories).
revenue_status: early-revenue (specific figure not publicly disclosed; revenue from paid pilots/deployments with Mercedes-Benz, GXO, Jabil; raised ~$935M total at a $5B valuation as of Feb 2026)
technical_differentiation: vs Figure AI and Tesla Optimus: Apptronik leverages a Google DeepMind Gemini Robotics VLA/foundation-model autonomy partnership (Figure now uses in-house Helix; Tesla uses in-house FSD-derived stack) plus NASA Valkyrie/UT-Austin heritage, prioritizing higher payload (25kg vs Optimus ~20kg) and reliability over Tesla's aggressive sub-$30K cost target. Edge is real industrial deployment (Mercedes) plus external frontier-model access rather than proprietary AI.

also_known_as: HASEL artificial muscle (University of Colorado Boulder spinout)
one_liner: Commercializes HASEL electrohydraulic artificial-muscle actuators with integrated high-voltage electronics, providing soft, compliant, force-controlled motion for robotics, haptics and automation.
notes: University of Colorado Boulder spinout (2018) commercializing HASEL (Hydraulically Amplified Self-healing ELectrostatic) electrohydraulic artificial muscles — soft, variable-stiffness, force-controllable actuators plus required HV electronics. Sold to robot/OEM integrators as a critical actuator subsystem (L4 AI-Native Hardware); not a foundation-model builder. Compliant/variable-stiffness actuation is a key enabler for AI-policy/force-controlled manipulation, so in_scope=true per the compliant-actuator angle. Funding is predominantly grant-based (DOE, NSF, CO Advanced Industries, ARIA); Feb 2025 ARIA award (£1.5M, ~$1.9M) for dexterous robotic hardware is the most recent disclosed round. Total ~$4.5M. Round size in USD is approximate from GBP conversion. Team size ~8 (website team page) to ~10 (third-party trackers). source_url is company site; corroborating: https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/artimus-robotics-wins-uk-aria-award-to-accelerate-the-development-of-dexterous-robotic-hardware-302381120.html and https://www.colorado.edu/venturepartners/startup-portfolio/artimus-robotics-inc
business_model: Sells HASEL electrohydraulic artificial-muscle actuators and custom high-voltage driver electronics as products and dev kits for research and OEM use. Also funded by government grants and contracts from DOE NSF Office of Naval Research and UK ARIA.
customers: Mainly grant and contract funders plus evaluation partners. Named ones include US Office of Naval Research DOD Navy UK ARIA agency DOE and NSF. No commercial product customers publicly named.
revenue_status: early-revenue unverified about 4.45M USD grant and award funding plus small product sales no disclosed commercial deployments around 7 employees
technical_differentiation: HASEL electrohydraulic actuators give silent soft force-controllable motion driven by electrostatics not air unlike Festo pneumatic bionic actuators and offer higher power-to-weight with a self-healing dielectric versus conventional dielectric-elastomer actuators. Edge is bundling the high-voltage driver electronics needed to use them.

also_known_as: 方舟无限; ARX; ARX-X; Fangzhou Unlimited; ARX5/X5/R5/L5
one_liner: Makes low-cost lightweight 6-DoF force-control robot arms (ARX5/R5/X5) adopted by AI labs as bimanual teleoperation and data-collection platforms for imitation learning and VLA training.
notes: Chinese company 方舟无限（北京）科技有限公司, founded 2023-06-27 in Beijing; distinct from German military 'ARX Robotics' (UGVs). Founder Zhang Xinliang (张鑫亮), Harbin Univ. of Science and Technology; RoboMaster background. Claims to be first to mass-produce lightweight force-control arms; reported zero external funding (self-funded/bootstrapped) as of 2024 sources. Products: ARX5/X5 (~60k RMB), L5/L5 Pro (~29.8k RMB), R5, Ultron bimanual robot, mobile bases. Heavily adopted by robot-learning community: Stanford real-stanford/arx5-sdk C++/Python controller, BRMData bimanual dataset, used as imitation-learning/VLA data-collection and teleoperation rigs. In_scope=true: although a robot-arm is a pre-existing category, ARX positions and sells these specifically as AI-native embodied-intelligence / VLA data-collection and learned-policy research platforms, and adoption is overwhelmingly by robot-learning labs. Team size and any funding details not publicly disclosed; multiple 2024 Chinese sources report zero funding.
business_model: Sells low-cost ultra-lightweight 6-DoF force-control robot arms (X5/R5/L5, roughly RMB 30k-60k per unit) and bimanual ALOHA-style teleoperation/data-collection kits directly to embodied-AI research labs, universities, and robotics companies as hardware purchases. Note: this Beijing-based "方舟无限/ARX" is distinct from the German military-robotics firm ARX Robotics GmbH.
customers: Largely undisclosed individual buyers, but adoption is concretely evidenced by Stanford's robotics group (real-stanford), which built and open-sourced the arx5-sdk controller and uses the ARX5 arm in the UMI-on-Legs project (paired with a Unitree Go2). Broader customer base is academic/embodied-AI labs and integrators buying arms for imitation-learning data collection; specific named paying enterprise accounts are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (Chinese-language press reports annual revenue/ARR in the tens of millions RMB, "千万级 ARR"; company reported as bootstrapped with zero external funding as of 2024, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Versus Stanford's open-source ALOHA reference design (which relies on Trossen Robotics ViperX 300 / WidowX 250 servo arms, ~750g payload) and Trossen itself, ARX delivers true full-joint force/torque control with higher payload (1.5-3kg) in a 3.3-3.4kg arm at a far lower price point (~RMB 30k for an ALOHA-equivalent L5 bimanual setup), making it a cheaper, force-controlled drop-in for teleoperation/data collection. Trade-off: planetary-gear actuators give lower absolute precision than harmonic-drive arms, acceptable for mobile/data-collection use.

one_liner: Builds cable-driven AI robots (S1) with imitation-learning manipulation policies and foundation models (Lumo-1, DuoCore) to perform complex multi-task household and retail manipulation.
last_round_lead: undisclosed (backers incl. Bohua Capital fund, Thundersoft, Ant Group, Guoke Investment)
team_size: unknown (core founding team of 6 ex-Tencent RoboticsX)
notes: Founded Dec 2022 in Shenzhen by Lai Jie (first employee at Tencent RoboticsX). Flagship product Astribot S1, a cable/wire-driven humanoid robot that went viral April 2024 doing household chores at high speed. Assigned Layer 1 (Robot Foundation Models): launched Lumo-1 reasoning foundation model and DuoCore fast-slow system model, deployed in real retail in China; imitation-learning manipulation policies for S1. Funding history: pre-A/angel (Jul 2024), Series A (Apr 2025), Series B over 1B yuan closed ~June 2026 valuing the company over 10B yuan (~$1.4B unicorn). Exact total funding and dollar amounts undisclosed; figures are RMB-reported. Team size not publicly disclosed.
business_model: Sells cable-driven AI robots via direct hardware sales (flagship S1 at ~$96K-$150K and lower-cost T1 at ~$13K) for industrial material handling, commercial/retail service ("Robot MART" deployments), and research teleoperation; revenue also includes licensing and maintenance services on bulk/multi-year deployment orders.
customers: Xian Gong Intelligence (thousand-unit AI robot deployment order, 2025) and Jin Ma Amusement (joint "Robot MART" launch Dec 2025, batch delivery of ~1,000 units to Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou). Additional industrial material-handling and scientific-research/teleoperation pilots; specific named buyers otherwise largely undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (limited commercial availability began late 2025 with thousand-unit-level orders; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Claims to be the first/only mass producer of cable-driven (tendon-driven) humanoid arms, prioritizing manipulation speed (>=10 m/s end-effector), +/-0.1mm repeatability and human-like compliance/safety plus its proprietary Lumo foundation model and embodied OS. Versus Unitree and AgiBot, which compete on low price and shipment volume with conventional rigid actuators, Astribot positions S1 as a premium dexterity-optimized platform.

one_liner: Builds AI-driven vision-guided robotic arms that grasp, move, and stack irregular passenger luggage in airports, automating costly, error-prone manual baggage handling between belts, carts, and containers.
notes: YC S24 batch. Founded by David Millard (PhD CS USC, ex-Google X Everyday Robots, soft-body manipulation) and John B. Stroud (MBA Kellogg, ex-United Airlines ground ops); met at University of Georgia. HQ Berkeley, CA. Total funding ~$4.36M per Crunchbase (includes YC + pre-seed). Most recent: $3.5M seed announced 2025-04-29, led by Zero Infinity Partners, with Vantage Futures (Vantage Group), Y Combinator, Soma Capital, angels. ARR reported ~$330K (getLatka, unverified). Robots learn/improve with every bag transferred. Layer 1 (robot foundation models / learned manipulation policies) applies but business is a strong vertical (L6) hardware (L4) play.
business_model: Sells (and intends to lease/RaaS-deploy) its REACH autonomous robotic-arm baggage-handling systems to airports and airlines worldwide, which currently outsource or staff this manual labor; revenue is hardware/system sales targeting the $20B+/year baggage-handling spend. Specific contract structure (outright sale vs. recurring service) is not publicly disclosed.
customers: No paying airport/airline customers publicly named; system is at pilot stage (TRL 6, controlled pilots). Strategic backing from Vantage Group (airport/transport infrastructure operator) via Vantage Futures signals an intended deployment channel, but specific airport pilots are undisclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (raised ~$3.5-3.86M seed in April 2025 led by Zero Infinity Partners with Vantage Futures, Y Combinator, SOMA Capital; system in pilots, no product revenue disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates with a mobile wheeled six-axis arm (REACH) using a dual-mode gripper (suction for soft bags, mechanical grip for rigid/irregular loads) plus AI vision to handle bags of any shape on ramps and in bag rooms, rather than fixed conveyor automation from incumbent baggage-handling-system integrators (Vanderlande, BEUMER/Crisplant). Versus general AI-grasping robotics players, its edge is airport-domain specialization; differentiation is execution-stage and unproven at scale (unverified).

one_liner: Builds Alex, a 19-DoF humanoid upper torso with in-house high-speed joints for manipulation in manufacturing, logistics, and maintenance, partnering for high-level AI.
notes: HQ Pensacola, FL (US); founded 2017 as a commercial robot-building partner to IHMC (Institute for Human and Machine Cognition), having built IHMC's Nadia research humanoid. Launched Alex, a general-purpose humanoid upper torso (19 DoF, 10kg payload, high-speed joints up to 9 rad/s, in-house actuators), mountable on pedestal or mobile base; legs planned later. CTO Brandon Shrewsbury. Acquired by Foundation Robotics Labs (Dec 1, 2024), which inherited military contracts including a ~$1.8M USAF SBIR award. No disclosed venture funding. In scope: general-purpose manipulation hardware intended for learned policies (AI-native humanoid category). L4 primary; L6 secondary given target verticals (warehouse/manufacturing/maintenance). Funding/team size not found from primary sources.
business_model: Before its acquisition, Boardwalk planned to deliver Alex via a robots-as-a-service model (recurring fees for deployed humanoid torsos), starting with paid pilot programs in aviation, logistics, and food processing; it also sold the platform outright to research customers. Its earlier line of business was building robots commercially for IHMC (e.g., the Nadia humanoid).
customers: No named paying customers publicly disclosed. Boardwalk ran early pilots with unnamed "select" partners, with its first pilot in aviation and additional pilots targeted in logistics and food processing; it also supplied/co-built robots with IHMC (Institute for Human and Machine Cognition). Post-acquisition, Foundation Robotics piloted the renamed Phantom robot in undisclosed industrial use cases.
revenue_status: early-revenue (pilot programs plus research-unit sales; revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by a deliberately legless 19-DoF upper-torso design with custom in-house high-speed actuators (joints up to ~9 rad/s, 10 kg payload), prioritizing fast manipulation over costly bipedal locomotion. Versus full-body humanoids like Figure (Figure 02) and Apptronik (Apollo), Alex traded mobility for lower cost and faster manipulation on a fixed pedestal or AMR base, leveraging IHMC's dynamic-control research from Nadia.

company_name: Booster Robotics (Accelerated Evolution / 加速进化)
also_known_as: Jiasu Jinhua; Beijing Accelerated Evolution Technology Co., Ltd.; Booster
one_liner: Develops affordable full-stack humanoid robots (T1/K1) with in-house embodied-AI motion-control policies, targeting developers, research/RoboCup competition, and education markets.
last_round_lead: Beijing Robot Industry Development Fund / Beijing AI Industry Investment Fund
notes: Beijing Accelerated Evolution Technology Co., Ltd. founded 2023-06-20. Founder/CEO Cheng Hao (程昊), Tsinghua BS/MS, ex-ByteDance exec, former Tsinghua robot soccer team. Funding rounds: Angel (Oct 2023, tens of millions RMB), Pre-A (Apr 2024, Source Code Capital + Innoangel), Pre-A second tranche (~100M RMB, Aug 2024), Series A (Jun 2025, led by Shenzhen Capital Group), Series A+ (Jul 2025, >100M RMB, Beijing state funds). Cumulative "A-series" reported near 500M RMB; Tracxn lists ~$27.9M disclosed total. Products: T1 and K1 humanoids; mass production Oct 2024, first 100 units delivered Mar 2025; RoboCup 2025 AdultSize gold (Fire God team). Layer 1 fit: develops embodied-AI policies/models for humanoid control; also strong hardware (L4) and competition/education vertical (L6). Total/round USD figures approximate from RMB conversions.
business_model: Sells full-stack humanoid robots as developer/research hardware platforms: the T1 (~$33,949 / from ~9,000 EUR) and the entry-level K1 (~$4,999 base, ~$12,500 configured), with an open-source software ecosystem, targeting universities, research labs, and RoboCup teams. Revenue is primarily upfront unit sales of robots plus accessories/support, not enterprise SaaS or service contracts.
customers: Over 200 clients and 70+ universities/research institutions across 20+ countries (700+ units shipped since 2023). Named users include Tsinghua University (Hephaestus / TH-MOS teams), China Agricultural University, and Germany's HTWK Leipzig team via RoboCup; sold internationally through resellers (Generation Robots, K-Robotic).
revenue_status: early-revenue (700+ units shipped to 200+ clients since 2023; exact revenue figure not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Competes vs Unitree (H1/G1) and Fourier (GR-series) by focusing on low-cost, developer-friendly, RoboCup-validated bipeds with in-house embodied-AI motion-control; the K1 undercuts most rivals at ~$5,000 and the lineup is purpose-built for research/competition rather than industrial deployment. Differentiation is largely price-accessibility and a championship-proven open dev platform rather than unique hardware IP.

one_liner: Builds compact 6-axis force-torque and tactile sensors plus software so robots can feel, learn, and safely interact with the physical world, enabling teleoperation and learned manipulation.
notes: ETH Zurich spin-off founded 2020 (some databases list legacy 2013/2015 prototype origin; company site and The Robot Report confirm 2020). Founders: Klajd Lika, Ilias Patsiaouras, Marco Hutter. HQ Zurich (Hagenholzstrasse 85, 8050); additional Customer Experience Center in Fremont, CA and Athens subsidiary. Total funding ~$2.66M over 2 rounds: $164K seed (Oct 2020, Venture Kick) and $2.5M seed (Oct 2023, led by Marathon Venture Capital with angels). Other backers: StraightWalk, ESA BIC Switzerland, Kickstart Accelerator. BORDERLINE for scope: force/tactile sensing predates AI and the product category (F/T sensors) exists without AI, so this is a Layer 4 component supplier rather than an AI-native robot. However, it is explicitly positioned/marketed for Physical AI, haptic teleoperation, kinesthetic teaching and learned manipulation, supplying the tactile data modality those policies depend on. Kept in_scope=true per 'when in doubt' rule, with ai_intensity flagged as enablement-only (no learned policy in-house). Team size not disclosed publicly. Sources: botasys.com/about, The Robot Report, EU-Startups, Crunchbase/Tracxn/PitchBook.
business_model: Designs and manufactures compact 6-axis force-torque and tactile sensors (e.g. SensONE, PixONE, MiniONE) plus calibration/ROS2 software, selling them as plug-and-play hardware to robotics OEMs, integrators, and research labs directly and through 10+ distributors; revenue is per-unit sensor sales with associated software/support, not subscription.
customers: Serves 100+ customers across 30+ countries via distributors and robotic integrators; named partner is cobot maker Kinova (joint force-torque sensor offering). Most end customers are undisclosed, spanning manufacturing/automation, medical/surgical robotics, agriculture, and research.
revenue_status: early-revenue (raised $2.66M total, incl. $2.5M Seed Oct 2023 led by Marathon Venture Capital; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Versus incumbents ATI Industrial Automation and OnRobot, Bota (ETH Zurich RSL spin-off) differentiates on ultra-compact, lightweight integrated designs - e.g. the PixONE through-hole sensor ~30% lighter than market alternatives and SensONE T5 with 3-5x finer sensitivity (0.05 N / 0.002 Nm) - plus native EtherCAT/Ethernet/USB/ROS2 plug-and-play connectivity, targeting small-payload cobots and humanoids where ATI's larger industrial sensors are less suited.

one_liner: Built lovable, emotionally expressive humanoid robots (Yogi) for the home, using a proprietary Motion Language Model to generate natural, personalized movements from text or voice commands.
