last_round_lead: New Wave (undisclosed lead); investors include Battle Born Venture, Humanoid Global Holdings, ALM Ventures, Reno Seed Advisors
notes: Founder/CEO Scott LaValley (ex-Boston Dynamics, ex-Disney Imagineer). Focused on social/companion humanoids for the home rather than industrial labor; prototypes Yogi (child-proportioned companion) and Speedy (commercial character platform). Developed a proprietary Motion Language Model (MLM) generating natural, diverse, personalized real-time behaviors — basis for Layer 1 Robot Foundation Models classification. Company SHUT DOWN in February 2026 after failing to raise a Series A bridge; only ~$3M raised. Founding year 2021 reported across multiple sources (4-year journey ending early 2026).
business_model: Pre-shutdown, made money in two ways: (1) robotics engineering/design services for corporate clients, which bootstrapped R&D, and (2) selling/placing variants of its "Speedy" bipedal character robot to museums, science centers, and entertainment/costume customers; the eventual intended model was selling or renting the "Yogi" lovable home humanoid to consumers (rental akin to a housekeeping/gardening service).
customers: No major named consumer customers (Yogi never shipped). Near-term buyers were undisclosed museums and science centers, plus an unnamed customer receiving a costumed Speedy variant. Backers/partners included Humanoid Global Holdings and New Wave Holdings, but these were investors, not product customers.
revenue_status: early-revenue (modest service and character-robot revenue; raised only ~$3M total outside capital and never reached consumer-product sales before shutting down/bankruptcy in March 2026)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by targeting emotionally expressive, "lovable" small toddler-proportioned home companion humanoids driven by a proprietary Motion Language Model for natural personalized movement, versus 1X (Neo) and Figure AI, which build adult-sized utility/labor humanoids focused on physical task completion rather than social/emotional companionship.

one_liner: Builds biomimetic humanoid robots using water-actuated Myofiber artificial muscles on a synthetic skeleton, aiming for human-like dexterity and a full android body.
notes: AI-native humanoid hardware (Layer 4). Novel actuator: Myofiber water-actuated artificial muscles (pioneered 2021), ~1000 muscles, 200+ DOF, sweating/water cooling system. Products: Protoclone (V1 full-body android), Clone Alpha (planned consumer humanoid ~$20k), plus biomimetic robotic hand. Founders Dhanush Radhakrishnan and Lukasz Koziej; began muscle R&D as early as 2014. HQ Warsaw, Poland with US/New York ties; sources list both. Founded Nov 2021. Funding: Crunchbase reports ~$7.1M total; PitchBook reported a $6.5M seed; round details (date/lead) not clearly disclosed. Backed by Trevor Blackwell (YC co-founder), plus investors cited as Initialized Capital, Pioneer Fund, Access VC, Tango VC, Wikus Ventures; also raising via Republic crowdfunding. In scope: AI-native humanoid body category that exists to be driven by learned policies, not a conventional pre-programmed industrial machine. Company does not build its own foundation model (Layer 4 hardware focus). Team size not findable.
business_model: Sells biomimetic humanoid androids and components (the "Hand of Clone," torso, and full-body Clone Alpha) directly via preorder/reservation, starting with a limited "Alpha Edition" run of 279 units pitched like a limited-edition supercar, then targeting a roughly $20,000 mass-market consumer "robo-butler" price point. Funds operations largely through venture capital (~$7.1M raised plus an announced $50M round) and crowdfunding (Republic) ahead of mass production.
customers: Largely pre-launch reservation holders rather than paying enterprise customers: ~16 AI labs reportedly preordered early "Hand of Clone" units and 100+ customers placed torso-prototype orders (academic research and light industrial use); 279 Clone Alpha Alpha-Edition reservations are open. No named paying commercial deployments publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no disclosed product revenue; deposits/preorders only; ~$7.1M raised plus announced $50M round, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Unlike Tesla Optimus and Figure, which scale rigid electromechanical actuators (electric motors), Clone is effectively the only humanoid maker using proprietary water-actuated "Myofiber" artificial muscles on a 206-bone anatomically accurate polymer skeleton, claiming faster-than-human contraction (<50ms) and more fluid, compliant, human-like motion with high hand DOF. Differentiation is the biomimetic hydraulic muscle architecture itself, though it remains unproven at scale versus the better-capitalized motor-based incumbents.

one_liner: Biomimetic tactile sensors giving robot grippers human-hand-like sensing—measuring 3D force, torque, slip and friction—to enable dexterous, adaptive grasping for AI-driven manipulation.
notes: UNSW Sydney spin-out established mid-2019. Co-founders: Dr. Heba Khamis (CEO), Benjamin Xia (CTO), Assoc. Prof. Stephen Redmond — ~8 years of tactile sensor research at UNSW. Tech measures 3D deflection/force/vibration per array element plus torque, incipient slip and friction. Seed round (US$2.5M, May 2022) led by True Ventures with Flying Fox Ventures, Radar Ventures, UNSW Founders. Earlier research funded by Australian Research Council and US Office of Naval Research Global. In_scope=true: tactile sensing for AI-driven variable-item dexterous manipulation is foundational to robot-learning grasping; the friction/slip-prediction capability is AI-native enabling and would not be used in conventional pre-programmed arms. Note assigned subcategory is 6h_other though primary layer is L4 hardware. Funding figures from Crunchbase/Tracxn; PRNewswire confirms US$2.5M seed and True Ventures lead.
business_model: Sells biomimetic tactile sensor hardware to robotics developers and integrators — its PapillArray optical 3D force/torque tactile sensors, standalone force/torque sensors, and two-finger smart grippers — pitched for logistics, agriculture, medical and space robotics, plus paid pilot deployments to demonstrate tactile sensing value. Revenue is hardware unit/dev-kit sales and pilot engagements (no public per-unit pricing).
customers: Not publicly disclosed by name. The May 2022 seed round was raised to fund "a number of pilots" to demonstrate tactile sensing, but specific paying or piloting organizations are not named in public sources. Spun out of UNSW Sydney (an institutional backer via UNSW Founders).
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified; selling sensors/grippers and running pilots, but no revenue figures disclosed publicly)
technical_differentiation: Unlike the dominant vision/camera-based optical tactile sensors GelSight (GelSight Mini, MIT spin-out) and Meta's DIGIT — which infer geometry from elastomer deformation imaged by an internal camera — Contactile's PapillArray uses a patented camera-free optical method across an array of silicone pillars to directly measure 3D force, torque, incipient slip and friction at each element, optimized for real-time grip-control rather than high-res image reconstruction.

one_liner: Builds vision-based tactile sensors and VTLA foundation models giving robot hands a high-resolution sense of touch for dexterous manipulation.
last_round_lead: China Mobile Industrial Chain Fund (Lianchang Fund)
notes: Founded 2023, incubated by HKUST team; co-founders Prof. Wang Yu (dean of HKUST robotics institute) and Dr. Duan Jianghua. HQ Shenzhen with R&D in Hong Kong. Core products: DM-Tac W tactile sensor (40,000 sensing units/cm2), DM-Hand1 dexterous hand, DM-EXton wearable data-collection device, and VTLA (vision-tactile-language-action) manipulation models. Aug 2025 Angel++ round >RMB100M (~USD14M) led by China Merchants Venture with Orient Renaissance Capital and Bridge Capital. Dec 2025 4th/strategic round led by China Mobile Industrial Chain Fund (Lianchang Fund); amount undisclosed (described as tens of millions USD), second round in three months. Prior investors include Lenovo Capital, Jinding Capital, Incubator Group. Genuinely applies learning to physical robot hands (tactile manipulation foundation models) - qualifies as L1 Robot Foundation Models. Some sources list a 2021 founding date but official press release and primary reporting state 2023. Team size not publicly disclosed. Sources: globenewswire, evertiq, SCMP/Yahoo (China Mobile round), Pandaily.
business_model: Sells vision-based tactile sensor hardware (DM-Tac W), tactile dexterous robotic hands (DM-Hand1), and wearable data-collection devices (DM-EXton) to robotics developers, plus a VTLA (Vision-Tactile-Language-Action) data/model stack ("Devices, Data, Deployment"); revenue comes from sensor/hand product sales to humanoid makers, manufacturers, and research labs, with early traction in industrial inspection, logistics, and intelligent manufacturing.
customers: Partners/users include Google DeepMind, Northwestern University, National University of Singapore, and China Mobile, plus unnamed "leading humanoid robotics companies"; products reportedly secured overseas export orders. Specific paying enterprise customers beyond these are largely undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (product line launched April 2025 with reported overseas orders; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Uses a proprietary monochromatic (single-wavelength) light vision-tactile approach claiming ~40,000-110,000 sensing units per fingertip-sized module at high frequency, far denser than GelSight's RGB photometric-stereo sensors and Meta AI's lower-resolution DIGIT; also pairs sensors with a VTLA foundation model and 10,000-hour open dataset, going beyond pure-hardware tactile rivals.

one_liner: Builds ultra-redundant soft robotic hands using 100+ muscle-array actuators with compliance, redundancy and proprioception, enabling high-dexterity learned manipulation that reroutes around motor failures.
notes: Philadelphia-based startup founded 2023 by Tom (Jiahao) Zhang (Cornell ME/CS; Penn Engineering). Unveiled Muscle v0 (July 2025): soft robotic hand with ~108 cheap ($5) actuators (~20 per finger), ~3x a human hand's muscles, marketed as "infinite DOF." AI-driven control dynamically explores actuator combinations and reroutes through remaining muscles if up to ~20% of motors fail. L4 hardware/component maker, not its own foundation model; designed for advanced learned manipulation, so in_scope=true. Total raised ~$1.35M; investors include Unshackled Ventures, ZhenFund, UpScaleX, Innova Global, UC Berkeley (6th undisclosed). Specific round type/size/date/lead not publicly confirmed (listed seed inferred). Note overlap with "Daxo Industries" name (same founder, earlier agtech framing). Early-stage / lower-confidence on financials.
business_model: Intends to sell/deploy its ultra-redundant soft robotic hand (Muscle v0, with V2 launching Q3 2026) as a dexterous manipulation platform targeting the "long tail of automation" - high-mix, high-dexterity tasks (~$1,200 hardware build cost using cheap $5 motors). Go-to-market specifics (hardware sale vs. robotics-as-a-service vs. licensing to humanoid makers) are not publicly disclosed; appears pre-commercial.
customers: Undisclosed - no named paying customers or pilots are public. The company is exhibiting at the Automate show and is in early commercialization ahead of its V2 hand launch.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (seed-stage; $1.35M raised from Unshackled Ventures, ZhenFund, UpScaleX, Innova Global, UC Berkeley; first commercial V2 hand slated for Q3 2026)
technical_differentiation: Differs from precision-rigid dexterous hands like Shadow Robot's Dexterous Hand and Sanctuary AI's hands by embracing extreme actuator redundancy: 108 cheap actuators (20 per finger) forming "muscle arrays" with infinite-DOF compliance and graceful degradation - the AI reroutes motion through remaining muscles even if up to 20% fail, trading precision-engineered components for low-cost, fault-tolerant complexity.

also_known_as: Zhejiang/Hangzhou Deep Robotics; Jueying (绝影); 云深处科技 (Yunshenchu); DeepRobotics
one_liner: Builds AI-native legged robots (Jueying X20/X30 quadrupeds, Lynx wheel-legged) whose locomotion runs on sim-to-real reinforcement-learning policies trained in NVIDIA Isaac Lab instead of hand-coded gaits.
last_round_lead: CMB International (with China Asset Management, China Telecom, China Unicom funds)
notes: Founded 2017 in Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China by Qiuguo Zhu (legal entity Hangzhou Yunshen Technology / 云深处科技). Major quadruped 'robot dog' maker; flagship Jueying X20/X30 industrial/inspection quadrupeds and Lynx M20 wheel-legged robot; also unveiled DR01 humanoid in 2024. Locomotion driven by sim-to-real RL policies trained in NVIDIA Isaac Lab; markets 'experience-based / Physical AI' learned locomotion rather than scripted gaits. Total funding ~US$140M across ~4 rounds; latest Series C ~RMB500M (~US$70M) Dec 2025 led by CMB International with China Asset Management, China Telecom and China Unicom funds; earlier Series B Oct 2022 (Cybernaut, F&G Venture, West Lake fund) plus a Series B+. Other backers include JD.com, China Fortune-Tech Capital. In scope: AI-native legged hardware whose core locomotion would not exist without learned policies; not a pure AV. Primary L4 AI-native hardware; secondary L6 (inspection/industrial vertical deployments). Team size not precisely disclosed in sources; left unknown rather than estimate. Sources: Wikipedia, Crunchbase, Tracxn, SCMP/Yicai (Series C), TMTPost (Series B+).
business_model: Sells industrial-grade legged robots (Jueying X20/X30 quadrupeds, Lynx wheel-legged) primarily for scheduled deployment in high-risk inspection and emergency-response tasks — power-grid inspection, firefighting, police/security, industrial monitoring, infrastructure patrol, and logistics — at roughly RMB 287,500 (~$40K) average unit price (sold 681 X-series units in 2025). Industry applications drove ~79% of main-business revenue, supplemented by research/education unit sales and growing overseas exports (~18% of revenue).
customers: Buyers are Chinese power grids, fire departments, police/security operators, industrial facilities, infrastructure managers, and logistics firms; specific org names not individually disclosed in the IPO filing, though JD.com is a strategic pre-IPO investor. Roughly 18% of revenue (~CNY 61M) is overseas.
revenue_status: profitable (RMB 337.5M / ~$49.6M revenue in 2025, first profitable year with ~RMB 28.68M / ~$4.2M net profit; 52.8% gross margin; ~159% revenue CAGR from 2023; filed for ~$367M STAR Market IPO)
technical_differentiation: Vs Unitree (closest Layer-4 competitor) DEEP Robotics positions on ruggedized industrial reliability — comparable IP67 rating, payload, and battery life to industrial Unitree models while targeting deployment-grade safety-substitution use cases; vs ANYbotics/Boston Dynamics Spot it undercuts on cost (~30-50% of Spot's price). Differentiation is execution- and cost-led within industrial inspection rather than a unique RL/sim-to-real moat, as RL locomotion pipelines are broadly comparable across leading quadruped vendors.

also_known_as: 灵巧智能; DexHand021; Zhejiang DexRobot Technology Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Builds AI-native high-DOF biomimetic five-finger dexterous hands with dense tactile sensing and tendon drive, mass-produced as manipulation-learning end-effectors for humanoid robots.
last_round_lead: Jetyond (捷昌驱动) and CICC Capital (中金资本) among investors
notes: Founded ~Feb 2024 (prototype May 2024, DexHand021 mass production Oct 2024 — exceptionally fast). Dual HQ: Shanghai (R&D/management) and Xinchang, Shaoxing, Zhejiang (manufacturing); ICP filing is Zhejiang (浙ICP备2024093404号), so registered entity is Zhejiang DexRobot Technology Co., Ltd. Product: DexHand021 (19-DOF, 23 sensors, ~96,000 RMB, >150k-cycle life, first domestic mass-produced high-DOF five-finger hand) and DexHand021 Pro (22-DOF / 18 active, wrist-hand integrated, dual-tendon omnidirectional drive, 400+ tactile units, palm camera, 50N payload). Recognized as one of China's dexterous-hand 'Four Little Dragons.' Showcased at CES 2026. Layer 4 AI-native hardware: dexterous hand component purpose-built for learned manipulation; secondary L5 (closed-loop dexterous manipulation software/solution). Funding: equity round with Jetyond/捷昌驱动 and CICC Capital/中金资本; an A round reported in 2025 but exact size, date and lead not disclosed in available sources. Note: distinct from 因时机器人 (a separate dexterous-hand company) — confirmed different entity. Team size and total funding not findable.
business_model: Sells mass-produced high-DOF biomimetic five-finger dexterous hands (DexHand021 line, spanning a ~thousand-yuan three-finger "S" model to the 22-DOF wrist-integrated "Pro") as components/end-effectors to humanoid, industrial, and service-robot builders and research labs. Layers on a manipulation-learning ecosystem (DexCap exoskeleton data-capture rigs sold as products plus the DexCanvas dataset released open-source on Hugging Face) to drive hand adoption and developer lock-in.
customers: Not publicly disclosed by name; positioned for humanoid-robot makers, industrial automation, service robots, and research labs. The DexHand021 is marketed with ROS support and as mass-produced for these segments, but no specific paying or piloting organizations are named in public sources.
revenue_status: early-revenue (mass-producing and selling the DexHand021 line including a low-cost three-finger variant; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed). Backed by strategic/financial investors including Jiechangqudong (捷昌驱动) and CICC Capital (中金资本); exact round size unverified.
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a closed-loop "perception-decision-action" stack: dense multi-modal tactile/visual sensing (23 sensors, position/force/slip/proximity), dual-tendon omnidirectional drive, and a proprietary 7,000-hour real-plus-synthetic manipulation dataset (DexCanvas) with physically consistent force profiles, versus closest named Chinese competitors Lingxin Qiaoshow (灵心巧手/LinkerHand) and Linkerbot, which compete largely on hand DOF/cost and full-stack dexterity platforms. The data-foundation (DexCanvas/DexCap) tied to mass-produced hardware is its main edge, though hardware specs are otherwise comparable to rivals.

one_liner: Builds direct-drive (gearless) actuators and modular legged robots like the D1, which converts between biped and quadruped gaits, replacing reduction gearboxes for dynamic legged control.
last_round_lead: Beijing Advanced Manufacturing and Intelligent Equipment Industry Investment Fund
notes: Assigned primary layer L4 (AI-Native Hardware): maker of direct-drive/gearless actuators and robot joints, the core component. Also ships legged robot platforms (D1 convertible biped/quadruped, TITA, Diablo wheel-leg robots) using RL locomotion policies, hence L6 secondary. Founded 2018 in Hong Kong, relocated to Dongguan Songshan Lube (Songshan Lake), Guangdong, China. Legal/Chinese entity name reportedly "Benmo Technology" (some sources list a 2020 founding for that entity vs 2018 per company site; used company-site 2018). Robotics team established 2022. Latest funding: combined Series B and B+ (announced June 2025), hundreds of millions of RMB, led by Beijing Advanced Manufacturing and Intelligent Equipment Industry Investment Fund; other investors include Legend Capital, Legend Star, Shunxi Fund. Exact USD totals and team size not disclosed. D1 marketed as "world's first fully modular embodied intelligence robot." In-scope: dynamic legged robots with learned policies are AI-native; gearless direct-drive actuators are AI-native enabling hardware. Sources: company site en.directdrive.com, shop.directdrive.com, 36kr (eu.36kr.com/en/p/3324371342944770), New Atlas, IEEE Spectrum, Crunchbase.
business_model: Two revenue lines: (1) sells direct-drive (gearless) joint/actuator modules as core components to robotics, AGV/AMR, smart-appliance, fitness-equipment and pet-robot makers, supplying ~1,000 robot enterprise customers/year with projected 10M+ module deliveries in 2025; (2) sells its own wheeled-legged robot platforms (Xingtian, TITA, and the modular D1) directly, with the D1 priced at $7,499 (single biped) to $13,999 (dual/quadruped).
customers: No specific named buyers publicly disclosed; customers are described categorically as ~1,000 annual robot-enterprise component customers plus integration into sweeping robots, smart appliances, fitness equipment and pet robots, with robot exports to 50+ countries. Named orgs are investors (5Y Capital, MiraclePlus, Legend Star, Beijing Advanced Manufacturing fund), not customers.
revenue_status: profitable (revenue undisclosed in figures; reported "several-fold" YoY growth for consecutive years and described as a rare profitable Chinese robotics firm; raised hundreds of millions RMB in Series B/B+)
technical_differentiation: Uses true direct-drive (fully gearless) actuators across all joints, eliminating reduction gearboxes for low backlash, quiet operation and high backdrivability, versus Unitree and T-Motor/CubeMars, whose legged-robot actuators are quasi-direct-drive (still using planetary gear reductions). The D1's magnetic-coupler modular reconfiguration between biped and quadruped gaits is a further differentiator not offered by Unitree's fixed-form quadruped/humanoid line.

also_known_as: EngineAI Robotics; Shenzhen EngineAI; 逐际动力
one_liner: Builds general-purpose humanoid and legged robots (SE01, PM01, T800) with in-house bipedal hardware and locomotion controllers, leaving the high-level policy stack open for external models.
last_round_lead: Henan Investment Group (Huirong Fund) and Luxshare Precision
notes: Founded October 2023 in Shenzhen; founder/chairman Zhao Tongyang. Products: SA01/SA02, SE01, PM01, T800 (CES 2026 debut, $25k, 450 N·m peak torque), JS01 quadruped. PM01 marketed as open-source general-purpose platform (ROS/Isaac/MuJoCo/ONNX). Funding: pre-A++ and A1 rounds (2025) ~RMB 1B / $139.7M led by Rocket Capital and XPeng, with JD.com, CATL Capital, SenseCapital, Highlight Capital, Baidu Ventures; Series B closed 2026-04-10 at $200M led by Henan Investment Group's Huirong Fund and Luxshare Precision, valuation >RMB 10B (~$1.4B). Total funding estimated ~$340M (sum of disclosed rounds). New Shenzhen plant claims one humanoid every 15 min. Distinct from LimX Dynamics. Layer 4 AI-native hardware; in scope as AI-native general-purpose humanoid maker.
business_model: Sells in-house designed bipedal/legged humanoid robots (SA01 ~$5.4K, SE01 ~$20-30K, PM01 ~$12-14K, T800 full-size) as hardware to research, education, and commercial/industrial buyers, undercutting rivals on price; layers an "open-source hardware + ecosystem profit-sharing" model with developer/partner add-ons. Moving from low-volume sales toward mass production (10,000-unit scalable delivery from its May 2026 Shenzhen manufacturing base).
customers: Strategic collaborations/partners named: NVIDIA, Amazon, JD.com, Tencent, and ByteDance. Reported deployment interest in industrial (Foxconn factories - unverified), education (universities buying SA01), and commercial services/patrol/tourism; specific paying-customer unit counts not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (selling robots at low volume, targeted ~1,000 units in 2025, entering mass delivery 2026; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed). Raised nearly RMB 1B (~$140M) in Pre-A++/A1 rounds led by JD.com with Rockets Capital/XPeng, CATL Capital, plus earlier ~$28M and ~$14M angel rounds.
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on aggressive price/performance and in-house bipedal hardware plus RL locomotion controllers (notably fast, dynamic walking/running demos) versus closest named rival Unitree (G1/H1), undercutting Unitree's pricing; also competes with Shenzhen neighbor UBTECH on full-size humanoids, where EngineAI emphasizes faster iteration and 15-min-per-unit mass-production cadence rather than UBTECH's enterprise integration heritage.

one_liner: Builds expressive social humanoid robots (Ameca, Mesmer) with lifelike facial/gesture hardware, integrating external LLMs for conversational interaction in entertainment, customer service, and research.
notes: Founded 2004 in Falmouth, Cornwall, UK by Will Jackson; restructured as a US company headquartered in Redwood City, CA in Dec 2024 (UK operations retained). Series A $10M led by Helium-3 Ventures (Dec 17, 2024), other participants incl. ThirtySeven Holdings, Belvoir Investments, Figueira Capital, Nicolas Desmarais; total funding ~$16.2M (per businesswire/Crunchbase). Flagship Ameca expressive humanoid plus Mesmer/RoboThespian. In_scope=true: general-purpose expressive humanoid hardware category is AI-native (social robotics), though the AI is largely external LLMs rather than proprietary learned policies — assigned L4 AI-Native Hardware. Sources: businesswire Series A release, siliconangle, VentureBeat, Crunchbase.
business_model: Sells expressive social humanoid robots (Ameca, Mesmer, RoboThespian) outright at roughly $100K-$500K per unit depending on configuration (head-only to full body), with professional engineer-led installation, plus an integrated robot-management software suite; deployed to museums, brand/trade events, science centers, and entertainment venues rather than for factory labor. Roughly 200+ robots delivered across ~27 countries.
customers: Named deployments include Computer History Museum (Mountain View, "Chatbots Decoded" exhibit), Museum of the Future (Dubai), Sphere Las Vegas, National Robotarium, and Copernicus Science Centre; pharma firm GSK uses units for trade-event engagement.
revenue_status: early-revenue (unit sales ongoing; ~$16.2M total funding including a $10M Series A in Dec 2024 led by Helium-3 Ventures; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Unlike Figure (Figure 03) and Tesla (Optimus), which target industrial/manufacturing labor with locomotion and manipulation, Engineered Arts focuses on lifelike facial expression and gesture hardware (Mesmer animatronics) for social/conversational interaction, integrating external LLMs rather than building task-execution autonomy. It is among the few with a sizable installed commercial base (200+ units), though in social humanoids specifically rather than the labor market its industrial peers chase.

one_liner: Builds Tacta, an ultra-thin high-density multidimensional tactile sensor (361 sensels/cm2 at 1000Hz) plus a tactile-operation model training platform giving dexterous robot hands human-like touch for learned manipulation.
notes: Shenzhen (Shenzhen Bay) embodied-intelligence startup founded January 2025; founder/CEO Ningzhe Hou. Debuted at CES 2026 the Tacta sensor (361 multidimensional sensels/cm2 at 1000Hz, "spatially-encoded multidimensional piezoresistance"; fingertip sensor priced ~1/10 of competitors, unaffected by material/temperature/magnetic fields) and HexSkin large-area electronic skin; demoed a dexterous hand fully covered with 1,956 sensels. Offers mass-producible sensor hardware + tactile data acquisition/decoupling software toolkit + a tactile-operation model training/generalization platform. Pure component/subsystem maker; does NOT build its own humanoid foundation model. In scope: AI-native sensing subsystem purpose-built to enable learned variable-object manipulation. Website: www.ensuringtech.com. NOTE: distinct from the unrelated "Tacta Systems" (Palo Alto, $75M raise) — same product word "Tacta" but different company; do not conflate. No funding round, total funding, or team size publicly disclosed/findable; left undisclosed/unknown rather than invented. Founding year, name, and one-liner sourced from CES 2026 PR Newswire release.
business_model: Sells tactile-sensing hardware to robotics/humanoid makers: the Tacta multidimensional fingertip/dexterous-hand sensor and HexSkin large-area electronic skin, positioned as deployable "tactile perception infrastructure," paired with a tactile-operation model training platform. Competes explicitly on cost, pricing mass-produced fingertip sensors at ~1/10 of rival solutions and full-body humanoid skin coverage in the hundred-dollar range (likely component sales plus the data/training-platform layer).
customers: Not publicly disclosed. As of CES 2026 the company named no paying or piloting customers; demonstrations (e.g., a dexterous hand fully covered with 1,956 sensels) were shown on its own hardware rather than a named partner's robot.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (founded January 2025, Shenzhen Bay; no revenue, funding round, or customer contracts publicly disclosed as of mid-2026 — products newly launched/showcased at CES 2026)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on a "spatially-encoded multidimensional piezoresistance" architecture delivering high density (361 sensels/cm2 at 1000Hz) in an ultra-thin 4.5mm module with integrated edge computing, plus a HexSkin hexagonal-tiling e-skin marketed at roughly 1/10 the fingertip cost of rivals. Closest named competitors are Paxini (帕西尼/PaXini, CN multidimensional tactile sensors) and US-based Tacta Systems; Ensuring's edge is cost-per-coverage and full-body deployability rather than a fundamentally unique sensing modality (claim unverified — no third-party benchmarks public).

one_liner: Builds general-purpose humanoid robots for labor in industry and the home, driven by its in-house Helix System1/System2 vision-language-action model for generalist manipulation and control.
notes: Founded 2022 by Brett Adcock; HQ San Jose, CA (also Sunnyvale offices). Series C exceeded $1B at $39B post-money valuation (Sept 2025), led by Parkway Venture Capital with Brookfield, NVIDIA, Intel Capital, Microsoft, OpenAI Startup Fund, Bezos Expeditions. Prior Series B was $675M (Feb 2024) at $2.6B valuation. Total funding ~$2.9B+ across rounds (reports vary). Helix is its System1/System2 VLA model for generalist humanoid control; Figure 01-03 robots developed. Genuine robotics-AI: physical humanoid hardware plus in-house foundation model. Team size not reliably found.
business_model: Sells/leases general-purpose humanoid robots to industrial enterprises, primarily via a Robots-as-a-Service model (estimated ~$1,000/month per unit) for factory and warehouse labor, with planned expansion to home consumers; manufactures in-house at its BotQ facility (12,000 units/yr capacity, scaling toward 100,000).
customers: BMW (Figure 02 deployed at BMW Plant Spartanburg, SC, supporting production of 30,000+ X3 vehicles); other commercial/logistics pilots not publicly named. Note: BMW selected Hexagon's AEON rather than Figure for its 2026 Leipzig pilot, so Figure is now one supplier among several.
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified; est. ~150 robots shipped 2025, RaaS pricing implies low-tens-of-millions ARR for 2026 per third-party estimates)
technical_differentiation: Vs Tesla Optimus and 1X: Figure's edge is its in-house Helix vision-language-action model (S0/S1/S2 layers) running fully onboard on low-power embedded GPUs without cloud, plus proven real commercial deployment at BMW Spartanburg, which Optimus lacks. Weaker vs Chinese vendors on price and vs 1X on consumer/home rollout.

one_liner: Japanese university spinoff building vision-based tactile sensors (transparent skin plus embedded camera) that give robots touch feedback to grasp and handle variable, deformable items like food.
notes: Founded Oct 2021 (some aggregators list 2022 = first funding year). Spun out of vision-based tactile sensing research at Carnegie Mellon University and Tohoku University. Series A of JPY 410M closed 2023-10-18, led by existing investor Keio Innovation Initiative with new investors Tohoku University Venture Partners, KSP, Monozukuri Ventures; prior seed ~JPY 100M (2022-03). Total funding ~$7.47M per Tracxn (USD conversions of yen rounds approximate). HQ in Tokyo (Bunkyo/Koto ward area), with an additional Kyoto base. Applications span food, automotive, manufacturing, pharma; food (bento/deli picking of deformable items) is the lead vertical, hence 6c_food. In scope: AI-native variable-item tactile manipulation that would not exist as conventional pre-programmed automation.
business_model: Sells integrated robot systems (robot hands embedded with its vision-based tactile sensors) plus customized automation software/GUIs and support, primarily for food-handling tasks like bento plating; in 2025 it added a Robot-as-a-Service "Okazu" plan with zero upfront cost at ~¥250,000/month (3-month trial, 1-year+ commitment, optional buyout). Revenue comes from hardware/system sales, RaaS subscriptions, and ongoing maintenance/consumables.
customers: Named deployments include food/prepared-meal factories Hirai Co., Ltd. and Home Delica Co., Ltd.; broader targets are Japanese food, automotive, manufacturing, and pharmaceutical companies (including those with production bases in Thailand). RaaS plan aimed at ready-made-meal makers hesitant about automation cost.
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercial product sales and RaaS subscriptions live; ~$3.57M raised over 4 rounds per Tracxn/Dealroom, unverified; specific revenue figure not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Like GelSight (MIT spinoff) and Meta AI's DIGIT, FingerVision uses a transparent elastic skin over an embedded camera to sense force and slip distributions; its differentiation is verticalizing this into deployed full robot systems for direct food handling (irregular, soft items on bento lines) and a RaaS go-to-market, whereas GelSight and DIGIT sell sensors/research modules rather than integrated production-line automation.

also_known_as: Foundation Robotics Labs; Foundation Future Industries
one_liner: Builds general-purpose humanoid robots (Phantom MK1) using novel actuators and a hybrid AI stack (Cortex) for autonomous work in manufacturing, logistics, and defense.
notes: General-purpose humanoid maker (Phantom MK1), hardware-first with novel actuators plus hybrid AI (Cortex). AI-native category (general-purpose humanoid) => in_scope=true despite defense applications. Founded April 2024 by Sankaet Pathak (ex-Synapse), Arjun Sethi (Tribe Capital), Mike LeBlanc (ex-Cobalt Robotics). HQ San Francisco (617 Bryant St); also Munich office. Funding: $11M pre-seed (Aug 2024, led by Tribe Capital); ~$21M total after a Q1 2025 raise. Mid-2025 reports of a ~$100M round at ~$1B valuation (and seeking up to $500M at $3B+) were not confirmed closed at time of research, so last round recorded as the confirmed pre-seed. Also holds ~$24M in U.S. DoD (Army/Navy/Air Force) SBIR contracts. Targets 50,000 units by 2027. Primary layer L4 AI-native hardware; vertical exposure noted (manufacturing/logistics/defense). Team size not findable.
business_model: Sells/leases its Phantom MK1 humanoid robot via a Robotics-as-a-Service subscription model (~$100K/unit target, may reach $150K) to industrial manufacturers and defense customers on service contracts rather than outright sales; also pursuing US defense sales (e.g., units sent to Ukraine for battlefield testing in early 2026). Targets >$1B ARR via fleet deployment.
customers: Undisclosed by name; pilots run with an unnamed automotive manufacturing OEM (reported near Atlanta, handling bumpers) plus consumer goods, beverage, and glass manufacturers, with ~10 units operating 24/7. Defense: deployed 2 Phantom MK-1 units to Ukraine for frontline testing (Feb 2026). No specific paying customer publicly named.
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified) - ~10 RaaS units deployed in industrial pilots since mid-2025 generating early service revenue; no public revenue figure. Targets shipping 40-50 robots in 2025 and >$1B ARR longer-term. Reportedly raising ~$100M at a ~$1B valuation (unverified).
technical_differentiation: vs. Figure and Tesla Optimus: Foundation uses proprietary rolling-contact gearbox actuators (claimed 90-95% efficiency vs. competitors' harmonic/cycloidal ~50-60%, back-drivable for safety) and a camera-only perception stack. Its Cortex AI hybrid combines imitation learning with physics/kinematics-aware state-based models, claiming task learning in ~30 min with minimal data versus Figure/Tesla's heavier reliance on large-scale imitation learning and teleoperation fleets.

also_known_as: Fourier (傅利叶智能), Shanghai Fourier Intelligence Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Develops general-purpose GR-series humanoid robots with embodied AI software, plus rehabilitation robotics, building learning-based 'brains' for humanoids targeting rehab, research, and service applications.
last_round_lead: Guoxin Investment (with Prosperity7 Ventures, Pudong VC, Zhangjiang Hi-Tech)
notes: Founded 2015 by Alex Gu (Gu Jie); HQ in Shanghai Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park. Began humanoid GR-1 development in 2019; GR-1 unveiled July 2023 at WAIC Shanghai, later GR-2. Originally a rehabilitation/exoskeleton robotics company that expanded into general-purpose humanoids. Series E (~800M RMB / ~$109M) announced Jan 7, 2025, led by Guoxin Investment with Prosperity7 Ventures (Saudi Aramco), Pudong VC, Zhangjiang Hi-Tech, Junshan Capital. Earlier Series D ($55M, Jan 2022) led by SoftBank Vision Fund 2. ~$1.1B valuation. Total funding reported ~$193M-$246M across sources. Layer 1 fit: building embodied AI brain/learning models for humanoids; also significant hardware (L4) and rehab vertical (L6). Sources: Crunchbase, PitchBook, DealStreetAsia, kr-asia, CMRA.
business_model: Dual revenue engine: sells 30+ rehabilitation/exoskeleton robot products and its RehabHub platform to hospitals and clinics (3,000+ institutions across 40-60+ countries on a hardware-plus-platform basis), and sells GR-series bipedal humanoid robots (GR-1, GR-2, GR-3 Care-bot) plus embodied-AI software/SDK to research institutions, enterprises, and elder-care/companionship buyers. The mature rehab division funds the still-emerging humanoid line.
customers: Rehab robotics deployed in 3,000+ hospitals/institutions in 40-60+ countries serving 1M+ patients. Humanoid customers: 100+ GR-1 units delivered; GR-2 placed at research institutions including ETH Zurich and Carnegie Mellon University, plus industrial collaborators. RoboCup Federation is a named education/research partner.
revenue_status: early-revenue (rehab division has real recurring sales — 2023 overseas rehab revenue up >50%, ~10% of total; humanoid line still pre/early-revenue with ~300 bipedal units targeted for 2025. No total figure publicly disclosed; ~RMB 8B pre-money / $1.1B valuation as of May 2025, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Unlike pure-play humanoid rivals Unitree (research/education focus, accessible pricing, profitable) and UBTECH (manufacturing/service focus, listed), Fourier leverages a decade of medical-rehabilitation pedigree — clinical-grade human-safe interaction, biomechanics expertise, and in-house FSA actuators — to target healthcare, eldercare, and companion (Care-bot) niches with a clearer near-term clinical use case rather than competing head-on in general-purpose industrial labor.

also_known_as: Galaxy General Robotics; 银河通用; Beijing Galbot Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Builds general-purpose embodied-AI robots (G1 semi-humanoid mobile manipulator) driven by VLA foundation models like GraspVLA trained on billions of simulated interactions for retail and manipulation tasks.
notes: Founded May 2023 by Peking University Professor He Wang. Develops AGI-powered general-purpose robots; flagship product is the G1 semi-humanoid mobile manipulator, powered by task-specific VLA models (GraspVLA, TrackVLA, GroceryVLA) - strongly fits Layer 1 Robot Foundation Models. Funding history: Angel/Angel+ rounds (2023-2024, ~CNY 700M+ led by Meituan, BAIC, SenseTime, iFlytek funds, Qiming, IDG, etc.); Series A June 2025 (~$153M, >$1B valuation, investors incl. CATL/Puquan, Bosch investment arm); Series B Dec 2025 ($300M at $3B valuation, led by China Mobile); March 2026 round CNY 2.5B (~$362-363M) described as first national-level fund investment in a Chinese embodied-intelligence firm (incl. National AI Industry Investment Fund, Sinopec, CITIC, SAIC Financial). Total funding estimate ~$967M is approximate; exact USD total not precisely disclosed. Team size not found. Last-round type labeled as a large strategic late-stage round; exact lettered designation for March 2026 not consistently reported.
business_model: Sells G1 semi-humanoid mobile manipulators plus its VLA software stack (GraspVLA, TrackVLA, GroceryVLA) into vertical deployments: unmanned 24/7 smart pharmacies and autonomous "Galbot Store" convenience retail (per-unit/fleet sales and operated-store models), and industrial manipulation units sold to manufacturers, reportedly in orders of several thousand units. Also monetizes eldercare/hospital pilots.
customers: Named: CATL (also a strategic investor) and Bosch in manufacturing; Toyota and Hyundai explored for automotive; Xuanwu Hospital for healthcare/eldercare. Deployments in 10+ unmanned pharmacies in Beijing (targeting 100) and autonomous Galbot Stores across 30+ Chinese cities.
revenue_status: early-revenue (specific figure not publicly disclosed; claims thousands of units ordered and 30+ city retail deployments, but no published revenue. Heavily capitalized: ~$800M-$1.2B raised cumulatively, $3B valuation as of Dec 2025 - unverified across sources)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on simulation-first, sim-to-real synthetic data: GraspVLA/grasping models trained on billions of synthetic frames (released what it calls the first large-scale synthetic dexterous-grasping dataset), versus Figure AI's Helix hierarchical VLM-plus-reactive-policy trained largely on real teleoperation data, and Unitree which leads on low-cost humanoid hardware but relies on third-party foundation models (e.g., NVIDIA GR00T) rather than a proprietary VLA stack. Also first to integrate NVIDIA Jetson Thor in a humanoid.

one_liner: Builds high-resolution optical/vision-based tactile sensors that digitize touch at micron scale, co-developing Digit 360 with Meta AI to train AI robotic manipulation policies.
notes: Founded 2011 (some sources cite founders ex-MIT research scientists; Crunchbase/PitchBook list founders Janos Rohaly, Bill Yost, Kimo Johnson). HQ at 179 Bear Hill Road, Waltham, MA. Total funding ~$16.2M across 6 rounds (Crunchbase/Tracxn); most recent DISCLOSED round is $10M Series B led by Anzu Partners, announced Jan 2020 (investors also include Omega Funds). No publicly disclosed round after 2020. Digit 360 (announced Oct 2024 with Meta AI / FAIR) is a 360-degree artificial-fingertip tactile sensor with 18+ sensing features, detecting forces as low as 1 millinewton; Meta open-sources the code/designs. Partnership began with the original Digit sensor in 2021. Scope: borderline as a sensor component, but in_scope=true because the high-res tactile sensors exist specifically to enable learned robotic grasping/manipulation (AI-native tactile subsystem). Team size not disclosed publicly; growjo/LinkedIn estimates exist but not authoritative. Primary product line also serves non-AI industrial metrology/inspection (aerospace, forensics), but the assigned Digit 360 robotics use case is AI-native.
business_model: Sells vision-based tactile sensor hardware plus accompanying software/SDKs, spanning industrial 3D surface-inspection units (GelSight Mobile/Max for aerospace, automotive, oil & gas NDT and forensics) and research-grade robotic touch sensors (GelSight Mini, DIGIT, Digit 360); also earns OEM/manufacturing-and-distribution revenue by commercially producing sensors co-developed with Meta AI and through reseller partnerships (e.g., global reseller agreement with Hexagon).
customers: Meta AI (co-development and manufacturing partner for DIGIT/Digit 360); Hexagon (global reseller partner). End markets include aerospace/defense MRO, automotive precision engineering, oil & gas inspection, law enforcement/forensics, and academic robotics labs; specific named paying end-customers are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercial hardware sold; ~$16.2M-$30.1M raised per Tracxn/PitchBook, $10M Series B led by Anzu Partners in 2020; exact revenue not publicly disclosed - unverified)
technical_differentiation: Pioneer and category-leader in elastomer/camera-based tactile sensing with micron-level spatial resolution and millinewton force sensitivity; Digit 360 adds 18+ sensing modalities and 360-degree fingertip coverage. Vs Meta's open-source DIGIT (which it manufactures) it offers higher-fidelity, productized industrial-grade sensors; vs PaXini and other tactile-array competitors its vision-based approach yields far higher spatial resolution at lower per-unit cost.

one_liner: Builds rugged quadruped robots (Vision 60 Q-UGV) for defense, security, and inspection, using learned biomimetic blind-mode locomotion to traverse unstructured terrain without pre-mapped paths.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (acquired; ~$240M transaction value for 60% stake at $400M enterprise value)
last_round_lead: LIG Nex1 (with Korea Investment Private Equity)
notes: HQ Philadelphia, PA, USA; founded 2015. Maker of Vision 60 (V60) quadruped Q-UGV for defense/security/industrial inspection. Acquisition by South Korea's LIG Nex1 (defense) and Korea Investment Private Equity closed 2024-07-26: 60% controlling stake for ~$240M, $400M enterprise value; remainder funded by PE. Pre-acquisition venture funding was small/undisclosed. Now active as a US-based subsidiary of LIG Nex1. Assigned Layer 4 (AI-native legged hardware); secondary L6 (defense/security vertical). In-scope: legged-locomotion category is AI-native, relying on learned proprioceptive control policies. Team size not reliably found. Sources: ghostrobotics.io, Crunchbase, PR Newswire (acquisition close), The Robot Report, technical.ly.
business_model: Sells ruggedized Vision 60 and Spirit 40 quadruped unmanned ground vehicles (Q-UGVs) plus mission-specific payloads (e.g. manipulator arm, sensors, armed/EW modules) to militaries and government security buyers, primarily via direct hardware sales and defense procurement contracts. Now operates as the US legged-robotics arm of South Korean defense prime LIG Nex1 (60% owner), which feeds it manufacturing scale and weapons-integration pull-through.
customers: U.S. Marine Corps, U.S. Army, and U.S. Air Force (notably Tyndall AFB perimeter security); also Department of Homeland Security/CBP border-patrol piloting, and commercial buyers in energy, data centers, and construction. Parent company LIG Nex1 is a strategic channel into South Korean and allied defense markets.
revenue_status: scaling (reportedly ~$40M+ annual sales and described as profitable at time of 2024 acquisition; acquired by LIG Nex1 for $240M at a ~$400M valuation, with a US IPO targeted for 2029)
technical_differentiation: Vs Boston Dynamics (Spot), Ghost positions on defense/tactical hardening rather than commercial inspection: sealed leg joints, field-repairable architecture, higher speed (~3.0 vs 1.6 m/s) and ~3x endurance, plus learned "blind-mode" proprioceptive locomotion that traverses terrain without relying on vision/LIDAR, and a willingness to support armed/military payloads Boston Dynamics prohibits. Vs Unitree (Go2/B2), Ghost differentiates on military-grade ruggedization and defense integration rather than low-cost consumer/industrial volume; Boston Dynamics' 2022 patent suit against Ghost was settled confidentially.

one_liner: Builds expressive humanoid social robots (Sophia) with lifelike faces for engagement, research, and entertainment; conversation relies on external/third-party AI rather than an in-house foundation model.
notes: Founded 2007 in Dallas, TX (US) by David Hanson; relocated HQ to Hong Kong Science Park in 2013. Best known for Sophia (activated Feb 2016) and Desdemona social humanoids. Layer 4 AI-native hardware: hardware-first expressive humanoid maker. Total funding lightly documented; Crunchbase/PitchBook list a $14.5M Series B (March 2018) as the most recent disclosed round; lead investor not clearly disclosed. Team ~58 (getlatka) / 51-200 (LinkedIn). David Hanson is also co-founder of SingularityNET; the two entities are closely linked. In scope: general-purpose social humanoid, AI-native category.
business_model: Sells/leases expressive humanoid robots and licenses its Frubber skin and robotics platform: full-scale robots (Sophia, Grace) are leased for corporate/conference appearances (reported ~$50K-$250K per engagement) and research/exhibition partnerships, while consumer/STEM units like Little Sophia (~$149) are sold to teach kids to code. Grace, a healthcare/eldercare android, targets sales to medical and care institutions.
customers: Largely undisclosed; buyers are mostly conference organizers, universities/research labs, museums, and media/event producers hiring Sophia for appearances. Grace was positioned for healthcare and eldercare facilities (COVID-era), but no named paying institutional customers are publicly confirmed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$5.8M cumulative reported by GetLatka, unverified; ~$21.7M raised in funding)
technical_differentiation: Patented Frubber nanotech skin over a face-actuator system gives Sophia ~60 lifelike expressions, positioning it as a "social/personality" robot brand vs Engineered Arts' Ameca (silicone skin, more degrees of facial freedom, focused on entertainment/exhibition) and Realbotix (companion/lifelike androids). Differentiation is brand recognition (Sophia as a media icon) and expressive realism rather than autonomous conversation, which relies on external/third-party LLMs and scripting.

also_known_as: LeArm AI; NexArm; Shenzhen Hiwonder Technology Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Sells AI-native research manipulators (NexArm, LeArm AI, LeRobot SO-ARM101) with dual-camera vision and leader-follower sync for imitation/RL data collection and embodied-intelligence learning.
notes: Shenzhen Hiwonder Technology Co., Ltd. is primarily a STEAM-education and open-source robotics vendor (40+ patents, 80+ countries). The AI-native research manipulators (NexArm leader-follower arm, LeArm AI, and the LeRobot SO-ARM101) are a product line built for embodied-intelligence research: dual-camera vision, millisecond leader/follower sync, integrated with Hugging Face LeRobot for imitation learning, RL, and validation against community pre-trained models. NexArm uses ESP32+AT32 dual-chip architecture with inverse-kinematics and end-to-end large-model validation. core_or_division=division because the AI-native learning hardware is one line within a broader education-robotics business; the company does not build a foundation model. Funding ~$1.57M (PitchBook/Tracxn), investor Bell Tech Education; round size/type/date undisclosed. Team size ~50 per PitchBook. Founded year 2015 per company About page and LinkedIn (one source noted 2012; company self-reports 2015). In_scope=true: the research-arm category is AI-native (built specifically for learned-policy data collection and validation) and would not exist in this form without imitation/RL learning workflows.
business_model: Sells low-cost open-source AI/embodied-intelligence robotic arm kits (NexArm, LeArm AI, LeRobot SO-ARM101, ~$254+ per kit, leader-follower pairs for imitation/RL data collection) plus broader STEAM robotics kits, servos, sensors and curriculum, sold direct via hiwonder.com and through resellers (Amazon, Seeed Studio, RobotShop, OZ Robotics) in 80+ countries. Revenue is hardware unit sales with bulk/institutional pricing.
customers: Primarily education and research buyers: per company claims, products are used in 2,000+ schools and institutions and by 300,000+ teachers and students across 80+ countries; specific named paying organizations are not publicly disclosed. Distribution partners include Seeed Studio, RobotShop, Amazon, and OZ Robotics.
revenue_status: early-revenue (third-party estimate US$5M-US$10M revenue, ~50 employees, $1.57M raised from Bell Tech Education per PitchBook/RocketReach - unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on being among the lowest-cost, deeply LeRobot-compatible manipulators with dual-camera (gripper + external) vision, magnetic-encoder bus servos, and turnkey leader-follower teleoperation for imitation/RL data collection, plus its own NexArm ESP32+AT32+K230 (6 TOPS) stack. Vs closest named competitors Seeed Studio (which sells the bare SO-ARM101 servo kit it co-distributes) and The Robot Studio / Hugging Face reference SO-ARM designs, Hiwonder adds assembled, vision-integrated, curriculum-bundled kits rather than DIY-only hardware - largely an execution/cost play on a shared open-source design.

also_known_as: INSPIRE-ROBOTS; 因时机器人; Beijing Inspire Robots Technology Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Builds China's first mass-produced five-finger dexterous hands and micro linear servo actuators used as tactile end-effectors and components on humanoid and learned-manipulation robots.
last_round_lead: C1: China Mobile Chain Leader Fund & Shenzhen Capital Group; C2: Beijing AI Industry Investment Fund
notes: Founded May 2016 in Beijing as Beijing Inspire Robots Technology Co., Ltd. (Intime Robotics). Makes micro linear servo actuators and RH56/FTP five-finger dexterous hands (FTP series integrates 17 tactile sensors, fingertip force up to 3kg, 1M+ cycle lifespan). 2025 annual deliveries topped 10,000 dexterous hand units; product featured in 2026 Year of the Horse CCTV Spring Festival Gala. Feb 24 2026 announced C1 and C2 rounds totaling several hundred million RMB; C1 co-led by China Mobile Chain Leader Fund and Shenzhen Capital Group (with Boyuan, Fortune, Primavera); C2 led by Beijing AI Industry Investment Fund (with Qiming, Boyuan, Primavera, Jinshi, TCL Ventures). Primary layer L4 AI-Native Hardware. In scope: tactile dexterous hands/actuators are end-effectors for learned AI manipulation on humanoids; not its own foundation model. Total funding USD, exact round sizes in USD, and team size not disclosed.
business_model: Sells mass-produced five-finger dexterous hands (RH56 series, ~RMB 30,000-48,000 / ~USD 3,000-8,000 per unit) plus micro linear servo actuators and electric grippers as components to humanoid robot OEMs, industrial automation firms, and research institutions; also operates an online store for direct/distributor sales. Revenue is hardware unit sales of end-effectors and motion-control parts.
customers: 100+ customers (per company). Named: Unitree (宇树), UBTech (优必选), AgiBot/Zhiyuan (智元), Galbot/Galaxy General (银河通用), Skild AI, CATL, ATL, BYD, Ecovacs (科沃斯); plus universities and research institutes in China, Europe, and the US.
revenue_status: scaling (early-revenue commercialized since 2020; cumulative dexterous-hand shipments surpassed 1,000 units by Sept 2024 and reportedly crossed ~10,000 units in 2025; claims ~50% domestic market share; specific revenue figure not publicly disclosed - third-party USD 30-80M estimate unverified)
technical_differentiation: First in China to commercialize and mass-produce humanoid-grade five-finger dexterous hands, vertically integrating its own micro linear servo actuators to drive prices down to the ~RMB 30k-48k tier, far below Zhejiang BrainCo (强脑科技) BrainRobotics at ~RMB 108k. Competes on cost/volume and component integration vs Linker Hand / 灵心巧手 (~RMB 49.9k, higher DoF) and parts supplier Zhaowei (兆威机电), which sells hollow-cup motors rather than complete hands.

also_known_as: Kepler Exploration Robot; Shanghai Kepler Robot Co., Ltd; Keplerbot
one_liner: Builds industrial general-purpose humanoid robots (K2 'Bumblebee', hybrid architecture) with full-stack self-developed actuators, dexterous hands and learned motion-control policies to address labor shortages.
notes: Founded Aug 2023 by Yang Hua (founder of Xiaomi smart-home supplier Chunmi Tech) and Hu Debo, HQ Shanghai Pudong. Builds full-stack legged/humanoid platforms (Forerunner series, K2 'Bumblebee' hybrid-architecture industrial humanoid; claimed world's first commercially available hybrid-architecture humanoid, mass production Sept 2025). 80% self-sufficiency in core components incl. self-developed rotary/linear actuators, six-axis force sensors, dexterous hands, and learned motion-control policies => AI-native hardware (L4). Also operates as L6 industrial/manufacturing vertical deployment (auto plant tasks). In-scope: general-purpose humanoid driven by learned policies, would not exist without robot learning. Funding: total ~$14.6M reported by Tracxn over 2 rounds (first round Apr 2025, Series A Apr 2026); figures uncertain. Separately, Hangzhou Kelin Electric (A-share listed) acquiring controlling 51% stake (9.43% closed Dec 31 2025 for ~CNY100M; further 41.57% for up to CNY300M), implying ~CNY720M valuation — status still active as operating subsidiary, not shut down. Distinct from Galaxea. Team size not found. Sources: PRNewswire (K2 Bumblebee IROS 2025 / mass production), Tracxn, Crunchbase, Gasgoo/Yicai (Kelin acquisition).
business_model: Sells full-size industrial humanoid robots, primarily the K2 "Bumblebee" priced around RMB 248,000 (~$35K) per unit, to factories and logistics operators, plus framework/volume purchase agreements; also supplies units to universities for R&D/education. Scenario-driven model: deploys robots into real industrial tasks (auto manufacturing, logistics, welding) to accumulate data and iterate, then scales delivery.
customers: Mostly unnamed: orders from "multiple automotive companies" for logistics and five universities for research/education; framework agreements covering several thousand units (hundreds of millions of yuan) across logistics, manufacturing and exhibitions. Specific named paying customers are not publicly disclosed. Now majority-controlled/strategically invested by A-share listed firm Hangzhou Kelin (per Gasgoo).
revenue_status: early-revenue (FY2025 revenue 4.34M CNY, net loss 66.94M CNY; Q1 2026 revenue 2.64M CNY, loss 17.09M CNY per controlling shareholder Hangzhou Kelin disclosures)
technical_differentiation: vs UBTech (Walker S) and Unitree (G1/H1): all three are Chinese full-stack players, but Kepler differentiates on a hybrid serial-parallel mechanical architecture combining roller-screw linear actuators with rotary actuators for high stiffness/low backlash and an aggressively low industrial price point, plus a hybrid local+cloud compute approach. UBTech and Unitree instead emphasize vertically integrated proprietary rotary servo actuators; differentiation is real on architecture but modest, competing largely on cost, deployment speed and capital.

also_known_as: Leju Robot; KUAVO; Kuafu; 乐聚机器人; Leju (Shenzhen) Robotics Technology Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Builds full-size KUAVO/Kuafu humanoid robots for industrial, education and service use, running on Huawei's HarmonyOS and Pangu embodied-intelligence model rather than an in-house foundation model.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (multiple rounds incl. Series B and ~$210M pre-IPO; cumulative not consolidated)
last_round_lead: undisclosed (participants: CITIC Goldstone, Shenzhen Investment Holdings, Tencent, Shenzhen Capital Group)
notes: Founded 2016 by a Harbin Institute of Technology (HIT) team; HQ Shenzhen, Guangdong. Hardware-first general-purpose humanoid maker (KUAVO / Kuafu line, KUAVO 5 modular). Fits Layer 4 AI-Native Hardware: relies on third-party Huawei Pangu embodied-intelligence model and HarmonyOS for the AI "brain" and contributes data to external VLA/embodied datasets rather than building its own foundation model. General-purpose humanoid = AI-native category => in_scope=true. Pre-IPO round Oct 2025 raised ~RMB 1.5B (~$207-210M); investors include Tencent, Shenzhen Capital Group, CITIC Goldstone, Shenzhen Investment Holdings, Dongfang Precision, Tuopu Group, China-US Green Fund. Lead investor of the pre-IPO round not clearly identified. Sources: Wikipedia (Leju Robot), Bloomberg/DealStreetAsia/RoboticsAndAutomationNews (Oct 2025 round), Huawei Central / WAIC coverage (Pangu + HarmonyOS integration), company site lejurobot.com.
business_model: Sells full-size KUAVO humanoid robots via direct unit sales across three tiers, with data-collection units (45% of 2025 revenue) and scientific-research/education units (32.5%) as the largest segments, plus commercial-service deployments (18.7%); also sells lower-cost AELOS education kits and Roban robots to schools. 2025 revenue ~RMB 258M (~$36M).
customers: Nio (validating KUAVO at its EV factory) and Jiangsu Hengtong Group (factory pilot); AELOS education robots deployed at 4,000+ schools/institutions nationwide, including a Sichuan school; partnered with Huawei's embodied-AI center. Delivered 100+ full-size humanoids by 2025.
revenue_status: early-revenue (2025 operating revenue ~RMB 258M / ~$36M; pre-IPO, targeting Shanghai STAR/ChiNext listing)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates by being China's first open-source HarmonyOS humanoid running Huawei's Pangu embodied-AI model, giving Pangu access without separate licensing costs, vs Unitree (ultra-low-cost, ~$5,500 R1) and UBTech/Fourier; competes more on the Huawei/HarmonyOS ecosystem lock-in and discount pricing than on hardware leadership.

one_liner: Develops full-size general-purpose humanoid and bipedal robots powered by learning-based motion-control foundation models and an agentic operating system (COSA) for autonomous tasks in dynamic environments.
last_round_lead: Stone Venture (no single lead disclosed; multiple investors including JD, Oriental Fortune Capital, CoStone Capital)
notes: Founded January 2022, HQ Shenzhen. Series B $200M announced Feb 2026 with participation from Stone Venture, JD.com, Oriental Fortune Capital, CoStone Capital, plus existing shareholders NIO Capital, Shangqi Capital (SAIC), Future Capital; no single lead clearly disclosed. Prior Series A (~July 2024) led by China Merchants Venture Capital and Shangqi Capital. Products: Oli full-size humanoid, TRON 1/TRON 2 bipedal platforms; software COSA (Cognitive OS of Agents) plus motion-control foundation models. Total funding ~$272M (some sources cite up to $296M). Qualifies for L1: builds general learning-based locomotion/manipulation foundation models for its own robots.
business_model: Sells full-size general-purpose humanoid robots (Oli, from ~RMB 158,000/$22,730) and modular bipedal/development platforms (TRON 1, TRON 2) to research labs, robotics developers, and systems integrators, plus its COSA agentic operating system and motion-control foundation models as the embodied-intelligence "brain" layer. Positions itself as a platform company (hardware + software product ladder) rather than a hardware-only vendor, with commercial/service deployments via strategic partners.
customers: JD.com is both a strategic investor and intended customer, planning to deploy LimX humanoids across its supply chain and retail operations; other strategic partners/investors include Alibaba and auto-parts supplier Zhongding Group. Most buyers to date are AI researchers, robotics developers, and systems integrators purchasing TRON/Oli platforms; specific named paying enterprise deployments are otherwise undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (Oli began taking pre-orders July 2025 from ~RMB 158,000; TRON dev platforms sold commercially; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from Unitree and AgiBot (Zhiyuan) — the two leading Chinese humanoid shippers — by bundling a learning-based whole-body motion-control foundation model with COSA, a "physical-world-native" agentic operating system, and a tiered hardware ladder (TRON 1/2 to full-size Oli) rather than competing mainly on low-cost hardware as Unitree does. Versus US rivals like Figure, it emphasizes a cheaper, developer-accessible China-manufactured platform plus open agentic OS.

also_known_as: PsiBot; Psi Robot; Proto-Sentient Intelligence; LumosAI
one_liner: Builds hierarchical end-to-end vision-language-action foundation models and dexterous-hand hardware enabling robots to perform long-horizon, open-world manipulation via reinforcement learning and scenario-driven data.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (Chinese reports cite ~2 billion yuan cumulative as of 2026, unverified per-round)
last_round_lead: GL Ventures (高瓴创投) and Lanchi Ventures (蓝驰创投), co-led; AgiBot (智元机器人) participating
notes: CEO/founder Dr. Wang Qibin (王启斌, "Viktor Wang"), robotics industry veteran (CloudMinds, JD). Co-founder Dr. Chai Xiaojie. Chief Scientist Yang Yaodong (Peking University AI Institute). Joint PKU-PsiBot Embodied Dexterous Manipulation Lab. Products: Psi R0/R0.5/R1 VLA models, Psi Sim, Psi Data, PsiBot H1 dexterous hand, wheeled robot, teleop/exoskeleton. Released Psi-SynNet multimodal dataset. Legal entity: 上海灵初智能科技有限公司. Seed round Nov 13 2024. Later reports (2026) cite roughly 2 billion yuan cumulative financing including state-backed capital, but per-round sizes undisclosed. NOTE: some search results conflated this firm with "Lingqi Wanwu" (Zhu Qingxu, ex-Tencent Robotics X) — a different company; data here excludes that conflation.
business_model: Develops end-to-end reinforcement-learning VLA foundation models (Psi R-series) plus self-built dexterous-hand hardware and wheeled dual-arm robots (e.g. Psi V1), and monetizes by deploying these robotic systems into commercial operations workflows — initially selling/piloting automated picking, sorting and packing solutions for semi-structured logistics and retail clients, charging for the integrated robot+model deployments and large-scale data-collection services. Pre-revenue/early-commercial; currently funded primarily by venture capital (2 billion yuan raised across angel and Pre-A rounds).
customers: Undisclosed by name. The company states it has completed small-scale, real-world scenario verification in the warehouses of unnamed logistics clients (sorting/picking efficiency pilots), targeting semi-structured logistics and retail as its entry market. No named paying customers are publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (early commercial pilots only; raised ~RMB 2 billion total across angel + Pre-A rounds led by GL Ventures/高瓴 and Lanchi Ventures/蓝驰, plus state-backed capital. No revenue figure disclosed.)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on data efficiency via end-to-end reinforcement learning rather than pure imitation learning: its DexGraspVLA framework reportedly generalized to thousands of objects from ~2 hours of dexterous-grasp data — claimed ~250x more data-efficient than Figure AI (~0.4% of Figure's data) — and it builds its own 16-DOF dexterous hand. Versus US peer Physical Intelligence (π0/imitation-data-scaling approach) and Figure AI (humanoid + large-scale teleop data), PsiBot bets on RL-driven long-horizon dexterity and an open-source 1,000-hour human-hand manipulation dataset (Psi W0/R2). Differentiation is real but unproven at scale; key competitors include Physical Intelligence and Figure AI globally and Chinese embodied-AI peers (e.g. Galbot, Unitree).

also_known_as: Linker Robotics; Linker Hand; 灵心巧手 (Lingxin Qiaoshou); LinkerSkillNet
one_liner: Builds high-DoF dexterous robotic hands (6-42 DOF) paired with a cloud brain, using its LinkerSkillNet dataset (500+ skills) to train learned manipulation models.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (multiple rounds; multi-hundred-million RMB through Series B+; ~$3B valuation reached)
last_round_type: Series B+ / pre-IPO (Series B reached ~$3B valuation; next round targeting ~$6B)
notes: Beijing-based (Haidian district) dexterous-hand leader; founder/CEO Zhou Yong. Founding year 2019 per official company "About Us" page (灵心巧手成立于2019年); some secondary sources imply later, but official site and qcc/aiqicha registry corroborate 2019. World's only mass-producer of high-DoF dexterous hands, ~80%+ global market share, monthly orders 1,000+ units, scaling to ~10,000/month. Strategy: 'dexterous hands + cloud brain.' LinkerSkillNet = world's largest real-world dexterous manipulation dataset (500+ skills/behaviors); Open TeleDex is open teleoperation/data-capture architecture. Investors include Ant Group (Alibaba), HongShan, Zhongguancun Science Park Fund, Bank of China Asset Mgmt, Fosun Capital, CICC Capital. Series B reached ~$3B valuation; next raise targets ~$6B; exploring Hong Kong IPO 2026 (advisors CMBI, CITIC Securities, HSBC). IN SCOPE: hand + AI manipulation data/learning system; AI-native variable-item manipulation, not a pre-programmed industrial arm. Total funding and team size not publicly disclosed. Last round date ~2026-02 for Series B per Chinese press (valuation up 10x in 7 months).
business_model: Sells high-DoF dexterous robotic hands (LinkerHand O/L series, ~RMB 3,999–99,900 / ~USD 560–14,000 per unit) to humanoid robot makers, industrial integrators, and research labs, undercutting Western rivals on price; in-house vertical integration (motors, gear reducers, electric cylinders) drives margin. Layers on a cloud "brain" and LinkerSkillNet skill dataset (500+ skills) to upsell learned-manipulation software/full-stack dexterity platform.
customers: Named customers/partners include Samsung Electronics, Siemens, and Foxconn (industrial/humanoid), plus research deployments at Stanford, MIT, Cambridge, University of Hong Kong, and Tsinghua.
revenue_status: scaling (shipped 10,000th hand, monthly capacity >4,000 units, targeting 50,000–100,000 units next year; specific revenue figure not publicly disclosed; raised ~$150M+ and reportedly a larger Series B near $217M since April 2025, targeting ~$6B valuation - unverified)
technical_differentiation: Versus Shadow Robot (UK, ~$65k 24-movement research hand) Linkerbot offers comparable/higher DoF (6–42) at roughly 1/20th the cost with true mass production (>1,000 high-DoF hands/month, claimed ~80% global high-DoF share); versus China rival Wuji Tech (20-DoF Wuji Hand) it differentiates on DoF range plus the cloud brain and LinkerSkillNet 500+ skill dataset bundling learned manipulation rather than hardware alone.

one_liner: Builds the waterproof HoneyBadger quadruped and its motion-control stack to inspect sewers, tunnels and extreme underground environments using learned omnidirectional locomotion policies.
notes: Polish legged-robotics startup, first spin-off from Poznan University of Technology, founded ~2016-2017 (sources vary; LinkedIn/most list 2017) by Jakub Bartoszek, Lukasz Antczak and Jakub Matyszczak. Flagship product is the waterproof, rugged HoneyBadger quadruped (HoneyBadger 4.0 operates fully underwater) for industrial inspection of sewers, heat-district tunnels and extreme environments; also sells motion-control components (motor controllers, actuators, encoders). AI qualifier: on-robot reinforcement learning for locomotion, e.g. 'Gait in Eight: Efficient On-Robot Learning for Omnidirectional Quadruped Locomotion' (arXiv:2503.08375) demonstrated on the MAB HoneyBadger. Primary layer L4 AI-native hardware; secondary L6 inspection vertical. No disclosed VC rounds found (Crunchbase/PitchBook list it but without round data); company appears grant- and revenue-funded (research grants NCN/DFG/HMWK funded the Gait in Eight work, not the company). In scope: AI-native legged hardware whose locomotion depends on learned policies. RBR50 2025 recognized.
business_model: Three revenue streams: (1) selling/leasing the IP67 waterproof HoneyBadger quadruped to industrial inspection clients with billing by time/mileage, (2) performing underground inspections as a service and delivering reports, and (3) selling its in-house motion-control components (MD80 brushless motor controllers, MD-series actuators, CANdle bus) to other robotics builders and labs.
customers: Delivered jointed-spine HoneyBadger units to Poznan University of Technology (PUT) and TU Darmstadt; ran field inspections for water/sewage utilities Veolia and Aquanet, plus heat-distribution tunnel and sewer inspections in Poznan and Zdunska Wola, Poland.
revenue_status: early-revenue (raised ~$319K pre-seed per PitchBook/Crunchbase; revenue figure not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Unlike ANYbotics' ANYmal and Boston Dynamics' Spot, the HoneyBadger 4.0 is fully IP67 submersible and demonstrably walks underwater and through sewer pipes, and MAB designs its own actuators/motor controllers (MD80) in-house rather than sourcing them, giving it a low-cost, sealed platform tuned for confined extreme-underground inspection niches the larger players do not target.

also_known_as: Mangdang Robotics; Mangdang Technology Co., Limited; Mini Pupper
one_liner: Makes Mini Pupper, low-cost open-source ROS/ROS2 quadruped robot kits enabling students and researchers to build legged robots and run sim-to-real reinforcement-learning locomotion.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (crowdfunded: ~HK$3.9M Kickstarter for Mini Pupper 1 + ~HK$1.09M for Mini Pupper 2, roughly US$640K combined)
notes: AI-native legged dev platform (Layer 4 hardware). Founded 2020 in Hong Kong (Mangdang Technology Co., Limited, based at HKSTP per their directory listing); offices/team in China (Beijing) and Japan (Tokyo); founder Afreez Gan. Launched first ROS quadruped Mini Pupper in 2021, inspired by Stanford Pupper. Funded via crowdfunding rather than VC: Mini Pupper 1 Kickstarter raised ~HK$3.9M (915 backers, 2021); Mini Pupper 2 Kickstarter raised ~HK$1.09M (225 backers, 2022). No disclosed equity rounds. In scope: widely used as a low-cost sim-to-real RL/IL locomotion research and education platform; the product category (affordable open robot-learning quadruped) is AI-native. Team size not publicly disclosed (global distributed team). Last-round figures are approximate USD conversions of the HKD crowdfunding totals.
business_model: Sells low-cost open-source quadruped robot kits (Mini Pupper 1/2/Pro, ~$300-700) and components direct-to-consumer through its own Shopify store (mangdang.store), Amazon, and resellers like RobotShop, plus initial crowdfunding (Kickstarter/Indiegogo/Makuake). Primary buyers are STEM students, makers, and educational institutions; the software/designs are open-source, so revenue comes from hardware kits and spare parts rather than software licensing.
customers: Primarily individual makers, hobbyists, and K-12/university STEM programs rather than named enterprise accounts; no specific paying institutional customers are publicly named. Ecosystem ties include collaboration with Nathan Kau / Stanford Student Robotics (original Pupper creator) and community workshops run with ROS, Ubuntu, AWS, and IEEE. Named paying orgs: not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (Kickstarter campaigns for Mini Pupper 1 in 2021 and Mini Pupper 2 in 2022 were heavily oversubscribed with ~900+ backers; exact total revenue not publicly disclosed; sells via Amazon/RobotShop at ~$300-700 per kit)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on being the lowest-cost, mass-production-ready, fully open-source 12-DoF ROS/ROS2 quadruped kit with built-in AI/LLM (ChatGPT/Gemini/Claude) and SLAM/sim-to-real support, versus Stanford's original Pupper (a DIY reference design, not a productized kit) and Unitree's Go1/Go2 (far more capable but ~10x+ the price and closed/proprietary). Its edge is affordability and open-source education focus rather than performance.

one_liner: Pairs AI-driven dexterous robotic hands with off-the-shelf arms; trains physical-AI foundation models via imitation learning from human demonstrations captured by proprietary wearable data devices.
notes: ETH Zurich spinout (2024); CEO Stefan Weirich, thesis at ETH Soft Robotics Lab on tendon-driven biomimetic hands. Builds proprietary AI-driven dexterous robotic hands (sub-mm fingertip precision) attached to standard industrial arms rather than full humanoids. Trains frontier physical-AI foundation models via imitation learning from real human demonstrations collected with proprietary wearable devices on factory floors. $16M seed (Nov 2025) led by Elaia and Speedinvest, with Founderful, 1st Kind, 10X Founders, 2100 Ventures, Sequoia Scout Fund; prior ~$2.5M pre-seed (2024) plus non-dilutive Innosuisse funding bring total to over $20M. Primary layer L4 AI-native hardware (the hand); secondary L1 (foundation model), L3 (demonstration-data collection), L6 (manufacturing deployments). In scope: AI-native dexterous manipulation that would not exist without learned policies. Team ~25.
business_model: Sells AI-driven dexterous robotic-hand modules paired with off-the-shelf industrial arms, plus service contracts covering workflow analysis, tailored robotic-station installation/integration, maintenance, and scalability for added tasks over time; targets labor-intensive manufacturing, automotive, logistics, and retail tasks.
customers: Undisclosed by name; technology is being piloted with unnamed Fortune 500 manufacturers, global automotive brands, and multinational logistics providers across manufacturing, automotive, logistics, and retail.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no figure public) - running paid/unpaid pilots with top-tier manufacturers; founded 2024 as ETH Zurich spinout, raised ~$16M seed (Nov 2025) led by Elaia with Speedinvest, Founderful, Sequoia Scout, bringing total to ~$20M+. (unverified whether pilots are paid; could also be characterized pre-revenue)
technical_differentiation: Unlike Physical Intelligence (PI, software/VLA foundation models for any robot) and Figure (full humanoids), mimic ships a self-contained dexterous hand + tactile sensing + imitation-learning model that bolts onto standard arms, avoiding locomotion/full-body costs; differentiated also vs hand-makers like Shadow Robot by training physical-AI models on wearable on-the-job human demonstrations captured in live production rather than selling hardware alone.

also_known_as: NDT; Shenzhen New Degree Technology; 新度技术
one_liner: Maker of piezoresistive force/touch sensors; supplies tactile fingertip and skin sensing arrays for robot dexterous hands and humanoids, providing contact data for manipulation.
notes: Founded 2011 as a force-touch sensor joint venture; HQ Shenzhen with a San Jose design center. Origin is consumer electronics force-touch (ZTE Axon Mini 2015, Google Pixel 4; investor Xiaomi). Now positioned as a supplier of piezoresistive robot tactile/force sensors used for fingertip and skin sensing on dexterous hands and humanoids; appears in CMRA's overview of humanoid tactile-sensor companies. Layer 4 borderline component: NDT builds the sensing hardware, not learned policies or a foundation model, but tactile data is the input substrate for learned manipulation, so in_scope=true. Total funding ~$23.2M per Crunchbase; latest round labeled Series C (Series C-III subrounds), date approximate (March 2022 referenced); round size and lead not clearly disclosed. Team size and exact latest-round details not findable. Sources: company site en.newdegreetech.com; Crunchbase (shenzhen-new-degree-technology); CMRA humanoid tactile-sensor company overview (cnmra.com).
business_model: Sells piezoresistive MEMS force/touch sensor components and end-to-end "force touch" solutions to device and robot OEMs on a per-unit/design-win basis (over 100 million sensor sets shipped), spanning mobile phones, TWS earbuds, laptop trackpads, AR/VR, styluses, and automotive switches/center consoles; now extends the same piezoresistive tech into fingertip tactile and skin sensing arrays sold to robot dexterous-hand and humanoid makers.
