customers: Named brand customers (consumer/auto force-touch business) include Google, Lenovo, Microsoft, Asus, Huawei, Xiaomi, vivo, OPPO, Baidu, and Midea. Specific paying humanoid/dexterous-hand robotics customers are not publicly disclosed by name.
revenue_status: scaling (revenue figure not publicly disclosed; 100M+ sensor sets shipped across major consumer-electronics brands indicates established commercial-scale revenue)
technical_differentiation: Founded 2011 and claims to be first to commercialize "flexible MEMS" piezoresistive micro-strain force sensing (Wheatstone-bridge differential output) at mass scale, a proven high-volume consumer/automotive supply chain it now repurposes for robot tactile arrays; differs from capacitive/AI-haptic-chip players like Tashan Tech (mixed-signal AI haptic chip, electronic skin) and TacSense by using a mature, durable piezoresistive force-MEMS approach and an existing 100M+ unit manufacturing base rather than newer capacitive sensing.

one_liner: Builds NeuraGrasp, an AI-native gripper combining learned grasping intelligence, vision and a soft membrane end-effector so warehouse robots reliably pick high-variability items.
last_round_lead: BDC Capital Industrial Innovation Venture Fund
notes: North Vancouver, BC, Canada firm founded 2021. Flagship NeuraGrasp end-effector developed over ~5 years; combines AI grasping intelligence, computer vision, onboard sensing and a patented soft membrane gripper to adapt to item shape/texture/weight/porosity for high-variability warehouse picking (items up to ~5 lb). Raised $4.5M in 2025 led by BDC Capital's Industrial Innovation Venture Fund. Acquired by Locus Robotics in May 2026 (announced 2026-05-19); NeuraGrasp being integrated into Locus Array physical-AI platform; full Nexera team joined Locus, hence acqui-hired. Round date YYYY-MM precise month not confirmed (2025; reported as 2025); last_round_date is approximate. Total funding reflects only the disclosed $4.5M; earlier seed amounts undisclosed. In scope: AI-native learned variable-item manipulation gripper that would not exist as a pre-programmed product. Layer 4 AI-native hardware with L6 6a_warehouse vertical.
business_model: Sold/licensed its patented NeuraGrasp AI gripper (soft-membrane end-effector plus learned grasping intelligence and vision) as an end-effector to warehouse-automation and mobile-robot OEMs, working with automation providers worldwide to add high-variability SKU picking to their robots. Pre-acquisition it was a venture-funded startup (raised ~$4.5M in 2025 from BDC Capital, RiSC Capital, Supernode, Defined) monetizing via hardware/IP supply to integrators rather than at scale.
customers: Not publicly disclosed by name; the company stated NeuraGrasp was validated through "tens of millions of picks" and "broadest SKU testing with commercial partners," and it worked with automation providers worldwide, but no specific paying or piloting customer is named publicly. Locus Robotics became the de facto acquirer/end customer (acquisition closed ~May 13, 2026).
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified; ~$4.5M raised 2025, commercial-partner SKU testing underway but no disclosed revenue figure; effectively pre-scale at acqui-hire by Locus Robotics)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a patented soft-membrane end-effector that physically conforms to each item combined with learned (AI) grasp planning and onboard vision, letting a single gripper handle porous textiles, loose bagged items, perforated polybags and delicate/irregular SKUs that rigid or pure-suction grippers miss. Versus closest Layer-4 grasping competitors Ambi Robotics (AmbiSort, suction-based) and RightHand Robotics (RightPick, suction-cup gripper), Nexera's compliant membrane targets the long tail of hard-to-grasp SKUs those suction systems struggle with.

one_liner: Builds AI-powered robotic picking arms for warehouses that learn to grasp variable items, using vision-language-action models and operational data to handle pick, pack, and sort tasks.
notes: Warsaw, Poland-based warehouse robotics company founded 2017 by Kacper Nowicki, Marek Cygan, and Tristan d'Orgeval. AI 'brain' pairs robotic arms with grippers/tool-changer to learn picking of variable items (95%+ coverage). Uses physical AI and vision-language-action models trained on operational data — AI-native variable-item manipulation that improves with experience, so in_scope=true. Series A $22M (2022); $44M round Feb 2025 led by EBRD VC arm with Khosla Ventures, Almaz Capital, EIB venture debt; $10M (EUR 8.51M) Series B extension led by Cogito Capital Partners closed Feb 2026, bringing total to over $84M. Team size not publicly disclosed. Sources: therobotreport.com/nomagic-raises-44m, techcrunch.com 2025/02/26, theaiinsider.tech 2026/02/05, nomagic.ai.
business_model: Sells AI-powered robotic picking/packing/sorting cells primarily via a Robotics-as-a-Service (RaaS) model, where warehouse/3PL customers sign multi-year service-level agreements priced on business metrics (e.g. per-pick throughput) rather than buying hardware outright. Nomagic integrates its "AI Brain" software onto best-in-breed robot arms deployed into existing warehouse systems (including AutoStore setups).
customers: Named customers include ASOS (sortation), Komplett (Nordic e-commerce packing), Fiege and Arvato (3PL picking), Brack.ch/Brack.Alltron (picking + VLA systems), Cdiscount, and Fenix.
revenue_status: scaling (specific revenue not publicly disclosed; reported 220% growth in contracted ARR in 2024 with a target of ~200% in 2025; over $84M total funding raised)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via vision-language-action (VLA) models that let arms detect anomalies (lost items, disordered bins) and adapt autonomously to changing inventory, plus task-specific grippers (e.g. Shoebox Picker for unsealed shoeboxes). Vs RightHand Robotics, which relies on a fixed multi-modal suction+finger gripper, and Covariant (its closest VLA-style rival, now largely absorbed by Amazon in 2024), Nomagic competes on European deployment focus and a software-led RaaS approach layered onto third-party robot hardware.

also_known_as: ROHand; 傲意科技; OYMotion Technologies Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Builds ROHand dexterous five-finger robotic end-effectors with tactile force sensing and EMG/neural-signal decoding, serving as manipulation hands for humanoid and embodied-AI robots.
notes: Shanghai (Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park) company est. 2015; makes ROHand dexterous five-finger hands (e.g. ROH-AP002 with hybrid 3D/1D force sensing, 11 joints, 6 active DOF) used as end-effectors on humanoid/embodied-AI robots, plus non-invasive BCI and AI decoding of neural/EMG signals. CEO/founder Hualiang Ni. Funding history per Crunchbase: Series B ~CN¥99M (Jan 2023, Guangda Huitong et al.); most recent ~CN¥150M C1/Series C closed ~March 2026 led by CICC (first investment of CICC Brain Science Fund; CEC Capital advisor). Total funding ~$60.8M (Crunchbase); USD conversions for last round approximate. Borderline due to prosthetics/bionic heritage but kept in_scope=true: dexterous hand is an AI-native manipulation component used for learned manipulation and gesture-learning on embodied-AI/humanoid platforms; component maker, not its own foundation model (L4). Team size not found.
business_model: Sells ROHand dexterous five-finger robotic hands/end-effectors (e.g. ROH-A002 listed ~$12,999) plus integrated joint motors, EMG/neural-signal-decoded OHand bionic prosthetics, EEG devices and exoskeletons as hardware components to humanoid-robot makers, prosthetics/clinical buyers and research/education institutions; distributed via resellers (RCDrone, AIFITLAB) and direct sales.
customers: Not publicly disclosed as named accounts; buyers are humanoid robot developers, inspection-robot makers, intelligent bionic prosthetics users (global user base), and educational/research labs. Sold through distributors including RCDrone and AIFITLAB. No specific named paying customers found in public sources.
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercial products on sale via distributors; PitchBook reports ~$60.8M raised across 16 investors including Hangzhou Radical Energy Saving Technology, Zhejiang State-Owned Capital Operation, GDHT Capital, Heying Asset Management, Huafa Group; specific revenue figure not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via hybrid tactile force sensing (3D + 1D, 0.1N resolution) and in-house EMG/neural-signal decoding (BCI) heritage from its OHand prosthetic line, combined with high static grip (up to ~30kg) at ~540g and ±0.05mm repeatability. Vs Inspire Robotics (similar ±0.05mm, ~35Nm force-controlled hand) and Wuji/other Chinese hand makers, OYMotion's edge is its neural-decoding/EMG prosthetics background and combined tactile perception rather than raw DOF count.

one_liner: Builds open ROS-based humanoid and mobile-manipulator robots (TALOS, TIAGo, ARI, Kangaroo) as research and service platforms for deploying embodied-AI and learned policies.
notes: Founded 2004 in Barcelona by engineers led by CEO Francesco Ferro; first robot REEM-A (2005); TIAGo (2015), TALOS bipedal humanoid (2017), ARI (2019), Kangaroo legged robot. Part of PAL Technology Group (UAE-linked). Privately held; no disclosed VC rounds — funded via parent group and substantial EU grants (Horizon 2020, Eurostars). Team ~100 per company site (~150 per Tracxn Apr 2026). Offices in Barcelona, Toulouse, Bari. In scope: general-purpose research humanoid hardware that is AI-native as an embodied-AI deployment platform (open ROS for learned policies); L4 primary with L5 middleware secondary. AI-intensity is moderate — PAL supplies the platform, not its own foundation model.
business_model: Sells ROS-based humanoid and mobile-manipulator robots (TALOS, TIAGo, ARI, Kangaroo) plus the StockBot retail inventory robot directly to universities, R&D labs, and enterprises as research and service platforms, supplemented by custom engineering/integration services and EU-funded research-consortium contracts (e.g., CANOPIES, CORESENSE, MANOLO). Retail deployments like StockBot generate recurring revenue via multi-store, multi-country rollouts.
customers: Decathlon (global StockBot inventory-robot rollout across stores in ~15-40 markets); research customers include Technical University of Munich, KU Leuven, University of Freiburg, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, Tampere University, Aarhus University, CEA, Siemens, and Comau (often via EU research projects).
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified third-party estimates ~$5.6-7M annual revenue, 2026; bootstrapped/no disclosed VC funding, ~151 employees)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates as a European, fully open ROS/ROS2-native platform vendor with 20+ years of deployed research robots and a broad catalog (bipedal Kangaroo, full-size TALOS, mobile-manipulator TIAGo) spanning research and commercial service use, versus China's Unitree (mass-produced, low-cost, state-backed humanoids) and OpenAI-backed 1X Technologies (consumer/labor-focused EVE/NEO). PAL competes on openness, customizability, and research-platform breadth rather than scale or capital.

one_liner: Sells low-cost LeRobot-compatible robot arm kits (SO-ARM101 leader+follower, LeKiwi mobile manipulator, Aloha Mini) plus cloud training for teleoperation and imitation-learning data collection.
notes: US-based LeRobot-community hardware vendor selling low-cost open-source arm kits (SO-ARM101 leader+follower from $329, LeKiwi mobile manipulator from $699, Aloha Mini from $999) explicitly built for teleoperation and imitation-learning/RL data collection compatible with Hugging Face LeRobot. Also runs train.partabot.com, a cloud-native training pipeline letting users upload datasets to Hugging Face or use existing LeRobot datasets to train models (hence L3 data-infra secondary). In_scope=true: AI-native research arms for learned policies, would not exist without the imitation-learning/teleoperation paradigm. Corporate details thin: Shopify storefront shows "© 2026, PartaBot" and "United States" region; About page is a placeholder. No Crunchbase/LinkedIn/PitchBook profile found; founding year, HQ city, team size, and funding not publicly disclosed. Social: X @partabot_. Docs at partabot-1.gitbook.io. Company existence and product line verified via official site; founded_year unverifiable so left unknown.
business_model: Direct e-commerce sales of low-cost, LeRobot/Hugging Face-compatible robot arm hardware kits (SO-ARM101 leader+follower, LeKiwi mobile manipulator, Aloha Mini, Koch v1.0) sold assembled or as DIY kits via partabot.com and Amazon, plus an emerging cloud-native GPU training platform (train.partabot.com) for training/deploying imitation- and reinforcement-learning policies (ACT/diffusion) - likely intended as a paid SaaS/subscription, though pricing is not publicly disclosed.
customers: Hobbyists, makers, educators, students, and research/automation teams buying low-cost arm kits; PartaBot is also a named hardware partner in Hugging Face's SO-101 ecosystem alongside The Robot Studio, Seeed Studio, and WowRobo. No specific paying enterprise customers are publicly named - undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (hardware kit sales via own store and Amazon; cloud training product appears pre-launch/early; no revenue figures publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Competes directly with other SO-101/LeRobot hardware resellers Seeed Studio and WowRobo, plus The Robot Studio (the open-source design originator); the kits are essentially the same open-source SO-ARM101/LeKiwi reference designs, so hardware is largely undifferentiated - PartaBot's only would-be differentiator is its bundled cloud GPU training/one-click deploy pipeline (train.partabot.com), which is early-stage and unproven versus simply using Hugging Face's free LeRobot stack.

also_known_as: PaXini Tech; 帕西尼感知; PaXini Perception Technology (Shenzhen) Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Builds 6D multidimensional tactile sensors, dexterous hands, and TORA humanoid robots, fusing touch and vision into multimodal manipulation models for embodied AI robots.
last_round_lead: Huangpu River Capital, Kaitai Capital, CIM International Group (co-led)
notes: Shenzhen-based; founded 2021 by team from Waseda, Tsinghua, UIUC. Develops ITPU (Intelligent Tactile Processing Unit) 6D Hall-array tactile sensors, DexH series dexterous hands, and TORA-ONE humanoid (47-53 DoF, 2000+ tactile units). Backed by BYD (>100M yuan strategic, 2025) and JD.com (led ~1B yuan Series A, Aug 2025). Series B March 2026: >1B yuan (~$145M) co-led by Huangpu River Capital, Kaitai Capital, CIM International Group; valuation >10B yuan (~$1.4B). Weighing Hong Kong IPO (June 2026). Total funding approximate (Series A + B + BYD ~2B+ yuan). Team size not found.
business_model: Sells a vertically integrated "tactile infrastructure" stack to robotics/industrial customers: 6D ITPU multidimensional tactile sensors and electronic skin as components, DexH13 dexterous hands as subsystems, and TORA-ONE humanoid robots as full platforms, plus the embodied-data/multimodal manipulation models trained on them. Revenue comes from hardware sales (sensors, hands, robots) and full-stack deployment solutions to clients in automotive, warehousing/logistics, medical, and industrial manufacturing.
customers: BYD is a strategic investor and partner (over CNY 100M strategic investment), with TORA humanoid bodies and dexterous hands piloted in automobile manufacturing factories and large-scale logistics warehouses. Specific named paying end-customers beyond BYD-linked automotive/logistics pilots are largely undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (specific revenue figure not publicly disclosed; raised over CNY 1 billion Series B in March 2026, unverified total ~$145M per Crunchbase)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on a Hall-effect-based 6D multidimensional tactile array (ITPU) measuring up to 15 perception dimensions including 6-axis force, texture, and rebound, fused with in-finger AI vision, versus GelSight/DIGIT (camera-based optical tactile, higher fidelity but bulkier, sensor-only) and China rival Tashan (mixed-signal AI haptic chips/e-skin, also component-focused). PaXini's edge is owning the full stack from sensor to humanoid plus the manipulation models, where GelSight, DIGIT, and Tashan sell only sensing components.

one_liner: Builds purpose-built industrial humanoid robots (e.g. welding) for shipyards, steel and heavy manufacturing to address labor shortages and improve safety; HD Hyundai deployment partner.
notes: Founded 2024, Houston TX. Founders: Nic Radford (CEO, ex-Nauticus Robotics founder/CEO, 15 yrs NASA Dexterous Robotics Lab), Jerry Pratt (CTO, ex-Figure AI CTO), Jide Akinyode (COO, ex-Nauticus VP Eng). Raised oversubscribed $27M pre-seed in May 2025 co-led by Unity Growth and Tides Ventures (Mirae Asset, Invicta Growth, Fathom Fund, Embedded Ventures, Wave Function Ventures participating); earlier $10M pre-seed. By Dec 2025 reported ~$42M total pre-seed alongside unveiling of a welding humanoid. Signed agreement with HD Hyundai to deploy humanoids into shipyards (delivery expected within ~18 months); also a Louisiana steel-fabrication pilot. In scope: AI-native general-purpose humanoid maker despite hardware-first positioning. Primary layer L4 (AI-native hardware); also touches L6 vertical (manufacturing/shipbuilding). Team size not publicly disclosed. Total funding figure approximate ($27M named round; ~$42M cumulative pre-seed cited Dec 2025).
business_model: Builds purpose-built industrial humanoid robots for heavy tasks (welding, grinding, inspection, material handling) and plans to deploy them via a Robotics-as-a-Service (RaaS) model that eliminates large upfront capital costs for clients, primarily in shipyards, steel, and heavy manufacturing. Currently pre-commercial; first prototype targeted for late 2026 with field testing and commercial deployment in 2027.
customers: HD Hyundai (world's largest shipbuilder) is the signed flagship partner/customer for shipyard welding deployment. Strategic investor-partners include POSCO (via POSCO DX) for steel manufacturing. Additional first industrial customer reportedly close to being announced but not yet named.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (raised ~$27M oversubscribed pre-seed in May 2025, with reports of $42M total raised; no product revenue yet, prototype due late 2026)
technical_differentiation: Unlike general-purpose humanoid makers Figure AI and Tesla Optimus, Persona AI builds task-specific, ruggedized humanoids hardened for hazardous heavy-industry environments (high-heat welding, shipyards) rather than warehouse/automotive pick-and-place; founders Nic Radford (NASA Valkyrie) and Jerry Pratt (ex-Figure CTO) bring deep legged-robotics and industrial-deployment pedigree, and it co-engineers protective robot materials with Under Armour.

one_liner: Makes palm-sized open-source programmable quadruped robots (Bittle, Nybble) and the OpenCat framework, providing affordable legged hardware for robotics education and RL/robot-learning research.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (crowdfunded; ~$567k Kickstarter Oct 2020 plus prior Indiegogo)
team_size: unknown (small; founder Rongzhong Li, COO Kai Mai)
notes: Founded 2016 by Rongzhong Li (physics PhD) as the OpenCat open-source quadruped framework; commercialized as Nybble (robot cat, 2018) and Bittle (robot dog, 2020). Bittle raised $567k on Kickstarter Oct 2020; Nybble crowdfunded via Indiegogo earlier. No known venture rounds; bootstrapped/crowdfunded. Shipped 30k+ robots. HQ Pittsburgh, PA per Crunchbase. Assigned L4 AI-Native Hardware: affordable legged platform widely used for robot learning/RL (community-built OpenCat Gym sim-to-real RL environment). In-scope=true: an accessible legged-robot-learning hardware platform fitting the AI-native legged hardware / robot-learning-framework scope, not a pre-programmed industrial arm. Petoi itself is a hardware/education maker, not a foundation-model builder. Team size not publicly disclosed.
business_model: Sells palm-sized open-source programmable quadruped robot kits direct-to-consumer and via resellers (Bittle robot dog from ~$279, Nybble robot cat), plus accessories, sensor modules, and STEM curriculum, with volume discounts targeting K-12 schools, universities, and individual makers/researchers. Grew out of crowdfunding (Indiegogo 2018, $567K Kickstarter for Bittle in 2020) into ongoing hardware sales.
customers: Mostly K-12 schools, universities, and individual makers/educators. Named institutional users (research/education, not necessarily large contracts) include Carnegie Mellon University, Haverford College, Temple University, CU Boulder, and Bluefield State University; broader paying customer base is undisclosed (15,000+ robots shipped worldwide).
revenue_status: early-revenue (specific revenue not publicly disclosed; raised ~$567K on Kickstarter in 2020, 15,000+ units shipped)
technical_differentiation: Competes on extreme affordability and footprint vs Unitree (Go1/Go2, ~$2,700+) and Boston Dynamics Spot, offering a palm-sized ~$279 quadruped with the most-starred open-source OpenCat framework supporting Scratch-style blocks, Python, and Arduino C++. Differentiation is price-per-unit and open hackability for education rather than the high-performance locomotion/research payloads of Unitree and Boston Dynamics.

one_liner: Develops microstructured flexible tactile sensors with mass-production capability for humanoid robot e-skin and dexterous-hand fingertips, supplying tactile signals to robot integrators.
notes: Layer 4 AI-Native Hardware. Chinese tactile-sensor startup founded by Dr. Ma Daolin (PhD Peking University in theoretical/applied mechanics; postdoc on robotic manipulation and tactile perception at MIT MCube lab). Technology relies on research from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, with support from Huangpu Materials Research Institute (Guangzhou) — hence HQ inferred as Guangzhou. Makes novel microstructured flexible tactile sensors with decoupled, reliable, mass-producible design targeting humanoid robot e-skin and dexterous-hand fingertips; also consumer electronics, smart home, medical, IoT. Raised tens of millions of yuan angel round (exact size, date, lead investor not disclosed in sources). in_scope=true (borderline component): the sensor itself is a component, but it is purpose-built to supply the tactile signals that learned manipulation policies depend on — AI-native fingertip/e-skin sensing for embodied AI, not a pre-AI category. verification=incomplete because founded_year, exact funding amounts, round date, lead investor, and team size could not be confirmed with a sourced figure; company existence, founder, technology, and angel funding are corroborated. Caution: do not confuse with similarly named Chinese sensor firms 普晟传感 (gas sensors) or 赛感科技/Sensetics.
business_model: Sells microstructured piezoresistive flexible pressure/tactile sensors as both standard components and custom array systems, plus integrated detection systems (pressure-distribution mapping, gait/balance analysis, TCM pulse-diagnosis devices). For robotics specifically, it supplies tactile-sensor modules for humanoid dexterous-hand fingertips and e-skin; revenue comes from component sales and customized application/measurement systems across semiconductor, medical, industrial-inspection, wearable, and robotics customers.
customers: Company website lists 200+ customers including Chinese Academy of Sciences, Tsinghua University, Xiaomi, Huawei, BYD, and ByteDance, plus aerospace organizations; specific paying contract terms for humanoid-robot tactile modules are not publicly disclosed (claimed clients, unverified as to revenue split).
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue figure; commercial component/system sales to 200+ disclosed customers but no disclosed funding round or revenue numbers; founded July 2021, Guangzhou)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on surface-microstructure piezoresistive design (skin/fingerprint-mimicking microstructures changing electrode contact area) with claimed mass-production capability spanning materials-to-packaging, versus optical/camera-based tactile imaging competitor GelSight and capacitive 3D-force e-skin maker XELA Robotics; positioning is mass-producible thin-film flexible arrays rather than high-resolution single-fingertip imaging, though it competes with many similar Chinese e-skin entrants (e.g., Hongxin/福莱新材) largely on execution and cost.

one_liner: Warehouse piece-picking robot (PickoBot) using AI vision and multiple gripper types (vacuum, finger, patented adhesive) to autonomously pick and pack diverse e-commerce items.
notes: Founded 2022, HQ Petah Tikva, Israel. Founders: Kfir Nissim (CEO), Amir Shapiro (CTO), Elon Rimon (founder/advisor). $3.4M Seed round closed 2024-09-17, led by IL Ventures; investors include ZIM Ventures, Fusion VC, InNegev, Israel Innovation Authority. Team ~30. PickoBot is an autonomous pick-and-pack system targeting the final piece-picking step in logistics warehouses, addressing labor shortages. AI-native variable-item manipulation: vision + ML perception identifies items of varying size/weight/texture, AI-driven algorithm selects among vacuum/finger/patented adhesive grippers. In scope: category (general variable-item robotic piece-picking) would not exist without learned perception/grasp selection. Crunchbase lists HQ as Petah Tikva; some sources cite Tel Aviv region.
business_model: Sells/deploys its PickoBot autonomous pick-and-pack robotic stations to warehouse and logistics operators (e-commerce, apparel, retail, pharma, agriculture, spare parts) to replace scarce human pickers; pre-commercial, monetizing through hardware system sales/installations (and likely robotics-as-a-service in future) rather than a proven recurring contract base.
customers: Havivian Farm (one of Israel's largest organic farms) has an installed PickoBot line automating fresh-produce packing. Strategic investor ZIM Ventures (ZIM Integrated Shipping) signals a logistics channel/pilot partner. No other named paying customers publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified; pre-Series-A startup founded 2022, raised $3.4M seed Sep 2024 led by IL Ventures with ZIM Ventures, Fusion VC, InNegev, Israel Innovation Authority; at least one live installation at Havivian Farm — no revenue figure publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a multi-gripper end-effector combining vacuum, finger, and a patented adhesive gripper, with an AI vision/decision algorithm selecting the optimal grasp per item — versus RightHand Robotics (RightPick: compliant three-finger + suction, petabyte-scale execution data) and Covariant (the "Covariant Brain" reinforcement-learning foundation model on conventional grippers). Pickommerce's moat is the adhesive-gripper hardware breadth; competitors lead on far larger operational AI datasets.

one_liner: Builds multi-fingered anthropomorphic robotic hands (Mia Hand, IH2 Azzurra) sold as dexterous end-effectors for prosthetics, industry and manipulation/grasp-learning research.
notes: Spin-off of Scuola Superiore Sant'Anna BioRobotics Institute / ARTS Lab (Pisa), founded 2009; HQ Viale Rinaldo Piaggio 32, Pontedera (PI), Tuscany. Products: IH2 Azzurra (2011) and Mia Hand (developed 2016 in EU DeTOP project; Red Dot 2019, Compasso d'Oro 2022). April 2025 seed round led by RoboIT with Pariter Partners/Pariter Robotics and CDP Venture Capital SGR; amount undisclosed. Layer 4 AI-native hardware classification: company supplies dexterous-hand hardware components used by learned-manipulation/grasp-learning research; it does not itself develop a learned policy or foundation model. In scope=true per assignment and in-doubt rule despite myoelectric/prosthetic heritage — these hands are widely used as research end-effectors for RL/IL manipulation. Funding amounts not publicly disclosed; team size not findable. Other sources: IEEE Spectrum startup spotlight, Crunchbase, Dealroom, cdpventurecapital.it press release.
business_model: Sells anthropomorphic multi-fingered robotic hands as products across three segments: the Mia Hand myoelectric upper-limb prosthesis (medical device sold to amputees via O&P clinics/distributors), the Mia Hand Industrial/R&D and IH2 Azzurra units sold as dexterous end-effectors to research labs, universities and humanoid/automation companies, plus paid custom integration and third-party R&D services. Revenue comes from per-unit hardware sales and engineering/integration contracts.
customers: Research institutions and universities worldwide (the original IH2 Azzurra was marketed as a research kit to top-ranked labs), prosthetics/O&P clinics distributing the Mia Hand (e.g. Fourroux Prosthetics in the US references the Mia Hand), and humanoid robotics/industrial automation firms buying the hand as an end-effector. Specific named paying enterprise accounts beyond clinic adopters are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercial product sales since ~2011 R&D hands and 2016 prosthetics; no public revenue figure; closed an undisclosed-amount seed round ~April 2025 led by RoboIT with CDP Venture Capital and Pariter Partners to expand commercial network)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by a CE-marked, fully externally customizable myoelectric prosthetic hand (Mia Hand, Red Dot 2019, ~280ms closing time) plus a lightweight human-sized research hand with embedded actuation/sensing/control born from Sant'Anna BioRobotics IP - sitting between prosthetics leader Ottobock (bebionic/Michelangelo, larger but less open for research integration) and Shadow Robot Company (Dexterous Hand, far more DoF/costly, research-only). Prensilia's edge is a single dexterous-hand platform spanning prosthetics, industry and research at lower cost than Shadow and more open/integration-friendly than Ottobock.

one_liner: Builds the Ability Hand, a low-cost waterproof multi-finger touch-sensing robotic hand used as a dexterous prosthetic and as an end-effector for robot-learning and humanoid manipulation.
notes: Founded Nov 2015 by Aadeel Akhtar; originated at University of Illinois Research Park (EnterpriseWorks incubator), now HQ in San Diego, CA. Makes the Ability Hand, a multi-articulating, waterproof, touch-sensing (force feedback) bionic hand. Primary market is prosthetics, but the hand is increasingly adopted as a low-cost dexterous end-effector for robot-learning/manipulation research and humanoid integration, which is why it is in scope as L4 AI-native hardware (despite prosthetic origin; per in-doubt=true). Component maker, not its own foundation model. Funding mostly via NSF/NIH/DOD grants plus equity crowdfunding (StartEngine raise ~$4.1M closed early 2024); reported >$10M cumulative equity+grant funding by 2025. Crunchbase lists smaller equity-only figure (~$2.2M) excluding grants/crowdfunding. Appeared on Shark Tank S15 (2024), landed a $1M offer. Team size estimates range 21 to ~50 across sources; celebrated 10-year anniversary in 2025. Vertical subcategory left blank as primary L4 hardware classification. Sources: psyonic.io, cnbc.com, sdbj.com, crunchbase.com, startengine.com, tracxn.com.
business_model: Sells the Ability Hand via two channels: direct prosthetic device sales through clinics/prosthetists to amputees (bionic hands starting ~$10,000, Medicare/insurance-reimbursable, ~60% of revenue), and licensing/selling the hand as a dexterous end-effector to humanoid and industrial robotics companies and research labs.
customers: Prosthetics side: 100+ individual amputee users (Medicare/insurance reimbursed). Robotics/research side: integration partners include Apptronik (Apollo humanoid, demoed at a Mercedes-Benz facility), Universal Robots, and Phantom Neuro; research/tech interest from NASA and Meta.
revenue_status: early-revenue (lifetime sales ~$2M; ~$1M+ in 2022 with reported ~4x YoY growth; raised ~$6.9M total across VC, crowdfunding, and government grants, plus a $1M Shark Tank deal)
technical_differentiation: Unlike pure-play prosthetics makers (Ossur i-Limb / Touch Bionics, Open Bionics Hero Arm), PSYONIC offers a lower-cost, waterproof, multi-finger hand with onboard force/touch sensing and a compliant, durable build. Its dual-use positioning as both a Medicare-eligible prosthetic and a robot end-effector differentiates it from in-house humanoid hands (e.g., Tesla Optimus), which are not sold as standalone touch-sensing modules.

also_known_as: Pudu Tech, Shenzhen Pudu Technology, PuduTech, PUDU D5/D7
one_liner: Builds service and industrial robots, including the D5/D7 autonomous quadrupeds that use RL-based all-terrain locomotion and onboard high-performance compute for delivery, patrol, and inspection.
notes: Founded 2016, HKUST roots; HQ Shenzhen, China. Launched PUDU D5 Series (legged D5 and wheeled D5-W) industry-grade autonomous quadruped robots Dec 3, 2025 at iREX 2025, for complex/outdoor industrial ops (30kg payload, 14km range, maps up to 1M sq meters). The D7 is the related larger industrial quadruped line. April 2026: raised nearly $150M, valuation exceeds $1.5B, cumulative funding now exceeds $300M (round series/lead not disclosed; investors historically include Tencent, Sequoia China, Meituan, Puhua Capital). 2025 revenue up ~100% YoY; cleaning ~70% of revenue; 4,000+ industrial delivery units shipped in first year. Also unveiled PuduFM 1.0 foundation model and PuduAgent for embodied AI/humanoids. Assigned primary layer L4 AI-Native Hardware; vertical aspects (service/industrial robotics) noted as secondary. In scope: AI-native legged platforms with learned RL locomotion that would not function without learned policies. Total funding figures vary by source ($192M-$300M+) due to reporting dates; most recent company statement says >$300M cumulative. Team size not reliably found.
business_model: Sells commercial service robots as hardware units (plus ongoing software/support) to enterprises worldwide—commercial cleaning robots (~70% of revenue), cabinet-style food-delivery and flat-platform delivery robots for restaurants/hospitality, and newer industrial delivery robots and D5/D7 all-terrain quadrupeds. Has shipped ~120,000 units across 80+ countries.
customers: Named global retail/hospitality customers include Carrefour, Walmart, and EDEKA; industrial delivery robots surpassed 4,000 units shipped within one year of launch (specific industrial buyers undisclosed).
revenue_status: scaling (revenue grew ~100% YoY in 2025; absolute revenue figure not publicly disclosed; raised ~$150M in April 2026 at a >$1.5B valuation, >$300M cumulative funding)
technical_differentiation: Versus quadruped-focused rivals Unitree (which holds ~70% volume share, strong on low-cost research-grade hardware) and Boston Dynamics (Spot, premium leg-only stair-climbing prioritizing stability), Pudu differentiates with the D5 hybrid wheeled-leg design that switches locomotion modes fluidly at speed (claimed 1.5 m/s stair climbing) and a 360-degree fisheye + dual 192-line LiDAR perception stack. Its broader edge is a large installed commercial service-robot base and a unified embodied-AI "single brain" deployed across robot form factors rather than research/standalone quadrupeds.

one_liner: Builds quadruped (RBQ-3/RBQ-10), humanoid, and collaborative robot platforms for surveillance, defense, and industry, increasingly using learned locomotion controllers; KAIST HUBO-Lab spin-off, now Samsung-controlled.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (KOSDAQ-listed 2021; Samsung paid ~$181M for 35% controlling stake)
last_round_type: strategic acquisition (controlling stake by Samsung Electronics)
notes: KAIST HUBO-Lab spin-off founded 2011 in Daejeon, South Korea; IPO on KOSDAQ (ticker 277810) in Feb 2021. Samsung Electronics built its stake from 10.2% (Jan 2022) to 14.99% (Mar 2023), then exercised a call option to reach ~35% controlling interest, paying roughly $181M; transaction completed March 2025, making Rainbow Robotics a Samsung Electronics consolidated subsidiary. Product line: RBQ-3/RBQ-10 quadrupeds (surveillance, reconnaissance, counter-terrorism/defense), bipedal humanoid research robots, and RB-series collaborative arms (cobots). Assigned primary layer L4 (AI-native hardware): legged hardware maker that does NOT build its own robot foundation model. Scope: classic hardware-platform company, but legged locomotion is driven by learned controllers and the category (general-purpose legged robots) is AI-native, so in_scope=true. Team size from LinkedIn/Tracxn (51-200). Funding figures: pre-IPO totals reported vary ($8.73M per some trackers); as a listed company total private funding is undisclosed/not the relevant metric — most recent material capital event is Samsung's ~$181M controlling-stake acquisition. Sources: Wikipedia, Samsung Global Newsroom, TechCrunch (Dec 2024), KED Global, Crunchbase, company site rainbow-robotics.com.
business_model: Sells hardware platforms (RB-series collaborative robots, autonomous mobile manipulators, RBQ-3/RBQ-10 quadrupeds, and HUBO-derived humanoids) plus astronomy mounts and serving robots, on a per-unit/equipment basis to industrial, defense, and research buyers. Now a Samsung Electronics subsidiary (~35% stake), it increasingly supplies cobots for captive deployment in Samsung and LG factory automation programs.
customers: Samsung and LG internal factory automation (~27% of Q1 2026 revenue via captive orders); CJ Logistics (warehouse humanoid partnership). Earlier HUBO humanoid buyers include MIT, Google, and the U.S. Naval Research Laboratory. US cobot sales run through a Schaumburg, Illinois branch.
revenue_status: scaling (TTM revenue ~$24M as of Dec 2025 per PitchBook; +117% YoY growth in Q1 2026 driven by Samsung internal deployment)
technical_differentiation: Vertically integrated, designing core components (motors, controllers, harmonic reducers) in-house for cost/performance, with KAIST HUBO humanoid heritage and learned-locomotion quadrupeds, plus Samsung's AI/software and global sales backing. Vs Doosan Robotics (Korean cobot market leader, 10,000+ cobots sold across 40 countries, stronger export channel) and Hanwha/Neuromeka, Rainbow leans on in-house componentry and the Samsung captive-demand pipeline rather than installed-base scale.

also_known_as: Realbotix (Aria, Melody); formerly Tokens.com; Abyss Creations / RealDoll lineage
one_liner: Builds lifelike humanoid robots whose conversational AI plugs into user-selected third-party LLMs (ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama, DeepSeek, local models) for companionship, customer service, and entertainment.
notes: Layered corporate structure. Robotics roots: Abyss Creations / RealDoll, founded by Matt McMullen in San Marcos, California (1990s); Realbotix robotics brand and Harmony AI launched ~2017 (used as founding year for the robotics entity). The publicly traded parent, Realbotix Corp (TSXV: XBOT; OTCQB: XBOTF; FSE: 76M0.F), was created July 2024 when shell Tokens.com Corp (founded 2021, Toronto) acquired Abyss Creations and renamed itself. Listed parent HQ is Toronto, Canada; robotics ops and manufacturing remain in California (Las Vegas/Nevada also cited for some operations/deployments). Founded_year=2017 reflects robotics product lineage; could alternatively be 2024 (public entity) or 1990s (Abyss). Funding murky given reverse-merger/public-shell history; ~$5M post-IPO round (Oct 2025) per Tracxn; total private funding undisclosed. Team size ranges 5-23 across sources. Feb 2026: Realbotix Corp agreed to sell the Realbotix, LLC operating subsidiary to NASDAQ-listed Onconetix (ONCO) all-stock, expected to close 2H 2026, after which the humanoid business would trade on Nasdaq. SCOPE: qualifies as L4 AI-Native Hardware / external-model humanoid — open hardware humanoid platform explicitly designed to plug into third-party AI models rather than building its own foundation model. Category (interactive LLM-driven humanoid companion) is AI-native, in_scope=true. Motion is more animatronic than learned-policy.
business_model: Sells customizable lifelike humanoid robots directly to consumers and enterprises (priced ~$10K for a robotic bust to ~$175K for a full-bodied unit), plus recurring revenue from AI companion-app subscriptions and emerging enterprise deployments of robotic customer-service agents (e.g. Aria at Las Vegas ticket kiosks). Robots' conversational layer connects to user-chosen third-party LLMs (ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama, DeepSeek, local models).
customers: Largely individual consumers (companionship/adult market) plus early enterprise pilots; named deployment is Aria as a multilingual customer-service robot at Tix4Tonight ("Tix4") ticket kiosks in Las Vegas. Most other paying customers are undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (FY2025 ended Sept 30, 2025 revenue ~$2.0M, up 121% YoY; still operating at a loss; ~$6M cash reserves)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on highly realistic full-sized silicone humanoids with patented lifelike appearance/movement and proprietary lip-sync, paired with an open, LLM-agnostic architecture letting owners plug in any of ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama, DeepSeek or local models. Versus companion-robot peers like RealDoll/Abyss Creations (Harmony AI, more closed and intimacy-focused) and Engineered Arts (Ameca, research/expressive demos rather than consumer-purchasable humanoids), Realbotix competes on commercial productized realism plus model choice.

one_liner: Builds a low-cost general-purpose wheeled humanoid manipulator for warehouse and manufacturing labor, starting with teleoperation and demonstration data to develop autonomy over time.
notes: NY/Brooklyn Navy Yard-based startup founded 2022 by ex-Boston Dynamics (Stretch), Tesla, Oculus, ASML engineers. Builds a wheeled humanoid mobile manipulator (torso + two arms on mobile base, ~50 lb payload, 16+ hr battery), claiming ~2-3x lower BOM vs legged humanoids and far lower cost (cited ~$10k-$50k). Strategy is teleoperation/human-in-the-loop first to collect demonstration data and build toward autonomy, not a proprietary foundation model — fits Layer 4 AI-native hardware with L3 data-infra and L6 warehouse/manufacturing exposure. Funding: $7M led by Khosla Ventures, with co-founders of Dropbox and Cruise, Crossover VC, Invariantes Fund, Julian Capital, SNR. Round date not precisely confirmed (reported ~2024). Team ~19 (Built In NYC / CB Insights). In scope: general-purpose AI-native humanoid manipulator built around learned/teleop-bootstrapped policies — would not exist as a pre-programmed industrial arm.
business_model: Deploys low-cost (under $50K) wheeled humanoid manipulators into warehouse and fulfillment operations, monetizing via robot deployment/pilot agreements with logistics operators; robots start teleoperated (operators control them remotely as data-collection) and ramp to autonomy by learning from human demonstrations. Likely an RaaS/per-deployment model, though exact pricing structure is not publicly disclosed.
customers: GXO Logistics (world's largest pure-play contract logistics provider) is running live warehouse trials/pilots, co-developing use cases in an omni-channel fulfillment operation for an unnamed Fortune 100 retailer; second-gen unit also in selective pilots with Fortune 100 retailers.
revenue_status: early-revenue (pilot-stage; specific revenue not publicly disclosed. Raised ~$7M led by Khosla Ventures with Cruise and Dropbox co-founders)
technical_differentiation: Versus bipedal humanoid competitors Figure and 1X, Reflex deliberately uses a wheeled base instead of legs to cut manufacturing cost (under $50K, roughly 20x cheaper), improve stability and battery life, and prioritizes a teleoperation-first deployment (operators control robots from up to 3,000 miles away) that generates value and demonstration data before full autonomy is ready.

one_liner: Builds RightPick, a robotic piece-picking system pairing a compliant gripper with machine-learning grasp intelligence to autonomously pick highly variable SKUs in e-commerce/warehouse fulfillment.
last_round_lead: Safar Partners, Thomas H. Lee Partners, SoftBank Vision Fund 2
notes: Founded 2015 by a DARPA-challenge-winning team from Harvard Biorobotics Lab, Yale GRAB Lab, and MIT. HQ Somerville, MA (Greater Boston). RightPick is a hardware-software piece-picking platform: compliant end-effector + sensors + ML grasp intelligence for autonomous picking of variable SKUs. Total funding ~$125M across ~9 rounds (Crunchbase); most recent disclosed round is the $66M Series C announced 2022-02-23, led by Safar Partners, Thomas H. Lee Partners, and SoftBank Vision Fund 2, with Zebra, Epson, Global Brain, GV, F-Prime, Menlo, Matrix, Future Shape. In scope: AI-native learned grasping for variable-item manipulation; the product would not function without learned grasp policies. Team size not reliably findable. Founded year occasionally listed as 2014 elsewhere but official sources state 2015.
business_model: Sells the RightPick piece-picking workstation (hardware ~$150K including a compliant multi-modal gripper, vision system, and AI software) to e-commerce, pharmacy, grocery, and CPG warehouse operators plus material-handling system integrators, with recurring software licensing/subscription fees and pre/post-sales services like fleet management and exception handling. Integrates its gripper+AI "hand and brain" onto third-party robot arms (Universal Robots, Fanuc) rather than selling a full robot.
customers: Named customers include PALTAC Corporation (Japan's largest CPG wholesaler, multiple workstations at its RDC Saitama facility), apo.com Group, Apotea, and Staples; cited as early RightPick 4 customers.
revenue_status: early-revenue (CEO Yaro Tenzer cited ~$10M revenue expected in 2022; unverified since; raised $66M Series C in Feb 2022 at ~$240M valuation, $120M+ total, plus strategic investment from Rockwell Automation)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a multi-modal gripper combining three compliant fingers plus a central suction cup, with AI that decides in milliseconds which mode to use, widening the range of handleable SKUs, backed by a data flywheel of 1+ petabyte of execution data. Versus Berkshire Grey (full-stack turnkey systems for retail/grocery giants) and Covariant (the "Covariant Brain" foundation-model software layer), RightHand stays arm-agnostic and sells the gripper+intelligence module integrated onto standard industrial arms.

one_liner: Builds full-stack humanoid robots powered by embodied-AI generalist policies, enabling general-purpose machines for logistics, manufacturing, and service tasks across daily-life and industrial scenarios.
notes: Founded Aug 2023, incubated by Tsinghua University's Institute for Interdisciplinary Information Sciences; founder Chen Jianyu (Tsinghua assistant professor). Full-stack self-developed embodied-AI software + hardware ("industry's only full-stack" per company site). Funding history: Nov 2025 Series A+ ~RMB 1B (~$140M) led by Geely Capital with BAIC Capital, Alibaba, Haier Capital; March 2026 ~$143M strategic; May 2026 >$200M led by SF Group (also HSG, IDG Capital, Hillhouse, CICC Capital). Total funding reported ~$411M-$480M (figures vary; treat as approximate). Secured >RMB 500M commercial orders for 2025; robots deployed in 10+ logistics centers via China Post and SF Group; partners include Geely, Renault, TCL, Haier, Lenovo. Total/team-size figures not consistently sourced. Sources: theaiinsider.tech, prnewswire.com (RMB 1B Series A+), crunchbase.com/organization/robot-era, robotera.com.
business_model: Sells full-stack humanoid robots (STAR1 biped, wheeled service robots) plus standalone components like the XHAND1 12-DoF dexterous hand, both as deployed labor in logistics/industrial settings and as hardware/research platforms to robotics developers. Began thousand-unit deliveries in Q2 2026, monetizing via unit sales to logistics partners and component sales to robotics R&D labs.
customers: China Post and SF Group (SF Express, also a lead strategic investor) operate its robots across 10+ logistics centers; its dexterous-hand/hardware platforms are reportedly used for R&D by Nvidia, Apple, and Boston Dynamics. Expanding into automotive and electronics manufacturing.
revenue_status: early-revenue (scaling; began thousand-unit deliveries Q2 2026 with reported >300% period growth; ~$411M raised total, valuation surpassing RMB 10B / ~$1.4B; specific revenue figure not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: vs Unitree (its closest China full-stack rival) and Figure AI: RobotEra emphasizes full-stack software+hardware self-development with >95% of core components in-house, and a market-leading 12-DoF quasi-direct-drive dexterous hand (XHAND1) that exceeds Unitree's 3-finger Dex3-1, paired with Tsinghua-incubated embodied-AI generalist policies. Differentiation is real but narrowing, as Unitree matches its vertical integration and Figure leads on AI-dexterity for manufacturing.

one_liner: Builds general-purpose humanoid robots (Phoenix) powered by its Carbon AI control system, enabling human-like dexterous manipulation and reasoning to automate labor across industries.
last_round_lead: BDC Capital and InBC Investment Corp
notes: Founded 2018 in Vancouver by Geordie Rose, Suzanne Gildert, Olivia Norton, Ajay Agrawal. Flagship: Phoenix humanoid robot run by Carbon AI control system that mimics human-brain subsystems for perception, tactile sensing, and fine-dexterity hands (large-behavior-model-style control for manipulation) — fits Layer 1 Robot Foundation Models. Total funding ~$148.6M per Tracxn/CB Insights; includes $30M Canada Strategic Innovation Fund grant (Nov 2022) and $58.5M earlier round (Mar 2023, EDC/Bell/Magna/Verizon/Workday/Accenture). Most recent confirmed equity-type event: strategic financing from BDC Capital and InBC (Jul 2024). In April 2025 reported to be raising up to US$175M (in progress, unconfirmed close). A small ~$2.5M convertible note reported Jan 2025. Round size for the BDC/InBC financing not disclosed. Note: the "$400M from Bezos/OpenAI" Medium headline refers to a different company (Figure AI), not Sanctuary AI.
business_model: Sells/pilots general-purpose Phoenix humanoid robots powered by its Carbon AI control system to enterprises for dexterous manipulation tasks (automotive sub-assembly, electronics, logistics), currently via paid multi-unit pilot/deployment partnerships rather than volume hardware sales. Also monetizes IP/research collaborations (e.g., compute and model work with Microsoft via Azure).
customers: Magna International (multi-unit Phoenix pilot in automotive sub-assembly plants); Microsoft (strategic AI/Azure research alliance, 2024); robots tested across ~400 customer-defined tasks in ~15 industries. Strategic investors include Magna, Accenture, Bell, Verizon Ventures, Workday Ventures, and SE Health. Most paying enterprise customers remain undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (pilot-stage; no public revenue figure disclosed; cumulative funding ~$140M+ USD including ~CAD $30M Canadian Strategic Innovation Fund grant - unverified exact revenue)
technical_differentiation: Versus Tesla Optimus and Figure AI (electric/tendon-driven, locomotion- and low-cost-volume-focused), Sanctuary is dexterity-first: proprietary miniaturized hydraulic actuators giving 21-DoF hands for fine manipulation, plus Carbon AI's hybrid/explainable reasoning approach (vs Figure's Helix foundation model) targeting precision-manipulation niches rather than mass-production price points.

also_known_as: Seeed Technology Co., Ltd.; Seeed-Projects; reBot Arm
one_liner: Open-hardware maker producing low-cost AI robot arm kits (SO-ARM100/101, reBot Arm B601) and Jetson edge gear for teleoperation, imitation/RL learning and sim-to-real robot research.
notes: Primary layer L4 AI-native hardware. Seeed Studio (Shenzhen, founded 2008; founder Eric Pan) is an open-hardware/IoT electronics company. Robotics is a product line, hence core_or_division=division. Official LeRobot hardware partner selling SO-ARM100/101 low-cost AI arm kits and its own fully open-source reBot Arm B601 (6+1 DoF, <$1000, ROS1/ROS2/Pinocchio/LeRobot support), launched April 2026, explicitly marketed for teleoperation, imitation learning, RL, and sim-to-real on Jetson edge. In scope: these robot-learning research platforms are AI-native — they exist specifically to enable learned manipulation policies, not pre-programmed industrial automation. Funding: CB Insights/Tracxn report ~$15.71M total across 2 rounds, latest Series A Sept 2015 with IDG Capital (Shenzhen Capital Group also an investor); individual round size undisclosed. Crunchbase's $7.2B figure is a data error. Team size and exact HQ-vs-division headcount not separately findable. Founding year shows minor source discrepancy (PitchBook lists 2009) but 2008 is the consistent and official figure.
business_model: Sells low-cost open-hardware kits (SO-ARM100/101 and reBot Arm robot-arm kits, sensors, Grove modules) direct via its e-commerce store and Amazon, plus acts as an NVIDIA Elite Partner reselling and ODM-manufacturing Jetson-powered edge-AI devices (reComputer/carrier boards); also earns from contract manufacturing, fulfillment, and licensing/integration services for hardware customers.
customers: Primarily hobbyists, researchers, universities, and edge-AI/robotics developers buying kits direct; partners include NVIDIA (Elite Partner) and Hugging Face (co-distributor for the LeRobot SO-101 arm). Named enterprise/paying customers for the robot-arm line are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: scaling (~$47.1M estimated annual revenue per third-party Growjo data; unverified, not company-confirmed)
technical_differentiation: The SO-ARM design is itself open-source (originated by TheRobotStudio / Hugging Face LeRobot), so Seeed competes against other kit vendors selling the same arms (WowRobo, PartaBot) on supply-chain scale, low pricing (~$100-220 kits), and bundled NVIDIA Jetson edge-AI hardware/ODM integration rather than proprietary IP. Largely undifferentiated on the arm itself - competing on manufacturing execution, distribution, and Jetson ecosystem integration.

one_liner: Builds AI-native dexterous manipulation hardware — a 22-DoF tactile robotic hand (SharpaWave) and North humanoid — enabling learned in-hand manipulation policies for robot OEMs and humanoids.
notes: Singapore-HQ AI robotics company (manufacturing/R&D in Shanghai; business ops in Mountain View, CA). Founded by ex-Hesai Technology executives David Li (CEO), Xiang Shaoqing (CTO), Sun Kai (chief scientist). Products: SharpaWave (22-DoF tactile hand, >1,000 tactile pixels/fingertip, 0.02N sensitivity), Sharpa North humanoid, and CraftNet (Vision-Tactile-Language-Action manipulation model). NVIDIA selected Sharpa Wave hands for the Isaac GR00T reference humanoid (Unitree H2 Plus + Jetson Thor), available Oct 2026; CES 2026 Innovation Award honoree. Primary layer L4 hardware/component supplier but now building its own VTLA manipulation model (L1) — hence in_scope=true (AI-native dexterous manipulation hardware built for learned policies). FOUNDING YEAR DISCREPANCY: NVIDIA/company-aligned sources say 2024; kr-asia reports 2025 (company described as roughly a year old at CES 2026); used 2024 per majority/NVIDIA sourcing. FUNDING: reported RMB ~1.7B (~USD 238M) angel round (per 36Kr/press reports); round date and lead undisclosed; Qiming Venture Partners reportedly an investor. Sources: sharpa.com, prnewswire NVIDIA Isaac GR00T release, kr-asia, CES 2026 Innovation Awards, 36Kr.
business_model: Sells AI-native dexterous manipulation hardware as components and full systems: the SharpaWave 22-DoF tactile robotic hand (mass-produced since Dec 2025, priced ~USD $50,000/unit) sold to robotics OEMs, research labs and humanoid integrators, plus its North full-body humanoid. Hands are designed in to third-party platforms (e.g., supplying the five-fingered hands for NVIDIA's Isaac GR00T reference humanoid built on a Unitree H2 Plus body).
customers: NVIDIA (Isaac GR00T Reference Humanoid Robot integrates SharpaWave hands); downstream the GR00T platform has been signed up by research institutions including Stanford Robotics Center, ETH Zurich, UC San Diego, and Ai2 (Allen Institute for AI). Primary buyers are academic/frontier robotics labs and humanoid integrators rather than consumers.
revenue_status: early-revenue (SharpaWave entered mass production Dec 2025 at ~$50K/unit; raised ~RMB 1.7B / ~USD $238M angel round per third-party reports, unverified; unicorn valuation reported)
technical_differentiation: Versus Shadow Robot and Tesla Optimus hand efforts, Sharpa's SharpaWave packs 22 active DoF in human-hand size plus a proprietary Dynamic Tactile Array (miniature fingertip cameras + ~1,000 tactile pixels per fingertip sensing contact to 0.005 N), giving far higher tactile resolution and dexterity than typical 5-7 DoF grippers; its selection by NVIDIA for the first open Isaac GR00T humanoid is a strong differentiation signal vs competitors.

also_known_as: Qianxun Intelligence; Spirit Intelligence; 千寻智能（杭州）科技有限公司
one_liner: Builds general-purpose embodied foundation models (Spirit VLA) and force-controlled humanoid robots (Moz1) to give robots a learning "brain" for industrial and retail manipulation tasks.
notes: Founded Jan 2024 by Han Fengtao (CEO, ex-CTO Luoshi Robotics), Gao Yang (chief scientist, Tsinghua assistant professor, UC Berkeley PhD under Trevor Darrell), and Zheng Lingyin (COO). Self-developed Spirit v1/v1.5/v1.6 VLA embodied foundation model; v1.5 open-sourced and topped RoboChallenge real-world benchmark surpassing pi0.5; v1.6 topped RoboArena global leaderboard. Released full-body force-controlled humanoid Moz1 (26 DOF) June 2025. Investors across rounds include Prosperity7 Ventures (Aramco, Pre-A 528M RMB), JD.com (Pre-A+ ~600M RMB), Shunwei Capital and Yunfeng Capital. Reported ~5B RMB raised across four rounds within ~3 months in early-mid 2026; valuation >10B RMB. Commercial deployments at CATL battery lines and JD retail. USD figures approximate conversions of RMB amounts. Hangzhou HQ (Yuhang District), Beijing office. Last-round lead for the 1.5B RMB A+ not clearly named in sources.
business_model: Develops embodied foundation models (Spirit VLA) and force-controlled humanoid robots (Moz1/"Xiao Mo") and deploys them into industrial and retail operations, selling/leasing robot units plus the AI "brain" to manufacturers (e.g., battery PACK assembly lines) and retailers for task automation; revenue is robot deployment contracts and model-driven automation services rather than pure software licensing.
customers: CATL (Contemporary Amperex) — large-scale deployment of "Xiao Mo" robots on power-battery PACK production lines (99%+ wire-harness plugging success rate); JD.com — Moz robots deployed in JD MALL smart retail stores via a March 2025 strategic partnership. CATL, JD, Bosch are also strategic/industrial investors.
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercial deployments at CATL and JD live since late 2025; no public revenue figure. Heavily VC-funded: ~4.5-5 billion yuan / ~$600M+ raised across rounds through 2026, ~$280-290M in Feb 2026, valuation >10 billion yuan — unverified exact revenue)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via its in-house Spirit VLA base model (v1.5/v1.6), which topped North America's RoboArena/RoboChallenge real-world benchmarks, reportedly beating Physical Intelligence's Pi0.5 and NVIDIA Cosmos — positioning it as the first Chinese embodied model to rank #1; pairs this with full-body force-controlled hardware (Moz1, 26 DOF, ~1:1 load-to-weight) targeting fine-manipulation industrial tasks. Closest named competitors: Physical Intelligence (US, model-first, no proprietary humanoid hardware at scale) and NVIDIA (Cosmos/GR00T platform).

one_liner: Builds household robots (Memo) powered by ACT-1, a robot foundation model trained on human glove demonstrations for long-horizon home chores like dishwashing and laundry.
notes: Founded by Tony Zhao and Cheng Chi (action-chunking transformer / Diffusion Policy lineage). Emerged from stealth Nov 2025 with $35M seed (Benchmark, Conviction). March 2026 Series B: $165M at $1.15B valuation led by Coatue, with Tiger Global, Benchmark, Bain Capital Ventures. ACT-1 trained on ~10M chore episodes from 500+ homes via $200 gloves; combines long-horizon manipulation with map-conditioned navigation in single end-to-end model. Robot product is Memo (wheeled domestic robot). Founding year inferred as 2024 from "two years in" (March 2026); not explicitly stated.
business_model: Pre-commercial: plans to sell the Memo household robot direct-to-consumer (hardware sales), targeting a retail price of roughly $5,000-$10,000 at scale. Currently running a paid 2026 "Founding Family" beta (~50 numbered units with included direct support) ahead of broader consumer sales; no subscription model publicly confirmed.
customers: Undisclosed (no paying enterprise customers). Earliest users are ~50 consumer households selected for the 2026 "Founding Family" beta program; not yet generally available for purchase.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (product in beta, not yet for sale; raised ~$200M total including a $165M Series B at $1.15B valuation, March 2026)
technical_differentiation: ACT-1 foundation model trained on ~10M chore "episodes" from 500+ homes captured via a patented Skill Capture Glove (human, "zero robot data") rather than teleoperated robot demos, and a non-humanoid wheeled home-specific form factor focused on long-horizon chores. Contrasts with general-purpose humanoid players Figure AI (Helix model) and Tesla Optimus, which pursue bipedal humanoids aimed first at industrial/factory work and rely heavily on teleoperated robot data.

also_known_as: RIVR (rebranded Jan 2025); Swiss Mile (ETH Zurich spin-off)
one_liner: ETH Zurich spin-off building wheeled-legged quadruped robots that walk, drive, stand upright, and manipulate packages using learned RL locomotion and navigation policies for last-mile logistics.
notes: ETH Zurich Robotic Systems Lab (Marco Hutter) spin-off, formed April 2023, Zurich. Raised $22M seed Aug 2024 (announced 2024-08-28/29) co-led by Bezos Expeditions and HongShan, with Amazon Industrial Innovation Fund, Armada Investment, and existing investor Linear Capital; ~$100M valuation. Earlier CHF 3.5M round (~$3.5M) preceded the seed, hence total_funding ~$25.5M (approximate). Rebranded to RIVR in Jan 2025. Reportedly acquired by Amazon (per Deep Tech Nation Switzerland, 2025/2026) -> status acqui-hired; entity now operates as RIVR. Assigned L4 AI-native hardware (wheeled-legged platform) per task; tagged L6_6a as it targets last-mile/warehouse logistics. In scope: AI-native legged manipulation platform reliant on learned policies, not a conventional pre-programmed arm.
business_model: Before its acquisition, RIVR generated revenue by deploying its wheeled-legged delivery robots in paid commercial pilots/operations with last-mile logistics and food-delivery platforms (a robotics-as-a-service / fleet-deployment model rather than outright robot sales). It was venture-funded ($25M total raised) and was acquired by Amazon in March 2026 to integrate the tech into Amazon's doorstep delivery operations.
customers: Named piloting/commercial partners: Veho (U.S. parcel-delivery platform, Austin TX, May 2025), Just Eat Takeaway.com (food delivery, Zurich Oerlikon, Aug 2025), plus a Pittsburgh test deployment. Now wholly owned by Amazon (the ultimate "customer"/acquirer).
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercial pilots in 2025; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed. Acquired by Amazon March 2026 for undisclosed terms; last valuation ~$100M, Aug 2024)
technical_differentiation: RIVR's hybrid wheeled-legged quadruped (learned RL locomotion, ETH Zurich Robotic Systems Lab heritage) climbs stairs, curbs, and uneven terrain to reach the actual doorstep ("last-100-yards"), whereas sidewalk-bound competitors Starship Technologies and Serve Robotics are limited to flat surfaces and cannot handle stairs or porches. This terrain capability plus upright standing/package manipulation is its core moat.

also_known_as: Tashi Zhihang; Homestone Intelligence; 它石智航
one_liner: Builds general "super embodied intelligence" physical-world foundation model (VLTA multimodal, AWE engine) plus its own robot hardware and dexterous hands for work-capable robots.
notes: Founded 2025-02-05. Founders: Chen Yilun (CEO, ex-Huawei/Baidu autonomous driving) and Li Zhenyu (Chairman, ex-Baidu IDG head); core team from Huawei, Baidu, DJI. HQ Shanghai (also Beijing presence). Funding history: angel round ~$120M (2025-03, led by Lanchi/BlueRun, Qiming, Linear Capital, Hengxu, Hongtai) then Pre-A ~$455M (2026-04), the largest single round in China's embodied-intelligence history; Pre-A co-led by Hillhouse and Sequoia China with Meituan as cornerstone strategic investor, plus CICC, TCL Ventures, Chow Tai Fook, state funds. Products: AWE (AI World Engine) 3.0 general embodied foundation model, dexterous hand, and humanoid/robot bodies; open-sourced WIYH VLTA real-world multimodal dataset (opened Dec 2025). Total funding is approximate sum of disclosed rounds. Team size not publicly disclosed.
business_model: Full-stack embodied-AI play: develops its own VLTA foundation model (AWE / AI World Engine) plus humanoid/dexterous-hand hardware, intended to monetize by selling general-purpose robots and embodied-AI systems into precision industrial/flexible-manufacturing settings (e.g. sub-millimeter wire-harness assembly for automotive/electronics). Currently financed primarily by venture capital rather than product sales.
customers: Undisclosed - no named paying customers found; public materials cite industrial use-cases (automotive wire-harness assembly, flexible/textile manufacturing) and demos (A1 robot Guinness record, embroidery) rather than named buyers. Strategic/industrial investor Shoucheng Holdings participated in funding but is not confirmed as a deployment customer.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no disclosed product revenue; ~$575M total raised across a $120M+ angel round in Mar 2025 and a record $455M Pre-A round in Apr 2026 led by Hillhouse, Sequoia, Meituan - unverified that meaningful product sales exist yet)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from Chinese full-stack peers AgiBot (智元) and Unitree by building a VLTA (Vision-Language-Tactile-Action) architecture that adds tactile sensing as a first-class modality, backed by its proprietary "World In Your Hands" real-world tactile dataset and AWE 3.0 model targeting sub-millimeter dexterous manipulation - versus competitors' more vision-language-action (VLA) and locomotion-centric stacks. Differentiation is early-stage and demo-driven; commercial moat vs. Figure-style rivals is unproven.

also_known_as: 他山科技; 它山智能; Beijing Tashan Technology Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Builds AI tactile-sensing chips (mixed-signal SNN haptic chips) powering fingertip tactile sensors and electronic skins for humanoid robots, enabling force/texture perception.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (multiple rounds; A3+A4 Q3 2025 totaled several hundred million RMB)
last_round_size_usd: undisclosed (A3+A4 combined "several hundred million RMB", ~tens of millions USD)
last_round_type: Series A4 (A3 and A4 closed together)
last_round_lead: undisclosed (13 investors incl. CITIC Securities Investment, GF Xinde, 360 fund, Keli Sensing)
notes: Beijing Tashan Technology (他山科技 / aka 它山智能), founded 2017, HQ in Mentougou/Zhongguancun tech park, Beijing. Core team from Tsinghua University and University of Manchester; in 2019 co-founded with Manchester Univ. what it calls the world's first AI tactile-sensing lab. Developed "Ruby," described as the world's first mixed-signal (analog-digital hybrid) AI tactile chip and first productized SNN-based neuromorphic chip, on a proprietary R-SpiNNaker architecture. Products: AI tactile chips, fingertip tactile sensors, electronic skin, 3D-force/texture multimodal sensing modules for robotics, automotive (Mercedes-Benz, BMW supplier qualification; BYD mass-production), smart appliances. Claims ~80% shipment share in humanoid-robot tactile sensors, 100+ robot customers, 2024 revenue exceeding RMB 100M. ~170+ patents filed incl. ~40 core humanoid-robot patents. Funding history includes Softbank China Venture Capital (B round noted in 2024) and A3+A4 rounds in Q3 2025 (announced Nov 28 2025) totaling several hundred million RMB with 13 investors; no single lead disclosed. In scope: AI-native hardware component (neuromorphic tactile/haptic chip) that enables robot learning/embodied perception; the chip's SNN-based perception would not exist as a category without AI. Sources: pedaily.cn (pedaily/投资界), 36kr/Tencent News, Crunchbase, company site tashantec.com.
business_model: Sells AI tactile-sensing chips, fingertip tactile sensors, and electronic-skin modules (roughly 5 sensors per dexterous hand) to humanoid-robot and dexterous-hand makers, expanding into software/algorithm models and tactile data-collection/training services as add-ons. Revenue is per-unit hardware sales scaling with robot production volumes.
customers: Named Chinese dexterous-hand/robot makers including Yinshi (Yinsike) Robotics, Strongbrain Technology, Aoyi, Lingxin Qiaoshou, and Lingqiao Intelligent; claims ~70-80% global share of humanoid dexterous-hand fingertip tactile sensor shipments. Also serves automotive, appliance, medical, and smart-building sectors.
revenue_status: early-revenue (revenue figures not publicly disclosed; reports "tens of thousands" of tactile sensors shipped in 2025 and electronic-skin mass production since 2024; completed ~9 funding rounds including A3/A4)
technical_differentiation: Claims the world's first mixed-signal AI haptic chip and first SNN-based neuromorphic tactile chip, plus a TS-V vision-tactile fusion platform (0.01N force accuracy, 1mm resolution, 30+ material ID), positioning it as the only player owning chip + sensor + algorithm full stack. Versus competitors like Paxini (PaXini Tech) and Wuhan Linktouch/MIT-spinout Meta Robotics, its differentiation is the proprietary in-house tactile chip rather than off-the-shelf-sensor integration.

one_liner: Builds the Optimus humanoid robot using an end-to-end neural-net policy stack derived from its FSD self-driving system, learning manipulation and locomotion from human demonstrations and simulation.
notes: Optimus is a division of publicly-traded Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ: TSLA), not a standalone funded startup, so funding/round/team-size fields are N/A. Tesla incorporated July 2003 (Eberhard/Tarpenning); legal HQ moved from Palo Alto to Gigafactory Texas (Austin) Dec 2021. Optimus led by Ashok Elluswamy (VP AI Software, Autopilot/FSD) after Milan Kovac departed June 2025. Optimus reuses FSD camera-based perception and AI chips; motor-control policies trained end-to-end via imitation learning and RL in sim. Units deployed in Fremont/Giga Texas factories; mass production reported starting 2026. Qualifies for L1 (foundation-model policy stack) per assignment; L4 (humanoid hardware) and L6 (vertical robot deployment) as secondary.
business_model: Tesla intends to sell the Optimus humanoid robot as a hardware product priced at roughly $20,000-$30,000 per unit, initially deployed internally in its own factories to perform manufacturing tasks (parts sorting, material transport) and later sold to external industrial and consumer buyers; broader monetization is expected via fleet sales plus likely software/autonomy services layered on top.
customers: Currently only an internal customer: Tesla deploys Optimus units within its own Fremont/manufacturing facilities. No external paying customers are publicly disclosed; limited external sales are targeted for late 2026 onward (unverified).
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no external Optimus product revenue; only internal pilot deployment as of mid-2026 — Tesla's overall corporate revenue comes from vehicles/energy, not Optimus)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via reuse of its FSD self-driving data pipeline, end-to-end neural-net policy stack, and in-house Tesla SoC "bot brain," plus aggressive vertical integration targeting a sub-$30K price (vs Boston Dynamics Atlas at ~$150K-$320K). Versus Figure AI, Tesla relies on its own proprietary AI/data infrastructure rather than an OpenAI partnership, and bets on its automotive-scale manufacturing for cost/volume advantage.

one_liner: Builds anthropomorphic multi-joint robotic hands (Delto DG-5F: 20-DOF five-finger hand) for humanoids and AI-driven dexterous manipulation, enabling learned grasping of varied objects.
notes: South Korean dexterous-hand maker (founded 2019, HQ Posco Tower, Yeonsu-gu, Incheon; R&D center in Gwangmyeong, Gyeonggi-do). Flagship Delto DG-5F is a 20-DOF five-finger hand (4 joints per finger, direct-drive), ~20cm, ~1.4kg, up to 7kg grip; newer DG-5F-S uses Tesollo's own actuator. Marketed for humanoid/AI manipulation; partnership with Techman Robot and linked to Samsung humanoid efforts (Samsung Ventures is an investor). Layer 4 AI-Native Hardware: anthropomorphic hand designed as a component for learned dexterous manipulation; does NOT build its own foundation model. In scope: this five-finger dexterous hand category is enabling for learned manipulation policies, not a traditional pre-programmed industrial gripper. Funding: CB Insights/PitchBook report total raised ~$8.97M across rounds (investors include Samsung Ventures, KB Investment, POSCO Capital, BeHigh Investment, The Invention LAB); Crunchbase lists a lower total (~$4.36M) and a round closing 2025-03 led/participated by BeHigh Investment — totals and last-round lead/size are partially conflicting across trackers and the exact size is undisclosed. Team size not findable.
business_model: Sells anthropomorphic robotic hands and multi-joint grippers as hardware products (flagship Delto DG-5F, a 20-DOF five-finger hand, plus a 15-DOF research variant and the 3-finger DG-3F), priced per unit to humanoid builders, automakers, and research labs, increasingly via regional distribution partners across the US, Japan, Europe, and Asia. Also pursues co-developed automation solutions (e.g., with Techman Robot) for high-mix, low-volume manufacturing.
customers: Tesollo cites Samsung, LG Electronics, and Hyundai as Korean customers; validated its gripper at Kia Motors' manufacturing site; and partnered with Taiwan's Techman Robot Inc. to launch a humanoid/collaborative automation product. Backed strategically by Samsung Ventures and POSCO Venture Capital.
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercializing DG-5F hardware; raised $4.2M Series A in March 2025; specific revenue figures not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Versus Wonik Robotics (Allegro Hand) and Shadow Robot Company (Shadow Dexterous Hand), Tesollo differentiates the DG-5F on a fully integrated, lightweight/compact 20-DOF five-finger design with direct force output and lower cost/size barriers aimed at humanoid integration, rather than the larger, more research-oriented or tendon-driven incumbent hands.

one_liner: Designs low-cost open-source biomimetic robots, notably the SO-ARM100/SO-101 leader-follower arms (with Hugging Face LeRobot) built for imitation/RL learning and teleoperation data collection.
notes: Founded 2006 by Rob Knight (CEO, robot hardware director; Robot Wars/BattleBots veteran). HQ in France: company site lists Bel Air Camp, 11 Avenue du Bel Air, 69100 (Lyon metro area, Auvergne-Rhone-Alpes); some directories list Divonne-les-Bains/Ferney-Voltaire. Long history of open-source biomimetic/humanoid hardware (CRONOS, ECCE, ROSA, DexHand, Robot Nano Hand). Original designer of the SO-ARM100 (SO-100) and SO-101 low-cost 3D-printable open-source arms, developed with Hugging Face for the LeRobot framework; SO-ARM-class is the de facto reference platform for affordable imitation/RL and teleoperation data collection. SO-101 added leader-follow capability explicitly for reinforcement learning. In scope: AI-native learning hardware that would not exist without learned-policy robotics; the hardware exists to enable robot learning rather than pre-programmed automation. No external venture funding found (Crunchbase/Tracxn list none); appears bootstrapped. Team size and exact funding not publicly disclosed. Crunchbase page returned 403 (not directly readable); details corroborated by Tracxn and company site.
business_model: A small French design studio (founder Rob Knight) that open-sources its biomimetic robot designs (SO-ARM100/SO-101, HOPE-Jr humanoid, DexHand) and monetizes via custom robot development/engineering contracts, plus paid tutorials, support, and design customization; it does not mass-manufacture itself but co-designs hardware that licensed partners (Seeed Studio, WowRobo, PartaBot) sell as $100+ kits, with revenue flowing largely to those resellers rather than to the studio directly.
customers: Primary collaborator/customer is Hugging Face (LeRobot), with the SO-ARM/HOPE-Jr designs adopted into its open ecosystem; partners include Seeed Studio, WowRobo, and PartaBot (who manufacture/sell the kits), plus NVIDIA, Maxon Motors, Science Museum London (ROSA exhibit), Silicon Highway, and Codethink. Named paying clients for custom work are otherwise undisclosed.
revenue_status: undisclosed (no public figures; bootstrapped small studio, no disclosed VC funding; revenue is from custom-build/consulting contracts and a share of partner kit sales — likely early-revenue, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on biomimetic, tendon/compliant-spring mechanical design and radically low-cost 3D-printable open hardware (the SO-101 leader-follower arm builds for ~$100), versus closest open-hardware peers Trossen Robotics (ALOHA/ViperX, far costlier proprietary-leaning arms) and Hugging Face's own LeRobot stack — though TRS is effectively the upstream hardware designer feeding LeRobot rather than a direct competitor to it.

one_liner: Builds ultra-thin electronic skin, thinner than human skin, that wraps robots to sense pressure, shear and slip, enabling dexterous teleoperated manipulation in healthcare and hazardous settings.
notes: Edinburgh deep-tech startup (The National Robotarium / formerly Higgs Centre for Innovation). Founder/CEO Dr Zaki Hussein. Develops retrofittable ultra-thin e-skin (thinner than human skin) for hard/soft robots detecting pressure, location, direction, shear and slip in real time. Full-stack: e-skin hardware + software to interpret tactile data + integration. Applications: telerobotics in extreme/nuclear environments (Sellafield), grocery grasping, and healthcare — piloted e-skin on PAL Robotics TIAGo robot for hospital tasks. Funding: £3.5M (~$4.8M) Seed Feb 2022 led by Octopus Ventures with Creator Fund and Techstart Ventures; total funding estimated ~$7.5M including earlier grants/pre-seed (exact total undisclosed). Status active. In_scope=true: AI-native tactile sensor suite enabling teleoperation/dexterous manipulation — a sensing component for robot-learning/teleop stacks rather than a pre-programmed industrial arm; the e-skin sensory feedback is core to learned/teleoperated dexterity. Note: ai_intensity is low/unclear because Touchlab itself is a sensor/hardware layer (L4) maker, not a learned-policy builder; flagged honestly.
