Founding year 2018 confirmed by The Robot Report and Crunchbase. Sources: therobotreport.com, tech.eu, digit.fyi, crunchbase.com/organization/touchlab, pal-robotics.com blog.
business_model: Sells ultra-thin robotic e-skin and tactile fingertip sensors as hardware components/integration solutions to robotics makers and research labs (B2B, including a product shop and HaptX-glove-compatible robot fingertips), and earns revenue from funded pilot/R&D contracts (e.g. nuclear, healthcare teleoperation) and government/EU grants (ARIA, FinEst Twins). Transitioning toward volume manufacturing of robot fingertips for the humanoid/dexterous-manipulation market.
customers: Sellafield Ltd (nuclear decommissioning); City of Helsinki / Laakso Hospital with Forum Virium Helsinki (6-month stroke-ward teleoperation pilot). Development/integration partners include Honda, Shadow Robot Company, NVIDIA, and Prensilia; funded by UK ARIA grant.
revenue_status: early-revenue (raised $4.8M/£3.5M seed led by Octopus Ventures in 2022; preparing Series A; revenue from pilots, hardware sales, and grants but no figures publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on an e-skin thinner than human skin that retrofits onto existing robots/grippers without reducing degrees of freedom while sensing pressure, shear and slip, versus competitors GelSight (camera-based optical tactile sensing, bulkier) and Shadow Robot/SynTouch BioTac (fingertip-localized rather than full-body conformable skin). Touchlab claims advantage in dynamic range and large-area conformability.

one_liner: Builds research-grade leader-follower teleoperation arms (WidowX/ViperX, official ALOHA kits, Trossen AI) so AI labs can collect imitation-learning demonstration data and train VLA robot-learning policies.
notes: Founded 2005 by Matt Trossen; HQ Downers Grove, IL (US). Interbotix is its research-arm brand. Commercial hardware maker of WidowX/ViperX/PincherX manipulators and the official Stationary/Mobile ALOHA bimanual teleoperation kits used in Stanford ALOHA and Open X-Embodiment robot-learning research. New Trossen AI / WidowX AI line ("Aloha Evolved") is built expressly for ML/VLA training and imitation-learning data collection, sold to AI labs; also offers a Data Collection SDK and Trossen MCP Server. In_scope=true: leader-follower teleop arms for learned-policy demonstration data collection are AI-native (category exists because of robot learning); secondary L3 data infra applies via its data-collection SDK. Not building its own foundation model — pure hardware/component vendor (Layer 4 AI-Native Hardware). No external funding rounds found on Crunchbase; appears bootstrapped (Latka cited ~$3.1M revenue, third-party estimate). Team size: 10 (2022), ~28-30 (2025-2026).
business_model: Sells research-grade robotic manipulators and imitation-learning data-collection hardware directly to labs: X-Series arms (WidowX/ViperX), official Stationary and Mobile ALOHA leader-follower teleoperation kits, Trossen AI workstations, plus parts and support. Revenue is per-unit hardware sales (single arms ~$3K, full bimanual ALOHA kits $30K-$50K+) to academic and corporate buyers, not contracts or SaaS.
customers: Sold to most major US universities and research institutions plus Fortune 100 tech firms; named users of its platforms include Stanford (IRIS lab, origin of ALOHA), Google DeepMind, UC Berkeley, and Carnegie Mellon, with collaboration cited with Berkeley AI Robotics on ALOHA Unleashed/DemoStart.
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified third-party estimates ~$1M-$3.1M annual revenue; bootstrapped/unfunded, founded 2005 by Matt Trossen, no disclosed VC funding per Crunchbase)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated as the official commercial supplier of turnkey, pre-assembled, calibrated ALOHA leader-follower kits using durable Dynamixel-based X-Series arms with full software/docs support - versus Hugging Face LeRobot's SO-100/SO-101 (~$2.5K, open-source but DIY 3D-printed and self-assembled) and GELLO; it competes on reliability and out-of-the-box ALOHA compatibility rather than lowest price.

one_liner: Builds humanoid robots (Walker S series) with embodied AI for industrial task execution, plus consumer/education service robots, addressing labor shortages in manufacturing and logistics.
notes: Founded March 2012 by Zhou Jian in Shenzhen. First Walker humanoid unveiled at CES 2018; Walker S/S1 industrial humanoids deployed in factory trials (e.g. Audi China, automotive plants). IPO on HKEX (9880) Dec 29, 2023, raising ~HK$1B (~US$130M) at HK$90/share, ~HK$38B market cap; Dec 2023 valuation ~US$4.8B (CB Insights). Pre-IPO total funding ~US$1.05B from investors including Tencent, CDH Investments, Charoen Pokphand Group. Team size ~334 per CB Insights (Feb 2026). Primary layer L1 (embodied AI models for Walker task execution); also major hardware (L4) and vertical industrial/service deployments (L6). Funding figures are approximate; IPO is the most recent capital event.
business_model: Sells Walker S-series industrial humanoid robots to automakers and electronics manufacturers for factory tasks (material handling, sorting, quality inspection), increasingly on bulk multi-unit orders; also sells consumer/education service robots (e.g. AlphaMini) and AI education solutions to schools. Full-size humanoid robots are now its largest segment at ~41% of revenue.
customers: Named automotive/industrial customers piloting and deploying Walker S robots: BYD, Geely, FAW-Volkswagen, Dongfeng Liuzhou Motor, NIO, and Foxconn. Walker series orders exceed RMB 800M (~US$112M); Walker S2 began mass delivery in late 2025 with 1,079 full-size units delivered in 2025.
revenue_status: scaling (FY2025 total revenue RMB 2.0B / ~US$291M, up 53% YoY; full-size humanoid segment RMB 820M, up 2,203% YoY; still loss-making with net loss RMB 703M)
technical_differentiation: Unlike Tesla Optimus (still in testing) and Figure AI (limited BMW pilots), UBTECH's Walker S is already deployed at scale on real factory lines and is the first to exceed 1,000 annual unit deliveries; differentiators include higher DOF (~41), autonomous battery swapping for 24/7 operation, and its BrainNet multi-robot swarm coordination framework. Closest named competitors: Tesla Optimus and Figure AI.

one_liner: Builds AI packaging robots for ecommerce fulfillment centers, trained by example via teleoperation data instead of rigid programming, enabling fast deployment and adaptation to variable items.
notes: YC Summer 2024 (S24) batch. Founded by 3x repeat founders (prior: Voodoo Manufacturing W17, Layer By Layer S13): Jon Miller Schwartz (CEO), Max Friefeld, Chetan Parthiban (Chief Scientist), Oliver Ortlieb. Robots use neural nets trained on teleoperation RGB-camera data outputting joint/gripper positions ~10Hz - learned manipulation policies, fitting Layer 1 Robot Foundation Models. Already deployed robots generating revenue. Funding ~$500K pre-seed led by Y Combinator with Pioneer Fund per secondary sources; round figure not on primary YC page (treat as approximate). Also exhibits L4 hardware and L6 vertical (warehouse/ecommerce packaging) characteristics.
business_model: Sells access to its OP1 ("Operator") AI packing robot to e-commerce fulfillment centers on a Robotics-as-a-Service basis: a 3-month trial then scale-up, priced to deliver ~30-40% monthly cost savings per robot vs. human labor (effectively a per-robot/per-month subscription rather than capital sale). Robots drop into existing workstations with no fixed integration.
customers: Not publicly disclosed by name. Ultra states robots are deployed and "generating revenue" in U.S. fulfillment/warehouse customer sites (reporting 96.4% packing autonomy at customer locations), but no named clients appear in public sources as of mid-2026.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no figure disclosed; company says robots are live and generating revenue in U.S. warehouses; raised ~$3.75M total incl. $500K YC-led pre-seed, S2024 batch - funding figure unverified across sources)
technical_differentiation: Trains robots by example from teleoperation/RGB-camera data via neural nets outputting joint/gripper positions at 10Hz, focused narrowly on the packing/kitting/returns step (poly bags, mailers, boxes next) rather than full pick-to-pack systems. Differs from Dexterity AI and Ambi Robotics (AmbiSort/AmbiStack), which target broader heavily-engineered sortation/induction cells; Ultra competes on zero-integration plug-and-play deployment and learned (vs. programmed) manipulation.

also_known_as: Unitree, Unitree Technology, Hangzhou Yushu Technology
one_liner: Builds affordable quadruped and humanoid robots (Go2, B2, H1, G1) plus learned locomotion/manipulation policies, making advanced legged robotics accessible for research, industry, and consumers.
last_round_lead: China Mobile, Tencent, Alibaba, Ant Group, Geely Capital, Jinqiu Capital, HongShan
notes: Founded 2016 by Wang Xingxing. Series C ~RMB 700M (~$97.4M) closed June 2025 at ~$1.4-1.7B valuation; jointly led by China Mobile, Tencent, Alibaba, Ant Group, Geely Capital, Jinqiu Capital, HongShan. Total ~$252M across 6 rounds (Tracxn). Became unicorn 2025; preparing STAR Market IPO targeting ~$7B (2026 Q2). Primary business is legged robot hardware (quadrupeds Go2/B2, humanoids H1/G1); also develops learned RL policies and a humanoid foundation/world model effort, qualifying it for L1. Team size not reliably found. Sources: TechNode, 36Kr, TheAIInsider, Caproasia, Tracxn.
business_model: Direct hardware sales of quadruped robots (Go2/B2), humanoid robots (G1/R1/H2), and self-developed components (motors, reducers, dexterous hands, LiDAR) across research, industrial inspection, and consumer/demonstration segments. Sells at aggressively low prices (R1 ~$5,900, G1 ~$13.5K-27K) enabled by vertically integrated in-house core components, achieving ~60% gross margins.
customers: Quadrupeds used for inspection by State Grid, China Southern Power Grid, PetroChina, Sinopec, and Baowu Group. Humanoid sales skew ~74% to universities/research institutions; ~17% to commercial/consumer buyers using them as demonstration units in retail, tourism, and exhibitions.
revenue_status: profitable (FY2025 revenue ~1.71B yuan / ~$250M, up 335% YoY; adjusted net profit ~600M yuan / ~$90M, first profitable year; filed for ~$610M Shanghai STAR Market IPO at ~42B yuan / ~$6B valuation)
technical_differentiation: Competes on radical cost leadership versus Boston Dynamics (Atlas/Spot, ~$150K) and UBTech (Walker, ~$40K): Unitree delivers ~70-85% of capability at 50-95% lower price via in-house motors/reducers, plus an open SDK and ROS2 compatibility that make it the default research platform. World's top humanoid seller in 2025 (~5,500 units). Trails Boston Dynamics on raw locomotion reliability and advanced AI partnerships.

also_known_as: VISEVI; ViseGrip; ViseTac; RoVi Robot Vision
one_liner: TUM spinout building the first fully camera-based tactile and force/torque sensing for grippers, deriving rich low-cost tactile data from deformation images for learning-based contact-rich manipulation.
notes: Spin-off from TU Munich's EXIST RoVi Robot Vision research transfer center; patented camera-based sensing replaces hardware joint/force/tactile sensors with cameras + image processing on passive deformable elements. Products: ViseGrip (gripper with stereo vision + ViseTac tactile), ViseTac camera-based tactile sensor, intelligent bin picking, sensitive grasping of variable objects. Founders: Nicolas Alt (CEO), Stefan Lochbrunner, Clemens Schuwerk, Gerd Denninger. Seed-stage; total funding amount and round date/lead not publicly disclosed (one round/one investor per Tracxn; Munich Impact Award noted). Team listed 1-10 (Munich Startup lists 1 employee). In-scope: AI-native low-cost tactile data subsystem enabling learned contact-rich manipulation. 6h_other (cross-vertical end-effector/sensing supplier). Sources: visevi.com, munich-startup.de, startbase.com, tracxn.com, cbinsights.com.
business_model: Sells integrated camera-based tactile/force-torque sensing hardware-software products for robotic grippers: ViseTac sensor finger pads, the ViseGrip intelligent gripping system (gripper + stereo vision + tactile sensing), and VisePick/VisePick eCommerce pick-and-place systems, priced per-deployment ("price depends on requirements"). Revenue is from selling these systems/sensors to industrial automation, logistics and e-commerce integrators/end-users.
customers: Not publicly disclosed; no named paying customers are listed on the company site or in third-party databases (Tracxn, CB Insights, Munich Startup). Public funders/affiliations include the EXIST/EU/BMWi grant programs, WERK1, EIT Health and the Munich Impact Award, but these are backers, not customers. Stated target markets are logistics, e-commerce, and later food/farming.
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified; revenue figures not public). TUM spinout (founded ~2019) selling productized grippers/sensors but with no disclosed financials or named customer contracts.
technical_differentiation: Uses a single camera observing the deformation of a passive rubber-foam pad to derive both rich tactile data AND force/torque across the entire large gripper-finger surface at low cost, vs GelSight Inc. (MIT spinout) and Meta's DIGIT, which produce very high-resolution but small fingertip-patch images and do not natively output force/torque. Visevi's claim of "first fully camera-based tactile + F/T for grippers" trades GelSight's sub-mm geometric resolution for whole-finger coverage and lower cost.

one_liner: Builds self-balancing exoskeletons for paralyzed patients and the Calvin-40 industrial humanoid, leveraging dynamic-walking control and NVIDIA Isaac GR00T foundation models on Jetson compute.
notes: French robotics company founded 2012 by Nicolas Simon, Matthieu Masselin, Jean-Louis Constanza. HQ Paris, France with a US office (NYC, ~10 staff). Known for Atalante X self-balancing rehab exoskeleton (FDA-cleared, 100+ rehab centers) and a forthcoming Personal Exoskeleton. Unveiled Calvin-40 industrial humanoid (built in 40 days reusing exoskeleton dynamic-walking control), running NVIDIA Isaac GR00T N1 foundation model on Jetson compute; Renault Group is first customer and a minority strategic investor. Layer 4 AI-Native Hardware fits: hardware-first general-purpose humanoid maker relying on third-party (NVIDIA) foundation model rather than building its own. Secondary L6 due to vertical exoskeleton/healthcare and industrial deployment. Series D $75M closed 2025-06; total funding reported ~$142-150M across ~6 rounds (prior Series C $45M, EIB ~$27M). Lead investor for Series D not clearly disclosed (Renault participated as minority investor). In scope: AI-native humanoid + learned policies.
business_model: Two revenue lines: (1) selling/leasing self-balancing rehabilitation exoskeletons (Atalante X, ~€200k/$228k each, FDA-cleared) to hospitals and rehab centers via central purchasing agencies like UniHA/UGAP on multi-year installment plans, plus an upcoming personal exoskeleton (Eve) for individual wheelchair users; and (2) selling the Calvin-40 industrial humanoid robot to manufacturers for physically demanding factory tasks, with Renault as launch customer.
customers: Over 100 rehabilitation centers across four continents (110 Atalante units sold, mostly France with growing US adoption); Renault Group is the first commercial customer and strategic investor for the Calvin-40 industrial humanoid.
revenue_status: early-revenue (exoskeleton sales growing ~10x over past three years; humanoid pre-revenue with Renault as initial customer; specific figure not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Repurposes a decade of dynamic-walking/self-balancing exoskeleton control (enabling crutch-free walking and an 88-lb payload, ~2x rivals) for its Calvin-40 humanoid, which deliberately omits head and hands unlike Tesla Optimus and Figure AI's Figure 02 to prioritize rugged industrial reliability and fast deployment (built in 40 days). On the medical side it is largely uncontested as the first FDA-cleared self-balancing personal exoskeleton, ahead of crutch-dependent peers like Ekso Bionics and ReWalk.

one_liner: Sells low-cost SO-ARM100/101 open-source 6-axis arm kits and high-torque bus servos with native Hugging Face LeRobot support for teleoperation and imitation-learning data collection.
notes: Founded 2006, HQ Shenzhen (offices also in Hong Kong); broad open-source electronics manufacturer (dev boards, displays, e-paper, sensors). Per Crunchbase/Tracxn no external funding raised — privately held and bootstrapped; team size not publicly disclosed. Robotics relevance: manufactures/sells SO-ARM100 (19.5 kg·cm @7.4V servos) and SO-ARM101 (30 kg·cm @12V servos) leader/follower arm kits, 3D-printed parts, and bus servos, with native Hugging Face LeRobot support for data collection, model training, and autonomous grasping. Marked in_scope=true: although the arm hardware itself is general-purpose, the product is explicitly built and marketed as an AI-native robot-learning/imitation-learning data-collection platform (LeRobot teleoperation), a category that would not exist without learned policies. Waveshare is the hardware vendor only and ships no foundation model. SO-ARM design originally by TheRobotStudio in collaboration with Hugging Face. Funding/team-size confirmation came from search summaries of Crunchbase/Tracxn; company about_us page returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch.
business_model: Sells low-cost open-source hardware online and through a global reseller network (Amazon storefront, DigiKey, RobotShop, PiShop, regional distributors): Raspberry Pi accessories, e-paper displays, sensors, and a robotics line including SO-ARM100/101 6-axis arm kits ($897-$1,432) and high-torque bus servos with native Hugging Face LeRobot support. Also offers custom design and in-house Shenzhen assembly/OEM services.
customers: Sells primarily to hobbyists, educators, makers, AI/robotics researchers, and developers via direct e-commerce and distributors (RobotShop, Parallax, PiShop.us, DigiKey, PiMoroni, regional resellers in India/Europe/Turkey). Specific named end-customer organizations for the SO-ARM/robotics line are undisclosed.
revenue_status: scaling (unverified third-party estimate ~$100M-$200M annual revenue per ZoomInfo; founded 2006, 201-500 employees; broad electronics catalog, robotics is one growing segment)
technical_differentiation: Competes against Feetech (the original SO-ARM servo supplier in the LeRobot reference design) and other LeRobot kit vendors like WowRobo; Waveshare differentiates through low-cost mass manufacturing scale, an established global distribution/e-commerce footprint, and bundling first-party high-torque bus servos plus extensive wiki/documentation with native LeRobot teleoperation/imitation-learning support. Hardware itself is open-source and largely commoditized, so it competes mainly on price, supply-chain execution, and ecosystem support rather than proprietary IP.

also_known_as: Nanjing WEILAN Intelligent Technologies; AlphaDog; BabyAlpha; 南京蔚蓝智能科技
one_liner: Builds AI-native quadruped robots (AlphaDog inspection-grade and BabyAlpha consumer petbot) using learned locomotion for high-speed dynamic gaits plus AI-driven interaction.
notes: Founded 2019, Nanjing Jiangbei New Area (No. 99 Tuanjie Road, Yanchuang Park). Makes AlphaDog quadruped (marketed as among fastest robodogs, 5th-gen 4.15 m/s) and BabyAlpha consumer petbot, plus inspection/industrial variants. Completed over-100M-yuan (~$14M) Series A1 round announced Aug 30, 2023, led by a state-owned market-oriented fund with existing shareholders following on; also received park grant of 35,000 sqm R&D/manufacturing base and 50M yuan support. CB Insights lists Series A totaling $13.73M (consistent). Tracxn shows "unfunded," which is stale/incorrect. Team grew from ~10 to ~200; founder Liu Weichao (刘维超). In scope: AI-native legged hardware whose high-speed dynamic gait depends on learned/optimized locomotion control plus AI interaction — category would not exist without it. Per task assignment, primary layer L4 (AI-native hardware); consumer/inspection vertical noted as secondary. funding figures approximate (over 100M yuan / undisclosed exact).
business_model: Sells AI-native quadruped robots as hardware: the AlphaDog series (C100/C200/E300/E400L, ~US$5,670 to US$27,690) targeted at developers and enterprises for inspection/patrol/delivery applications, plus the consumer BabyAlpha petbot (launched Oct 2024) sold to households for companionship. Revenue is primarily unit sales of robots (often via channels like Oz Robotics) plus professional services for custom application development.
customers: Largely undisclosed at the named-organization level. Public sources describe buyers as software developers, tech enthusiasts, and consumers/families rather than named enterprises; early sales reported ~1,800 units in the first month. No specific named paying industrial inspection or municipal patrol customers are publicly confirmed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified; ~1,800 AlphaDog units sold in first month at ~16,000 yuan/~US$2,400 each per press; no audited revenue figure public. CB Insights cites ~$13.73M total funding, Series A, marked unverified)
technical_differentiation: Positions on learned, high-speed dynamic locomotion (claimed ~15 km/h, marketed as fastest robot dog) and aggressive price-to-performance, undercutting Boston Dynamics Spot by roughly 10-12x. Versus closest rival Unitree (Go2), WEILAN differentiates mainly on consumer/petbot branding and design rather than a clear technical moat; Unitree has matched or exceeded it on cost and ecosystem, so WEILAN is largely competing on execution, design, and capital.

also_known_as: Wonik Robotics (Allegro Hand V4/V5); formerly SimLab Co., Ltd.
one_liner: Builds the Allegro Hand, a 16-DoF anthropomorphic dexterous research hand widely used as the manipulation subsystem for learning-based dexterous grasping and embodied-AI policy research.
notes: Subsidiary/robotics division of Wonik Holdings (KOSDAQ-listed conglomerate; Wonik Holdings 030530.KQ), so not independently VC-funded — funding/round fields are undisclosed/unknown as it is corporate-owned. Founded 2004 originally as SimLab Co., Ltd. (dynamics simulation software), later became Wonik Robotics. HQ at Pangyo-ro 255-20, Bundang-gu, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do, KR. Flagship product is the Allegro Hand (V4/V5), a low-cost 16-DoF four-fingered dexterous hand that is a de-facto standard manipulation platform in robot-learning/RL/imitation research labs. Also makes AMRs, AMMRs, and automation systems. Allegro Hand V5 launched with tactile sensing; robotics unit reported expanding globally via collaboration with Meta (per Asia Business Daily, 2025-05). L4 AI-native hardware: critical manipulation component for learned dexterous policies; does not build a foundation model. In scope because the dexterous-hand platform is primarily consumed by learning-based manipulation research. Team-size estimates from third-party sources (Tracxn/CB Insights ~7-10) likely reflect only a narrow legal-entity headcount and are unreliable, so left unknown.
business_model: Sells hardware: the Allegro Hand (16-DoF / V5 anthropomorphic research hand, priced ~1/5 of Shadow Hand, i.e. roughly the low tens of thousands USD vs Shadow's ~$120K) plus the RoboticsLab control/simulation software (~$10K/license academic), sold per-unit to robotics labs; also derives revenue from industrial AMR/AMMR autonomous mobile robots and logistics automation systems for smart factories. The Allegro Hand is commercialized under license from KITECH's Humanoid Robot Hand Research Group.
customers: Primarily university and corporate robotics/manipulation research labs that standardize on the Allegro Hand for learning-based dexterous grasping (e.g. it is the dominant research hand cited across academic in-hand manipulation papers); industrial AMR customers span semiconductor, display and secondary-battery manufacturers. Specific named purchasing accounts are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (specific figure not publicly disclosed; subsidiary of publicly listed Wonik Group conglomerate, but Wonik Robotics' standalone revenue is not broken out)
technical_differentiation: vs Shadow Robot Company (Shadow Dexterous Hand): the Allegro Hand delivers a 16-DoF current/torque-controlled anthropomorphic hand at roughly 1/5 the cost (~tens of thousands vs ~$120K), making it the de-facto affordable standard for academic dexterous-manipulation research; V5 adds 360-degree fingertip tactile sensing. vs Schunk SVH and KITECH's own designs, Wonik competes on price, ROS/simulation tooling ecosystem maturity, and breadth of installed research base rather than peak DoF count.

one_liner: Open-hardware manufacturer; an official LeRobot/Hugging Face partner selling assembled and DIY SO-ARM101 leader-follower robotic arms for imitation-learning data collection and policy training.
notes: L4 AI-Native Hardware vendor. Official Hugging Face/LeRobot hardware partner (alongside The Robot Studio, Seeed Studio, PartaBot) producing the open-source SO-ARM100/SO-ARM101 leader-follower robotic arm kits used for robot-learning demonstration data collection and imitation/reinforcement-learning training in the LeRobot/PyTorch ecosystem. Sells assembled and DIY versions via shop.wowrobo.com; also offers contract manufacturing/fulfillment for robotics teams (client roster cited includes NVIDIA, MIT, Google, Sony). HQ/manufacturing in Dongguan (Qingxi Town), near Shenzhen, Guangdong, China. Founder: Leo Xiao (leo.xiao@wowrobo.com). In scope: hardware is AI-native, purpose-built for manipulation-learning data collection and policy training; the leader-follower teleoperation kit category would not exist without learned imitation policies. No own foundation model. Verification=incomplete: company existence and LeRobot-partner role and one-liner are well-sourced, but founding year could not be confirmed from any public source (GitHub org appears recent ~2024-2025 but no explicit date) and funding is undisclosed/likely bootstrapped — no Crunchbase profile found.
business_model: Sells low-cost LeRobot-compatible robotic arm hardware directly to consumers/researchers via its Shopify store (shop.wowrobo.com): the SO-ARM100/SO-ARM101 leader-follower arms as printed-parts kits, DIY unassembled kits, and fully-assembled units (~$199 each), plus spare Feetech servos, cameras, robotic hands and sensors. Revenue is per-unit hardware sales and accessories/spare-parts, not subscriptions or contracts.
customers: Undisclosed individual buyers, makers, and AI/robotics researchers/students worldwide; the store states it has shipped "10,000+ units worldwide," but no named institutional or enterprise customers are publicly disclosed. It is an official ecosystem partner of Hugging Face/The Robot Studio for the LeRobot SO-101 program.
revenue_status: early-revenue (unverified; no public figures. Implied scale: "10,000+ units" shipped at ~$199 each, roughly low single-digit $M lifetime hardware revenue — unverified estimate)
technical_differentiation: Largely undifferentiated at the hardware level — it resells the open-source SO-ARM100/101 design (by The Robot Studio/Hugging Face) that direct competitors Seeed Studio and Hiwonder also sell. Competes on execution: being a named official LeRobot/Hugging Face partner, offering pre-assembled "ready-to-use" units (vs. DIY-only sellers), color options, and a deep spare-parts/servo supply chain rather than any proprietary IP.

also_known_as: 五八智能 / 58 Intelligent Technology / RoboWolf / Skywolf
one_liner: Builds rugged RL-trained quadruped robots (RoboWolf/Skywolf) and humanoids for defense and heavy industry, using learned locomotion to traverse rough terrain and carry mission payloads.
last_round_lead: Guoxin Fund (国新基金) and Zhejiang Industrial Investment (浙江产投), co-leads
notes: Founded 2022-02-16 in Hangzhou (Xihu district). State-backed embodied-AI "national team": owned by China South Industries Group Automation Research Institute (NORINCO-affiliated, 60%) and Hangzhou Zhiyuan Research Institute (40%). RoboWolf quadrupeds (Q20A platform, ~70kg, ~3hr runtime, 7km combat radius, swarm of up to 30 units) appeared at China's Sept 2025 V-Day parade; first public showing at Airshow China Nov 2024. Also makes Da Sheng D12 humanoid (won ~$5.5M industrial order, Dec 2025). ~RMB 500M (~$69M) first strategic round Oct 2025 co-led by Guoxin Fund and Zhejiang Industrial Investment, with multiple state/provincial funds following. Layer 4 AI-native legged hardware; not building a foundation model. In scope: AI-native legged platform relying on RL-trained locomotion. Funding USD figure is an approximate conversion of ~RMB 500M.
business_model: Sells rugged RL-trained quadruped robots (RoboWolf/SkyWolf) to Chinese military/defense buyers via the PLA and its NORINCO state-defense parent for reconnaissance, logistics and armed payloads, and increasingly sells D12 industrial humanoids on commercial deployment contracts (e.g., a December 2025 ~$5.5M industrial order). As a NORINCO/China North Industries Group subsidiary, it leans on national-level R&D missions and military-civil fusion procurement.
customers: People's Liberation Army (RoboWolf quadrupeds featured in China's September 2025 Victory Day parade) is the de facto anchor customer via state-defense channels; the buyer for the ~$5.5M D12 humanoid order is undisclosed. Also has a strategic partnership with BWTON. Reported >$14M of deliveries across safety/industrial segments.
revenue_status: early-revenue (>$14M reported deliveries; ~$5.5M D12 contract Dec 2025; raised ~$69M funding; founded Feb 2022 with ~$42M registered capital)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from commercial quadruped maker Unitree (Go2) by purpose-building rugged, defense-grade ~70kg RL-locomotion quadrupeds with swarm/pack coordination (up to 30 units) and weapon/recon payloads, backed by state-defense (NORINCO) integration and PLA procurement access that Unitree lacks. Versus China South Industries Group's (CSGC) military "machine wolves," WuBa competes mainly on execution, RL locomotion maturity and state backing rather than a clearly unique technical moat.

company_name: Wuji Tech (Shanghai Wuji Technology Co., Ltd.)
also_known_as: Wuji Hand; Pan-Motor; 无际科技; 舞肌科技
one_liner: Builds the Wuji Hand, a 20-active-DOF five-finger dexterous hand with embedded direct-drive micro-actuators, enabling learned dexterous manipulation for humanoid and embodied-AI robots.
last_round_lead: ZhenFund; Sequoia Capital China Seed Fund
team_size: small (~4 reported on social-insurance filings as of 2024; likely larger operationally)
notes: Shanghai Wuji Technology Co., Ltd. registered 2019-03-15; brand/parent is Pan-Motor (Chinese name 舞肌科技), a high-torque-density motor maker — note the task's romanization "无际科技" appears to be a variant; the entity behind the Wuji Hand is 舞肌科技/Pan-Motor (wuji.tech). Wuji Hand revealed Sept 2025: 20 active DOF (4 independent joints/finger), <600g, ~15N fingertip force, ~10kg static grasp, 1000Hz feedback, USB/RS485/EtherCAT, 300k+ grasp-cycle factory test (>1M internal). Vertically integrated via Pan-Motor F-series direct-drive actuators (8Nm peak at 200g). Confirmed hardware partner manufacturing Genesis AI's "Genesis Hand 1.0." Teasing next-gen Wuji Hand 2 (~20% higher transmission efficiency, 0.05Nm back-drive torque). Funding: Series A Aug 2023 (Zhuiji Power, Mingshi Capital) and Series A+ Nov 2023 (ZhenFund, Sequoia China Seed); amounts undisclosed; no public 2025 round found. In_scope=true: AI-native dexterous-manipulation end-effector enabling learned policies for humanoid/embodied AI — not a pre-programmed industrial gripper. AI-intensity is enabling hardware (L4), not a learned-policy maker. Other sources: https://wuji.tech/ ; https://www.pan-motor.com/page/en-index.html ; https://baike.baidu.com/en/item/Shanghai%20Wuji%20Technology%20Co.,%20Ltd./658112
business_model: Sells the 20-DOF Wuji Hand dexterous robotic hand as hardware to robotics researchers and humanoid-robot integrators (priced ~$16,000/hand from China, ~$24,000 duty-paid from a US warehouse), plus its parent Pan Motor brand's high-torque-density brushless joint motors and data-acquisition devices as components for robot builders.
customers: Undisclosed. Marketed to research labs, humanoid-robot OEMs, and developers (distributed via resellers such as Robotics Center of Silicon Valley); no specific named paying customers are publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercial product shipping since Sept 2025 at ~$16k-$24k/unit; closed Series A in Feb 2026 from Hone Capital, China Mobile Hechuang Capital, Huafang Capital, and Alphaist Partners; revenue figure not public)
technical_differentiation: Uses embedded direct-drive micro-actuators in each finger segment, eliminating the tendon/cable routing used by closest competitors Tesla Optimus hand and Sharpa (SharpaWave); this removes tendon slack and calibration drift, enabling ~1 kHz control, uncoupled repeatable joints, and 300,000+ rated grasp cycles. Heritage in Pan Motor high-torque-density motors underpins the actuator advantage.

also_known_as: WALL-A, WALL-OSS, 自变量机器人, Variable Robotics Technology Co.
one_liner: Builds end-to-end embodied AI foundation models (WALL-A/WALL-OSS) fusing vision-language-action with world models, enabling general-purpose robots to perform precise mobile manipulation in unstructured environments.
notes: Founded Dec 2023 (per Chinese sources). Formally Variable Robotics Technology Co. (自变量机器人). Series A++ of RMB 1B (~$140M) announced Jan 13 2026, led by ByteDance and HongShan; total ~$240M across multiple rounds (Chinese sources cite 9 rounds / RMB 3B+ cumulative, figures vary). Prior backers include Meituan, Alibaba/Alibaba Cloud, Sequoia China. Only Chinese embodied-AI firm backed by ByteDance, Meituan, and Alibaba simultaneously. Products: Quanta X1/X2 wheeled and semi-humanoid robots; WALL-OSS open-sourced Sept 2025. Layer 1 (foundation models) primary; also builds hardware and deploys in manufacturing/logistics/healthcare verticals.
business_model: Sells general-purpose wheeled-humanoid robots (Quanta X2, ~$80K, priced per use case) bundled with its WALL-A embodied AI foundation model to commercial buyers in education, hospitality, elder care, manufacturing, and logistics; runs a closed-loop strategy linking hardware sales to real-world data capture and model iteration. Also open-sources WALL-OSS to build developer mindshare while monetizing the proprietary WALL-A stack and household-services deployments.
customers: Schools, hotels, and retirement/elder-care homes are early paying buyers; named partner 58.com (Chinese classifieds platform) for large-scale home/household-services deployment, with discussions reported for customers in Japan and Singapore. Specific named enterprise accounts beyond 58.com are largely undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (specific figures not publicly disclosed; revenue confirmed from education, hospitality, and elder-care sales; company has raised ~$588M total and plans an IPO)
technical_differentiation: Versus Physical Intelligence (π0) and Figure AI, X Square deeply fuses vision-language-action with explicit world models plus large-scale real-world RL for zero-shot generalization in unstructured mobile-manipulation tasks (e.g. clothing folding, cable routing), claiming one of the world's largest unified embodied foundation models via a three-stage training paradigm and shared-attention/expert-routed-FFN architecture. Differentiation is the VLA-plus-world-model fusion and a household/service focus rather than the factory-humanoid emphasis of Figure; its edge over Chinese peers leans heavily on capital (Alibaba, ByteDance, Xiaomi, HongShan backing) and execution speed.

one_liner: Robots lack a sense of touch; XELA's uSkin 3-axis magnetic tactile sensors measure shape, contact force, and slip in real time, feeding AI manipulation policies for robot hands.
notes: Founded 2018 as a spin-out from Waseda University, Tokyo. Flagship product: uSkin tri-axial (3D) magnetic-based tactile sensor modules with soft skin, measuring normal and shear forces, shape, contact force, and slip in real time. Integrated into Tesollo (DG-5F shown at CES 2026), Wonik, and Robotiq robot hands for humanoid and industrial applications. Investors include Rogers Investment Advisors/FSR Angel, Berkeley SkyDeck, HAX Tokyo, Forge Accelerator, Waseda University. Latest round: Seed, Nov 2024, undisclosed (reported 7-digit USD from FSR Angel 1 LPS). Total funding undisclosed (databases vary widely, $100K-$200K reported but inconsistent with the 7-digit seed; treating as undisclosed). Team size ~18 per PitchBook; site lists ~8 core members. In scope: tactile sensing is an AI-native enabling component for learned manipulation (variable-item grasping with feel), not a pre-programmed industrial-arm enhancement. Layer 4 AI-native hardware, with L6 manipulation enablement. ai_intensity caveat: XELA supplies sensors; the learned policy lives in customer stacks.
business_model: Sells uSkin 3-axis magnetic tactile sensor modules (fingertip uSCu series, flat/customizable uSPa patches) plus accompanying software to robot-hand makers, integrators, and research labs as hardware components, including OEM integration into third-party robot hands (e.g., Tesollo DG-5F). Revenue is per-unit/per-module hardware sales with customization, spanning academic and industrial/commercial customers.
customers: More than 50 customers across four continents (many in assembly robotics) plus integration/OEM partners: Tesollo (DG-5F hand, commercial orders expected Q1 2026), Wonik Robotics (Allegro hand), Sake Robotics, Weiss Robotics, and Robotiq. Many remaining buyers are research labs and undisclosed industrial clients.
revenue_status: early-revenue (PitchBook lists no disclosed revenue; ~$200K raised - unverified; commercial unit orders ramping into 2026)
technical_differentiation: Uses magnetic transduction (permanent magnets in elastomer over a Hall-sensor grid) to capture 3-axis normal+shear force and slip in compact, durable modules that mount on curved surfaces - versus camera/gel-based optical sensors from GelSight (high-res imaging but bulkier optics) and resistive/optical approaches from Contactile, trading image resolution for robustness, small form factor, and high-rate force/slip output.

one_liner: Builds multimodal visuo-tactile sensors plus tactile world models and vision-tactile-language-action (VTLA) models, giving dexterous hands and humanoids learned touch-based manipulation.
total_funding_usd: ~28000000 (three rounds within one year; only Pre-Series A ~$14M publicly sized, earlier seed/angel rounds undisclosed)
notes: Founded May 2024 in Shanghai by Dr. Ma Daolin (PhD Peking University in theoretical/applied mechanics; postdoc at MIT mcube lab on robotic manipulation and tactile perception; now associate professor at Shanghai Jiao Tong University). Full-stack tactile intelligence: visuo-tactile sensors (fingertip, gripper, flat, curved), XenseSim tactile simulator, TacCap/TacCap-Gripper data-acquisition grippers for demonstration data, tactile world models, and VTLA (vision-tactile-language-action) models. Sold to dexterous-hand and humanoid integrators. In scope (true): AI-native critical subsystem — tactile perception and learned policies (VTLA, tactile world models) explicitly for learned manipulation; category does not exist without learned policies. Pre-Series A RMB 100M (~$14M) closed Oct 16, 2025, led by Fortera Capital (also translated "Futeng Capital"), with Li Auto, Before Capital, Keshi Capital, plus existing investors GL Ventures (Hillhouse), ORIZA SEED, Gobi China. Reported as third round within a year; earlier round sizes undisclosed, so total_funding is an estimate. Showcased VTLA-driven dual-arm long-horizon compliant manipulation and debuted TacCap-Gripper at ICRA 2026. PitchBook profile exists (id 688735-99). Team size not publicly found. Primary layer L4 (AI-native hardware); secondary layers reflect foundation-model, simulation, and demonstration-data components.
business_model: Sells multimodal visuo-tactile sensors and a full tactile stack (the TacCap-Gripper data-acquisition device, XenseSim simulator, perception algorithms, and VTLA/tactile-world-model software) to humanoid and dexterous-hand robot makers and industrial users; moving toward a "tactile cloud platform" bundling sensors, data collection, and simulation. Revenue comes from hardware sales plus data-acquisition/simulation tooling rather than from selling complete robots.
customers: Named partners/clients include AgiBot, X Square Robot, Galbot, EngineAI, and Google DeepMind, with pilot/industrial collaborations at Li Auto (also an investor), CATL, and Haier.
revenue_status: early-revenue (specific figure not publicly disclosed; founded May 2024, raised ~100M yuan Pre-Series A in 2025 across three rounds, investors include Li Auto, AgiBot, GL Ventures/Hillhouse, Oriza Seed, Gobi China)
technical_differentiation: Versus GelSight (US optical tactile sensors, largely research/inspection focus) and Chinese rivals XELA Robotics and PaXini, Xense differentiates on micron-resolution, compact (~4 cm3) multimodal vision+touch sensors rated for millions of compression cycles for high-speed industrial use, paired with an in-house software stack (XenseSim simulator, tactile world model, VTLA models) that reportedly cuts data-collection cost up to 95% — a sensor-plus-AI-stack play rather than sensor hardware alone.

one_liner: Builds robot foundation models (Yondu General Manipulation) so off-the-shelf and humanoid robots autonomously do warehouse bin-picking, fulfillment, and grocery shelf-stocking, trained via in-house teleoperation.
notes: YC Winter 2024 (W24) batch. Founders: Michael Chen (CEO), Tahmid Jamal (CTO), MIT-affiliated. Investors include Link Ventures, SBXi, XPV, Y Combinator. Autonomy stack branded YGM (Yondu General Manipulation); teleoperation system used to collect demonstration data, transitioning toward full autonomy. Targets 3PLs/warehouses with grocery shelf-stocking and fulfillment use cases. Total funding ($500K) reported by Dealroom/Tracxn-type trackers and not officially confirmed by company; round size therefore approximate. Lead investor for the round not disclosed.
business_model: Deploys general-purpose/off-the-shelf robots running its own foundation models (Yondu General Manipulation) into brownfield 3PL warehouses to automate bin-picking, stocking and order fulfillment, sold as ROI-positive labor automation; robots start human-teleoperated (ULTRON system) and become autonomous over time, implying a pay-for-automated-work / robots-as-a-service model rather than upfront hardware sale. Exact pricing not publicly disclosed.
customers: Shipbots, a large West Coast 3PL, is the one named partner warehouse (pilot/test deployment of bin-picking robots). Other paying customers not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (YC W24 seed-stage; pilot deployments, no public revenue; a third-party "$79.5M revenue" estimate appears to be an unverified data-aggregator artifact and is not credible)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates by training a robot foundation model (YGM/General Manipulation Model) on hardware-agnostic, off-the-shelf and humanoid robots for brownfield warehouses with no new infrastructure, versus Covariant (deep-learning pick model tied largely to fixed robotic-arm picking cells) and Dexterity AI (full-stack proprietary robotic systems). Differentiation is plausible but early-stage and not yet proven at scale.

one_liner: Builds AGI-native general-purpose robots; its AlphaBrain embodied foundation model (GOVLA vision-language-action) powers AlphaBot wheeled humanoids that autonomously perform real-world tasks in retail, manufacturing and services.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (12 rounds in ~1 year; post-Series-B valuation ~RMB10B / ~$1.4B)
last_round_lead: Baidu Ventures / CRRC Capital (co-led; with Yusys Technologies, Sentury Tire, Yunbo Capital, Guotai Haitong)
notes: Founded April 2023, HQ Nanshan District, Shenzhen. Founder/CEO Yandong (Eric) Guo. AlphaBrain = embodied foundation model built on GOVLA (Global & Omni-body Vision-Language-Action) model. AlphaBot series are mass-produced wheeled humanoids; AlphaBot 2 launched 2025. Series B of >RMB1.2B (~$144.7M) closed Feb 2026 at >RMB10B (~$1.4B) valuation, led by Baidu Ventures and CRRC Capital. Reported as one of fastest-fundraising embodied-AI firms (12 rounds in ~1 year). Plans to scale production from 1,000 units (2025) to 10,000 (2026); HKC ordered 1,000 robots. Total cumulative funding amount not publicly disclosed. Verified via The Robot Report, Crunchbase, Caixin, Sina Finance, Baidu Baike.
business_model: Sells AlphaBot general-purpose embodied robots (wheeled dual-arm/humanoid units powered by its AlphaBrain GOVLA model) plus deployment/integration services to industrial and commercial operators, typically via multi-year fleet contracts (e.g., a ~3-year, ~500M RMB deal to deliver 1,000+ robots). Manufactures in-house at its own Shenzhen line (1,000+ units/yr capacity in 2025, targeting 10,000/yr by 2026).
customers: Named customers/partners include HKC (惠科, world's #3 LCD panel maker — ~1,000-robot, ~500M RMB order), Dongfeng Liuzhou Motor (东风柳汽, auto assembly/QC), Bloomage Biotech (华熙生物, sterile-room material handling), West Lake/Xizi United (西子联合) high-end manufacturing, and Shenzhen Huizhi IoT for semiconductor-display deployments; robots also piloted at tier-1 Chinese airports.
revenue_status: early-revenue (commercial fleet orders signed/deploying, e.g. ~500M RMB HKC contract; no audited total revenue figure publicly disclosed; ~$145M Series B raised at >10B RMB valuation, Feb 2026, backers incl. Baidu, CRRC)
technical_differentiation: Its GOVLA ("global whole-body" VLA) outputs unified whole-body control plus mobile-base trajectory rather than arm-only actions, using a fast/slow System1-System2 dual architecture; its FiS-VLA reportedly beats Physical Intelligence's π0 by ~30%. Versus Chinese embodied-AI peers Galbot/银河通用 (retail-focused GroceryVLA) and Galaxea/星海图, ZhiPingFang differentiates on whole-body mobile-manipulation generalization and verified industrial deployments plus owning its own production line.

one_liner: Plug-and-play software safety layer ('Supervisor') sitting between a robot's autonomy stack and actuators, using control-barrier-function guardrails to modulate commands so only safe actions reach hardware.
notes: Caltech spinout founded by Prof. Aaron Ames (chief scientist), Dr. Andrew Singletary (CEO), and Dr. Thomas Gurriet (CTO); HQ Pasadena, CA. Core product 'Supervisor' is a software safety runtime that monitors and modulates motion commands in real time (collision avoidance, geofencing, stabilization, fault management) via control-barrier-function dynamic guardrails, integrating into existing autonomy stacks without modification. $4.1M seed (Oct 2024) led by TenOneTen Ventures with participation from Amazon Industrial Innovation Fund. Deployed on drones, articulated robot arms, quadrupeds, and ground vehicles. in_scope=true: a runtime guardrail product that exists specifically to make AI-driven/learned-policy autonomous robots safe — would not exist as a product category without autonomous robots. Note: the safety algorithm itself is classical control theory (CBF), not a learned policy; AI relevance is that it guards AI autonomy stacks. Team size not findable. Sources: PRNewswire, The Robot Report, SiliconANGLE, 3laws.io/about.
business_model: Sells annual software licenses for its "Supervisor" / "Supervisor Pro" safety layer to robot OEMs and integrators, who embed the control-barrier-function guardrail between their autonomy stack and actuators. Two tiers: self-serve Supervisor for ROS 2/Nav2 AMR platforms (e.g., resold as a 1-year license via Rover Robotics) and Supervisor Pro for custom/complex platforms requiring per-integration engineering engagements.
customers: Friendly Robots Company (autonomous industrial vacuums) uses its collision-avoidance software; Rover Robotics resells a 1-year Supervisor license; deployed on the USAF VISTA F-16 testbed for geofencing/load-constraint enforcement. Strategic backer Amazon (Industrial Innovation Fund) has signaled interest in deploying it across Amazon automation, though a paid Amazon contract is not confirmed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; $4.1M seed raised 2024)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via control-barrier-function (CBF) math (rooted in co-founder/Caltech Prof. Aaron Ames' research) delivered as a vendor-agnostic, plug-and-play modulation layer that requires no changes to the existing autonomy stack. Versus functional-safety/middleware peers such as Apex.AI (safety-certified ROS-based OS/SDK) and Foretellix (scenario-based verification), 3Laws focuses on real-time runtime guardrails that actively override unsafe commands rather than certifying or testing the stack.

one_liner: Agentic Material Orchestration software with AI agents coordinating heterogeneous robot fleets and factory material flow above ERP/MES; also builds its own tugging, pallet, and lifting AMRs.
last_round_lead: Walden Catalyst Ventures and NGP Capital (co-led)
notes: Rebranded from Ati Motors to Ati Robotics on 2026-04-22, new domain atirobotics.ai. Origin/engineering HQ Bangalore, India (founded 2017); also maintains a US office in Rochester Hills, Michigan (used as HQ in some PR). Four solution families: Tugging, Pallet Handling, Lifting & Handling, and Material Orchestration Software. 70+ enterprise customers, 2M+ autonomous missions, 99% mission success rate; customers include Airbus, Hyundai, Samsung, Forvia, TVS, CEAT. Flagship Sherpa AMRs; RaaS + outright purchase models. Series B Jan 2025 ($20M, co-led Walden Catalyst Ventures + NGP Capital; True Ventures, Exfinity, Athera, Blume participating). Total funding reported ~$34.5M-$37M across sources. In scope: AI-native agentic orchestration layer learning factory workflows is core differentiator (L5); secondary L6 manufacturing AMRs and L4 hardware. ai_intensity left as unclear pending independent confirmation that core policy is learned vs. agentic-LLM-over-classical-scheduling.
business_model: Sells material-orchestration for factories combining its Ati Flow / Ativerse AI-agent software platform (sitting above ERP/MES) with its Sherpa autonomous mobile robots (Sherpa Tug, Lifter, Pivot, XT, 10K). Primary go-to-market is Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS) — manufacturers pay recurring monthly subscription fees with no capex, bundling hardware, software orchestration, support, and free replacement/upgrades; outright hardware purchase is also available.
customers: Named manufacturing customers (50+ globally, ~70 factories) include Hyundai, Bosch, Forvia, Airbus, Samsung, Daimler, Toyota Tsusho, Harley-Davidson, Cummins, Autoliv, Pirelli, Electrolux, CEAT Tyres, and TVS Motor; ~80% are automotive and the US dominates revenue.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$8.9M annual revenue 2025, per Tracxn; unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates by being full-stack — owning both the AI orchestration software layer AND the physical AMR fleet — positioning above ERP/MES as an "agentic material orchestration" layer, vs software-only fleet orchestrators that coordinate third-party robots. Versus pure-software middleware players like Freedom Robotics (acquired by Formant) or interoperability layers, Ati pairs its own factory-hardened robots and shop-floor IoT sensing; versus hardware-centric AMR vendors like Locus Robotics or Geek+, Ati's edge is the heterogeneous-fleet, ERP-integrated AI agent layer and 2M+ missions / 99% success-rate operating data.

one_liner: AI-Defined Robotics platform that packages trained models, dependencies and policies into lightweight edge runtimes, turning any robot or sensor into a programmable digital twin controllable via code.
notes: Founded 2025 in Milan, Italy by Simone Di Somma (ex-Askdata, acquired by SAP) and Vittorio Banfi (Botsociety); offices in Zurich and San Francisco. Raised €7M (~$7.6M USD at Oct 2025 rates) in Oct 2025, led by United Ventures with The TechShop SGR, Vento (Exor), Pi Campus and angels. Round labeled seed/early-stage; total funding equals this round. Layer 5 fit confirmed: open-source edge software that packages models/dependencies/policies into lightweight on-robot runtimes for edge AI inference and local control with cloud sync, plus model lifecycle management — on-robot model-serving middleware between learned policies and robot execution. Catalogue of 50+ supported assets (ANYbotics quadrupeds, drones, industrial arms, sensors). In scope: AI-native robot-policy serving/orchestration that would not exist without learned policies. Team size not disclosed in public sources (likely small, ~10-20 post-seed but unconfirmed). USD figures are approximate EUR conversions, not officially disclosed in USD. GitHub org: cyberwave-os. Sources: eu-startups.com, tech.eu, roboticsandautomationnews.com, cyberwave.com.
business_model: SaaS subscription (monthly/annual) sold to enterprise customers for the Cyberwave AI-Defined Robotics platform — one API to connect, simulate (digital twins), and orchestrate fleets of robots/sensors, with edge runtimes packaging models, dependencies, and policies. Buyers are manufacturers/automation teams; the platform also runs a two-sided digital-twin marketplace where hardware vendors integrate devices once and developers access a plug-and-play library (~50 twins catalogued at launch).
customers: Undisclosed. The company states it is running early commercial pilots with major European manufacturers in automotive, construction, and logistics (defect rework, logistics optimization, drone inspections, construction-site monitoring) but does not name them publicly.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (early commercial pilots only; no revenue/ARR figure publicly disclosed. Raised ~€7M seed in Oct 2025 led by United Ventures, with The TechShop, Vento (Exor), Pi Campus; total funding reported ~$8.2M)
technical_differentiation: Positions as the "Hugging Face of robotics" — a unified, vendor-agnostic API plus simulation/digital-twin marketplace abstracting heterogeneous robot stacks, versus lower-level open frameworks (ROS/ROS2) it integrates with rather than competes with, and versus NVIDIA Isaac/Omniprise simulation which lacks Cyberwave's two-sided plug-and-play twin marketplace and managed edge runtime packaging. Differentiation is on the integration/marketplace layer; defensibility is early-stage and execution-dependent.

also_known_as: 原力灵机; Dexmal (Yuanli Lingji); Beijing Yuanli Lingji Intelligent Technology; RoboChallenge operator
one_liner: End-to-end embodied-intelligence company; co-launched RoboChallenge with Hugging Face, the first large-scale real-robot benchmark evaluating VLA policies on physical hardware for reproducible, comparable scoring.
last_round_size_usd: ~140000000 (combined A and A+); A+ alone hundreds of millions RMB, undisclosed exact
notes: Founded March 2025, HQ Beijing Haidian (Beijing Yuanli Lingji Intelligent Technology Co.). Core founding team ex-Megvii (CEO Tang Wenbin, ex-Megvii co-founder/CTO; Fan Haoqiang, Zhou Erjin, Wang Tiancai). Full embodied stack: Dexbotic (open-source VLA toolbox/foundation model = L1), DOS-W1 robot hardware (L4), and RoboChallenge benchmark/standards (assigned L5). RoboChallenge co-launched with Hugging Face is the first large-scale multi-task real-robot evaluation platform for embodied policies (arXiv 2510.17950); fleet of real machines, 30+ tabletop tasks at launch, scaling to 80k+ real-world tests; used for CVPR/ICRA competitions. Funding: angel ~RMB200M (Mar 2025; Legend Capital, Jiukun, Qiming); Series A led by NIO Capital (Hongtai, Lenovo Capital, Xichuang, Zhengjing); Series A+ Nov 2025 hundreds of millions RMB, sole investor Alibaba. Cumulative ~RMB1.2B (~$165M). Also reported backers/partners include Zhipu AI, StepFun, SenseTime. Merged with logistics-robot firm Atomix. L5 assignment fits as robot-AI evaluation/benchmarking service ('evaluation on hardware'). in_scope=true: AI-native real-robot policy evaluation that only exists because of learned VLA policies. Sources: dexmal.com; arxiv.org/abs/2510.17950; pandaily.com; niocapital.com; eu.36kr.com; aibase news.
business_model: Early-stage commercialization. Plans to monetize its proprietary end-to-end multimodal embodied-AI foundation model (MMLA) plus robot hardware/software stack, sold to robotics OEMs and industrial/logistics operators (team has prior AI logistics-robot and flexible warehouse automation deployment experience). Its Dexbotic VLA toolkit, DOS-W1 hardware, and the RoboChallenge benchmark (co-launched with Hugging Face) are open-source / community ecosystem plays rather than direct revenue products. Specific pricing/contract terms not publicly disclosed.
customers: Undisclosed. No named paying customers publicly identified; the company (founded March 2025) is pre-commercial. Ecosystem partners on the RoboChallenge committee include BAAI (Zhiyuan), Unitree, Qwen/Alibaba, and Xinghai Technology, but these are collaborators, not disclosed paying customers.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; raised ~1B RMB / ~$140M across A and A+ rounds led by NIO Capital and Alibaba, Nov 2025)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via an end-to-end multimodal "MMLA" model fusing LLM/VLM/VLA with a world model for cross-scene/cross-embodiment generalization, plus owning RoboChallenge, the first large-scale real-robot VLA benchmark (with Hugging Face). Versus peers like Physical Intelligence (pi) and Figure AI, Dexmal leans open-source (Dexbotic toolkit, DOS-W1 hardware) and benchmark/standard-setting; founding team are Megvii (旷视) veterans led by Tang Wenbin, giving deep CV/AI-native productization pedigree.

one_liner: Open-source Rust middleware for AI robotics: low-latency, distributed dataflow pipelines (directed graphs) using Apache Arrow zero-copy shared memory, ~10-17x faster than ROS2.
notes: Open-source project (Apache-2.0), not a VC-funded company. Co-founded by Haixuan Xavier Tao (French software engineer, associated with 1ms.ai) and Philipp Oppermann (German, known for Rust "writing an OS in Rust" blog). Project ~2022; v0.5.0 released March 2026. Backed/promoted via GOSIM Foundation community; no disclosed external funding. Attracted attention from Hugging Face (Thomas Wolf) and collaborates with the LeRobot ecosystem (Remi Cadene). HQ inferred as Paris, France (Tao's base; GOSIM AI Paris). 100% Rust core, supports Python/Rust/C/C++ nodes; prepackaged nodes for AI models and hardware. AI-native middleware sitting between learned policy and execution -> in_scope=true. Founded year inferred from GitHub repo history (early 2022); not explicitly stated in a single authoritative source, but corroborated by release cadence and community write-ups.
business_model: N/A (research/open-source). dora-rs is a community-driven open-source project (Apache-2.0 licensed Rust robotics middleware) co-founded by Xavier Tao and Philipp Oppermann. It is sustained through open-source ecosystem support rather than commercial sales: GOSIM (Global Open-Source Innovation Meetup) Foundation backing/promotion, Google Summer of Code participation (2025, 2026), and contributor/community contributions. No commercial product, paid tier, or support contract is publicly disclosed.
customers: undisclosed. No named paying or piloting organizations are publicly listed in the README or project site; adoption is via the open-source developer/robotics community (Discord, GitHub Discussions, GOSIM robotics hackathons).
revenue_status: N/A
technical_differentiation: vs ROS2 (the dominant robotics middleware) and Zenoh/eCAL: dora-rs is a 100% Rust dataflow framework using Apache Arrow zero-copy shared memory and YAML-defined directed-graph pipelines, claiming ~10-17x lower latency than ROS2 Python with native (non-wrapper) Rust/Python/C/C++ node APIs and no IDL/code-generation step. Differentiation is the AI-robotics-oriented zero-copy dataflow design and native multi-language ergonomics versus ROS2's heavier DDS-based stack.

one_liner: Edge-first observability and diagnostics platform ("Datadog for robots") that monitors robot camera/sensor health, calibration, and telemetry to catch failures and reduce downtime for deployed robots.
last_round_lead: Y Combinator / Lombardstreet Ventures (per secondary sources)
notes: YC Summer 2024 (S24) batch. Founded by Shivani Mouleeswaran (CEO) and Ritika Shrivastava, both ex-Tesla Autopilot; Mouleeswaran also ex-Simbe Robotics (computer vision). Product: edge-first/offline-first robot observability — camera diagnostics, sensor calibration, real-time monitoring, root-cause analysis, field-service alerts for robots in low-connectivity zones. Primary layer L5 (software stack/middleware: observability for deployed robots); secondary L6 given vertical robotics focus. Scope: device/hardware monitoring exists without AI, but product is positioned as observability for the physical-AI / vision-perception stack and targets reliability of AI-driven robots, so kept in_scope=true (borderline, as flagged). Funding figures conflict: Crunchbase lists a 2024-09-25 pre-seed; one secondary source cites ~$500K led by Lombardstreet Ventures and YC; the official YC page lists total funding as not disclosed and team size 2. A separate "$4.3M seed" news item refers to a DIFFERENT company (an AI healthcare billing startup also named Ember) and is NOT this company. Sources: ycombinator.com/companies/ember-robotics; ycombinator.com/launches/MlX-ember-robotics-datadog-for-robots; crunchbase.com/organization/ember-robotics.
business_model: B2B SaaS: sells an edge-first robot observability and diagnostics platform ("Datadog for robots") to robotics/hardware engineering teams, deployed via standard Linux packages with ROS/eCAL integration. Likely per-robot/fleet subscription with free trial and demo-led sales; specific pricing tiers not publicly disclosed.
customers: Undisclosed - no paying customers or pilots named publicly. Targets robotics, drone, autonomy, aerospace/defense, automotive/ADAS, and industrial teams; backed by Y Combinator and member of NVIDIA Inception.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; seed-stage, ~$500K raised in a 2024 YC seed round)
technical_differentiation: Edge-first architecture with smart caching, prioritized sync, and offline-first data capture so diagnostics persist when the network is down, plus deep multi-camera/sensor health and calibration monitoring. Differentiates vs general robot fleet-ops platforms like Formant and Foxglove, which lean more on cloud-based teleop/visualization, by focusing on edge anomaly detection and camera/sensor reliability; still early-stage and unproven at scale.

also_known_as: ferronyx.com; The Intelligence Layer for Robotics
one_liner: ROS2 observability platform giving real-time fleet monitoring with AI-assisted root-cause analysis to remotely monitor, debug and fix robot fleets, cutting MTTR from hours to minutes.
notes: Early-stage/likely pre-seed or bootstrapped project. Founder Haarvish Chevula (per ferronyx.com and haarvish.com, "runtime intelligence for autonomous systems"), based in Bengaluru, India; previously studied ECE at JAIN University, Bengaluru, and has worked as a software engineer (Sanas). No founding year, funding round, or team-size data found in company site, news, Crunchbase, or LinkedIn searches — marked unknown/undisclosed rather than invented. Status set to "stealth" given minimal public/funding footprint. Verification=incomplete: company existence and one-liner are sourced, but founded_year could not be confirmed by any source. Scope: assigned Layer 5 (software stack/middleware - robot observability/data-ops). Borderline per brief: generic ROS2 observability could exist without AI, but the stated core value prop is AI-assisted diagnostics/root-cause analysis of autonomous robot fleets, so in_scope=true.
business_model: Intended/likely B2B SaaS: a ROS2 observability and fleet-monitoring platform sold to robotics fleet operators (AMR/service-robot deployers), with AI-assisted root-cause analysis pitched on reducing robot MTTR from hours to minutes; monetization presumably via per-robot/per-fleet subscription. Specific product packaging, buyer contracts, and pricing are not publicly disclosed (stealth stage).
customers: Undisclosed. No named paying customers or pilots are public; the company is in stealth and only surfaces a marketing site and the founder's (Haarvish Chevula) personal page.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue or ARR figure findable; stealth-stage, no disclosed funding round)
technical_differentiation: Positions on AI-assisted root-cause analysis and remote debug/fix of ROS2 fleets ("runtime intelligence"), versus visualization/data-platform incumbents Foxglove (developer-focused ROS data visualization/observability) and Formant (fleet management, teleoperation, device monitoring). Differentiation claim is the automated AI diagnosis-to-fix loop reducing MTTR, but at stealth stage this is unproven; against well-funded Foxglove/Formant it is largely undifferentiated - competing on execution.

one_liner: Cloud data and operations platform for managing heterogeneous robot fleets: teleoperation, fleet management, data ingestion, observability, and customer data portals for scaling deployed robots.
notes: L5 fleet data/operations middleware. Founded 2017 by former Google/Savioke/Redwood Robotics engineers. Initial $18M Series A Jan 2022 led by SignalFire; additional $21M round Oct 2023 led by BMW i Ventures with Intel Capital, GS Futures, SignalFire, Hillsven, Pelion, Holman, Ericsson, Goodyear Ventures, Picus Capital, Thursday Ventures. Total ~$45M across rounds (Crunchbase). HQ commonly listed as San Francisco; some sources list Mill Valley, CA. In_scope=true: teleoperation/data-collection and runtime ops middleware for robot fleets is part of the AI-native robotics stack, though Formant itself is a data/ops plane rather than a learned-policy provider. Sources: TechCrunch (2023-10-11), The Robot Report, SiliconANGLE, Robotics 24/7, SignalFire, Crunchbase, PitchBook, Tracxn.
business_model: SaaS subscription for its cloud robot-operations platform (teleoperation, fleet management, data ingestion/observability, customer data portals), sold to robotics OEMs and enterprises operating heterogeneous robot fleets. Pricing is subscription-based, oriented around devices/robots and outcomes rather than user seats; exact contract terms are not publicly disclosed.
customers: Named customers include SoftBank Robotics America (20,000+ deployed robots, expanded partnership Dec 2023), John Deere / Blue River Technology, BP, Knightscope, Burro, and Scythe Robotics.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$5M ARR as of 2023, per GetLatka/third-party estimate, unverified; company reports 400pct YoY growth in active devices on platform)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from InOrbit and Freedom Robotics via deep data pipelines/observability and remote-control teleoperation for constantly-evolving R&D fleets, with strong security posture (zero-trust auth, end-to-end encryption, SOC 2 Type II). InOrbit is more multi-brand/ROS2-native RobOps; Freedom Robotics targets SMB fleets with OTA/task management; Formant leans toward enterprise data analytics and remote operation.

one_liner: Safety and trust platform for physical AI: SIL 3-certified machine control plus supervised-autonomy layer (teleoperation, active safety) so autonomous machines operate reliably in the real world.
notes: Founded 2018 by Samuel Reeves (heritage in landmine clearance/robotics safety); HQ at 1608 Walnut St, Philadelphia, PA. Total funding $60.5M; latest tranche $18.9M added to Series B announced Aug 2025 (lead for that tranche not disclosed; prior investors include Tiger Global, Prime Movers Lab, Lovell Minnick). On 2026-05-27 FORT acquired Mapless AI (vehicle teleoperation / autonomy supervision; founders Philipp Robbel and Jeffrey Kane Johnson, ex-nuTonomy/Aptiv/Bosch) to add remote human-in-the-loop teleoperation and onboard active safety; terms undisclosed. Team-size estimates vary by source (88 as of 2022 per Tracxn; ~57 more recent; ZoomInfo 51-200). Scope: borderline per task note — heritage wireless e-stop/remote-control hardware predates AI, so partly a conventional-safety vendor, but current positioning as the trust/safety-runtime + supervised-autonomy layer for physical AI fits AI-robotics scope; in_scope=true per 'when in doubt.' Sources: fortrobotics.com/about, prnewswire $18.9M Series B release, prnewswire/fortrobotics Mapless AI acquisition release.
business_model: Sells safety/control hardware-plus-software for autonomous machines: wireless emergency stops, SIL 3-certified vehicle safety controllers, and remote/teleoperation control systems, plus the cloud-based FORT Manager / Vehicle Safety Controller subscription (configure, monitor, manage access to fleet devices). Buyers are robot OEMs and industrial fleet operators in defense, agriculture, logistics, and warehousing; revenue comes from device unit sales plus recurring SaaS/platform and design-services contracts (specific list pricing not publicly disclosed).
customers: Named customers include Amazon, Toyota, John Deere, Boston Dynamics, and Agility Robotics; reported 550-600+ customers including 60+ Fortune 1000 firms across defense, agriculture, logistics, construction, and warehousing.
revenue_status: scaling (~$33.2M revenue/ARR per Getlatka, 2023, unverified third-party estimate); raised $18.9M added to Series B in Aug 2025, total funding $60.5M (Tiger Global, Prologis Ventures).
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on functional-safety certification (SIL 3 / PLd machine-control hardware) combined with a software supervised-autonomy and cybersecurity layer (active safety, teleoperation, secure device management), which it bundles as a vendor-agnostic safety platform across heterogeneous robot fleets. Closest named competitors are Sea Machines-style autonomy controllers and traditional industrial safety-PLC vendors like Pilz and Sick AG; FORT's edge is wireless E-stop plus a cloud trust/management layer rather than fixed-installation safety PLCs.

one_liner: Cloud, platform-agnostic robotics management software for building, operating, monitoring, teleoperating, and scaling heterogeneous robot fleets without developing a full software stack in-house.
notes: Layer 5 fit: vendor-agnostic cloud Robotics Management Software (RMS) providing fleet monitoring, remote access, teleoperation, diagnostics, and orchestration across heterogeneous robots (UR, Fanuc, KUKA, AMRs, custom). Founded 2018 by Dimitri Onistsuk, Hans Lee, Joshua Wilson. HQ San Francisco. Confirmed $6.6M seed (July 2019) led by Initialized Capital, with Toyota AI Ventures, Liquid 2 Ventures, Green Cow VC. Some aggregators (PitchBook/Crunchbase derivatives) cite ~$17M total funding, but only the $6.6M seed is well-documented in press; the higher figure is unconfirmed, so total_funding reported as the verified $6.6M. No acquisition or formal shutdown found; status remains active but headcount reportedly shrank to ~3 employees by 2026, suggesting significant wind-down. In_scope=true: AI-native, software-defined remote-ops/observability/teleoperation layer for autonomous fleets matching the orchestration angle; borderline because it also supports traditional pre-programmed arms.
business_model: B2B SaaS: sells a cloud, platform-agnostic robot fleet-management platform (the Freedom Robotics control/monitoring/teleoperation software, installed via a one-line agent) to robotics companies on subscription/usage-based contracts; pricing is custom/enterprise (per-robot/fleet) rather than published tiers. Buyers are robotics OEMs and robot-operating teams who pay to monitor, operate, debug, and scale their fleets.
customers: Named paying customers are largely undisclosed; publicly the company describes serving robotics teams across agriculture, manufacturing, logistics, delivery, and restaurants, ranging from "the largest entertainment companies to sidewalk delivery startups and multibillion-dollar delivery companies" (specific named logos not publicly confirmed).
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; raised ~$6.6M seed in 2019, ~$8-17M total per third-party trackers, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on being fully platform-agnostic with a one-line-of-code install and a real-time "single pane of glass" control tower spanning heterogeneous, multi-manufacturer fleets, versus closest competitors Formant and InOrbit (formerly Rocos), which offer similar cloud robot ops/observability platforms; Freedom competes mainly on developer ease-of-integration and breadth rather than a clear proprietary moat.

one_liner: Cloud RobOps platform ('DevOps for robotics') with agentic-AI Space Intelligence orchestrating heterogeneous multi-vendor robot fleets, humans, and enterprise systems across sites.
notes: Founded 2017 by Florian Pestoni (CEO) and Julián Cerruti, Mountain View / Silicon Valley. $10M Series A announced 2025-09-30, co-led by L'ATTITUDE Ventures and Globant Ventures (Globant's CVC); prior $2.65M seed in 2020, so total ~$12.65M. Deployments across four continents; enterprise customers include Colgate-Palmolive and Genentech. Coined 'DevOps for robotics' / RobOps; now positions around agentic-AI 'Space Intelligence' integrating with WMS/ERP. In-scope: classic fleet-management has classical roots, but the new agentic orchestration layer coordinating learned robot policies across heterogeneous fleets is AI-native; assigned primary layer L5 (software stack/middleware), with L6 vertical applicability across logistics/manufacturing/retail/hospitality. Team size 11-50 per Crunchbase/CB Insights.
business_model: B2B SaaS subscription to its cloud RobOps platform, sold to robot manufacturers and enterprise fleet operators; usage-based pricing with a free starter tier escalating to a variable monthly fee keyed to the high-water-mark of daily-unique active robots, plus tiered subscriptions (Standard Edition base tier). It also released OpenRobOps (ORO) as an Apache-2.0 open-source fleet manager, with monetization via the hosted/commercial InOrbit platform layered on top.
customers: Named customers/partners include Relay Robotics, Bossa Nova Robotics, Brain Corp, plus reported relationships with Google, Qualcomm, and Sprint; InOrbit cites a dozen-plus robot manufacturers and "thousands of robots in production."
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$4.7M revenue, unverified third-party estimate per Growjo; raised ~$10M Series A in Sept 2025, ~$12.8-13.5M total funding)
technical_differentiation: Vs. Formant (data/teleoperation/fleet management) and Foxglove (robotics observability/data visualization), InOrbit differentiates on vendor-agnostic multi-fleet orchestration of heterogeneous robots plus humans, native support for interoperability standards (VDA 5050, MassRobotics AMR, Open-RMF), agentic-AI "Space Intelligence," and an open-source play (OpenRobOps/ORO under Apache 2.0) that neither core competitor matches.

also_known_as: Intrinsic.ai, Intrinsic Innovation LLC, Flowstate, Intrinsic Vision Model
one_liner: AI-native software stack (Flowstate) letting industrial robots be programmed via web-based modular 'skills' for perception, motion planning, and sensor-based control without thousands of lines of code.
notes: Robotics software subsidiary of Alphabet/Google. Spun out of Alphabet's X moonshot factory as an independent Alphabet company in 2021 (after ~5 years internal development). Flagship product Flowstate is a web-based dev/simulation platform assembling AI-enabled modular 'skills' (perception, motion planning, sensor-based control) for industrial robot applications. Released Intrinsic Vision Model (AI perception model) in late 2025. Oct 2025: JV with Foxconn for general-purpose intelligent robots in electronics manufacturing. NVIDIA partnership. Folded into Google (from independent Alphabet co) Feb 25, 2026, working closely with Google DeepMind and leveraging Gemini models + Google Cloud; remains a distinct entity within Google. Funded internally by Alphabet — no disclosed external venture rounds, so funding/round fields are undisclosed. In scope: AI-native robot software stack / robot learning + sim-to-real platform with learned perception models, not a pre-programmed arm product. One GetLatka third-party source reports ~$27.2M revenue / 247 staff (2025), unverified. Team size ~293-299 per Tracxn/third-party trackers (2026).
business_model: Sells/licenses its Flowstate web-based robotics development platform plus AI vision/intelligence models to industrial manufacturers and system integrators, who build and deploy robot applications using modular "skills"; pricing/contract terms are not publicly disclosed. As an Alphabet "Other Bet" (now folded into Google as of Feb 2026), it operated as a strategic R&D/platform investment rather than a standalone P&L, also monetizing via partnerships and joint ventures (e.g., Foxconn).
customers: Named: Foxconn (Oct 2025 joint venture on general-purpose factory robots); integrators/manufacturers Newston Automated Solutions, Burkert Fluid Control Systems, and MartinSystems (first solutions shown at Automatica 2025); customer base described as ranging from small manufacturers to global firms. Partners include NVIDIA (Isaac/Omniverse). Pricing-level customer terms undisclosed.
revenue_status: undisclosed
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via Flowstate, an AI-native, web-based, hardware-agnostic environment that abstracts robot programming into reusable perception/motion-planning "skills" with built-in simulation, now backed by Google Gemini/DeepMind models and Cloud. Vs Siemens (Tecnomatix/TIA Portal) and Rockwell Automation/RTOS-based stacks, which are vendor-locked and PLC/CAD-centric, Intrinsic targets developer accessibility and cross-vendor AI perception/planning; vs Universal Robots' Polyscope, it is hardware-neutral rather than tied to one robot arm.

one_liner: Full-stack data annotation and data-ops platform that turns raw teleoperation sessions and egocentric multi-sensor video into ML-ready training data via AI auto-labeling and a smart feedback loop.
notes: Layer 5 data-ops/middleware for robot policy training and human-in-the-loop evaluation. Platform (legal entity Tensor Matics Inc) is purpose-built for the robot-learning/physical-AI pipeline: ingests teleoperation sessions and first-person/wearable POV video, handles multimodal LiDAR/camera/depth/force/motion streams, and pairs AI-assisted auto-labeling with a Smart Feedback Loop for humanoid and warehouse-robot training. Borderline: general data labeling can exist without robot AI, but this offering targets the teleoperation/embodied-AI workflow, so in_scope=true. Funding from Tracxn (~$225K total, last seed Jan 2024 ~$125K); a yourstory/getlatka $12M mention appears unreliable and was not used. Founding year discrepancy: 2018 (PitchBook) vs 2019 (Tracxn/yourstory). HQ in US (Delaware) with back offices in Chandigarh and Noida, India. Team size not reliably findable.
