business_model: Subscription SaaS for its cloud data-annotation/data-ops platform sold to ML and computer-vision teams: a free Researcher tier, a self-serve Pro plan at ~$499/month (20% annual discount, no minimum commitment), and custom enterprise contracts; it also resells managed human annotation services starting at ~$6/hour from a pool of 1,000+ expert annotators. Buyers are ML/AI engineering teams across healthcare, automotive, energy, security, retail, manufacturing and agriculture.
customers: Spare-It (waste-segmentation CV, cited at 99% accuracy in 24 hours) is a named case-study customer; getLatka reports ~992 customers as of 2024. Specific enterprise account names are otherwise undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$1.4M revenue, Oct 2024 per getLatka; India legal entity Tensor Matics Pvt Ltd reported Rs 1.2Cr / ~$143K for FY ending Mar 2025 — figures unverified). Raised only ~$225K total seed funding.
technical_differentiation: Competes against Encord, Labelbox and Scale AI in the same CV data-labeling layer; differentiates on affordability and ML-assisted auto-annotation (claims users need to manually label only 10-20% of data, with the rest auto-labeled), targeting small-to-mid projects where Scale AI/Encord skew enterprise. Differentiation is real but modest; largely competing on price, automation UX, and managed-annotation cost rather than a defensible technical moat.

one_liner: Cloud-to-edge embodied-AI runtime that lets anyone teach industrial robots new skills by talking or demoing, using generative AI, agent orchestration and symbolic reasoning to turn instructions into safe robot actions.
notes: YC Spring 2025 (X25/P25) batch. Founded 2024 by ex-Google engineers Xavier (Tianhao) Chi (CEO, former Google Public DNS tech lead) and Sebastian Peralta (CTO, physicist/roboticist, UPenn GRASP lab). HQ New York, NY. Layer 5 middleware: cloud-to-edge agent runtime serving SOTA multimodal transformers/VLA models on robot hardware, coordinating perception, reasoning, planning and control in real time. Open-sourced 'embodied-agents' framework (model/policy-serving layer for transformers on robots, v1.0 released 2024). Joint commercialization agreement with ABB Robotics after winning ABB's global AI Startup Challenge; presented at TechCrunch Disrupt 2025. Total funding reported ~$530K (Crunchbase); investors include Y Combinator, Pioneer Fund, Fuel Venture Capital, SynerLeap. Exact YC round size undisclosed; date approximated to batch start. in_scope=true: AI-native middleware orchestrating learned VLA/transformer policies into robot execution; category would not exist without learned policies. Secondary layers L1 (builds/serves transformer/VLA models) and L6 (industrial manufacturing vertical, 6b) noted but primary is L5.
business_model: B2B sale of an agent-based embodied-AI runtime/software layer that runs on existing industrial robots, sold to manufacturers, system integrators, and robot makers (e.g., via a joint commercialization agreement with ABB Robotics). Monetizes through paid proof-of-concept engagements transitioning into production deployments; specific per-seat/per-robot or subscription pricing is not publicly disclosed.
customers: ABB Robotics (joint commercialization partner / 2024 ABB AI Startup Challenge winner) and an undisclosed Fortune 100 consumer goods company running a paid proof-of-concept for high-mix packaging; also cites Fortune 100 partners across manufacturing, logistics, and labs.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; paid PoC plus $30K ABB challenge grant and ~$500K+ seed funding are the only public figures)
technical_differentiation: Unlike foundation-model players Skild AI (single universal "Skild Brain" model) and Physical Intelligence (research VLA models), Mbodi offers a dynamic agent-orchestration runtime combining generative AI, multiple models, and symbolic reasoning that learns in production from natural language/demos and supports human correction, running on existing multi-brand robots rather than requiring new hardware or a single end-to-end model.

one_liner: Vendor-agnostic fleet management system (Meili FMS) that unifies mixed-brand autonomous mobile robot fleets onto one platform for traffic management, task allocation, and interoperability.
notes: Founded April 2019 by Aldus von der Burg (Swedish founder), HQ Copenhagen, Denmark. Layer 5: vendor-agnostic fleet management middleware (Meili FMS) unifying heterogeneous AMR fleets regardless of model/type/brand; provides traffic management, task allocation, unified location data; recently launched a Code License giving integrators/WMS providers full source-code access. Funding figures conflict: PitchBook reports ~$340K total raised (Odense Robotics, Nordic Asian Venture Alliance, TK Ventures, Kiuas Accelerator); Crunchbase shows a clearly erroneous $1.1B figure (platform noise, disregarded). Exact last-round size/date/lead not disclosed publicly. Team size estimated from LinkedIn band. in_scope=true per orchestration-of-AI-fleets angle, though AMR coordination exists in non-AI forms (borderline); the learned/unified intelligence coordination claim is the qualifying angle but not independently verified.
business_model: Licenses its vendor-agnostic Meili FMS fleet management software for mixed-brand AMR fleets, sold to system integrators, WMS providers, and large-scale automation customers via subscription/SaaS deployment plus a newer "Code License" tier that grants full source-code access for customers to run FMS on their own infrastructure. Pricing is positioned below OEM fleet managers; exact contract figures are not publicly disclosed.
customers: Targets system integrators, WMS/automation providers, and large-scale automation OEMs needing a fleet layer; specific named paying customers are undisclosed in public sources. Listed/partnered via Mobile Industrial Robots (MiR) and automation marketplaces (automate.org).
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$1.6M revenue, unverified third-party estimate per Growjo)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on true brand-agnostic interoperability across heterogeneous AMR fleets via ROS1/ROS2 and VDA 5050 protocol support plus unified traffic/route management, versus OEM-locked fleet managers like MiR Fleet and OTTO Fleet Manager; against horizontal RobOps platforms (InOrbit, Formant) it focuses specifically on multi-brand traffic coordination/orchestration rather than general observability.

also_known_as: MOV.AI Robotics Engine Platform; Ground Drone Ltd
one_liner: ROS-based Robotics Engine Platform that lets AMR manufacturers develop, deploy, navigate, and manage autonomous mobile robot fleets via one low-code, hardware-agnostic web stack.
notes: L5 software-stack/middleware fit: ROS/ROS2-based Robotics Engine Platform for AMR development, deployment, autonomous navigation, and interoperable fleet management in one web-based, hardware-agnostic stack. Founded 2016 (Crunchbase/CB Insights; some sources list 2017); legal entity Ground Drone Ltd. HQ Tel Aviv, Israel, with additional presence in Portugal (Sintra/Lisbon). Funding history: $3M seed 2018 (Viola Ventures, NFX), additional $4M extension, then $8.2M round Nov 2022 led by BOWE GROUP with State of Mind Ventures, NFX, Viola Ventures; total ~$15.2M per CB Insights. Round labeling inconsistent across sources (called Series A by some, Series B by Crunchbase). Team size not disclosed. In scope: software/middleware layer providing AMR autonomy + fleet orchestration is the product value itself; AI-native robotics stack, not a pre-programmed industrial arm. Sources: mov.ai, robotics247.com, therobotreport.com, calcalistech.com, crunchbase.com (organization/ground-drone-ltd), cbinsights.com.
business_model: Sells the ROS-based Robotics Engine Platform (a low-code visual IDE, navigation/perception stack, and fleet-management suite) to AMR manufacturers and automation integrators, monetized via subscription/per-robot software licensing fees plus support services. The platform lets OEMs build and deploy AMRs in days instead of months without building software from scratch.
customers: Named customer/OEM Qisda (integrated the platform into the Qisda Robot Platform for logistics, health, and retail AMRs). Hardware/ecosystem partners include Advantech, Lanner Electronics, and Velodyne Lidar; investor Bowe Group (Böwe Systec) is also a strategic deployment partner. Broader customer base spans logistics and manufacturing in Western Europe, Singapore, and the US.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$5M annual revenue 2025, third-party estimate, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Positions as the "Android for robotics" - a hardware-agnostic, ROS-based low-code platform spanning development (visual IDE, 3D physics/sensor simulation), autonomous navigation, and fleet management in one stack, versus navigation-only point solutions like BlueBotics ANT (now Zapata/BlueBotics) and broader robotics-OS players like ROS-based Freedom Robotics. Differentiation is the end-to-end "build-to-fleet" toolchain rather than navigation alone, though it competes on execution against well-capitalized AMR-software incumbents.

one_liner: Browser-based accessibility layer over ROS 2 giving non-roboticist developers cloud simulation, AI-assisted coding, visualization, and sim-to-real deployment without in-house ROS2 expertise.
notes: Sheffield UK startup, formerly BOW (Bettering Our Worlds), founded March 2020 by Daniel Camilleri; rebranded OLO ('óloi' = everyone). Raised £4m (~€4.7m / ~$5.0M) seed early 2025; PitchBook lists ~$5.32M total across 11 investors (Anticus Partners, Finance Yorkshire, Northern Gritstone, Pxn Ventures, SyndicateRoom). Lead investor not clearly disclosed. Pivoted from proprietary universal-robotics architecture to ROS2-based platform. Commercial launch May 2026 with distribution/manufacturing partners Deep Robotics, inMotion Robotic, and Fiction Lab (LEO Rover bundle). L5 software stack/middleware sitting between policy/code and robot execution; secondary L2 via cloud simulation. In_scope=true: AI-native (AI-assisted programming + sim-to-real deployment) developer platform for the robot-learning ecosystem, though core value is ROS2 accessibility tooling. Company UK reg no. 12516316 (OLO ROBOTICS LTD). Round size in USD approximate from £4m/€4.7m FX.
business_model: Seat-based SaaS subscription to the OLO browser-based ROS 2 platform: Free tier, Standard (£23/seat/mo or £275/yr), Premium (£69/seat/mo or £825/yr), and custom Enterprise, plus usage-based add-ons (cloud simulator £2/hr, AI tokens, storage £2.50/GB). Secondary channel is OEM/hardware bundling, where robot manufacturers and distributors offer OLO as an optional bundle with their hardware (e.g., Fiction Lab's LEO Rover ordered with OLO).
customers: Commercial launch (May 2026) named three manufacturing/distribution partners: Deep Robotics (China), inMotion Robotic (Germany), and Fiction Lab (Poland). Website also lists Sarcomere Dynamics and Production Park; University of Nottingham cited as an academic user. Distinction between paying vs. piloting/distribution partners is not publicly broken out.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; raised ~£4M / €4.7M / ~$5.3M seed Jan 2025 led by Northern Gritstone)
technical_differentiation: Positions as a fully browser-based, zero-install accessibility layer over ROS 2 (cloud sim, AI-assisted coding, JS/Python SDKs, sim-to-real) aimed at non-roboticist developers, versus The Construct (cloud ROS development/learning environment) and Foxglove (ROS visualization/observability) which each cover only part of the stack; OLO bundles dev environment, simulation, visualization, and deployment plus an OEM hardware-bundling go-to-market. Differentiation is more on integrated breadth and ease-of-onboarding than a defensible technical moat.

one_liner: Plug-and-play autonomy software stack (AI/ML, controls, ROS, safety) letting industrial off-highway vehicles drive themselves, commandable via a simple REST API.
notes: YC S22. Co-founded 2021 by Stefan Seltz-Axmacher (CEO, ex-Starsky Robotics) and Ilia Baranov. L5 horizontal autonomy middleware: bundles AI/ML, controls, ROS, safety, deployment as a plug-and-play stack commandable via REST API, sold to robot/vehicle makers rather than being a vehicle itself. Provides a simulation tool, Caladan (L2 secondary), for building/testing autonomous behaviors before physical deployment; serves verticals like agriculture, mining, construction, defense (L6 secondary). Borderline AV-adjacent (industrial-vehicle autonomy) but kept in_scope=true because the product is a horizontal learned-autonomy software layer, not an autonomous vehicle. Total disclosed funding ~$500K; most recent disclosed round a pre-seed/seed dated 2022-06 (Crunchbase) around YC participation; investors include Y Combinator, TRAC, Samsara Ventures, Pioneer Fund, Liquid 2 Ventures, Zillionize. Team size ~11 (Latka/Tracxn estimates). Round size/type labeling varies across sources (pre-seed vs seed).
business_model: Sells a hardware- and OEM-agnostic autonomy software stack ("Caladan" simulation tool plus ~40 Autonomous Navigation Modules covering path planning, hazard/human detection, controls and safety) that customers retrofit onto or integrate into industrial off-highway vehicles; revenue is recurring software/licensing (SaaS-style subscription plus integration), sold to OEMs and fleet operators rather than per-vehicle hardware. Specific list pricing is not publicly disclosed.
customers: Specific named customers are largely undisclosed; the company describes its buyers as top off-highway OEMs, "billion-dollar" fleet operators, and U.S. government/defense programs across agriculture, mining, construction and delivery robotics. No individually named paying logo is publicly confirmed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$1.7M ARR as of mid-2024, per Latka; unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates as a vehicle- and hardware-agnostic, plug-and-play "general autonomy" middleware (ROS-based, simulation-first via Caladan) that lets OEMs add autonomy without building an internal AV team, versus vertically integrated off-highway autonomy rivals like Forterra (formerly Robotic Research) and Outrider, which deliver full application-specific autonomous systems rather than a portable middleware stack.

one_liner: Cloud-robotics middleware (rapyuta.io) orchestrating multi-robot fleets — localization, motion planning, task assignment, route planning — across hardware-agnostic warehouse robots; also builds own PA-AMR, ASRS, and forklift robots.
notes: L5 cloud-robotics middleware (rapyuta.io): hardware-agnostic full-stack DevOps platform for cloud-connected robot fleets, spun from ETH Zurich / RoboEarth EU project. HQ Tokyo (Koto-ku); additional offices Osaka, Chennai (India), Chicago (USA). Founded July 2, 2014. Total raised ~JPY 10B (~$81M) across 7 rounds. Latest equity round: Series C, $51M (JPY 6.4B), led by Goldman Sachs, announced April 2022; subsequent debt/loan financing (BOT Lease 2024, UPSIDER Capital 2026 per third-party trackers, undisclosed amounts). Team 270+ (2026). Secondary L6 via own warehouse robots: PA-AMR (pick-assist AMR), ASRS, autonomous forklift — vertical 6a_warehouse. in_scope=true: the multi-robot orchestration/middleware layer is AI-native robotics infra and warehouse AMR fleet coordination would not exist as a category without robot-fleet intelligence; though core orchestration leans classical planning rather than learned policies.
business_model: Sells warehouse automation built on its rapyuta.io cloud-robotics middleware: flagship pick-assist AMRs (PA-AMR) and an ASRS, primarily under a Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS) subscription model with zero-upfront-cost options and an "ROI guarantee" tying fees to productivity targets, plus hardware sales, software licensing, and integration/consultancy services. Buyers are logistics operators (3PLs, e-commerce, manufacturing), with partner integrators like SVT Robotics extending reach.
customers: Named: ASKUL Corporation / ASKUL LOGIST (LOHACO e-commerce fulfillment, 34 + 12 PA-AMRs across Saitama and western Japan hubs), Nestlé Japan (end client at an ASKUL LOGIST center), and Nippon Express (early pick-assist pilot, a top Japanese 3PL). Strong PA-AMR share in the Japanese warehouse market.
revenue_status: scaling (~$38M revenue 2025, third-party estimate, unverified; ~$81-91M total funding raised incl. $51M Series C led by Goldman Sachs)
technical_differentiation: Unlike pure fleet-ops/observability middleware peers InOrbit and Formant (ex-Freedom Robotics), Rapyuta couples its rapyuta.io cloud platform (cloud-offloaded localization, motion/route planning, task assignment, multi-robot orchestration) with its own AMR/ASRS hardware delivered as RaaS, making it a vertically integrated warehouse solution rather than a hardware-agnostic software layer. ETH Zurich/RoboEarth research lineage underpins the cloud-robotics stack.

one_liner: Open-source visualization and a purpose-built database/cloud platform for Physical AI, managing multimodal robot data (video, 3D scenes, tensors) for robotics, drones and AVs.
notes: HQ Stockholm, Sweden (SE) with distributed team across US/Europe; legal entity also registered as Rerun GmbH (Germany). Founded 2022 by Emil Ernerfeldt (CTO, creator of egui), Moritz Schiebold, Nikolaus West. $17M seed (Mar 2025) led by Point Nine, with Sunflower Capital, Costanoa Ventures, Seedcamp; angels Guillermo Rauch (Vercel), Eric Jang (1X), Oliver Cameron (Odyssey), Wes McKinney (Apache Arrow/pandas), Nicolas Dessaigne (YC). Total funding $20.2M. Primary layer L5 middleware/observability (robotics visualization + SDK); strong overlap with L3 data infra (database/cloud platform for robot data). In scope: AI-native data/observability tooling for Physical AI; not a standalone product that exists without AI workflows. Visualizations used in open-source projects by Meta, Google, Hugging Face, Unitree.
business_model: Open-core: the Rerun SDKs and Viewer for multimodal robot data visualization are free (MIT/Apache 2.0), while monetization comes from a commercial, cloud-hosted database/data platform purpose-built for Physical AI (ingesting, storing, querying video/3D/tensor data), sold to robotics, drone, and AV teams. As of 2025 the commercial product was in development with select design partners; pricing is expected to be a subscription/usage-based cloud model but specifics are not publicly disclosed.
customers: Named paying customers for the commercial cloud are undisclosed (it was in private partner/development phase). The open-source tool is used/integrated by Meta, Google, Hugging Face, and Unitree, and adopted broadly across robotics and AV teams, but these are open-source users rather than confirmed paying accounts.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$7.5M revenue 2025, third-party estimate via GetLatka, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated as a Physical-AI-native data stack: a single open-source visualizer/SDK plus a from-scratch database/cloud optimized for time-aligned multimodal robot data (video, 3D scenes, tensors), versus general experiment/MLOps tooling like Weights & Biases and Foxglove. Closest competitor Foxglove targets robotics observability/visualization (ROS-centric); Rerun extends beyond visualization into a purpose-built storage/query database layer.

one_liner: Kubernetes-native open-source cloud robotics platform to develop, simulate, deploy, and orchestrate ROS/ROS2 robots and GPU-accelerated AI across cloud, edge, and real hardware.
notes: L5 middleware: Kubernetes-native cloud robotics platform (RoboKube; arxiv 2403.04440) providing orchestration/deployment plane between robot software (ROS/ROS2) and execution on cloud/edge/real hardware; secondary L2 (cloud simulation) and L3 (GPU-as-a-Service, Cloud IDE, data infra). Founded 2020, HQ Ankara, Turkey. Total funding ~$1.5M per Crunchbase/Lucidity; investors include Turkey Development Fund and Simya; exact round size/date undisclosed. Team ~6 (Crunchbase). Company has pivoted emphasis toward industrial Vision AI / GPU infrastructure for manufacturing but underlying platform remains cloud-native robot orchestration. In scope: AI-native cloud robotics/middleware stack enabling distributed robot learning and GPU-accelerated AI workloads. Sources: github.com/robolaunch, robolaunch.io, crunchbase.com/organization/robolaunch, lucidityinsights.com, arxiv.org/html/2403.04440v1.
business_model: B2B sales of "robolaunch Vision AI" / robolaunch VIA — inline surface-defect inspection systems (Press, Body, Paint, Final Assembly) layered on robolaunch's Kubernetes-native AI Infrastructure — to automotive/industrial manufacturers via custom-quote deployment and managed-service contracts (no public list pricing). The underlying cloud robotics platform and Kubernetes Robot Operator remain open-source, serving as a funnel/foundation rather than the primary revenue line.
customers: Undisclosed by name; the company cites "20+ Vision AI in Production" and live automotive surface-inspection deployments (Press/Body/Paint/Final Assembly) but its case-studies page lists no named customers ("coming soon"). No specific named paying org is publicly findable.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; total funding ~$1.5M from Türkiye Development Fund and Simya, unverified)
technical_differentiation: vs Cogniac (~$8.8M ARR, $40.7M raised) and Landing AI, robolaunch differentiates by coupling vision inspection with a Kubernetes-native, open-source cloud-robotics stack (Robot Operator, multicluster GPU orchestration, ROS/ROS2) spanning cloud and edge, rather than offering inspection as a standalone SaaS — and by a regional/automotive-OEM focus out of Turkey. Otherwise it is a small, late-entrant challenger competing largely on execution and capital against better-funded vision-AI incumbents.

one_liner: Real-time observability for production ROS2 robot fleets; an agent streams telemetry into an index queried via ROSQL plus an AI debugging assistant that root-causes failures.
notes: L5 observability/monitoring/debugging middleware for deployed robot fleets. TraceHouse agent installs on ROS2 robots (Humble/Jazzy/Kilted) in one command, no source changes, and streams LiDAR/cameras/pose/kinematics into an index; ROSQL (SQL-like query language, OpenTelemetry-based, available as library/CLI/gRPC/WASM) plus a natural-language AI debugging partner grounded in live telemetry surfaces evidence and root-causes failures. Robot-native: ingests URDF/XACRO, understands kinematics, actions, topic graph. GitHub org RobotOpsInc (rmw_robotops custom RMW for distributed tracing) and rosql.org confirm the product. HQ: 560 Sylvan Ave Suite 3160, Englewood Cliffs, NJ (NY metro). SCOPE: ROS2 fleet observability is partly non-AI infra, but the differentiated product is the AI debugging/root-cause assistant over deployed robot behavior — flagged borderline, kept in_scope=true. Founding year, team size, and funding not publicly disclosed (no Crunchbase/news funding record found), so verification=incomplete despite confirmed company and one-liner sources.
business_model: B2B SaaS sold per-robot/month for the TraceHouse observability platform, bought by robotics/ROS2 engineering teams operating production fleets; tiers are Starter, Professional, and Enterprise with included storage and data-retention windows (7/14/30 days), a 14-day free trial, and 15-20% discounts for 1-3 year commitments. Enterprise adds SSO, white-labeling, SLAs, and custom contracts negotiated via sales with invoicing.
customers: Undisclosed. The site shows only generic deployment archetypes (warehouse AMR fleet of 100+ robots, autonomous delivery, manufacturing robotic arms), with no named paying or piloting organizations publicly findable.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue or ARR figure disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from Foxglove and Roboto.ai (the closest ROS2 data/observability platforms) by being OpenTelemetry-native distributed tracing plus a purpose-built SQL-like query language (ROSQL) with first-class ROS2 semantics: nodes, actions, topics, named joints, URDF/XACRO-aware component naming, ParentSpanId message causality, and auto unit conversion. Versus Foxglove/Roboto's emphasis on multimodal log visualization and dataset curation, Robot Ops centers on live queryable telemetry plus a telemetry-grounded AI debugging assistant that drafts ROSQL and traces root cause.

one_liner: DevOps/CI-CD platform for ROS-powered robots: containerized OTA deployment, monitoring, and scaling so shipping robot apps feels like pushing a web app live.
notes: L5 RobOps/DevOps middleware. Continuous-deployment and observability layer for ROS robots (OTA updates, CI/CD, containerized cloud-native workflows). Positions itself as "Vercel/GitHub Actions for robots." HQ Stuttgart, Germany (DE) per LinkedIn; founded 2023; team 2-10. No funding round disclosed (Crunchbase profile exists but no announced raise found via search; Crunchbase page returned 403 to automated fetch) — likely pre-seed/bootstrapped; mark total_funding and round details as undisclosed/unknown, verify at GATE. In_scope=true: this is robot-learning-adjacent middleware (the deployment/observability layer for robots running learned software); it is AI-native infra in the sense that it serves the modern ML-driven robotics stack rather than a pre-AI product category. Note ai_intensity caveat: Robotair does not itself ship a learned policy.
business_model: B2B SaaS: a cloud-hosted "Auto DevOps" platform (robotair.io) sold to robotics companies / ROS engineering teams, who pay subscription fees to deploy, monitor, OTA-update and scale ROS/ROS 2 robot applications via containerized CI/CD pipelines. Specific pricing tiers (likely per-robot/per-fleet subscription) are not publicly disclosed.
customers: Undisclosed. No named paying or piloting organizations appear in the company website, blog, LinkedIn, or Crunchbase profile; the product targets ROS-based robotics startups and fleet operators.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue or ARR figure findable; small early-stage startup with no disclosed funding rounds on Crunchbase)
technical_differentiation: Positions itself as a ROS-native, fully managed CI/CD + OTA + monitoring stack ("push robot apps like a web app"), competing most directly with Airbotics (open-source Uptane-based ROS 2 deployment platform) and broader fleet-ops players like Formant and Transitive/Rapyuta Robotics. Differentiation is its end-to-end managed Docker-build-to-OTA-to-monitoring pipeline rather than self-hosted/open-source tooling, but the edge is largely execution-based and not clearly defensible vs. Airbotics/Formant.

one_liner: AI operating layer letting anyone deploy, orchestrate and manage heterogeneous robot fleets via natural language, spanning on-robot SLAM to cloud orchestration.
notes: Early-stage robotics middleware startup. Founders: Dr. Chanoh Park (Founder/CEO, 13+ yrs SLAM/spatial-AI, Korea-based, ex-GroundProbe/EasyRobotics, 2nd place DARPA SubT Challenge) and Samuel Lee (CTO, contact samuel.lee@rovifylab.com). Site lists presence in Seoul, Sydney, and Tokyo; primary HQ inferred as Seoul (KR) given founder base — not explicitly confirmed. Product: Fleet OS — natural-language mission creation that generates/validates/dispatches robot tasks; modular controller architecture validated across 3+ robot OEMs; proprietary SLAM stack for GPS-denied environments proven in underground mining. AI-native: natural-language orchestration layer sitting between policies and real-world fleet execution; would not exist without learned/LLM-driven planning => in_scope=true (Layer 5 middleware, with data-infra/spatial-map aspects). Founding year, funding, total funding, and team size not found in any public source (no Crunchbase/LinkedIn company page located) => verification=incomplete. No invented values.
business_model: Sells "Fleet OS," a software/middleware layer that orchestrates heterogeneous robot fleets via natural language, sold to enterprise robot operators (primarily mining/industrial) under multi-year technology partnership contracts. Specific pricing (per-robot, subscription, or platform license) is not publicly disclosed; deployment is described as a SaaS-style operating layer spanning on-robot SLAM to cloud orchestration.
customers: One named-but-anonymized strategic partner: a "Fortune Global 500 mining services company" with a multi-year technology partnership (used as the underground-mining beachhead). Validation done across 3+ unnamed robot OEM manufacturers. No specifically named paying customers are public; otherwise undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue or ARR figure disclosed; inferred from active multi-year mining partnership contract, but no dollar figure findable)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a proprietary GPS-denied/infrastructure-free SLAM stack (founder Dr. Chanoh Park, DARPA SubT Challenge 2nd place) combined with an LLM natural-language mission layer and a modular OEM-agnostic controller, spanning on-robot SLAM up to cloud orchestration. Versus InOrbit.AI and Boston Dynamics Orbit (closest fleet-orchestration competitors), Rovify's edge is owning the on-robot localization/mapping layer plus natural-language tasking rather than being a cloud-only management dashboard.

one_liner: AI software that helps hardware makers (robots, heavy machinery) achieve and maintain functional-safety certification by auto-generating HARAs, FMEAs, safety cases, and compliance reports.
notes: YC S24 batch. Founders Akshay Chalana (CEO) and Oscar Avatare; backgrounds at Tesla, Apple, Amazon, Twitter/Meta. Pre-seed $500K, 2024-09-25, per Crunchbase (Y Combinator standard investment). HQ San Francisco, ~8 employees. Positions as 'enablement/safety layer for Physical AI' and 'Vanta for hardware,' covering ISO 26262, ISO 10218, IEC 61508, UL, CE, TUV. BORDERLINE SCOPE: this is compliance-automation/safety-engineering middleware, not an on-robot learned-policy runtime — the broad category (safety certification) predates AI. Marked in_scope=true per 'when in doubt' rule because it is AI-native tooling specifically targeting safety/assurance of learned-policy physical-AI systems (humanoids, industrial robots). Layer 5 (software stack/middleware), vertical 6h_other. Adjacent to runtime guardrails but operates at the engineering/assurance layer rather than as an on-robot monitor.
business_model: B2B SaaS subscription: Saphira's compliance platform auto-generates traceable safety/cybersecurity artifacts (HARA, TARA, FMEA) and standards-aligned reports (ISO 26262, ISO 10218, IEC 61508, ISO/SAE 21434) for hardware engineering teams, sold as seat/team-based subscriptions to robotics, heavy-machinery, automotive, and aerospace makers needing certification from notified bodies (TUV, UL, CE). Specific pricing tiers are not publicly disclosed.
customers: Named users include 1X Technologies (humanoid robot compliance) and RobCo (achieved ISO 10218-1 certification using Saphira); broader customer base spans industrial robotics startups, hardware/retail UL-certification needs, and aerospace/defense firms.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public ARR/revenue figure found; YC S24, raised ~$500K pre-seed Sept 2024 led by Y Combinator)
technical_differentiation: Positions as "Vanta for hardware," differentiating from horizontal SaaS-compliance tools (Vanta, Drata) by targeting physical-product functional-safety standards rather than SOC 2/security, and using AI to auto-generate engineering safety analyses (HARA/FMEA/TARA) from design docs. Closest direct competitor is RoboCred (AI-native hardware compliance services); Saphira differentiates via a self-serve software platform with requirements-to-test traceability versus a services model.

one_liner: Unified observability platform turning high-frequency multimodal sensor telemetry from mission-critical hardware and robots into real-time, AI-ready insights, replacing brittle custom scripts and dashboards.
notes: Founded 2022 by former SpaceX engineers; official site lists founders as Austin Spiegel and Karthik Gollapudi (assignment-provided "Rowen Wu" not confirmed on company site — Karthik Gollapudi confirmed). HQ El Segundo, CA (LA-area). $42M Series B led by StepStone Group with GV as largest investor; total ~$67M (prior $7.5M seed Nov 2023 plus other rounds). Customers include ULA, Astranis, K2 Space, Parallel Systems, and defense programs. L5 observability/middleware runtime for mission-critical machines including robotics. in_scope=true: positioned as the data infrastructure layer that AI-controlled hardware/robotic systems depend on (AI-native telemetry alignment at 10kHz, real-time AI-ready insights), not a generic conventional monitoring tool bolted onto a non-AI category.
business_model: Enterprise SaaS: Sift sells its unified observability platform (data ingestion, purpose-built high-cardinality telemetry storage, no-code visualization, rule-based anomaly detection, root-cause analysis, and certification/reporting) to engineering teams building mission-critical hardware in aerospace, defense, robotics, and energy. Buyers are hardware/test/systems-engineering orgs; pricing is via custom enterprise annual contracts (specific list pricing not publicly disclosed).
customers: Named customers include ULA, Astranis, K2 Space, Parallel Systems, True Anomaly, Mach Industries, Astrolab, Impulse, and TEC, plus undisclosed enterprise defense programs.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; $42M Series B raised March 2026 at $274M post-money valuation, total funding ~$67M)
technical_differentiation: Versus Foxglove (robotics multimodal data platform) and Memfault (embedded firmware/device observability), Sift differentiates by targeting high-frequency, high-cardinality telemetry from mission-critical aerospace/defense hardware with purpose-built time-series storage, no-code analysis, and certification/hardware-genealogy workflows derived from its founders' SpaceX rocket-telemetry background, rather than Memfault's OTA/firmware focus or Foxglove's ROS-centric robotics visualization.

one_liner: Modular open-source software platform for building, deploying, and managing robot/smart-machine apps across heterogeneous hardware, with integrated data capture and ML model fleet deployment.
notes: L5 Software Stack & Middleware. Founded 2020 in NYC by Eliot Horowitz (ex-MongoDB co-founder/CTO). HQ and robotics lab in New York City. Total ~$117M over multiple rounds: $45M Series B (Mar 2024), $30M Series C (Mar 2025) led by existing investor Union Square Ventures (Albert Wenger), with Battery Ventures, Neurone, and other existing investors. Team ~260 employees (May 2026), up from ~51-200 range earlier. In scope: the platform is an AI-native deployment/management layer between learned ML models and physical machines — integrates data capture and ML model deployment to fleets across heterogeneous hardware; this orchestration/middleware category is built around AI/ML in the physical world rather than AI bolted onto a conventional product. Also serves IoT/industrial automation broadly. Secondary L3 data infra due to integrated data capture/management.
business_model: Open-core/freemium: the on-machine viam-server SDK is open source and free, while Viam monetizes consumption-based cloud services (fleet management, data capture/sync, ML model deployment, remote control) billed usage-based after a $5/month free tier, primarily to enterprises building and operating fleets of robots/smart machines. It also sells vertical turnkey solutions like SurfaceAI sanding cells (~$120K/year per two-arm cell) and offers $10K platform credits to startups.
customers: Named customers/partners include the New York Islanders & UBS Arena (Official AI Technology Partner), Viking Yachts, Kongsberg Discovery, Sbarro, Transmutex, Appetronix, and CompScience; spans robotics, QSR, marine, climate tech, and industrial manufacturing.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; raised ~$117M total incl. $30M Series C in March 2025 led by Union Square Ventures)
technical_differentiation: Unlike ROS (open-source middleware requiring heavy integration/self-hosting), Viam provides an integrated, hardware-agnostic platform with built-in cloud fleet/data management and an extensible module registry out of the box. Vs cloud-ops competitors Formant and Freedom Robotics, Viam differentiates by combining the on-device runtime/SDK and the cloud control plane in one stack rather than layering ops tooling on top of an existing robot framework.

one_liner: AI-powered warehouse robots that sort and stack parcels and variable items using Sim2Real reinforcement learning and foundation-model vision, handling never-before-seen items without pre-programming.
last_round_lead: Tiger Global, Bow Capital (existing); joined by Ahren and Pitney Bowes
notes: Spun from UC Berkeley (AUTOLAB), formerly Ambidextrous Laboratories. Founded 2018, HQ Berkeley, CA (1610 Fifth Street). Total funding ~$66.7M per Crunchbase/PitchBook: includes $26M Series A (2021, led by Tiger Global) and $32M growth round (Oct 2022, Tiger Global/Bow Capital with Ahren and strategic partner Pitney Bowes). No Series B found after 2022; latest disclosed round is the Oct 2022 $32M. Products: AmbiSort (parcel sorting, 1,200+ items/hr at 99%+ accuracy), AmbiStack (AI robotic stacking, launched Jan 2025), and PRIME-1 foundation model for warehouse robots (Jan 2025). Fleet had sorted 100M+ packages by 2024. AmbiStack named 2026 RBR50 award winner. In scope: AI-native variable-item manipulation enabled by learned policies, not a fixed pre-programmed conveyor. Team size not reliably disclosed. [AI_intensity: Manipulation/placement is driven by a learned policy: Sim2Real reinforcement learning lets robots pick, sort, and stack random never-before-seen items without pre-programming. Foundation-model vision (PRIME-1) added in 2025 for warehouse perception. Core task is learned, not classical scripted motion.]
business_model: Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS): Ambi builds, deploys, and manages its AmbiSort A-/B-Series parcel sorting cells, AmbiStack stacking robots, and AmbiOS software at customer facilities for a fixed recurring monthly fee, typically under multi-year contracts (e.g., a stated minimum four-year RaaS deal with OSM Worldwide). Buyers are parcel logistics, shipping/mailing, and e-commerce fulfillment operators.
customers: Pitney Bowes (anchor customer and strategic investor; expansion deal reported at ~$23M, 80+ AmbiSort A-Series units across US hubs), OSM Worldwide (4-year RaaS deployment), and UPS. Combined fleet had sorted 100M+ parcels by 2024.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; ~$66.7M total funding raised, including $26M Series A led by Tiger Global and a $32M 2022 round; a ~$23M Pitney Bowes deployment deal is the largest disclosed contract value, but recurring revenue is not publicly reported)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via Sim2Real reinforcement learning (AmbiOS, rooted in UC Berkeley's Dex-Net) plus a "soft-touch" end effector that handles mixed deformable and rigid parcels at 1,200+ items/hour with 99.5%+ accuracy, deployed as full turnkey sorting/stacking cells. Vs Covariant (foundation-model RFM-1 software layered onto third-party arms; largely absorbed by Amazon in 2024) Ambi sells an integrated hardware+software sortation product; vs Pickle Robot (focused on truck/trailer unloading and dock induction) Ambi targets downstream parcel sortation and stacking.

one_liner: MujinOS unifies perception, learned grasping, and real-time motion planning into a teachless platform for variable-SKU piece/case picking and truck unloading without manual programming.
notes: Founded 2011 in Tokyo by Issei Takino, Rosen (Ross) Diankov, Kazuyuki Takino. HQ in Koto-ku, Tokyo, JP; offices in US, Europe, China. Flagship MujinOS / MujinController. Borderline L6: strong heritage in classical real-time motion planning, but modern teachless piece-picking stack relies on learned perception and grasp policies for variable packaging/items, qualifying as AI-trained variable-item manipulation. Series D (Dec 2025): $233M total = $133M equity (lead NTT Group; co-lead QIA; with Mitsubishi HC Capital Realty, Salesforce Ventures) + $100M debt from Japanese financial institutions. Prior Series C $85M (Sept 2023). Total funding ~$411M. Team size varies by source (~450 PitchBook; 201-500 Indeed). Products: random-bin/variable-SKU piece picking, case picking, palletizing/depalletizing, TruckBot truck unloading. [AI_intensity: Perception and grasping use learned policies (vision-based recognition and grasp planning for unknown/variable items, enabling teachless piece picking). Motion planning is historically classical real-time planning rather than a learned policy. Manipulation grasp selection is the AI-trained component.]
business_model: Sells MujinOS, an intelligent robotics operating system/controller, bundled into automation systems (palletizing, bin picking, order fulfillment, truck unloading) sold to manufacturers, retailers and logistics providers via direct sales and a growing network of system integrators (e.g., Yaskawa/Motoman, North American integrators). Historically a capital-purchase model; in April 2026 it launched a recurring MujinOS software subscription (Standard and Premium tiers covering upgrades, support hours and dashboards), beginning with Single-SKU Palletizing, shifting toward an OpEx/recurring software revenue model.
customers: Named deployments include Walmart Canada (Calgary DC, ~90% cut in product processing time), Fast Retailing/Uniqlo (T-shirt picking/boxing, strategic global supply-chain partnership), and Japanese logistics distributor PALTAC (automated palletizing/inbound inspection). Mujin states 1,000+ robotic systems are in production and cites Fortune 100 customers and leading robot OEMs.
revenue_status: scaling (~$81.5M revenue 2025, Growjo third-party estimate, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on a unified, "teachless" controller (MujinOS) combining real-time digital-twin perception, learned grasping and motion planning that removes manual robot programming and is robot-OEM-agnostic, versus US warehouse-automation peers like Berkshire Grey (acquired by SoftBank; integrated picking hardware+software) and Symbotic (full warehouse system integration); Mujin sells an OS/brains layer that runs across third-party robot arms rather than a single proprietary cell.

one_liner: Hardware-agnostic AI software for warehouse robots that learns piece-picking, bagging, kitting, and depalletizing of variable SKUs via reinforcement and imitation learning from 2D video.
last_round_type: Debt (per CB Insights); most recent equity round was Series C
notes: Founded 2015 in San Francisco by Derik Pridmore and Michael Kahane (one source lists 2014/2015 discrepancy; majority say 2015). HQ at 500 3rd St, San Francisco, CA (some directories list a Sacramento address). Products: OSARO Pick (piece-picking), bagging, kitting, depalletization, powered by SightWorks perception and AutoModel. First deployment OSARO Pick in Japan early 2018; production deployments across multiple countries. Funding: Seed $3.3M (2015), Series A $10M (2017), Series B $16M (2019, extended to ~$37M total), Series C $30M (Aug 2021, led by Octave Ventures, brought total to ~$67.5M). Latest funding event per CB Insights is a ~$17.64M debt round dated Dec 2023. Total funding ~$86.8M per Tracxn (varies by source: PitchBook ~$79.3M, CB Insights ~$104M). In scope: AI-native learned grasping for variable-item manipulation that would not exist without learned policies. Sources: osaro.com, Crunchbase, Tracxn, CB Insights, TheRobotReport, finsmes. [AI_intensity: Core manipulation/grasping uses learned policies: deep reinforcement learning plus behavioral cloning (imitation learning) trained from 2D video demonstrations and production data; integrated with NVIDIA GR00T foundation model for semantic control. AI-native, not classical CV/planning.]
business_model: Sells hardware-agnostic AI software (SightWorks perception and AutoModel) that runs piece-picking, autobagging, kitting and mixed-case depalletizing robotic cells, monetized primarily via Robotics-as-a-Service (RaaS) subscription contracts plus feasibility studies/audits, and via software licensing to systems integrators (e.g., IHI licenses OSARO robotic depalletizing systems). Buyers are e-commerce, 3PL, CPG, manufacturing and distribution warehouse operators.
customers: Named deployments include Zenni Optical (robotic autobagging cells in its Novato, CA fulfillment center) and IHI Logistics & Machinery Corporation in Japan (licenses OSARO depalletizing systems for warehouse integrators). OSARO cites production deployments across five countries.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$5-25M est. annual revenue per Owler, unverified; ~$89.8M total funding raised)
technical_differentiation: Hardware-agnostic perception/control software (vs. closest rivals Ambi Robotics and Covariant, now part of Amazon); OSARO trains robots from a few 2D video demonstrations using deep/reinforcement learning without 3D sensors, calibration, or object modeling, contrasting with Covariant's large-dataset cloud "Covariant Brain" and Ambi's full-stack robotic-arm hardware systems. Its AutoModel feature adapts to new SKUs in real time.

one_liner: Physical-AI robots that autonomously unload floor-loaded trucks and containers, handling variable parcels via learned manipulation policies that improve from operational data.
notes: Founded 2018 by Ariana Eisenstein, Andrew Meyer, Dan Paluska. HQ Cambridge/Charlestown, MA (Greater Boston). Total funding ~$87M over 7 rounds per Crunchbase/Tracxn. $50M Series B closed Nov 20, 2024 (some sources date the round Apr 2024); participants: Teradyne Robotics Ventures, Toyota Ventures, Ranpak, Third Kind VC, One Madison Group, Hyperplane, Catapult Ventures — no single lead explicitly named. With Series B, 6 customers ordered 30+ production unload robots. In scope: AI-native variable-item manipulation; unloading unstructured floor-loaded trailers requires learned policies, not classical pre-programmed arm motion. Team size not publicly disclosed. [AI_intensity: Manipulation uses a learned policy. Pickle builds custom learned perception+grasp models per use case on pre-existing models; robots "work day 1 and get better over time" from operational data to handle variable, unknown packages in floor-loaded trailers/containers. Not pre-programmed pick poses.]
business_model: Sells/leases its "Pickle Unload System" (an AI-driven robotic arm on a mobile base, e.g. "Dill," that unloads floor-loaded trucks and containers) to logistics and distribution operators. Reported as a robots-as-a-service/lease model at roughly $70,000 per robot per year (one remote operator supervises 5-6 robots), and also via outright capital orders such as UPS's $120M purchase of 400 units.
customers: Named customers include UPS (Dec 2025 order: ~$120M for 400 robots, deploying H2 2026-2027; UPS first piloted in 2023), Ryobi Tools, and Yusen Logistics; in Q3 2024 six customers ordered 30+ robots spanning footwear, apparel, power tools, and general-merchandise distributors.
revenue_status: scaling (no public ARR figure disclosed; largest known contract is UPS's ~$120M order for 400 robots announced Dec 2025; company has raised ~$97M total including a $50M Series B in Nov 2024)
technical_differentiation: Versus Boston Dynamics Stretch and Dexterity, Pickle is a purpose-built unitasker optimized solely for unloading floor-loaded trailers/containers, citing a peak rate of ~1,800 boxes/hour (roughly 2x Stretch's ~800) and a human-in-the-loop supervision model (one operator per 5-6 robots) rather than Boston Dynamics' fully autonomous, multi-task approach.

one_liner: Robot-agnostic 3D vision and AI perception (PickOne) generates pick points for variable parcels in warehouse induction/sorting, with human-in-the-loop (Yonder) fallback.
notes: Borderline-but-included per in-doubt rule. AI is perception-centric: PickOne uses 2D/3D imaging plus ML to find pick points for variable, unstructured parcels (AI-trained variable-item manipulation enables a category that pre-programmed arms cannot serve), but the control stack is largely classical/robot-agnostic and relies on human-in-the-loop supervised autonomy (Yonder crew chiefs) rather than a learned end-to-end policy. Total funding figures vary by source (~$94M-$99M; "nearly $100M" per company); includes 2018 seed (led by Schematic Ventures), 2021 ~$33M Series B, and 2023 $50M Series C led by Scale Venture Partners (other participants: Top Tier, Tyche, ROBO Global Ventures, Translink, McRock, Pritzker Group VC). Team size estimates range 51-200 (LinkedIn) with PitchBook/RocketReach ~64-66. Sources: plusonerobotics.com/about; businesswire.com 2023-03-06; techcrunch.com 2023-03-07; therobotreport.com; crunchbase.com/organization/plus-one-robotics; prnewswire stealth-exit 2018. [AI_intensity: Perception/pick-point generation uses machine learning (2D/3D image processing + ML) for variable-item handling. Manipulation/motion is largely classical and robot-agnostic; no learned end-to-end control policy (RL/IL/VLA) evidenced. Planning leans on supervised autonomy with human-in-the-loop (Yonder). AI is concentrated in perception, not in a learned control policy.]
business_model: Sells robot-agnostic 3D vision/AI software (PickOne) and the Yonder supervised-autonomy platform, plus integrated robotic work cells (DepalOne, InductOne, PickOne) to warehouse, parcel, and logistics operators; deals combine upfront hardware/integration (e.g., DepalOne reportedly starting ~$155K) with recurring software licensing and subscription fees for the human-in-the-loop "Crew Chief" remote-supervision service. Robots are typically sold/integrated alongside arm partner Yaskawa Motoman.
customers: FedEx (16+ robotic arms across the Memphis Express World Hub plus mixed depalletizing at Reno), DHL, GEODIS, and MSC Industrial are named deployments; Plus One states its systems perform over 1 million parcel picks/day and have surpassed half a billion lifetime picks.
revenue_status: scaling (~$17.4M revenue, unverified third-party estimate per ZoomInfo/KonaEquity; other trackers range $5M-$100M, so figure is uncertain)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on being robot/arm-agnostic 3D vision plus its Yonder human-in-the-loop remote intervention (Crew Chief) model that keeps lines running on edge-case parcels, vs. Ambi Robotics (AmbiSort, more vertically integrated sortation cells) and Berkshire Grey/Dexterity (full integrated pick-and-place systems); Plus One sells the perception/supervision layer rather than locking customers to its own arm.

one_liner: Tackles diverse-SKU warehouse picking/sorting via a 3D-AI vision processor with deep-learning grasp planning and tool-changing, enabling one robot to handle variable items.
notes: Shanghai-based (Minhang District), founded 2018 from a campus lab project; top-3 in Amazon Robotics Challenge three consecutive years. Core product is a 3D-AI Vision processor for pick-anything/place-anywhere with deep-learning grasp planning and tool-changing for diverse SKUs. AI-native variable-item manipulation => in_scope=true. Funding history: Series B $35M (Jul 2021, Capital Today lead), Series B+ $40M (Jun 2022, Capital Today lead); aggregate reported >$100M. Some sources list a 'Series B-II' and total ~$103M; most recent clearly-dated/led round is the Jun 2022 Series B+. Team size not reliably found (Crunchbase profile returned 403). also_known_as Chinese name approximate. [AI_intensity: Perception and manipulation use learned policies: deep-learning 3D vision for instance segmentation/pose and a learned grasp-planning policy for variable, unseen items (hand-eye grasp synthesis). Motion planning is more classical.]
business_model: Sells AI-powered robotic picking/sorting workcells (depalletizing, mixed-case palletizing, piece/bin picking) built on its XYZ Studio Max 3D-vision software and deep-learning grasp planning, sold as integrated hardware-plus-software systems to warehouse and factory operators via capex purchase/system-integration contracts (often deployed in multi-station fleets, e.g., 25 units at one electronics DC). Revenue comes from robot/cell sales and accompanying vision software and integration/support.
customers: Named deployments include Trancom (large Japanese 3PL, via partner Chinoh.Ai) and an unnamed electronics distribution center running 25 XYZ picking systems; broader base spans automotive, new energy, pharmaceuticals, e-commerce/retail and 3PL across China, US, Germany, Japan and South Korea. Most end-customer names beyond Trancom are undisclosed.
revenue_status: scaling (third-party estimate ~$50M-$100M revenue range, unverified; ~$100M total funding raised incl. $20M Series A+ led by Source Code Capital)
technical_differentiation: Vs Mech-Mind Robotics and RightHand Robotics: XYZ emphasizes registration-free recognition of new/unseen SKUs (vision-matching plus automatic calibration) and a 3D-AI vision processor with deep-learning grasp planning and automatic tool-changing so one robot handles diverse SKUs across both case-level (de/palletizing) and piece-level picking. Mech-Mind is broader industrial 3D-vision platform; RightHand focuses on piece-picking end-effector/fleet, giving XYZ a wider single-arm task range as its edge.

one_liner: Argus OS uses 3D scans, vision and AI to auto-identify parts and program robotic workcells for surface finishing and welding in high-mix manufacturing without manual reprogramming.
notes: HQ in Brooklyn, NY (Industry City), US; founded 2022 (Techstars-backed). Funding: $50K FuzeHub grant (Apr 2023) and an undisclosed Angel round (Feb 2025) per Crunchbase; total funding not disclosed. Team 2-10 per LinkedIn. NVIDIA Inception member. Borderline scope: product is AI-native adaptive manipulation for high-mix, high-variability finishing/welding (sanding, grinding, deburring, polishing, welding) that targets workpieces conventional pre-programmed industrial arms cannot handle without per-part reprogramming — kept in_scope=true because the value prop is AI/vision-driven auto-programming and part adaptation. Whether a learned policy drives core motion is unconfirmed; perception is AI, motion may be classical toolpath planning. Sources: cohesiverobotics.com, Crunchbase, PitchBook, LinkedIn, Industry City. [AI_intensity: unclear — Argus OS clearly uses learned AI for perception (3D scan-based part identification/classification driving the workflow), but the core finishing/welding motion appears to rely on AI-assisted auto-generated toolpaths rather than a confirmed end-to-end learned manipulation policy (no public evidence of RL/IL/diffusion/VLA driving motion). Perception is AI-native; manipulation is likely classical toolpath planning seeded by AI perception.]
business_model: Sells turnkey "Smart" robotic workcells (Smart Finishing and Smart Welding) powered by its proprietary Argus OS software, bundling off-the-shelf industrial/cobot arms with 3D vision sensors and AI auto-programming for high-mix surface finishing, sanding/deburring, and MIG/TIG welding. Buyers are high-mix manufacturers (aerospace, defense, metal fab, marine, medical, food equipment) who own the hardware via capital purchase financed through partner Anthem Capital Group (100% financing, 24-84 month terms, from ~$2,500/month); the company explicitly rejects a pure robotics-as-a-service/subscription model.
customers: No paying customers are named publicly. Disclosed deployments are anonymized case studies (a surface-finishing workcell case study published via 3D-camera vendor Zivid) serving aerospace, defense, metal fabrication, marine/boatbuilding, medical, and food-processing-equipment makers; the company cites a project backlog and U.S. deployments but discloses no org names. Effectively undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on Argus OS, which uses 3D scanning plus AI to auto-identify parts and generate robot programs with "no programming required" for high-mix, low-volume jobs, layered on standard robot-arm OEMs rather than proprietary hardware. Versus closest named competitors GrayMatter Robotics (AI sanding/finishing, Scan&Plan) and Path Robotics (autonomous AI welding), Cohesive spans both finishing and welding under one OS and goes to market on owned/financed equipment instead of RaaS; otherwise the vision-plus-AI auto-programming approach is broadly similar to GrayMatter and Path.

one_liner: AI machine-vision platform for manufacturing quality inspection; self-training deep-learning models learn what 'good' looks like and continuously improve detection of surface, dimensional, assembly and label defects.
team_size: ~37-200 (37 per CB Insights end-2023; ~200 per PitchBook)
notes: Founded 2017 by Arye Barnehama and Bill Gross (Idealab); HQ South Pasadena/LA area, CA with offices in Chicago and Mexico City. Funding history: pre-seed/$3.6M seed (Fika, Fathom, Toyota AI Ventures), $12.7M Series A 2020-06 (Threshold Ventures), $30M Series B 2021-12 led by Tiger Global. A later strategic round (~2024-05) added Rockwell Automation; total funding reported between $47.5M (2021) and $66.3M (PitchBook 2026) — exact most-recent round size undisclosed. SCOPE: Borderline L6 — vision inspection rather than learned manipulation. Included per 'when in doubt, in scope': the product is AI-native (adaptive self-training defect-detection models that improve per inspection); traditional rule-based machine vision exists, but Elementary's continuous-learning approach is the defining AI element. ai_intensity is perception-only; no learned robot control policy. Funding amount approximate due to source discrepancy. [AI_intensity: Perception is the learned core: self-training/adaptive deep-learning vision models classify and continuously improve defect detection (anomaly/defect classification). No learned manipulation, navigation, or planning policy — it is a fixed-station vision inspection appliance, not a robot motion policy.]
business_model: Sells an integrated AI machine-vision quality-inspection stack to Fortune 500 manufacturers: the QualityOS cloud platform (analytics, traceability) plus on-prem AI vision controllers/cameras and the VisionStream self-training defect-detection engine. Goes to market via consultative enterprise sales (no public list pricing); revenue comes from hardware deployments plus recurring cloud/software subscriptions and professional services, sold per production line/inspection station.
customers: Named manufacturing deployments include Toyota (Indiana plant), Milwaukee Tool, and Topcon; serves Fortune 500 manufacturers across automotive, CPG, electronics, food & beverage, and life sciences, inspecting 1B+ parts annually. Strategic investor Rockwell Automation also acts as a go-to-market channel partner.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; ~$66M+ total funding raised including $30M Series B led by Tiger Global, Dec 2021, plus 2024 strategic investment from Rockwell Automation and Toyota Ventures)
technical_differentiation: VisionStream's self-training "learn by observation" model reaches ~99.9% accuracy in seconds with no manual labeling or rule tuning, versus Landing AI (LandingLens), which uses a more data-centric, human-labeled training workflow. Vs Instrumental (ex-Apple founders, electronics/NPI-focused, combines visual + product data for root-cause), Elementary is broader cross-industry and pairs cloud QualityOS with its own edge vision controllers and OT/MES/ERP integrations rather than electronics-only deployment.

one_liner: AI-driven dual-arm robotic "blacksmiths" incrementally form sheet metal directly from CAD using learned forming decisions, eliminating custom dies and molds for flexible metal manufacturing.
notes: Founders Edward Mehr (CEO) and Babak Raeisinia; HQ in Chatsworth/Los Angeles, CA. Founded 2019 (some sources say 2018). Series B ($32M, Oct 2023) led by NVIDIA's NVentures and Innovation Endeavors brought total to $45M; earlier funding included a $21.8M round with Lockheed Martin Ventures. Most recent confirmable round is the Oct 2023 Series B. Scope: AI-native flexible forming — RoboForming reaches complex geometries directly from CAD without dies/molds via AI-driven adaptive manipulation and sensing; borderline on learned-policy vs model-predictive control, but vendor describes ML/RL-driven forming decisions, so in_scope=true. Team size not reliably found. Customers span aerospace, defense, automotive, heavy machinery. [AI_intensity: Manipulation: the incremental sheet-forming uses AI-driven sensors plus ML/RL-informed forming decisions to adaptively determine deformation amount and location to reach geometry; vendor describes RL/ML, though core may blend with model-predictive control. Perception via AI sensor arrays; digital twins for process characterization.]
business_model: Two-pronged hardware + parts model: Machina either sells/deploys its RoboCraftsman dual-arm robotic forming systems (including a containerized "Deployable" unit) to customer facilities, or operates them in-house as contract manufacturing, producing aerospace/defense-grade sheet-metal structures and hard-to-source legacy aircraft parts directly from CAD (no dies). Revenue comes mostly via government/defense manufacturing contracts and a planned U.S. "Intelligent Factory" producing parts at production scale.
customers: U.S. Air Force (Warner Robins Air Logistics Complex / Robins AFB), U.S. Department of Energy / Lawrence Livermore National Lab, and large aerospace, defense, and automotive OEMs. Strategic investors who are also partners/customers include Lockheed Martin (Ventures) and Toyota (Woven Capital). Reported ~$14M in defense contract awards as of April 2025.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$5M revenue FY2022 per CB Insights/PitchBook; ~$14M defense contract awards as of April 2025, unverified). No audited recent ARR figure publicly disclosed.
technical_differentiation: Vs. traditional die-stamping and vs. incremental-forming peers, Machina's RoboForming uses two coordinated 7-axis robots with learned/AI forming decisions and in-process sensing to form complex metal parts without geometry-specific dies, dramatically cutting tooling cost and lead time. Closest named competitors are Desktop Metal/Markforged (metal additive, but limited size/material vs. Machina's large aerospace-grade sheet structures) and conventional press/stamping shops; Machina is differentiated by die-less, software-defined, dual-arm robotic forming at production scale plus deployable on-site units.

one_liner: MIRAI is a neural-network robot control system that learns hand-eye-coordinated manipulation tasks from human demonstration, adapting in real time to variance in position, shape, color, and lighting.
last_round_lead: Metaplanet, VSquared Ventures, Ahren Innovation Capital (co-leads)
notes: AI-native: MIRAI replaces hard-coded programming with a learned (imitation) policy that adapts to environmental variance in real time, enabling automation of assembly, material handling, and quality-control tasks that could not previously be automated with traditional pre-programmed arms. Deployed by Siemens Energy, ZF Group, BSH. Founded 2014 in Berlin (R&D HQ); reincorporated as a US-based company in Sept 2024 with US offices in San Francisco and Los Angeles, so HQ attribution is increasingly US — primary operations/founding remain Berlin, DE. Funding history: Angel 2015, Seed (~$2.5M, Oct 2016/2017), Series A ($6.08M, 2020), Series B ($30M, Feb 2022). Total ~$40.34M per Crunchbase. Team size varies by source (62 PitchBook 2026, 67 EasyLeadz 2025, 69 GetLatka 2024). Sources: crunchbase.com/organization/micropsi-industries; businesswire.com Series B release 2022-02-10; therobotreport.com; pitchbook.com profile 102086-02. [AI_intensity: Manipulation uses a learned imitation/neural-network policy: MIRAI is trained from human demonstration, correction, and repetition rather than programmed, and generates real-time motion that adapts to variance in object position, shape, color, and lighting. Learned policy for the core manipulation task.]
business_model: Sells MIRAI / MIRAI 2, an AI robot-control software product (edge controller + cloud-based ML training service + training app) that retrofits onto third-party industrial robot arms (e.g. Universal Robots, FANUC) for manufacturers. Revenue comes from software licenses/subscriptions plus deployment, sold to factory automation buyers via direct sales and integrator partners; pricing is quote-based and not publicly listed.
customers: BSH Hausgeräte GmbH (Bosch/Siemens home appliances) uses MIRAI to automate leak testing; the company also cites deployments in automotive and electronics manufacturing. Most other named customers are undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$6.7M revenue/ARR, 2024, unverified per Latka); raised ~$43-45M total including a $30M Series B (Metaplanet, Ahren Innovation Capital, Vsquared Ventures)
technical_differentiation: vs Covariant and Intrinsic (Alphabet): MIRAI focuses on real-time, closed-loop hand-eye-coordinated fine manipulation/assembly learned from a few human demonstrations, adapting continuously to position/shape/lighting/background variance, and in MIRAI 2 removes the force-torque sensor requirement; this contrasts with Covariant's pick-and-place foundation-model approach and Intrinsic's broader developer-platform/programming toolkit.

one_liner: Builds AI-driven 'cognitive' robots and humanoids (MAiRA series) that combine environmental perception with adaptive decision-making for manufacturing, logistics and human-robot collaboration.
last_round_lead: Tether Holdings (reported; backing also from Amazon, Qatari investor)
notes: HQ Metzingen, Germany; founded 2019 by David Reger (CEO). MAiRA marketed as world's first cognitive robot (launched 2021). Jan 2025: €120M Series B led by Lingotto Investment Management (investors incl. Volvo Cars Tech Fund, HV Capital, Vsquared, Delta Electronics). March 2026: reportedly raising ~€1B/$1.2B (Tether-backed, Amazon + Qatari investor) at ~€4B valuation; round not yet officially confirmed by company, hence figures marked reported. Borderline L6: collaborative arms exist without AI, but the cognitive/learned-perception positioning plus general-purpose humanoid (4NE-1) roadmap put it in scope. Team grew from ~300 (2024) toward ~1200. Sources: neura-robotics.com/company; businesswire Series B; Bloomberg/SiliconANGLE €1B round. [AI_intensity: Markets learned perception (vision/touch sensor fusion) and adaptive task execution; cognitive robots and humanoid roadmap use learned policies for manipulation/perception. Exact policy type (RL/IL/VLA) not fully disclosed — unclear but AI-native positioning is central, not a bolt-on.]
business_model: Sells AI-driven "cognitive" robots and humanoids as hardware-plus-software: industrial cobots (MAiRA, LARA) priced ~EUR 5,000-40,000, the 4NE-1 humanoid (Studio F.A. Porsche design) at ~EUR 98,000 per unit dropping to ~EUR 60,000 for fleet orders of 20+, and the consumer/service MiPA below EUR 10,000. Also earns via white-label OEM deals where partners (e.g., Kawasaki) resell NEURA's platform, plus its Neuraverse app/skill marketplace for robot capabilities.
customers: White-label/OEM and strategic partners include Kawasaki Robotics (reselling cobots on NEURA's platform), Delta Electronics, Omron, Bosch, Schaeffler, SAP, NVIDIA, Vodafone, and Volvo Cars (investor/partner); reported order book approaching ~EUR 1 billion. Individual end-customer names beyond these partners are largely undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no specific revenue/ARR figure publicly disclosed; company cites "10x revenue growth" in 2024 and a ~EUR 1B multi-year order book/backlog, not recognized revenue)
technical_differentiation: Positions on "cognitive" robots that fuse onboard multi-modal perception (vision, touch, audio) with adaptive decision-making and a unified hardware+AI+app-marketplace (Neuraverse) stack, spanning cobots through humanoids. Versus humanoid rivals Figure AI and Agility Robotics, NEURA differentiates by leveraging its existing industrial cobot revenue/manufacturing base and OEM white-label channel (Kawasaki/Omron) rather than building only a single humanoid platform.

one_liner: Tackles welder labor shortages with autonomous welding cells powered by Obsidian, a foundational AI model trained on millions of weld inches to adapt in real time to part geometry.
last_round_lead: Matter Venture Partners and Drive Capital
notes: Founded 2018 by brothers Andy (CEO) and Alex Lonsberry; HQ at 3950 Business Park Drive, Columbus, OH. AI-native per bar: traditional robotic welding requires manual pre-programming per part; Path's Obsidian model learns to weld varying, never-before-seen parts autonomously (adaptive learned manipulation), so the category would not exist without learned policies => in_scope=true. Products: intelligent welding cells and Rove mobile welding platform (for shipbuilding/heavy industry), both powered by Obsidian. Total funding figures vary by source (~$250-271M cumulative incl. prior $170M and Oct 2024 $100M Series D); using Crunchbase/Tracxn ~$271M. Team size estimates range 156-198 (Mar 2026); using ~190. Series D (Oct 14, 2024) participants: Yamaha Ventures, Taiwania Capital, MediaTek, Catapult Ventures, Gaingels, Addition, Tiger Global, Basis Set. [AI_intensity: Manipulation (welding) uses a learned policy: Obsidian is a foundational physical-AI model trained on tens of millions of real weld inches, enabling the robot to perceive part geometry/fit-up and adapt weld execution in real time rather than running pre-programmed paths. Learned perception + decision/manipulation.]
business_model: Sells autonomous welding capacity to fabricators and OEMs via a Robotics-as-a-Service model: rather than selling capital equipment, Path operates its intelligent welding cells (AW-3, AF-1, and the new Rove mobile/quadruped system, all powered by its Obsidian welding foundation model) and charges a recurring subscription/per-capacity fee with $0 customer capex, bundling maintenance, software updates, and 24/7 Mission Control production support. Its Path Foundry offering also provides contract-manufacturing welding services where Path runs production on the customer's behalf.
customers: Named adopters/pilots include the U.S. Navy (partnership to deliver 20+ autonomous ships by 2027), Saronic Technologies (early Rove adopter for shipbuilding in Franklin, LA), and LAD Services. Verticals served include defense, shipbuilding, AI data centers, energy infrastructure, and construction/mining equipment fabricators.
revenue_status: scaling (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; ~$300-341M total raised, ~$4B valuation reported 2025-2026)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by Obsidian, a purpose-built welding foundation model trained on tens of millions of weld inches / 200,000+ hours of real welding data, enabling autonomous adaptation to high-mix, low-volume part variability that defeats conventional teach-and-repeat cells. Closest competitors are incumbents FANUC and ABB, whose adaptive welding modules adjust torch angle/gap on the fly but lack a learned foundation model and full RaaS turnkey operation; among startups, Novarc Technologies is the nearest named peer but is narrower (pipe/spool welding cobots).

one_liner: Deploys AI-enabled bi-manual robotic arms for food assembly in meal-production plants, using a diffusion-based imitation-learning "Food Foundation Model" to handle deformable, sticky, inconsistent ingredients.
notes: Founded 2019 by CEO Rajat Bhageria. AI-native variable-item manipulation: food is deformable/sticky/wet/inconsistent with no off-the-shelf sim data, so Chef uses diffusion-based imitation learning (30-40 human demos -> diffusion policy) plus KNN fine-tuning for its "Food Foundation Model" — in_scope=true. Series A April 2025 announced as $43.1M combined: $20.6M equity (led by Avataar Venture Partners; Construct Capital, Bloomberg Beta, Promus, MFV, Interwoven, HCVC, Alumni, MaC, Red and Blue, Tau, Siddhi, BOLD also participated) + $22.5M equipment-financing debt. Total raised ~$65.6M (~$38.8M equity, ~$26.75M debt). 44M+ servings produced; customers include Amy's Kitchen, Sunbasket, Chef Bombay, Cafe Spice. Team size ~140 per Tracxn/CB Insights (May 2026). Was not previously in census. [AI_intensity: Core manipulation (food assembly/plating) runs on a learned diffusion policy trained via imitation learning from 30-40 human demonstrations per item, with KNN fine-tuning — its "Food Foundation Model." Learned policy for manipulation; clears the AI-intensity bar.]
business_model: Robotics-as-a-Service: food manufacturers lease Chef's AI-enabled bi-manual food-assembly robots (the Chef system / "ChefOS" + Food Foundation Model) for prep-table portioning, paying a small upfront deployment fee plus recurring monthly/quarterly subscription with no large CapEx. Chef self-funds the hardware via equipment-financing debt against signed customer contracts.
customers: Named paying food manufacturers/brands: Amy's Kitchen (first customer, 2022), Sunbasket, Chef Bombay, and Cafe Spice; serves customers across the US and Canada with planned UK expansion. Amy's Kitchen reported 12% better run consistency, 4% less food giveaway, and 17% higher labor productivity.
revenue_status: scaling (no public ARR/revenue dollar figure disclosed; reports quadrupling recurring revenue 2022-2023 and surpassing 100 million servings in production by April 2026)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a diffusion-based imitation-learning "Food Foundation Model" trained on 100M+ real-world servings, letting bi-manual arms generalize across new ingredients, textures, and portion sizes vs. more rigid/single-arm peers. Closest named competitors are Dexterity (general industrial/logistics manipulation) and Hyphen (automated makeline systems); Chef's edge is a food-specific generalist AI plus a deployed data flywheel (claims more meals produced than all other food-robotics startups combined).

one_liner: Builds Alfred, a collaborative robot arm that scoops and assembles salads and bowls in existing commercial kitchens using everyday utensils, handling deformable foods.
notes: Spun out of Draper Laboratory (with MIT/Harvard research roots) in 2018; CEO/co-founder David M.S. Johnson. HQ in Somerville/Greater Boston, MA (near Cambridge; assignment listed Cambridge). Product 'Alfred' is a cobot that uses standard utensils to assemble salads/poke bowls in existing kitchens; differentiator is manipulation of deformable, shape-changing foods (ice cream, soft fruit, sushi-grade tuna, pico de gallo). Total funding ~$15.5M over ~3 rounds: pre-seed (~2019, NFX), $5.5M seed (2020, led by Hyperplane VC) — Crunchbase/Tracxn aggregate also cites an $8.76M seed and a 2022 round/debt; figures vary by source. Deployments include Travis Air Force Base and a $1.6M DLA contract for 10 robots, plus BONAPITA restaurant. IN SCOPE: AI-trained variable-item manipulation of deformable foods is an AI-native problem hard to solve with classical planning alone. Exact policy type (IL/RL vs model-based/heuristic) not confirmed in public sources — included with note per instruction. Team size and most recent round details not reliably findable. [AI_intensity: unclear — perception (machine vision for ingredient detection) and manipulation of deformable/shape-changing foods are core differentiators that company markets as breakthrough AI/ML; public sources confirm ML/computer-vision but do not specify whether the manipulation policy is learned (IL/RL) versus model-based/heuristic control. Deformable-food handling strongly implies learned perception, but exact policy type is unverified.]
business_model: Sells/leases Alfred, a collaborative robot-arm sous-chef that scoops and assembles bowls, salads, and similar dishes in commercial kitchens, via a robotics-as-a-service (RaaS) model charging a flat per-dish fee, plus system sales, maintenance contracts, and app-based data analytics. Buyers are foodservice operators, institutional cafeterias, and government dining facilities (e.g., a $1.6M Defense Logistics Agency contract for 10 units).
customers: U.S. Defense Logistics Agency / U.S. Department of Defense (10-unit, $1.6M contract; first deployment at the Monarch Dining Facility, Travis Air Force Base, CA) and BONAPITA restaurant in West Roxbury/Boston, Massachusetts.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$1.6M DLA contract; no company-wide revenue/ARR figure publicly disclosed; acquired by Sony Sept 2025)
technical_differentiation: Unlike Chef Robotics (which retrofits gantry/dispensing systems for high-volume food assembly lines) and Hyphen (modular under-counter "makeline" automation), Dexai's Alfred is a single collaborative arm that uses everyday human utensils and AI vision to adapt across diverse dishes in existing kitchens without retooling, and is NSF food-safety certified.

one_liner: Robotics-driven restaurant chain; its Fusion machine assembles salad bowls (~360/hr) via conveyor and 3D-printed food cartridges, using ML for inventory, weighing/dispensing, and allergen-detecting computer vision.
notes: London robotics restaurant startup, founded 2023 by Josef Chen (CTO Ivan Tregear; co-founders also cited as David Sharp and Piers Millar). Raised $1.8M seed (Feb 2024) led by HodlCo, 5 investors; PitchBook lists ~$4.45M total but the $1.8M seed is the confirmed/most-cited figure, so total_funding_usd reported conservatively as $1.8M. Fusion machine: ~360 bowls/hr, 10-26s per bowl, conveyor + 3D-printed food-safe cartridges. ML-powered inventory management plus self-trained CV model identifying food on shelves and detecting allergen cross-contamination. SCOPE: marked in_scope=true under when-in-doubt rule because it features AI-trained variable-item perception/handling in a food-automation context, though the core assembly hardware is largely mechanical/pre-programmed rather than a learned manipulation policy. ai_intensity for the robot's core manipulation is unconfirmed — learned component is CV/inventory, not manipulation. TIME (May 2026) profiled the restaurant. Source CTOL Digital and Crunchbase corroborate $1.8M seed. [AI_intensity: Borderline. The learned components are computer vision (identifying food on shelves, detecting allergen cross-contamination) plus ML for inventory and weighing/dispensing. The core manipulation/assembly task runs on a conveyor + 3D-printed food-cartridge mechanical system that appears pre-programmed, not a learned manipulation policy. No RL/IL/diffusion/VLA manipulation confirmed; AI is in perception/inventory rather than the robot's core manipulation.]
business_model: Vertically integrated robotics restaurant operator: Kaikaku runs its own robot-powered restaurant brand (Common Room, Brunswick Centre, London) selling salad/grain bowls assembled by its Fusion machine (~360 bowls/hr via conveyor and 3D-printed food cartridges), and intends to sell/license the cloud-based Fusion system (robot modules, ordering kiosks, ML inventory/QA/analytics) to third-party operators.
