Revenue today is primarily own-store food sales, with B2B hardware/software licensing as the intended scaling model; no public per-unit or contract pricing disclosed.
customers: Primary "customer" is its own-brand Common Room restaurant in London (Brunswick Centre). For third-party deployment it reportedly is in trial talks with UK salad chain Tossed; broader paying external operators are otherwise undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; company reports ~95% robot uptime and claims profit margins ~4x traditional restaurants, unverified; total funding ~$4.45M per Tracxn, latest reported raise $1.8M/£1.4M)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via an armless conveyor-plus-funnel modular dispensing system with custom food-safe 3D-printed cartridges and ML-driven inventory/QA, rather than the robotic-arm approach of peers; closest named competitors are Chef Robotics (US, robotic-arm food assembly) and Sweetgreen's Infinite Kitchen (ex-Spyce) plus UK's Karakuri. Kaikaku's edge is a vertically integrated own-restaurant proof point and lower-cost modular hardware, but it competes largely on execution and capital in a capital-intensive niche.

one_liner: Builds Flippy, a rail-mounted robot automating QSR fry stations using a proprietary AI/NVIDIA vision system trained on millions of real-world basket-frying data points.
total_funding_usd: ~96900000 (reported ~$96.9M; estimates vary $65M-$127M across sources due to crowdfunding rounds)
notes: [Restored to census at GATE2 review per Zian: counts as learned cooking manipulation of variable food items; overrides the earlier in_scope=false scope-filter call.] BORDERLINE case resolved to in_scope=false after verification. Fry-station/basket frying is a product category that exists without AI (conventional automated fryers and pre-programmed arms accomplish it). Miso's ML is concentrated in a perception/vision system (basket and food detection, trained on millions of real-world baskets, built with NVIDIA), while the manipulation is pre-programmed pick-and-place on a fixed rail — there is no learned end-to-end manipulation policy (no RL/IL/VLA). Per scope filter, AI is added as enhancement to an otherwise non-AI-native category, so excluded. real_robotics_ai=true (genuine learned vision in a real robot) but does not meet the AI-native manipulation bar. Founded 2016 (Pasadena, CA). CEO Rich Hull, CTO Bachir Kharraja (both 2023). Acquired Zignyl (restaurant ops software) early 2026. Funding is heavily crowdfunding-driven (Reg A+/SeedInvest/DealMaker plus strategic investor Ecolab), so totals vary widely by source. Newest Flippy processes ~100+ baskets/hr. Sources: company site misorobotics.com, qsrweb.com launch PR, therobotreport.com, SEC annual report 2025, Crunchbase/Tracxn/PitchBook for funding. [AI_intensity: Perception uses a learned ML vision system (food/basket detection, trained on real-world data, developed with NVIDIA). Manipulation/navigation are NOT learned — arm motion is pre-programmed pick-and-place on a fixed rail; no RL/IL/VLA end-to-end manipulation policy. Planning is classical. AI is centered on perception only.]
business_model: Sells/leases its Flippy Fry Station robot to QSR chains on a Robotics-as-a-Service (RaaS) subscription rather than upfront sale; reported base configuration averages ~$3,000/month and higher fry-cook configs ~$5,400/month, bundling software updates, support and maintenance. Distribution is augmented via a partnership with Ecolab, whose ~6,000-person sales force offers Flippy rentals to its restaurant customers.
customers: Named deployments/pilots include White Castle (fries), Jack in the Box (fries/drinks), Wing Zone (wings), Chipotle (chips), and CaliExpress by Flippy (Miso's own robot-run flagship). As of 2025, the arm was installed in roughly 13 restaurants across White Castle and Jack in the Box, with additional installs scheduled; distribution partner Ecolab.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$3M revenue FY2022 per third-party data; no verified FY2024/2025 figure publicly disclosed; ~$129M total raised including Reg A+/crowdfunding)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on a purpose-built, rail-mounted fry-station form factor with proprietary NVIDIA-based vision models trained on millions of real-world basket-fry events and a fast (~hours, 75% less install time) retrofit into existing QSR lines. Versus Chef Robotics (focused on high-mix food-assembly in food-manufacturing/co-packing) and Nala Robotics (full multi-cuisine autonomous kitchens), Miso is narrower and deeper on the QSR back-of-house fry/grill task.

one_liner: Countertop home cooking robot that uses AI and a top-mounted camera (computer vision) to dispense, stir, and adjust heat in real time, adapting across 500+ recipes.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (8000000+ disclosed via Series A plus earlier undisclosed seed/angel rounds)
notes: Formerly Nymble / Nymble Labs; rebranded to Posha and emerged from stealth May 2025. Founded 2017 by Raghav Gupta (co-founder Rohin Malhotra cited in some sources); originally began in Bengaluru, India, now San Francisco-based US company. Debuted product as Nymble at CES 2024. Series A $8M led by Accel (May 2025), with Xeed Ventures, WaterBridge Ventures, and angel Binny Bansal (Flipkart). Earlier seed/angel rounds undisclosed. IN SCOPE (borderline-strong): adaptive CV-based perception adjusts cooking of variable, deformable foods and tolerates ingredient substitutions across 500-1,000+ recipes, beyond fixed-pod appliances. However, dispensing/heating/timing is conventional automation, so learned-policy claim is partial -> included with note per when-in-doubt rule. Maintains 500+ dishes across ~10 cuisines, validated via user feedback. Sources: Wikipedia, TechCrunch (2025-05-06), Inc42, Entrackr, TechFundingNews. [AI_intensity: Partial/borderline. Core perception uses computer-vision models (top-mounted camera) to monitor and adjust cooking (browning, simmering, ingredient substitution) in real time. Much of the pipeline (dispensing, heating, timing) is conventional pre-programmed automation; the learned-policy claim applies mainly to adaptive CV-based monitoring/adjustment, not a fully learned manipulation/control policy.]
business_model: Direct-to-consumer hardware sales of the Posha countertop cooking robot at ~$1,500 (originally listed $1,750; sold via $25 refundable pre-order deposit with balance due before shipment), plus a recurring "Posha membership" software subscription at ~$15/month for unlimited recipes, meal planning, and real-time tracking. Buyers are individual home consumers in the US.
customers: Individual home consumers (D2C); no named enterprise/pilot customers. Company reports the robot is used in "1000+ homes," having shipped ~200 units by mid-2025 and sold out its first production batch (now waitlisted). Otherwise undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; ~200 units shipped by mid-2025, first batch sold out, est. unverified hardware revenue only in low single-digit millions)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from Suvie (Cambridge, MA countertop kitchen robot using pre-portioned meal cartridges/refrigeration) and Moley Robotics (London robotic-arm kitchen) by being a compact, fully automated single-appliance unit that uses a top-mounted camera plus computer vision to dispense, stir, and adjust heat in real time across 1000+ recipes using regular grocery ingredients rather than proprietary meal kits or expensive robotic arms. Also positioned against India-based upliance.ai and Thermomix.

one_liner: Builds robotic strawberry and apple harvesters that use real-time ML vision to detect ripeness/size, adapt navigation, and selectively pick fruit, addressing farm labor shortages.
notes: HQ: Davis, California, US. Founded 2017 by Kyle Cobb and Carl Allendorph (some sources cite 2018; Crunchbase and acquisition coverage cite 2017). Funding history: ~$2M seed (Nov 2018, Catapult Ventures + Impact VC); $7.5M Series A (Aug 2019); $25M Series B (Sep 2021, led by Catapult Ventures, with Kubota, Yamaha Motor Ventures, Impact Ventures). Total ~$34.2M. First U.S. company to commercialize robotic strawberry harvesting (2019); flagship TX/T-6 strawberry harvester for soil beds plus a robotic apple harvester. STATUS: On April 2, 2025, CNH Industrial (an early investor) acquired Advanced Farm's assets and intellectual property; key team members joined CNH's technology/innovation divisions. CEO cited a challenging agtech investment climate. Classified acqui-hired (asset/IP sale + team absorption). IN SCOPE: AI-native variable-item manipulation — selectively picking ripe fruit of varying size/position requires learned vision and cannot be done by pre-programmed arms; not an AV company. [AI_intensity: Learned perception is core: real-time machine-learning vision models detect strawberry/apple ripeness and size and drive navigation/path adaptation plus selective gripper picking. Perception and manipulation rely on trained ML models (not pure pre-programmed rules); navigation/planning appears classical guided by learned vision.]
business_model: Robotic harvesting-as-a-service rather than selling machines: AFT deployed fleets of its T-6/TX robotic strawberry harvesters (and later a robotic apple harvester) to growers' fields on a service contract, charging on a piece-rate/per-volume basis modeled on traditional human harvest-labor contracts (de-risking grower capital outlay). Each grower got a dedicated AFT field operations manager to integrate the robots and pack fruit for retail.
customers: Driscoll's (berry grower-shipper, also a partner/customer) and California strawberry growers in the Watsonville, Santa Maria, and Oxnard areas; Washington State apple growers ran field trials of the apple harvester during the 2024 harvest season. Specific named paying contracts beyond grower partners are largely undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; raised ~$34M total funding across Series A $7.5M and Series B $25M before asset sale)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by a food-grade soft-robotic gripper plus real-time ML vision for ripeness/size detection and selective picking of in-soil field strawberries, and was one of the few to extend the same autonomy stack to apples (acquired by CNH Industrial in early 2025). Vs. closest competitors: Agrobot (Spain, also field-strawberry robotic harvester) and Tortuga AgTech (greenhouse/table-top berry picking); AFT focused on open-field harvest-as-a-service rather than tabletop/greenhouse systems.

one_liner: Builds the E-Series autonomous strawberry harvester with 24 robotic arms; deep-learning color/depth vision identifies ripe berries and selectively picks them without damage.
notes: Spanish ag-robotics company founded by Juan Bravo, who began developing a strawberry-picking robot in his garage ~2008; the SW 6010 prototype with 24 arms appeared in 2012, followed by the commercial E-Series. Founding year estimated ~2010 (company formalized between 2008 garage origins and 2012 prototype; exact incorporation year not published). HQ in Huelva/Badajoz region, Spain, with a US office in Oxnard, California. Funding: Crunchbase lists Agrobot as having taken undisclosed funding; specific round size/type/date/lead not publicly findable (Crunchbase page returned 403). Driscoll's (US berry producer) has been an early trial partner. In scope: this is AI-trained variable-item manipulation (selective ripe-fruit harvesting driven by learned RGB-D perception) — the category would not exist as a selective harvester without learned perception. Borderline because the arm/cutting control is classical mechatronics; AI is concentrated in the vision/ripeness-detection stage. Status active per ongoing manufacturing/trial reports. Founded_year, company existence, and one-liner each sourced; team size and detailed funding not findable, marked unknown/undisclosed rather than invented. [AI_intensity: Perception uses a learned policy: the E-Series applies deep-learning vision over color and depth (RGB-D) imagery to detect and classify strawberry ripeness for selective picking. Manipulation/arm control and the blade-cutting mechanism appear classical (pre-programmed mechatronics), not a learned policy. So AI intensity is concentrated in perception, not manipulation/navigation/planning.]
business_model: Sells and leases its E-Series autonomous strawberry harvester (up to 24 robotic arms, deep-learning color/depth vision) and the Agrobot aided-harvesting platform to commercial berry growers; historical capital-equipment pricing was cited around $250,000 per machine, with California growers tending to buy and seasonal users leasing.
customers: Berry growers in Spain and the US (Huelva HQ plus Oxnard, California office); Driscoll's is the main strategic investor/partner and field-trial collaborator. Specific paying customer names are not publicly disclosed beyond grower trials.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue or ARR figure disclosed; only ~$250K per-unit equipment pricing is referenced, not company revenue)
technical_differentiation: Focuses on selective robotic harvesting in open-field/table-top systems using integrated short-range color + infrared depth vision and a high count of independent arms, versus outdoor full-platform rival Harvest CROO Robotics (also Driscoll's-backed) and controlled-environment/greenhouse-focused Tortuga AgTech. Differentiation is its multi-arm modular E-Series and per-berry ripeness AI for field conditions rather than indoor settings.

one_liner: Solar-powered autonomous row-crop robots that use onboard computer-vision AI to distinguish weeds from crops and mechanically remove them without chemicals or diesel.
notes: HQ Kirkland, WA (Seattle metro), US. Founded 2020. Product: Aigen Element solar-powered autonomous weeding robot for row crops (sugar beets, cotton); gen2 launched 2025 with Bowles Farming. Funding: $4M seed (NEA, AgFunder), $12M Series A Nov 2023 (led by ReGen Ventures), and $10M round July 2025 (led by NEA) — total roughly $29M; some trackers cite ~$26-29M. Backers include NEA, ReGen VC, Bessemer, Cleveland Avenue, Global Founders Capital, AgFunder. In scope: AI-native weeding — chemical-free mechanical weeding driven by learned weed/crop perception (quantized CV model trained on SageMaker), a category that depends on learned vision rather than a pre-programmed task. Team size not reliably found. Last round type labeled venture/Series A extension; exact label undisclosed. [AI_intensity: Perception is a learned policy: a proprietary quantized computer-vision model (trained on AWS SageMaker) classifies weed vs crop in real time to direct mechanical weeding. Navigation/manipulation appear more classical, but core weeding perception is learned.]
business_model: Sells/leases its solar-powered autonomous "Element" weeding robots to commodity row-crop farmers as a per-season "Element Service": ~$50,000 per robot unit plus a recurring ~$20,000-per-fleet fee for system coordination, software, and farm-data insights. Buyers are large U.S. row-crop growers (cotton, sugar beet, soy); a single robot covers ~20-40 acres and fleets are sold per growing season.
customers: Named partner/customer: Bowles Farming Company, a sixth-generation family farm in California's Central Valley running Element gen2 robots in its Pima cotton fields for the 2025 season. Also piloted with sugar-beet growers in North Dakota; ~30 units running across three (mostly undisclosed) farms as of 2025. 2024/2025 pre-orders reportedly sold out in one day.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; ~$22.7M total raised incl. $12M Series A, ~30-50 robots deployed across ~3 farms in 2025)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on "true-solar" energy autonomy (solar panels plus battery let robots run 14+ hrs/day across a full season without recharging/refueling) combined with onboard computer-vision AI for mechanical, chemical-free weed striking. Vs. Carbon Robotics (LaserWeeder, diesel-tractor-towed laser implement) and FarmWise/Naio Technologies (mechanical/tractor-based weeders), Aigen's edge is the fully self-propelled, off-grid solar robot fleet requiring no chemicals, herbicide, or fossil-fuel power.

one_liner: AI laser-weeding robots for farms: a deep-learning plant model identifies crops vs weeds in real time, then high-power lasers kill weeds without chemicals.
notes: Founded 2018 by Paul Mikesell (legal entity MaKa Autonomous Robotic Systems). HQ Seattle, WA, US; operations span ~14 countries / 4 continents. Flagship product LaserWeeder uses deep-learning perception (Carbon AI / Large Plant Model, 150M+ images) for real-time crop-vs-weed identification driving laser weed elimination — AI-native; product cannot function without the trained model, so in_scope=true. Funding totals differ across sources: GeekWire (Oct 2025) reports ~$177M total to date; $70M Series D led by BOND with NVentures (NVIDIA) participation in Oct 2024; latest round Oct 2025 ~$20M Series D extension led by Giant Ventures. Tracxn lists higher cumulative ($276M) and slightly different round dates; used GeekWire/company-aligned figures. Team size ~265 (Feb 2026). Status active; planning a new secretive AI farm robot. [AI_intensity: Perception is the learned policy: Carbon AI / Large Plant Model is a deep-learning CV model trained on 150M+ images that detects and classifies crops vs weeds in real time at sub-mm precision to drive laser targeting. Manipulation/navigation are classical (laser galvanometers, tractor-towed implement).]
business_model: Sells AI laser-weeding hardware (the LaserWeeder and modular LaserWeeder G2 line, e.g. G2 200/400/600) directly to farms via capital purchase, with units priced roughly $0.5M-$1.4M depending on width, plus recurring annual support/service contracts sold in three seasonal tiers. Buyers are row-crop and specialty-produce growers replacing herbicides and hand-weeding labor.
customers: 100+ growers across 15 countries; named customers include Braga Fresh and Triangle Farms (Salinas Valley, CA), Hungenberg Produce Co. (cut weeding labor from ~$700K to ~$300K), and growers covered in Western Growers case studies. Has weeded 250,000+ acres across 100+ crops.
revenue_status: scaling (>$100M annual revenue FY ending Jan 31, 2026, per Business Wire Mar 2026; not yet profitable). Total funding ~$276M including a $70M Series D (Oct 2024) and a ~$20.8M round in Oct 2025.
technical_differentiation: Only commercial vendor using high-power lasers (vs. mechanical/chemical removal) to kill weeds, guided by a real-time deep-learning crop-vs-weed vision model; chemical- and contact-free. Differs from FarmWise (mechanical weeding/thinning, now part of Trimble) and John Deere's Blue River "See & Spray" (precision herbicide micro-dosing), and Naio Technologies (autonomous mechanical row-crop robots).

one_liner: Autonomous field robot (CLAWS) using deep-learning vision to distinguish weeds from crops and zap weed meristems with concentrated light, replacing chemicals and manual weeding.
notes: UK ag-robotics startup. CLAWS = Concentrated Light Autonomous Weeding and Scouting; battery/solar-powered lightweight rover. AI-native qualification rests on learned weed/crop discrimination perception driving the core task — without the deep-learning model the robot could not selectively target weeds, so in_scope=true. Borderline: navigation is classical RTK/satellite GNSS (not learned). Funding figures aggregate grants (ESA, UK Farming Innovation Programme, EIT Food) plus small equity/crowdfunding; Crunchbase/Tracxn report ~$730K-$942K total across ~4 rounds, latest dated ~May 2021; exact recent round size/lead undisclosed. Customer trial units 2025, commercial availability targeted 2026. Distinct from unrelated 'Clean Earth Rovers' (marine cleanup) and Earth Rover robotics (Chinese embodied-AI firm). Status active per ongoing 2025-2026 demos and PMW partnership. [AI_intensity: Perception uses a learned policy: proprietary deep-learning CV models on 3D/high-res camera imagery perform weed-vs-crop discrimination and meristem localization, driving the core weeding task. Navigation is classical RTK/satellite GNSS; planning/manipulation (light targeting) is largely classical.]
business_model: Robotics-as-a-service: Earth Rover sells "weeding/scouting as a service" via its CLAWS (Concentrated Light Autonomous Weeding and Scouting) robot to high-value vegetable, salad and organic produce growers, charged on a per-hectare basis at prices comparable to existing weed-control methods. Order book opened Q3 2025 with deliveries/pilot production from Q1 2026, so revenue is forward-looking.
customers: Not publicly disclosed by name. CLAWS is in a "Beta Pioneer" stage being trialled by early-adopter UK growers (the "Pioneers 2026" program); no specific paying farm customers are named in public sources.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; total funding ~$942K across 4 rounds plus ~EUR2M raised including ESA/UKRI grants over EUR750K and ~EUR500K from Mercia; commercial deliveries not starting until Q1 2026)
technical_differentiation: Uses non-laser "concentrated light" pulses aimed at the weed meristem from a lightweight, 100% battery/solar-powered autonomous rover (derived from ESA ExoMars rover tech with RTK satellite navigation), versus Carbon Robotics' LaserWeeder, which uses high-power CO2 lasers on a heavy tractor-pulled implement, and FarmWise/Naio, which use mechanical blades. Earth Rover differentiates on a lower-cost, lightweight, fully autonomous solar platform plus per-plant scouting data, but is far less mature and capitalized (~$1M raised vs Carbon Robotics' ~$177M).

one_liner: Cuts crop herbicide use up to 95% via the ARA ultra-high-precision sprayer, using deep-learning plant recognition to target treatment plant-by-plant.
notes: Swiss precision-agriculture company founded 2011, HQ Yverdon-les-Bains. Flagship ARA "Plant-by-Plant" ultra-high-precision sprayer reduces herbicide/treatment use up to 95% via deep-learning plant recognition driving targeted micro-dosing. Funding: earlier rounds plus $52M (May 2023, led by AQTON/Cibus Capital); a combined $150M across Series C ($45M, 2024) and Series D ($105M, 2025, led by Highland Europe with ECBF and McWin Capital). total_funding_usd ~$250M is an approximation summing documented recent rounds plus earlier financing; exact cumulative total not cleanly disclosed (treat as undisclosed-precise). March 2026: announced $50M to expand US operations and assemble ARA in Lyons, Kansas. Team size ~137 (PitchBook); LinkedIn/Glassdoor list 51-200 range. In scope and borderline: core motion is classical (tractor-towed/simple autonomous sprayer, a category that could exist without AI), but plant detection is a trained deep-learning model that is essential to the 95% reduction value prop — AI-native targeting, so in_scope=true. Sources: ecorobotix.com; press.ecorobotix.com Series D/Series B announcements; agtechnavigator.com; tracxn/pitchbook for team size. [AI_intensity: Perception uses a learned policy: deep-learning computer-vision models classify crops vs. weeds plant-by-plant in real time to drive ultra-precise micro-dosing nozzle targeting. Core mechanical motion/towing and nozzle actuation are classical (the sprayer is tractor-towed or simple autonomous), so AI is concentrated in perception/targeting rather than manipulation or navigation policy.]
business_model: Hybrid hardware-plus-software model: sells the ARA ultra-high-precision sprayer (tractor-mounted/towed unit, list price ~EUR 200,000) to vegetable and specialty-crop farmers through a dealer network, then earns recurring revenue from annual software/algorithm licenses for the deep-learning plant-recognition crop-care models farmers apply per plant.
customers: Largely undisclosed by name; sold via dealers to growers of lettuce, spinach, onions, carrots, and sugar beets, with close to 300 ARA machines deployed across 20+ countries (Europe, US, Oceania). No individual named paying organizations are publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: scaling (no audited revenue figure publicly disclosed; Owler third-party estimate ~$5-25M annual revenue, unverified; company targets ~$500M sales by 2029 and has raised ~$230M total including a $150M Series C/D)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from John Deere See & Spray and Carbon Robotics LaserWeeder by combining centimeter-level (~6x6cm) plant-by-plant deep-learning targeting with selective microdosing of herbicide/fertilizer (up to 95% chemical reduction) on a dedicated standalone implement sold to mid-size specialty/vegetable growers, rather than retrofitting large-row-crop boom sprayers (Deere) or using laser/mechanical weeding (Carbon Robotics); claims category leadership in high-precision spraying.

one_liner: Builds autonomous machines that use AI computer vision to distinguish crops from weeds at plant level, enabling chemical-free mechanical weeding plus crop analytics for vegetable growers.
last_round_lead: Fall Line Capital and Middleland Capital
notes: Founded 2016 by Sebastien Boyer and Thomas Palomares; HQ Salinas, CA (early offices San Francisco; some records cite Santa Clara). Seed $5.7M (Dec 2017, Playground Global et al.), Series A $14.5M (Sep 2019, Calibrate Ventures), Series B $45M (Jul 2022, Fall Line Capital + Middleland Capital, with Taylor Farms participating) -> sum ~$65.2M disclosed. Note: aggregators differ (Tracxn cites ~$140M over 8 rounds; PitchBook ~$96M); used sum of disclosed equity rounds. Acquired by Taylor Farms in April 2025 (status acqui-hired/acquired). In scope: AI-native plant-level perception is core to selective weeding; the product would not function without learned crop-vs-weed classification. L6 vertical agriculture (6d) with L4 hardware as secondary. Funding/round figures are USD. [AI_intensity: Perception uses a learned policy: ML/computer-vision models classify individual plants (crop vs. weed) at high speed in-field, driving the selective mechanical weeding actuators. Manipulation/actuation is classical mechanical; navigation is largely conventional.]
business_model: Pre-acquisition, FarmWise sold weeding-as-a-service rather than hardware: it deployed its Titan (FT-35) AI computer-vision weeding machines and provided the operators and technical support, charging growers a per-acre fee that varied with crop density. The standalone Titan unit was listed around $350,000, but the primary revenue model was the managed per-acre service contract.
customers: Named growers/partners included Taylor Farms (a customer, Series B investor, and eventual acquirer), Mission Ranches, Dole Fresh Vegetables, and Ocean Mist Farms; reporting cited ~14 Titans serving 20+ growers across California and Arizona vegetable farms.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; company explicitly declined to disclose revenue. Raised ~$140M total incl. $45M Series B 2022; business acquired by Taylor Farms April 2025 after winding down operations)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by plant-level crop-vs-weed computer vision driving mechanical (chemical-free) weeding delivered as a full managed service, versus Carbon Robotics (laser-based weeding, sells the LaserWeeder unit) and FarmWise's struggle to scale economically was a key competitive weakness. Its service-not-sale model lowered grower adoption friction relative to Carbon Robotics' capital-sale approach, but it could not reach sustainable scale independently.

also_known_as: FF Robotics; Fresh Fruit Robotics Ltd.; FFMH-Tech
one_liner: Builds a fully automated multi-arm fresh-fruit harvester using computer vision to detect ripe apples and emulate human-hand picking, addressing farm labor shortages.
notes: Israeli AgTech founded 2014 by Avi Kahani; HQ at 12 Gesher HaEts, Emek Hefer, Israel (some sources list Tel Mond). Develops multi-arm (up to 12-arm) selective fresh-fruit harvester for apples, citrus, pears, peaches, cherries; claims ~10x human picking speed. In scope: AI-native variable-item selective harvesting requiring vision-based perception to pick ripe vs unripe variable fruit; would not exist as a generic conveyor/arm. Borderline per assignment note: vision perception is AI; grasp/picking motion appears scripted (mechanical hand-motion emulation) rather than a learned policy. Funding (Crunchbase/Tracxn): ~$700K convertible debt (Nov 2017, local kibbutz), $750K grant (Jan 2018, Washington Tree Fruit Research Commission), ~$2M Horizon Europe grant (Oct 2022); total ~$3.45M. Earlier 2019 partnership with Moses Lake (WA) for US deployment. Team size not findable. Status active (recent EU/ROBOHARVEST-related project activity). Not to be confused with shut-down competitor Abundant Robotics. [AI_intensity: Perception (ripe-fruit detection/classification) uses computer vision; sources confirm vision-based identification but it's unclear whether deep-learning/learned policies vs classical CV. Grasp/picking motion appears scripted (mechanical emulation of hand motion), not a learned policy.]
business_model: Sells/leases its multi-arm "FFRobot" autonomous fresh-fruit harvester (12 telescoping robotic arms with three-fingered grippers and AI vision) to fruit growers. CEO Avi Kahani has stated the company shifted to a robot-as-a-service / lease model, charging per bin picked or per month leased rather than selling the expensive machine outright, to ease grower adoption.
customers: Largely undisclosed. Ran multi-year commercial trials with apple growers in Washington State's Columbia Basin (US). Nursery/orchard firm Agromillora Group promotes the FFRobot harvester, suggesting a commercial/distribution relationship; no named paying customers are publicly confirmed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue or ARR figure disclosed; raised ~$5M Series A in 2020 plus a 2022 EU grant, still in commercial-trial / early-deployment stage)
technical_differentiation: Uses a ground-based platform with 12 telescoping arms and flexible-wrist three-fingered grippers emulating human-hand picking across multiple crops (apples, citrus, pears, peaches, cherries), claiming ~10x human pick rate. Differs from Israeli rival Tevel Aerobotics, which uses tethered flying drones with gripper arms, and from the now-defunct Abundant Robotics, which used a vacuum-tube suction approach.

one_liner: Multi-arm autonomous robots that selectively harvest delicate soft fruit like raspberries, using AI-enhanced 3D vision and machine learning to identify and pick variable, occluded berries.
notes: UK agritech startup spun out of University of Plymouth (founder Martin Stoelen), backed by Frontier IP Group. Builds the "world's first raspberry-harvesting robots" with a soft adaptive gripper and AI-enhanced 3D vision/ML for selective picking of delicate, variable, occluded soft fruit. April 2026 raised £3M (£2.2M equity led by Elbow Beach Capital + ~£1.6-1.7M grants incl. Innovate UK Farm ADOPT); follow-on to £1.5M 2023 seed (also Elbow Beach). Reported cumulative funding ~£8M+ / ~$11.3M per Crunchbase. Harvesting-as-a-service deployments underway with Place UK and Littywood Farm; multi-robot fleets targeted 2027; expansion to Portugal and Australia. In scope: AI-native variable-item manipulation/perception — selective soft-fruit picking would not work without learned perception of occluded, variable fruit; not a pre-programmed industrial arm. Team size and exact total funding not precisely confirmed (ranges from multiple sources). Sources: Crunchbase, Frontier IP, TechFundingNews, The AI Insider, company site. [AI_intensity: Perception is a learned policy: AI-enhanced 3D cameras with machine learning models identify and localize variable, occluded, soft ripe fruit for selective picking. Manipulation/grasping of variable items is informed by this learned perception (soft adaptive gripper). Navigation/planning appear classical. AI-trained variable-item perception is the core differentiator.]
business_model: Sells robotic raspberry harvesting on a "harvesting-as-a-service" basis: growers pay a per-berry / predictable service fee to have Fieldwork's multi-arm autonomous "Alpha" robots pick their soft fruit (fruit is then sold by the grower into retail), rather than buying the machines outright. Currently structured around multi-year service contracts (e.g. a 2-year programme in Norfolk/Staffordshire); specific per-kg/contract pricing is not publicly disclosed.
customers: Named partners/pilots: The Summer Berry Company (Portugal trials; first commercial fruit sales), Place UK (Norfolk) and Littywood Farm (Staffordshire) under a 2-year harvesting-as-a-service programme, and Costa Group (Australia, 12-month trial from 2025). Harvested fruit reportedly sold via M&S, Sainsbury's and Waitrose.
revenue_status: early-revenue (first commercial fruit sales since Feb 2022; no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed. Total funding raised ~$8.77M / £3M latest round 2026, grant- and equity-backed; described as "generating revenue / not profitable")
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by focus on delicate raspberries (softer, harder to grip than strawberries) using multi-arm robots with AI-enhanced 3D vision and soft selective grippers tuned for tabletop raspberry canes. Closest competitors Dogtooth Technologies (GB, selective strawberry/soft-fruit picker) and Agrobot (Spain, 24-arm E-series strawberry harvester) target strawberries; Fieldwork claims the first viable autonomous raspberry harvester, though it competes on execution/capital as commercial fleets are not yet at scale (planned 2027).

one_liner: Builds autonomous field robots (Oz, Dino, Ted, Orio) that use machine-vision crop-row detection and weed/crop discrimination to mechanically weed and tend crops, easing farm labor shortages.
notes: Founded 2011 by Aymeric Barthes and Gaetan Severac; HQ Toulouse, France. Funding history per Crunchbase/Tracxn: Seed EUR 3M (2015), Venture ~$2.4M (2017), Series A ~$15.6M (2020-01), Series B EUR 32M/$33M led by Mirova (Dec 2022). Total ~$54.7M. In scope: AI-native learned perception (CNN-based weed/crop discrimination and crop-row vision) is core to the weeding robots; product category (precision mechanical weeding by autonomous row-following robots) depends on this vision learning, though navigation also uses classical RTK/GPS guidance. Borderline as flagged but treated in_scope=true given learned perception core. Team size not reliably found. [AI_intensity: Perception uses learned ML vision for crop-row detection and weed/crop discrimination; navigation combines RTK-GPS/classical guidance with vision-based row following. The differentiated learned component is perception (crop/weed identification), not a learned manipulation or full RL navigation policy.]
business_model: Direct sales (and optional rental/lease) of autonomous electric weeding robots to farmers and winegrowers, sold through a dealer network across ~20 countries: Oz vegetable assistant (~$30K), Dino large-scale vegetable weeder (~$200K-300K), and Ted vineyard straddle robot (~€175K target price); rental contracts bundle technician setup and maintenance. Revenue is hardware sales plus maintenance/support service contracts.
customers: Named: French cognac/wine groups Hennessy and Grands Chais de France use the Ted vineyard robot; vegetable growers in Poland, the Netherlands, Germany, and Italy use Oz. Over 350 robots deployed across 5 continents in ~20 countries.
revenue_status: early-revenue (€2.4M revenue FY2024, down from €3.96M FY2021; entered judicial recovery June 2025, recapitalized by Mirova/Bpifrance/Occitanie ARIS fund; projecting €11M by 2030)
technical_differentiation: Camera/machine-vision crop-row detection with weed/crop discrimination driving mechanical (herbicide-free) weeding, across a multi-form-factor fleet from small (Oz) to vineyard straddle (Ted). Vs Ecorobotix (ARA, vision-guided ultra-targeted micro-spraying, still chemical) and FarmDroid (GPS-guided seeding/weeding, no crop-row machine vision), Naïo differentiates on purely mechanical weeding plus a vineyard-specific autonomous straddle platform.

one_liner: Autonomous field robot that uses deep-learning vision to distinguish weeds from crops and mechanically pull weeds, cutting herbicide use in vegetable production.
total_funding_usd: ~3.68M (mixed CAD grants/equity; includes a 2.6M CAD SDTC grant)
notes: Founded 2017 by Teric Greenan, originally in Dartmouth/Halifax, Nova Scotia; later HQ reported in Quebec (St-Bruno-de-Montarville / Brossard, Greater Montreal), current CEO Luc Labbe. Flagship product R2Weed2 uses cameras + neural network to differentiate weeds from crops, then a robotic arm/gripper extracts weed roots; aims to cut herbicide/fungicide use up to 50%. Won the agBOT Weed-and-Feed challenge. In scope: AI-native — the robot's value depends on learned weed-vs-crop perception, which would not exist without deep learning. Funding figures: 2.6M CAD SDTC grant (July 2022) is the largest/most recent disclosed; aggregate ~3.68M from Crunchbase across rounds (currency/mix not fully clear). Team size and any later rounds not findable. Status assumed active; no shutdown evidence found. [AI_intensity: Perception is a learned policy: a neural network classifies weeds vs. crops from camera images, driving the arm/gripper to remove weeds. Navigation/manipulation appear more classical, but the core perception task uses deep learning.]
business_model: Sells/leases autonomous mechanical weeding field robots to vegetable growers. Its commercial robot ("La Chevre," evolved from the R2Weed2/Hal-Bot prototype) is offered for purchase at ~US$500,000 or seasonal lease at ~US$50,000 per growing season (robot-as-a-service), targeting growers who want to cut herbicide use and replace manual weeding labor (one robot reportedly does the work of up to five workers).
customers: Operating in Canadian vegetable fields with second-generation units deployed, but no specific named paying customers are publicly disclosed. Customers: undisclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; has raised ~$2.6M SDTC grant plus ~$1.7M seed and deployed commercial units, but no revenue figure publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Uses a robotic arm with gripper to mechanically pull/pluck individual weeds guided by deep-learning vision, versus closest competitors Carbon Robotics (laser-burns weeds with its LaserWeeder, ~$1.4M, no mechanical removal) and FarmWise (blade/implement-based mechanical cultivation between rows). Nexus's plucking approach removes whole weeds including roots and works close to the crop, but it is a smaller, less-capitalized player competing largely on execution and price flexibility (lease option) rather than a defensible tech moat.

one_liner: Builds AI/machine-vision robots that detect, harvest, bunch, and tie cilantro and other specialty bunch crops to cut farm labor costs amid worker shortages.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (grant-funded: USDA SBIR, $175K Arizona Innovation Challenge, $15K Venture Devils; CB Insights cites ~$180K)
team_size: unknown (small; grew from 2-person operation to a handful of engineers, mostly former ASU interns)
notes: Founded by Raghu Nandivada (from a farming family) and Cole Brauer; grew out of a 2018/2020 challenge and ASU Venture Devils program. HQ moved from a Chandler, AZ garage to an office in Mesa, AZ (Phoenix metro); some databases list Phoenix/Chandler. Develops first automated robotic harvester for cilantro and other bunch-harvested specialty crops (harvest + bunch + tie), plus in-row weed removal, autonomous spraying, and an adaptive bird-deterrent. Uses machine vision for crop identification and manipulation of variable, non-uniform produce — qualifies as AI-trained variable-item manipulation (L6 6d). In scope: category is AI-native (variable-item produce manipulation would not work without vision-based detection). Customers include Blue Sky Organic Farms, Duncan Family Farms, Desert Premium. Funding is grant-based; founding year varies by source (CB Insights 2019; ASU/Tracxn/founder narrative 2020) — used 2020 per founder account and Tracxn. Total funding and team size not disclosed precisely; latest reported award Sep 2024 (~$650K cited by one source, undisclosed via USDA). Learned-policy depth is lower-confidence and not publicly confirmed. [AI_intensity: unclear — manipulation (cilantro harvest/bunch/tie) and perception use machine vision for crop identification of variable produce, consistent with learned detection models, but the company has not publicly confirmed a learned policy (RL/IL/diffusion/VLA) for manipulation; likely CV detection + classical grasp/cut planning.]
business_model: Plans to sell/deploy AI machine-vision robots that harvest, bunch and tie cilantro and other specialty bunch crops, plus weed-pulling and AI-scarecrow systems, to farms to cut labor costs (pitched at roughly halving cilantro harvest cost from ~$5,000 to ~$2,500/acre). Currently grant- and pilot-funded (USDA SBIR, Arizona Innovation Challenge) rather than product-revenue funded; commercial sale/lease pricing model not publicly disclosed.
customers: Pilots/development partners are Arizona farms: Duncan Family Farms (one of the largest US organic farms), Blue Sky Organic Farms (AI scarecrow), and Desert Premium. These appear to be development/pilot relationships rather than confirmed paying customers.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; ~$650K seed round Sep 2024 plus USDA SBIR and ~$175K+ Arizona Innovation Challenge grants, all development funding)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on a niche, hard-to-automate task: vision-guided harvesting that not only cuts but bunches and ties cilantro/specialty bunch crops, a step broad-acre harvest and weeding robots (e.g., Carbon Robotics' LaserWeeder, FarmWise) do not address. Positioning is execution/capital-stage in an early field; no closely matching named cilantro-bunching competitor is publicly identified.

one_liner: Tackles fruit-picking labor shortages with AI-driven Flying Autonomous Robots that use computer vision to detect, classify by ripeness/size, and selectively harvest fruit via collision-free flight.
total_funding_usd: ~33900000 (reported ~$33.9M as of 2020 Series B incl. $2.5M Israel Innovation Authority grant; some aggregators report higher cumulative totals)
last_round_lead: undisclosed (investors included Kubota, Forbon, Maverick Ventures Israel, OurCrowd, AgFunder)
notes: Founded 2017 by CEO Yaniv Maor; chairman Eyal Desheh. HQ Gedera, Israel with branches in Italy and the US. FAR (Flying Autonomous Robots) platform combines AI/computer vision, robotics, aeronautical engineering and flight control to selectively pick fruit. Well-documented funding: $1.4M seed (2017), $10M Series A (2019), $20M Series B (Oct 2020), plus a $2.5M Israel Innovation Authority grant; total reported ~$33.9M at 2020 with ~$45M valuation. Some aggregators (PitchBook/CB Insights) reference a later 'Series B-II' and an April 2025 M&A offer with cumulative totals up to ~$70M, but these are not independently confirmed by primary press, so the Series B is recorded as the clearly verified latest round. In scope: AI-native selective harvesting — the product (flying selective fruit-picking robots) would not exist without learned perception. Secondary L4 due to custom drone/gripper hardware. [AI_intensity: Perception is a learned policy: deep-learning computer vision detects fruit and classifies type/size/ripeness; this drives selective harvesting decisions. Flight/grasp planning appears largely classical (collision-free path planning, flight control), but the core harvesting task depends on learned perception.]
business_model: Sells fruit-harvesting as an on-demand robots-as-a-service offering: growers use Tevel's harvesting-management app to specify how many Flying Autonomous Robot (FAR) pickers they need and when, and Tevel deploys fleets (tethered drones with robotic arms orbiting a ground vehicle) to the orchard, billing for the harvesting service rather than selling hardware outright. Also generates revenue via manufacturing/commercialization partnership with Kubota.
customers: HMC Farms (California, apple/stone fruit pilot), Rivoira Group (fruit grower/distributor, Italy), and strategic partner/customer Kubota in Europe; expanding toward France, South America, and New Zealand.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$11.4M revenue/ARR FY2024, unverified — GetLatka estimate)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by its airborne picking architecture (AI/computer-vision tethered drones with grippers that classify ripeness and selectively pick whole-canopy), versus ground-based robotic harvesters from competitors like Tortuga AgTech (wheeled mobile robots) and Advanced Farm/FFRobotics (gantry/arm-on-platform pickers); the flying form factor enables faster reach across tree height and multi-crop flexibility.

one_liner: Autonomous dual-arm robots harvest strawberries, table grapes and berries in greenhouses, using on-edge AI models for ripeness discrimination, pick selection, and two-arm motion planning.
notes: Founded 2016 (Crunchbase), HQ Denver, Colorado. AI-native variable-item manipulation: selectively harvesting only ripe fruit from unstructured plant canopies requires learned perception/decision-making — category would not exist without AI, so in_scope=true. Operated world's largest commercial fleet (~150 harvesting robots) in 2024. Acquired by vertical-farming company Oishii (Jersey City, NJ) announced March 2025 — Oishii took Tortuga's IP, assets, AI models, robotics software, custom hardware, and engineering team; the standalone Tortuga entity is effectively wound down (acqui-hired). Tortuga's own site (tortugaagtech.com) now redirects/branded to Oishii. Total funding figures vary by source: Tracxn ~$27.7M across 5 rounds; PitchBook ~$47.7M; CB Insights ~$49.3M. Largest disclosed round was a $20M Series A on 2021-02 led by Lewis & Clark AgriFood (participants: Root Ventures, Spero Ventures, Ceres Partners, Colorado Impact Fund, AME Cloud Ventures, Morado Ventures, Grit Ventures, Remus Capital). Acquisition deal terms (price) undisclosed and not counted as a funding round. Team size not publicly disclosed. [AI_intensity: Perception and decision stack is clearly learned: ~17-20 AI/ML models run on the edge for fruit detection, ripeness discrimination, and picking decisions. Manipulation pick selection is learned-model-driven; two-arm asynchronous motion planning is borderline (likely classical/optimization-based rather than a learned policy). No evidence of RL/IL/diffusion/VLA for control — perception is deep learning, planning appears classical.]
business_model: Robotics-as-a-Service: Tortuga retained ownership of its dual-arm autonomous harvesting robots and charged greenhouse/indoor growers a "pay-per-kilo" flat rate per robot-picked box of strawberries, table grapes and berries, priced to be comparable to human labor, plus subscription updates/maintenance and data-analytics services. Robots stayed on Tortuga's balance sheet and revenue was recognized monthly based on volume harvested.
customers: Not publicly disclosed by name; buyers were indoor and greenhouse strawberry/berry/table-grape growers (e.g. operations in the US and Spain). By 2024 it ran a fleet of ~150 production robots across grower sites, but specific paying farms are undisclosed. Acquirer was Oishii Farm Corp (March 2025).
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; raised ~$55M total since 2016 and operated a ~150-robot commercial fleet generating per-kilo recurring revenue before the 2025 acqui-hire by Oishii)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by on-edge AI ripeness discrimination and dual-arm selective picking of delicate fruit (strawberries, table grapes, berries) under a RaaS/pay-per-kilo model where the robot risk stays on Tortuga's books. Vs Agrobot (strawberry harvester sold as capital equipment with sensor-array picking) and Advanced Farm Technologies (strawberry/apple harvesting robots), Tortuga competed on greenhouse multi-crop versatility and a labor-cost-parity service model rather than upfront hardware sales.

one_liner: RaaS precision-ag robot (SharpShooter) using AI computer vision to identify and aim at individual plants/weeds for targeted spraying, weeding and thinning at high speed.
notes: Co-founded 2018 by Gabe Sibley (CEO, AI/robotics, ex-NASA/self-driving) and Curtis Garner (CCO, farming ops). HQ Hayward, CA. Total funding ~$58M across rounds; most recent disclosed round $46.5M (Nov 2022, labeled Series A) led by Cleveland Avenue with DCVC Bio, Future Ventures, SeaX, Autotech, Cavallo, AgFunder. Flagship SharpShooter 'Aim & Apply' multi-action robot reduces chemical use up to 99% and labor up to 85%. In scope: AI-native per-plant targeting via learned vision; action delivery (spray) is classical. Team size not reliably findable. [AI_intensity: Learned perception (computer-vision deep learning) is core to per-plant/weed identification and targeting; the spray/action delivery itself is classical control, not a learned policy.]
business_model: Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS): growers of specialty/row crops pay a per-acre service fee for Verdant's SharpShooter precision-application implement (AI-vision targeted spraying, weeding, thinning, fertilizing), shifting cost from CapEx to OpEx. The platform also has a list/purchase price (~$500K), but the primary model is per-acre service contracts where Verdant operates the machines.
customers: Named deployments include TopFlavor Farms (~7,000 acres of leafy greens/specialty row crops, CA), My T Acres (~8,000-acre vegetable operation, NY), and Braga-style California specialty growers; company says it services "thousands of acres" with orders pending for tens of thousands more.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; commercially launched, servicing thousands of acres; ~$58M total funding incl. $46.5M Series A in 2022)
technical_differentiation: vs Carbon Robotics (LaserWeeder, laser-kill) and FarmWise/Naio (mechanical/spray weeding): Verdant's SharpShooter is a multi-action "Aim & Apply" platform that 3D-maps and targets individual plants for spray, weed, thin and fertilize in one pass at lower hardware cost (~$500K vs ~$1M+ for LaserWeeder), claiming up to 99% chemical reduction. Differentiation is breadth of actions per pass plus per-plant precision spraying rather than lasers.

company_name: AIREC (AI-driven Robot for Embrace and Care)
also_known_as: Dry-AIREC; AI-driven Robot for Embracement and Nursing Caring; Waseda University Sugano-Ogata Lab / Future Robotics Organization
one_liner: University research program building a dual-arm humanoid elder-care robot that learns compliant force-controlled caregiving motions (repositioning, lifting) via Deep Predictive Learning to address Japan's caregiver shortage.
last_round_lead: Japan Cabinet Office / JST Moonshot R&D Program
notes: University research program (not a commercial company), led by Prof. Shigeki Sugano with Prof. Tetsuya Ogata, Waseda University Future Robotics Organization, Tokyo. Part of Japan's government Moonshot R&D Program Goal 3 (program launched 2020; 2020 used as founding/start year). Dry-AIREC is the dual-arm humanoid (~149 kg) demonstrated rolling/repositioning patients to prevent bedsores and lifting from supine. Core caregiving manipulation policy is learned via Deep Predictive Learning (DNN imitation/predictive model with proprioceptive + visual + somatosensory attention, impedance/force control) per arXiv:2407.13376. in_scope=true: an AI-native learned-manipulation caregiving humanoid, not a pre-programmed device. Funded by public research grant; no VC rounds. Team targets nursing/medical readiness around 2030, broader autonomy goal by 2050. Sources: airec-waseda.jp; arXiv:2407.13376; Waseda University news; Interesting Engineering coverage. [AI_intensity: Core manipulation (dual-arm repositioning/lifting) uses a learned policy: Deep Predictive Learning, a deep-neural-network imitation/predictive model with proprioceptive, visual, and somatosensory attention generating compliant force-controlled motions. Learned, not pre-programmed; clears the AI bar.]
business_model: N/A (research/open-source). Academic program at Waseda University's Future Robotics Organisation, funded by Japan Science and Technology Agency (JST) under the government's Moonshot R&D Program (Goal 3, PM Shigeki Sugano). Not a commercial vendor; a commercial unit is only targeted for ~2030 at an indicative price near $67,000.
customers: Undisclosed. No paying customers; it is a pre-commercial research robot. Backers/collaborators include JST (government funder) and NVIDIA (GPU/compute partner). Targeted future buyers are Japanese nursing homes and elder-care facilities.
revenue_status: N/A
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on compliant, torque-sensed force control for physical-contact caregiving (repositioning, diaper changing, dressing, getting patients out of bed) via learned predictive-processing/imitation models on 7-DoF dual arms with per-joint torque sensing, vs. mobility/lift-assist robots like RIKEN/Sumitomo ROBEAR and Toyota's Human Support Robot, which focus on lifting or fetch-and-carry rather than learned compliant whole-body caregiving contact.

also_known_as: Moxi (hospital robot); subsidiary of Serve Robotics
one_liner: Makes Moxi, an autonomous mobile manipulation robot that fetches and delivers labs, meds and supplies in hospitals, freeing nurses from non-clinical logistics errands.
notes: Founded 2017 in Austin, TX by Andrea Thomaz (UT Austin CS) and Vivian Chu (ex-Honda Research Institute). Total raised ~$99.9M across ~10 rounds; notable rounds: $3M seed, $30M+ Series B (Apr 2022), $25M (Oct 2023), $44.3M Series C led by Canaan (June 2023). Acquired by Serve Robotics: merger signed 2026-01-19, closed 2026-01-27, all-stock deal valued ~$29M plus up to $5.3M earn-out (SEC 8-K cites ~$25.7M). Now operates as a Diligent Robotics subsidiary/division of Serve. Moxi deployed across 25+ hospital facilities (claimed 200+ hospitals / 22+ health systems at peak), 1.25M+ autonomous deliveries. SCOPE: kept in_scope=true per "when in doubt" as a vertical healthcare mobile-manipulation deployment; borderline because autonomous hospital delivery/navigation could exist without learned policies and a confirmed RL/IL/VLA core policy is not publicly evidenced — flagged pending confirmation. Last-round figures denominated in stock and approximate; verification relies on Serve Robotics press release, SEC 8-K, TechCrunch, and The Robot Report. [AI_intensity: Borderline / unclear. Core tasks (indoor navigation, fetch-and-deliver, elevator/door integration) appear to rely largely on classical autonomous-navigation and scripted/engineered manipulation. Diligent markets "socially-intelligent" interaction and learning-from-demonstration manipulation research (founders' academic background in robot learning/HRI), but public evidence of a deployed RL/IL/VLA policy driving a core perception/manipulation/navigation task is thin. No confirmed learned policy for a core task.]
business_model: Sells Moxi, an autonomous mobile manipulation robot, to hospitals under a robot-as-a-service (RaaS) subscription model rather than outright hardware sales; hospitals pay recurring fees for fleets that fetch and deliver labs, medications, and supplies. Reported annual contract value runs roughly $200K-$400K per hospital facility (unverified third-party estimate).
customers: Named hospital and health-system customers include Northwestern Medicine, ChristianaCare, Rochester General Hospital, Cedars-Sinai, Texas Medical Center, and Children's Hospital Los Angeles; deployed across 25+ hospital facilities in the US with nearly 100 robots completing 1.25M+ autonomous deliveries.
revenue_status: early-revenue (acquired by Serve Robotics for ~$29.0M in Serve stock plus up to $5.3M earn-out, Jan 2026; estimated ~$200K-$400K annual revenue per hospital facility, third-party/unverified; no standalone company-wide revenue or ARR figure publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: vs Aethon (TUG) and Relay Robotics: Moxi is a semi-humanoid with a 7-DOF manipulator arm and gripper enabling autonomous fetching/manipulation (pressing elevators, opening drawers) in unstructured human-centric environments, whereas TUG and Relay are wheeled delivery carts without manipulation. Diligent also differentiates on a subscription/RaaS model and one of the largest deployed fleets of mobile manipulation robots, versus Aethon's hardware-sale approach.

also_known_as: Hon Hai Precision Industry; Hon Hai Technology Group; Nurabot
one_liner: Collaborative nursing robot easing nurse shortages by delivering medication, transporting specimens, and patrolling wards, with manipulation policies trained in NVIDIA Isaac digital-twin hospitals via GR00T imitation/RL.
team_size: unknown (parent Foxconn ~700,000+ employees; Nurabot team size undisclosed)
notes: Nurabot is a product line within Foxconn / Hon Hai Technology Group, not a standalone company. Parent Hon Hai Precision Industry Co. was founded 1974 in Taipei by Terry Gou, HQ in Tucheng District, New Taipei City, Taiwan; IPO on TWSE June 1991 (ticker 2317). Nurabot is a collaborative nursing robot unveiled at Computex 2025 (May 2025), developed with NVIDIA and Kawasaki Heavy Industries, field-tested at Taichung Veterans General Hospital for medication delivery, specimen transport, and ward patrol. Powered by NVIDIA Jetson Orin and Holoscan; trained in NVIDIA Isaac for Healthcare / Omniverse digital twins using Isaac GR00T and GR00T-Mimic for learned manipulation via imitation and reinforcement learning. Integrates FoxBrain LLM (NVIDIA NeMo) for speech interaction. in_scope=true: AI-trained variable-task manipulation in a vertical healthcare deployment that depends on learned policies. Funding/round/team fields are not separately disclosed because this is a division of a large public company; total_funding and round details marked undisclosed/unknown accordingly. [AI_intensity: Manipulation uses learned policies: Nurabot's manipulation skills are trained in NVIDIA Isaac Sim/Omniverse digital-twin hospitals using Isaac GR00T and GR00T-Mimic via imitation and reinforcement learning. Autonomous navigation may rely partly on classical methods, but the GR00T-based learned manipulation meets the AI-intensity bar.]
business_model: Sells/leases the Nurabot collaborative nursing robot (co-developed with Kawasaki Heavy Industries, running Foxconn's FoxBrain LLM on NVIDIA Jetson Orin/Isaac) as a hardware-plus-software "total solution" to hospitals and long-term care/nursing-education institutions for medication delivery, specimen transport, and ward patrolling. Commercial launch targeted for 2026; retail price not yet set publicly. Within parent Hon Hai/Foxconn, this is a small new vertical relative to its core electronics contract-manufacturing revenue.
customers: Field-validated at Taichung Veterans General Hospital (since April 2025); expanding to Taipei Veterans General Hospital and Tungs' Taichung MetroHarbor Hospital, plus Baishatun Tung/Mazu Hospital and Cardinal Tien Hospital among Taiwan adopters; all currently pilots/early deployments rather than confirmed paid contracts, aligned with Taiwan's "Healthy Taiwan" plan.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (Nurabot-specific; commercial launch planned 2026, no robot-line revenue/ARR figure publicly disclosed; parent Hon Hai posted ~NT$6.9 trillion / ~$210B+ group revenue FY2024 but that is unrelated consumer-electronics manufacturing)
technical_differentiation: Differentiated by deep NVIDIA stack integration (Isaac for Healthcare digital-twin training in Omniverse, Holoscan sensor processing, Jetson Orin edge compute) plus dual-arm manipulation from Kawasaki and Foxconn's own FoxBrain LLM, versus wheeled hospital-logistics robots from Aethon (TUG, autonomous delivery only, no manipulation) and Diligent Robotics (Moxi), which focus on fetch-and-deliver without Nurabot's manipulator arms and integrated foundation-model/digital-twin pipeline.

also_known_as: Gatsby.bot; Gatsby Robotics; West Egg Labs
one_liner: Robot-agnostic consumer platform dispatching humanoid robots for on-demand apartment cleaning; routine tasks run autonomously via learned navigation/manipulation while harder steps are teleoperated, feeding model training.
notes: Founded January 2026 by Aron Frishberg (ex-Third Point; left Univ. of Chicago), operating under West Egg Labs in San Francisco. On May 14, 2026 completed the first autonomous humanoid residential cleaning for a US consumer. Flat $150/clean, robot-agnostic (partners with humanoid makers rather than building hardware). Backed by NVIDIA Inception and Entrepreneurs First; specific dollar funding not disclosed in press. A Crunchbase figure of $3.9M / 33 employees appears to belong to an unrelated 'Gatsby' (e.g., Gatsby Events) and was NOT used. In_scope=true: AI-native home-manipulation autonomy with learned navigation/manipulation and teleoperation-data flywheel; would not exist without learned policies. Borderline: hybrid human-in-the-loop teleoperation and hardware-agnostic distribution layer (hence L5_middleware secondary), but core value is autonomous home manipulation/navigation. Sources: businesswire.com, gatsby.bot/about, foxnews.com, interestingengineering.com, aronfrishberg.com/gatsby. [AI_intensity: Navigation and manipulation use learned policies — routine cleaning tasks (floors, surfaces, dishes) run fully autonomously via learned navigation/manipulation; harder segments are human-teleoperated, with teleoperation traces feeding model training (imitation learning). Hardware-agnostic, so the policy/autonomy layer is the core AI.]
business_model: On-demand consumer service: customers book a humanoid-robot apartment cleaning via Gatsby's iOS app at a flat $150 per clean (any apartment size, no tips/fees). Buyer is the individual consumer (B2C, transactional per-job); Gatsby is robot-agnostic and partners with humanoid hardware makers rather than selling robots, positioning itself as the consumer distribution/dispatch layer ("Cursor/Uber for robots"). Tasks run partly autonomously with harder steps teleoperated.
customers: Individual SF consumers booking through the iOS app; first US consumer cleaning completed May 14, 2026 for an unnamed San Francisco customer. Specific named customers undisclosed; service is SF-only with a waitlist for other cities.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; founded Jan 2026, first paid consumer clean May 2026 at $150/job, SF-only with waitlist)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates by being robot-agnostic and owning the consumer-facing distribution/dispatch + teleoperation layer, vs. vertically integrated humanoid hardware makers like Figure and 1X (and home-robot startup Sunday) that build and sell their own robots. Gatsby competes on consumer UX, multi-vendor robot orchestration, and learned navigation/manipulation software rather than proprietary hardware.

one_liner: Building Alfie, a general-purpose household robot butler that loads dishwashers, does laundry, cleans, and cooks simple meals via learned VLA-style policies trained on teleoperation data.
notes: London-based startup founded 2021 by Shariq Hashme (ex-OpenAI Member of Technical Staff). Building "Alfie," a general-purpose household robot butler for chores like dishwasher loading/unloading, laundry, surface/floor cleaning, and simple meal prep. Uses learned VLA-style policies with teleoperation-derived training data rather than fixed automation -> AI-native, in scope, 6f_home. Funding: angel/seed round (small, undisclosed amount); founder reportedly invested ~GBP 2m of own money. Angel investors include Alexandr Wang (Scale AI), Ben Mann (Anthropic), Simon Last (Notion), Debo Olaosebikan (Kepler). Soma Capital portfolio. No large disclosed priced round found as of mid-2026; specific round size/date/lead undisclosed. Team ~11 (LinkedIn/PitchBook). Companies House lists PROSPER ROBOTICS LTD (no. 13180130); some sources list registered office near Canterbury but company is consistently described as London-based. PitchBook lists 2020 founding; Sifted and most sources cite 2021. [AI_intensity: Manipulation: uses learned VLA-style policies trained on teleoperation-derived demonstration data for general-purpose home manipulation (varied objects, surfaces). This is a learned policy, not fixed automation.]
business_model: Plans to sell/rent Alfie, a wheeled general-purpose household robot butler, to consumers (and potentially hotels/hospitals): hardware priced roughly $5,000-$15,000 per unit plus a recurring monthly subscription (~£400-£500/$500/mo) covering maintenance, insurance, and the remote-teleoperation service that handles a large share of tasks. Revenue model is hardware sale/lease plus recurring subscription.
customers: Undisclosed. No product is shipping yet; initial units are planned for a small, tightly screened set of London households (no kids/pets/stairs), so there are no named paying or piloting customers publicly.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; product not yet shipping, first units targeted ~2026-2027; founder self-funded ~£2m plus angel investment)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from 1X (Neo) and Figure by using a wheeled, two-armed non-humanoid form factor with a 50-100 piece tool/attachment "toolbox" rather than a bipedal humanoid, and by explicitly leaning on overseas human teleoperators to remotely perform ~80% of tasks early on (versus 1X's autonomy-first-with-teleop-fallback positioning), collecting data to train VLA-style policies. Competes largely on execution, cost, and the human-in-the-loop bootstrapping strategy rather than a defensible model moat.

one_liner: Offers free home cleaning by camera-equipped operators capturing first-person chore footage (laundry, scrubbing, fridge reorganization), sold to AI labs to train household robots via imitation learning.
notes: Shift is the consumer-facing brand/app of MicroAGI, a Berlin-based embodied-AI startup (founded 2023, ~$63M raised, Series A per StartupHub/Crunchbase, ~44 employees). Shift app launched in NYC May 28, 2026; vetted operators wear head/camera rigs to capture anonymized (auto face/PII-blurred) egocentric home-chore footage sold to AI labs building physical-AI household robot models. Company claims 10,000+ operators across 15 countries earned $5M+ in Q1 2026; planned expansion to SF, London, Zurich, Munich, plus plumbing/cooking verticals. In_scope=true: AI-native human-demonstration/video data service for robot learning (analogous to teleoperation/demonstration-data providers in census); the category exists only to enable learned manipulation policies. Borderline: data service rather than robot OEM — included per 'when in doubt, in_scope=true.' core_or_division=division since Shift is a product line under MicroAGI. Series A size/date/lead not disclosed in public sources; total funding $63M per StartupHub/Crunchbase aggregation. One StartupHub field listed status 'inactive' but news coverage (May/June 2026) and active app launch indicate the company is operating — status set to active. [AI_intensity: Not a robot itself — it is a demonstration-data service. The enabled downstream task is household manipulation (folding, scrubbing, reorganizing) trained via imitation learning on egocentric human-demonstration video. The learned policy lives in the AI-lab customers' models, not in Shift; Shift supplies the proprietary real-world training data.]
business_model: Sells anonymized first-person (egocentric) video of real household chores to AI/robotics labs building physical-AI foundation models for home robots. The "Shift" app subsidizes free professional home cleaning (and planned cooking, repairs, errands) performed by vetted camera-rigged operators; MicroAGI absorbs the labor cost and monetizes by licensing/selling the resulting real-world chore datasets to robot-foundation-model developers (pricing/contract terms not publicly disclosed).
customers: Buyers described generically as "AI labs"/robot-foundation-model developers; no named purchasing organizations are publicly disclosed. End consumers receiving free cleaning are in NYC, with expansion to San Francisco, London, Zurich, and Munich.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no data-sales revenue/ARR figure publicly disclosed; company reports paying 10,000+ operators over $5M in Q1 2026 — a cost/payout figure, not revenue — and has raised ~$63M Series A)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates from gig-worker robot-data peers (Micro1, which has ~4,000 contributors filming chores via head-mounted iPhones; and Scale AI / DoorDash's recording programs) by directly subsidizing free in-home cleaning to capture uncontrolled, genuinely cluttered domestic environments rather than relying on self-filming gig workers, claiming higher-fidelity real-world variation; also vs egocentric-dataset firms like Cortex AI which focus on workplace/industrial settings.

one_liner: Builds Isaac, a personal home robot, and Isaac 0, a stationary laundry-folding robot using learned manipulation policies with remote-specialist corrections fed back to weekly model retraining.
notes: YC S24 batch. Founded 2024 by Evan Wineland (CEO) and Kaan Dogrusoz (CTO), CMU classmates and former Apple AI/robotics engineers. Product: Isaac (general home robot) and Isaac 0 (stationary laundry-folding robot), shipping to Bay Area homes at $7,999 one-time or $450/month. Folds a load in 30-90 min; learned manipulation handles deformable garments while a remote teleoperator handles tricky corrections, with that data used to retrain models weekly (HITL imitation learning). Total funding reported as $9.6M seed (Crunchbase/Tracxn/news); some aggregators show a $500K pre-seed entry — $9.6M is the most recent/total figure. HQ San Francisco, CA. Team ~10. In scope: AI-native variable-item deformable-garment manipulation driven by learned policies, not pre-programmed motion. Round date and lead investor not clearly disclosed. Sources: ycombinator.com, newatlas.com, techbuzz.ai, crunchbase.com. [AI_intensity: Manipulation uses a learning-based (imitation-learning) policy for deformable-textile handling; a remote specialist subs in for short corrections and that human-in-the-loop data retrains the autonomous models weekly. Perception/manipulation are learned, not pre-programmed.]
business_model: Sells/leases consumer home robots direct-to-consumer: Isaac 0, a stationary laundry-folding robot, is offered to Bay Area households at $7,999 up-front or a $450/month subscription (with $250 refundable deposit, 2-year warranty). Future flagship Isaac (a mobile home robot) is slated for later 2026 with upgrade pricing for early Isaac 0 buyers.
customers: Individual consumers/households, initially limited to the San Francisco Bay Area via pre-order. No named institutional buyers; customer base is undisclosed early-access preorder consumers.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; selling via preorders at $7,999 or $450/mo. Disclosed funding ~$500K seed per Crunchbase, Sept 2024, YC S24; a ~$9.6M seed figure appears in some third-party listings, unverified)
technical_differentiation: Hybrid autonomy + remote human teleoperation: learned manipulation policies run the robot, with a Weave specialist remotely taking over for 5-10 second corrections on difficult garments, letting them ship a working laundry product now rather than waiting on full autonomy. Vs. Physical Intelligence / Figure (general-purpose manipulation/humanoid players), Weave competes by being first to deliver a paid, narrowly-scoped consumer laundry robot in homes rather than pursuing fully autonomous general manipulation.

one_liner: Automates construction above the foundation using AI-designed buildings, robotic off-site factories, and Mantis, an autonomous legged on-site assembly robot trained with reinforcement learning.
notes: London/Belgrade construction-robotics startup founded 2023 by Rodion Shishkov and Slava Bocharov (previously co-founders of Samokat, a ~$1.5B food delivery exit). Raised $25M seed announced April 2026, led by RTP Global with SuperSeed, Begin Capital, s16vc, and VNV Global. Full-stack approach: (1) AI-driven building design, (2) modular robotic off-site factories producing timber-composite components, (3) Mantis — a four-legged autonomous on-site assembly robot carrying 100+ kg, reaching 4m, with plug-and-play interchangeable tools, trained with reinforcement learning. Initial launch market Germany. In_scope=true: this is an AI-native learned-policy robot for construction (6g); it would not exist without RL/embodied-AI control, distinguishing it from pre-programmed industrial arms. Some reports cite ~$30M from founder team to date (internal capitalization); the clearly-sourced external round is the $25M seed. UKTN reported it as £18.5m (GBP equivalent). [AI_intensity: Manipulation and locomotion use learned policies: All3 explicitly uses reinforcement learning to train the Mantis legged robot to automate multiple on-site tasks (installation, fastening, finishing, inspection) with interchangeable tools and embodied AI. Perception/manipulation are learned, not purely pre-programmed.]
business_model: Vertically integrated construction delivery: All3 designs (AI architecture platform), fabricates timber-composite components in robotic off-site factories, and assembles them on-site with its autonomous legged Mantis robot, delivering the finished building above the foundation as a turnkey service. Buyers are property developers (who source sites and handle permits); All3 monetizes by delivering completed buildings rather than selling robots or licensing software, claiming up to 30% cost, 50% timeline and 25% embodied-carbon reductions vs traditional methods. Specific pricing/contract terms not publicly disclosed.
customers: Property developers; All3 says "customers are signed" and its first commercial deployment breaks ground on active sites in Germany in 2026, but no named customers or pilot partners are publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; raised a $25M seed round in April 2026 led by RTP Global with SuperSeed, Begin Capital, s16vc and VNV Global; first commercial build breaks ground 2026)
technical_differentiation: Broader, fully vertically integrated stack (AI design + robotic off-site timber-composite factories + autonomous legged Mantis assembly robot with 100kg payload/4m reach) replacing the whole value chain above the foundation, versus Monumental (Amsterdam), which narrowed to robotic bricklaying and acts as a wall subcontractor, and 011h (Barcelona), a software-first prefab approach; AUAR's portable micro-factories are another adjacent peer. Differentiation is end-to-end scope rather than a single point solution.

one_liner: Retrofits existing excavators, dozers and loaders into fully autonomous machines via a reversible same-day "Bedrock Operator" upfit, using imitation learning trained on expert-operator demonstration data.
last_round_lead: CapitalG and Valor Equity Partners (Valor Atreides AI Fund)
notes: Founded 2024 by ex-Waymo/Segment leaders (CEO Boris Sofman, Kevin Peterson). Emerged from stealth July 16, 2025 with $80M Seed+Series A (Seed led by Eclipse, Series A led by 8VC; investors incl. NVentures, Two Sigma, Valor). Series B of $270M announced Feb 4, 2026, led by CapitalG and Valor Equity Partners with NVentures participating, valuing the company ~$1.75B and bringing total to >$350M. Flagship "Bedrock Operator" uses lidar/GPS/motion sensors, mounts in hours with no permanent modification. Targeting first fully operator-less excavator deployments in 2026. In scope: although heavy earthmoving equipment exists without AI, Bedrock is an AI-native autonomy stack (learned perception + planning via imitation learning on demonstration data) that would not function without learned policies; not a pure AV company. Team size not disclosed in sources. [AI_intensity: Perception and planning/manipulation use learned policies — the company explicitly applies the Waymo ML paradigm, training AI models on large-scale expert-operator demonstration data (imitation learning) to capture earthmoving nuances rather than hard-coding scenarios. Earthmoving manipulation is the AI-native task.]
business_model: Sells the "Bedrock Operator" autonomy upfit (lidar/GPS/sensor retrofit kit + imitation-learning software) directly to construction contractors and equipment-rental firms via a two-part model: one-time hardware install/upfit fee plus a recurring monthly subscription for the software platform and remote supervision. Currently transitioning from supervised-autonomy paid deployments toward fully operator-less excavator deployments in 2026.
customers: Named contractor customers/pilots include Sundt Construction (Phoenix-based GC) and Champion Site Prep (Texas); deployments span large earthmoving, industrial facilities, ports and data-center sites, including a supervised-autonomy mass-excavation job on a 130-acre manufacturing site.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; running paid supervised-autonomy deployments, pre fully operator-less commercialization)
technical_differentiation: Vs Built Robotics (closest retrofit competitor, which uses its Exosystem kit and waypoint/geofence automation focused on solar/renewables) and SafeAI/Pronto (mining haul trucks), Bedrock differentiates via a Waymo-pedigree imitation-learning stack that learns from expert operators and a reversible same-day upfit applied to existing excavators, dozers and loaders for general commercial earthmoving.

one_liner: Coordinates multi-robot autonomous blockwork/bricklaying via BIM-driven NVIDIA Omniverse digital-twin simulations, validating workflows virtually before deploying hardware-agnostic robotic teams to construction sites.
notes: Emerged from stealth Nov 2025 with $2M pre-seed led by Tim Draper. Founded 2025 by Slava Solonitsyn (ex-Mighty Buildings, YC alum) and Anton Glance. Construction vertical (6g). First commercial app: blockwork/partition-wall installation (~$13B US market), US deployments under shared-savings model starting Q1 2026; also UAE focus. Hardware-agnostic orchestration supporting 40+ robot types; Revit plug-in converts BIM to OpenUSD; digital twin handles high-level planning + real-time replanning, edge computer handles execution. Borderline on learned-vs-model-based policy — included per when-in-doubt rule; sim-to-real / physical-AI framing makes it AI-native (would not exist without the simulation/orchestration AI stack), hence in_scope=true. Dual US/UAE positioning across sources; HQ listed as San Francisco by The Robot Report. Team size not disclosed. [AI_intensity: unclear — system is sim-first ("simulation-first" / physical-AI framing) using NVIDIA Omniverse digital twins for planning and real-time replanning, plus on-site edge execution deciding block placement. Public materials do not confirm whether on-site manipulation/placement uses a learned policy (RL/IL) vs. model-based BIM-driven planning/control. Likely model-based orchestration with classical perception; no explicit learned-policy claim found.]
