business_model: Robotics-as-a-Service for autonomous blockwork/partition-wall installation, sold to general contractors on a shared-savings (outcome-based) contract: Buildroid deploys AI-coordinated multi-robot teams (two block-laying robots plus an autonomous mobile material-handling robot, orchestrated via BIM-driven NVIDIA Omniverse digital twins) and takes 50% of the net efficiency gains versus manual crews while committing to performance metrics. Targets the ~$13B blockwork segment of construction.
customers: Undisclosed. Ran early pilots in the UAE and plans to begin commercial deployments with "leading general contractors" in the US starting Q1 2026, but no specific partner orgs are publicly named.
revenue_status: pre-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; raised $2M pre-seed led by Tim Draper in Nov 2025, with first US commercial deployments slated for Q1 2026)
technical_differentiation: Vs. FBR (Fastbrick Robotics) and Construction Robotics (SAM/MULE) and Monumental, which sell single proprietary bricklaying machines, Buildroid is hardware-agnostic (claims 40+ compatible robot types) and "simulation-first," using NVIDIA Omniverse BIM digital twins to validate and coordinate multi-robot trade sequences virtually before any hardware reaches the jobsite, plus an outcome-based shared-savings commercial model rather than equipment sales.

one_liner: Retrofits excavators, dozers and pile drivers into autonomous robots using AI vision and sensor fusion, automating earthmoving and solar pile installation in unstructured terrain.
notes: Founded 2016 in SF by Noah Ready-Campbell and Andrew Liang. Exosystem aftermarket retrofit kit (GPS + cameras + AI) launched 2021; turns excavators/dozers into autonomous robots. RPD 35 (announced 2023) is the world's first fully autonomous solar-piling system, combining surveying, pile distribution, pile driving and data collection. Total funding ~$112M across Series A/B/C; latest disclosed round $64M Series C led by Tiger Global, April 2022 (no public round since). Team size small (~25 per Tracxn 2022-23 estimates); LinkedIn headcount estimates vary. Construction vertical = 6g. AI bar borderline-to-strong: learned perception/vision and sensor fusion referenced, though autonomy also leans on classical GPS-guided planning; included per assignment with note. in_scope=true because AI-driven autonomous operation in unstructured terrain (variable perception/control), not a pre-programmed industrial arm. Secondary L4 hardware due to retrofit kit. Other investors: NEA, Founders Fund, Next47, Building Ventures, Presidio Ventures, Lemnos. [AI_intensity: Manipulation/control and perception for autonomous earthmoving and pile driving rely on AI-powered vision and sensor fusion with learned perception/control referenced; GPS-guided planning is more classical. Borderline-to-strong on the AI bar — learned perception present but autonomy is heavily classical/sensor-fusion driven.]
business_model: Robotics-as-a-Service (RaaS): sells aftermarket autonomy retrofit kits (the "Exosystem") and purpose-built autonomous machines (e.g., RPD 35 solar pile driver, RPS 25) to construction/EPC contractors. Buyers rent rather than buy capital - reported pricing of ~$3,000/machine/month plus location-based hourly usage fees for the Exosystem, and per-pile / project-based RaaS rates for solar piling (installation, training, and updates bundled in).
customers: Named contractors include Blattner (3-year agreement signed Sept 2025 to deploy dozens of robots on Sun Belt solar projects; already on 5 Blattner sites) and Mortenson (agreement to expand autonomous equipment on renewable projects). Deployed across 2+ GW of solar installations and on a 1.1-GW solar farm.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$11.4M, est., unverified per Growjo; no official figure disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via aftermarket retrofit autonomy (vision + sensor fusion) that converts existing OEM-agnostic excavators/dozers/pile drivers into robots, plus a verticalized solar-piling robot (RPD 35) that fuses survey, distribution, driving and QA into one machine. Closest named competitor Bedrock Robotics ($80M, 2025) targets the same retrofit-autonomy-for-construction space but is later-stage/stealth-emerged and Caterpillar/Komatsu OEM efforts compete on factory-integrated autonomy; Built's edge is its installed solar-piling track record and RaaS commercial model.

one_liner: Hardware-agnostic AI software orchestrating robot/drone fleets (e.g. Spot, drones) for autonomous industrial inspections, using learned anomaly detection and autonomous navigation to build digital twins of critical infrastructure.
last_round_lead: Blue Bear Capital and Climate Investment (co-leads)
notes: TU Darmstadt 'Team Hector' spin-off (ARGOS Challenge winner for autonomous oil & gas inspection robots). Rebranded from Energy Robotics GmbH to Korial in 2025. Software is hardware-agnostic, deployed on Boston Dynamics Spot, drones, and other platforms; 1M+ inspections completed across five continents for Shell, BP, Repsol, BASF, Merck, E.ON. Series A ($13.5M / EUR 11.5M) co-led by Blue Bear Capital and Climate Investment with Futury Capital, Hessen Capital, Kensho VC, TADTech. Total funding approximate (earlier seed undisclosed; ~$20M cumulative estimate). SCOPE: borderline. The inspection-robot category can exist with classical CV/teleop, but AI (learned anomaly detection) is core to the product value and navigation skills are partly learned; included with note per 'when in doubt, in_scope=true'. Vertical mapped to 6g per assignment, though primary end-markets are energy/oil-gas/chemical/utility industrial inspection (arguably 6h/6b) rather than construction specifically. [AI_intensity: Perception uses learned policies: AI/ML anomaly detection on visual/thermal/gas/acoustic inspection data is central. Navigation is partly autonomous (learned/adaptive 'skills' atop robot platforms like Spot) but leans on classical SLAM/mapping; manipulation is minimal. Primary learned core is perception (anomaly detection), with navigation borderline learned-vs-classical.]
business_model: Sells a hardware-agnostic, cloud-based enterprise SaaS platform (rebranded "Korial") that orchestrates third-party robots and drones (Boston Dynamics Spot, DJI, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries) for autonomous industrial inspection; buyers are enterprise asset operators in oil/gas, chemical, and utilities, sold via custom per-facility deployment contracts (pricing quoted on request, not public). Value pitch is 30-40% inspection cost reduction and replacing hazardous manual labor.
customers: Named paying customers include Shell (drone tank inspections), Merck, BASF, BP, Repsol, E.ON, Dow Chemical, and Evonik; over 1 million inspections completed across five continents.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure; raised $13.5M Series A in Oct 2025 with named enterprise customers and 1M+ inspections, but revenue/ARR not publicly disclosed)
technical_differentiation: Full-stack, hardware-agnostic AI software layer (LLM + agentic workflows + learned anomaly detection) that orchestrates multiple OEM robot/drone fleets in one platform, vs. Percepto (drone-in-a-box, more hardware-tied autonomous inspection) and Flyability (proprietary inspection drones). Differentiation is the multi-vendor orchestration software model rather than owning hardware.

one_liner: Autonomous aerial and ground robots with ExynAI Level-4 perception software for real-time 3D mapping and navigation in GPS-denied environments like mines, construction sites and infrastructure.
total_funding_usd: ~57000000 (pre-IPO, across 5 rounds per BounceWatch; plus ~19.4M IPO gross proceeds May 2026)
notes: Spun out of UPenn GRASP Lab; founded 2014, HQ Philadelphia PA. Develops "Level 4B" autonomy software (ExynAI) + autonomous drones for GPS-denied 3D mapping/inspection in mining, construction, infrastructure, defense. Prior funding: Seed 2.5M (2016-12), Series A ~16.7M (2019-06), Series B 35M (2022-12, led by Reliance Strategic Business Ventures), small 5M round (2024-07); BounceWatch cites ~57M total pre-IPO (other sources up to 72M). IPO completed May 2026 on Nasdaq (EXYN/EXYNW), ~19.4M gross at $7.75/unit; reported ~76M accumulated deficit and going-concern warning at IPO. Status=ipo. In_scope=true per when-in-doubt: AI-native autonomy/navigation product (ExynAI markets learned perception-driven autonomy), though core is borderline SLAM/classical — see ai_intensity. Funding/round figures from secondary aggregators (Crunchbase/BounceWatch); IPO from company PR and news. Team size not reliably found. [AI_intensity: unclear — markets ExynAI perception-driven autonomy/navigation for GPS-denied environments, but the core stack is heavily SLAM/sensor-fusion and classical motion planning; learned-policy use for the core navigation/perception task is not clearly documented.]
business_model: Sells/licenses its ExynAI Level-4 autonomy and SLAM software, bundled with the Nexys modular mapping payload, sensors, and compute, to industrial operators and robot OEMs; revenue comes from hardware-enabled software sales, software/SDK licensing, plus services and support, generally via per-unit purchase and licensing/subscription contracts. ExynView (post-processing/mission planning) and integration via API/SDK round out the offering.
customers: ~49 customers as of 2025 (24 mining, 22 geospatial, 3 government). Named users include BMW (regularly remaps production facilities) and the Chelopech gold mine in Bulgaria; deployments span 30+ countries across mining, construction, infrastructure inspection, forestry, and defense.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$5.81M revenue TTM as of Dec 31, 2025; Nasdaq IPO May 2026 raised ~$19.4M, ticker EXYN, ~$48.5M market cap June 2026)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates on field-proven Level-4B full autonomy in GPS-denied environments (1,500+ autonomous missions/month) and hardware-agnostic deployment via API/SDK, versus Emesent (Hovermap), the closest LiDAR-SLAM autonomy competitor focused mainly on underground mining, and NavVis/Hexagon-Leica, which lead in handheld/static SLAM scanning but offer less onboard autonomous navigation.

one_liner: Robots that climb, crawl, swim and fly to inspect critical infrastructure, feeding the AI Cantilever platform that detects defects and predicts asset failures.
notes: Pittsburgh, PA HQ (100 S Commons, 15212). Founded 2013 by Jake Loosararian and Orion Correa (Troy Demmer also credited co-founder). Total raised ~$347M per company (assignment cited ~$354M; company statement says $347M to date). Series D $125M (Jun 2025) led by Cox Enterprises with USIT, XN, Founders Fund, Y Combinator; valuation $1.25B (unicorn), doubling from $633M Series C ($173M, Dec 2023). Borderline scope: product category (infrastructure inspection robots) could exist without AI, but Gecko is treated as in-scope because learned defect/anomaly detection (AI Cantilever platform / "AIR" technology) is central to the product value proposition, not merely bolted on. Navigation/locomotion likely classical rather than a learned policy — included with note per assignment. Team size not reliably found. [AI_intensity: unclear — Gecko uses learned anomaly/defect detection (deep-learning CV) on inspection sensor data within Cantilever, which is the strongest AI claim. Robot navigation/locomotion appears largely classical/teleop-assisted rather than a learned policy. So perception is the learned core task; manipulation/navigation are likely classical.]
business_model: Sells end-to-end infrastructure-inspection services: proprietary TOKA-family climbing/crawling/swimming/flying robots collect data on physical assets (power plants, ships, tanks, pipelines), which feed the Cantilever AI software platform sold as a recurring SaaS/asset-health subscription. Buyers are asset operators in defense, energy, and heavy industry, typically via multi-year contracts (e.g., a 5-year $71M U.S. Navy deal and a $100M NAES modernization partnership).
customers: U.S. Navy, U.S. Air Force, L3Harris, NAES (largest U.S. independent power operator), ADNOC, Freeport-McMoRan, U.S. Steel, Siemens Energy, BPMI, and Trident Maritime.
revenue_status: scaling (~$60M ARR per getLatka; 2023 revenue ~$34.5M, unverified third-party)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates by owning the full stack — purpose-built multi-modal inspection robots plus the proprietary Cantilever AI predictive-maintenance platform — creating data lock-in versus pure inspection-services or drone firms. Closest named competitors include traditional NDT/inspection providers and robotic inspection players like ANYbotics and Boston Dynamics (Spot-based inspection), but those lack Gecko's combined defense-grade hardware fleet and asset-health software with accumulated historical asset data.

one_liner: Tackles construction labor shortages with compact autonomous robots that lay bricks on-site, driven by Atrium AI software using computer vision to localize and coordinate placement.
notes: Amsterdam bricklaying-robotics startup founded 2021 by Salar al Khafaji (CEO) and Sebastiaan Visser (CTO), former founders of Silk (acquired by Palantir 2016). Exited stealth Feb 2024 with $25M / EUR23.2M seed round co-led by Plural and Hummingbird; Northzone, Foundamental, NP-Hard Ventures, Material Ventures and angels participated. Builds compact electric autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs) that move around jobsites to lay bricks, addressing labor shortages. Driven by Atrium AI software: computer vision for on-site self-localization and AI to coordinate tasks/placement. Borderline AI-intensity: brick placement likely uses modeled/CAD/BIM targets (classical), but vision-based localization and coordination of unstructured jobsites qualify as AI-native autonomy; included per when-in-doubt rule. in_scope=true: this is AI-trained variable-jobsite manipulation/navigation, not a pre-programmed factory arm. Total funding equals the single disclosed $25M seed. Team ~32 (late 2024). [AI_intensity: unclear — Atrium uses computer vision for on-site self-localization and AI to coordinate tasks, but brick placement targets appear to be derived from CAD/BIM models rather than a learned manipulation policy. No clearly documented RL/IL/diffusion/VLA policy for the core manipulation task; localization/coordination is the strongest AI element.]
business_model: Bricklaying-as-a-service: rather than selling its autonomous robots, Monumental is hired as a subcontractor and charges contractors per brick laid (priced comparably to human masons, the standard European pricing convention), with its Atrium "construction OS" software orchestrating the robots and quality control. This shifts financial/technical risk off the contractor and keeps the robots (with ~$25k component cost) on Monumental's balance sheet.
customers: Pilot/early customers are Dutch general contractors; the company reports partnerships with multiple top-25 general contractors and deployments on real sites including office/warehouse facades and social/low-income housing projects in the Netherlands. Specific named contractors are not publicly disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue
technical_differentiation: vs. Hadrian X (FBR, Australia) and the now-defunct SAM100 (Construction Robotics, US): those are large gantry/truck-mounted machines for greenfield single-wall layout, whereas Monumental uses small, electric, human-sized autonomous ground vehicles that move through doorways and tight corners like a human crew, coordinated by its Atrium AI/computer-vision software for on-site, occupied-environment work. Differentiation rests on compact mobile-robot form factor plus the service (per-brick) model rather than capital equipment sales.

one_liner: Builds AI-powered autonomous drones whose onboard Autonomy Engine (deep neural nets, learned visual navigation/obstacle avoidance) enables hands-free flight for inspection, construction-site monitoring, security, and defense.
notes: Founded 2014, HQ San Mateo CA with manufacturing in Hayward CA. Total funding ~$825M over 8 rounds (38 investors); prior backers include a16z and NVIDIA. Latest: Series F $110M (Apr 2026) led by existing investors at $4.4B valuation; ~$295M ARR reported. Team ~900 (LinkedIn/PitchBook ~912). Drones exist without AI, but Skydio's differentiator is learned onboard autonomy (deep neural pilot via imitation learning), so in_scope=true per AI-native autonomy rationale. Used heavily for construction-site monitoring and infrastructure inspection (6g), though it also serves security/defense/public-safety verticals. [AI_intensity: Navigation/perception use learned policies: the Autonomy Engine runs nine deep neural networks, and the Deep Neural Pilot was trained via imitation learning (CEILing) for obstacle avoidance and visual navigation. Learned flight policy, not classical planning alone.]
business_model: Hybrid hardware + software model: customers buy autonomous drones (e.g., X10, Dock for remote operations) outright and pay recurring per-drone software subscriptions (Skydio cloud, autonomy/inspection software) over multi-year contracts, which carry higher margins. Buyers span defense/government, public safety, and critical infrastructure (utilities, DOTs, construction/inspection).
customers: Named users include the US Department of Defense (over 50% of business is military as of late 2024), UK Ministry of Defence, Israel Defense Forces, Indian Armed Forces, Royal Canadian Navy, Ukraine, plus public-sector deployments like Ohio DOT (ODOT, infrastructure inspection) and Dallas Police Department (drone-as-first-responder).
revenue_status: scaling (~$295M revenue/ARR 2025, unverified; $4.4B valuation after $110M Series F)
technical_differentiation: Onboard Autonomy Engine (deep neural nets for learned visual navigation and obstacle avoidance) enables true GPS-denied, hands-free flight without a skilled pilot, differentiating from DJI (China; superior consumer hardware/scale but restricted in US gov markets and less autonomous) and US peers like Brinc and Anduril, where Skydio leads on self-flying autonomy and NDAA-compliant US manufacturing.

one_liner: Equipment-agnostic X1 retrofit kit turns existing construction machinery (pile drivers, excavators) into autonomous "Software-Defined Machinery," sold via Automation-as-a-Service subscription with task-specific licenses.
notes: Founded South Korea 2020 by Henri Lee (CEO), David Shin, Ryan Park; expanded to US in 2023, primary HQ now Santa Fe Springs, CA with Seoul office. Tech stack: Mango (machine control), M2 (environmental data processing/command), proprietary software; "Physical AI" / Software-Defined Machinery. Deployed on pile drivers (incl. solar pile drivers) and Develon excavators; reported 80% labor and 50% time reduction in piling. Series B bridge $18M (May 2026) led by Korea Investment Partners with KB Investment; total $38M. BORDERLINE: vendor describes continuous on-site learning, but no public source confirms the core autonomy is a learned RL/IL policy rather than ML-assisted perception plus adaptive/classical control — included per when-in-doubt rule. in_scope=true: AI-native retrofit autonomy/physical-AI category (not a conventional pre-programmed machine). [AI_intensity: Construction-machine autonomy (perception + manipulation/control). Company claims X1 "continuously learns and improves on the jobsite," but no source confirms a learned RL/IL policy vs. adaptive/classical control with ML-assisted perception — unclear; borderline per when-in-doubt rule.]
business_model: Sells the X1 retrofit kit that converts existing construction machinery (pile drivers, excavators) into autonomous "Software-Defined Machinery," monetized primarily via an Automation-as-a-Service (AaaS) subscription model with task-specific licenses (piling, material handling, trenching, grading) sold to construction/EPC firms; some customers remain on a perpetual model (one-time hardware purchase plus software), with full transition to subscriptions targeted by end-2026.
customers: Named customers include Mortenson, Black & Veatch, and QCells; company reports working with or in discussions with 19 of the top 20 U.S. solar EPC firms, focused on solar, BESS, and AI data center construction.
revenue_status: scaling ($21M revenue 2025, up from $7M 2024 and $3M 2023; Q1 2026 $8M revenue with ~$1M EBIT; targeting ~$60M ARR by end of 2026)
technical_differentiation: Equipment-agnostic, task-specific software retrofit (via its Mango precision-control and M2 perception stack) that adds discrete autonomy capabilities to existing machines rather than replacing hardware or automating whole machines end-to-end; investors frame this as avoiding the scalability wall hit by full-machine autonomy players. No direct named competitor in the same construction-retrofit layer was disclosed in public sources; closest adjacent players (e.g., full-stack autonomous OEMs like Built Robotics) differ by selling whole autonomous machines rather than an agnostic retrofit kit.

one_liner: BrainOS AI software platform powering autonomous floor-cleaning and retail/inventory robots; SelfPath AI lets robots plan and adapt cleaning paths without manual route training.
last_round_type: strategic / corporate (Tennant exclusive technology agreement investment)
notes: San Diego AI company (HQ 10182 Telesis Court, San Diego, CA 92121), co-founded 2009 by neuroscientist Dr. Eugene Izhikevich and Dr. Allen Gruber. BrainOS powers 40,000+ autonomous mobile robots across six continents, primarily commercial floor cleaners (exclusive Tennant partnership) plus retail restocking/inventory robots. Total funding ~$193M over 5 rounds incl. $114M Series C (Jul 2017, SoftBank Vision Fund). Most recent: $32M strategic investment from Tennant Company (Feb 2024) tied to exclusive technology agreement; Tennant CFO Fay West joined board. BrainOS Clean 2.0 with SelfPath AI launched Mar 2026 (22% expanded coverage, 55% improved autonomy, removes manual route training). Platform/software supplier, not robot OEM. Borderline on strict learned-policy bar (much of historic stack is classical SLAM/navigation); included per assignment note given SelfPath adaptive planning + contextual perception. Team size not findable in public sources. Crunchbase total-funding field appears obfuscated; $193M figure corroborated by Tracxn. NOT a pure AV company (indoor service robots). [AI_intensity: Manipulation is minimal; the relevant learned-policy claim is in navigation/planning. Historic stack is largely classical SLAM/odometry navigation. The new SelfPath AI (BrainOS Clean 2.0, 2026) adds adaptive self-planning and contextual perception that goes beyond fixed pre-trained routes, plus learned perception for obstacle/context classification. Borderline learned-policy; included per assignment note.]
business_model: Licenses its hardware-agnostic BrainOS AI autonomy software (Clean Suite, plus SelfPath AI path-planning) to OEMs like Tennant, Nilfisk, Kärcher, and Minuteman who embed it in commercial floor-scrubbing and inventory/retail robots; revenue comes from OEM software licensing plus a SaaS/subscription cloud platform (fleet management, analytics) sold per-robot, complemented by a direct sales force serving large retail/logistics accounts. Capital-light model: Brain supplies the "operating system," partners build and sell the machines.
customers: Walmart is the flagship named customer (fleet expanded to ~1,860 BrainOS-powered autonomous scrubbers); deployments reach 20,000+ robots globally via 10+ OEM partners (Tennant, Nilfisk, Kärcher, Minuteman, Dane Technologies, UniCarriers). Sam's Club and other large retail/grocery operators are also reported users.
revenue_status: scaling (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; SoftBank Vision Fund-backed, raised $114M in 2017 plus ~$36M (2020) and ~$32M (2024) rounds)
technical_differentiation: Unlike Avidbots (which builds its own purpose-built Neo robot hardware + software, an "Apple iOS" closed-stack approach) and ICE Cobotics (own-hardware robotic scrubbers), Brain Corp is hardware-agnostic, retrofitting existing manually-driven cleaning machines from many OEMs with BrainOS — an "Android" platform play that has produced the largest deployed fleet (35,000-40,000+ units) of commercial autonomous robots in public spaces. SelfPath AI adds adaptive, real-time path replanning versus fixed taught routes.

one_liner: Builds zero-emission cooler-sized sidewalk robots for urban last-mile delivery, training learned navigation/perception models on millions of miles of real-world data via hybrid teleop assist.
last_round_lead: undisclosed (investors included Sam Altman, Max Altman, Pelion Venture Partners, Offline Ventures)
notes: LA/Santa Monica-based last-mile sidewalk delivery robot company, founded 2020 by UCLA students Zach Rash (CEO) and Brad Squicciarini. Operates ~1,300 electric robots across Miami, Chicago, LA, and Helsinki; targets 10,000+ by end of 2026. Raised $80M in June 2025 (reported as Series C) from Sam Altman, Max Altman, Pelion, Offline Ventures and others; >$110-120M total to date. March/June 2025 OpenAI collaboration: Coco uses OpenAI models while OpenAI gains real-world data from 500k+ deliveries. Oct 2025 launched Physical AI Lab under UCLA professor Bolei Zhou (chief AI scientist) for simulation, neural rendering, generative AI, RL. Scope note: last-mile delivery robots can exist with pure teleop/pre-programming (borderline AV-adjacent), and Coco today is explicitly hybrid teleop-assisted; however last-mile delivery robot is an explicit 6h example and the autonomy stack is increasingly learned/foundation-model driven, so in_scope=true. Team size not disclosed in sources. Round lead not clearly stated; named investors listed instead. [AI_intensity: Navigation and perception are the AI-relevant tasks. Historically driven by classical autonomy plus human teleop assist, but the company is building a Physical AI Lab (chief AI scientist Bolei Zhou, Oct 2025) using reinforcement learning, simulation, neural rendering and generative AI, plus an OpenAI data collaboration, to move toward foundation-model-driven navigation/perception. Currently hybrid (teleop-assisted), trending toward learned policies.]
business_model: Operates a fleet of zero-emission cooler-sized sidewalk delivery robots ("Coco bots") and monetizes two ways: per-delivery service fees charged to delivery platforms (DoorDash, Uber Eats, Wolt) for last-mile fulfillment, and a hardware/robots-as-a-service subscription sold directly to merchants and restaurant chains for dedicated, brandable robot fleets (cheaper than human couriers). Coco owns and operates the fleet rather than selling the hardware.
customers: Named delivery-platform partners include DoorDash (April 2025 deal covering ~600 merchants in LA and Chicago, with a 100,000+ delivery LA pilot), Uber Eats, and Wolt; serves 3,000+ merchants/restaurants across Los Angeles, Chicago, Miami, Dallas, and Helsinki. Specific paying enterprise restaurant chains are not individually disclosed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; 500,000+ paid deliveries completed, ~1,000 bots deployed; raised ~$120-123M total incl. $80M Series B June 2025)
technical_differentiation: vs. Serve Robotics and Starship Technologies: Coco uses simplified, lower-cost robot designs with a larger ~90-liter cargo compartment and stores bots at merchant locations to avoid centralized charging/maintenance infrastructure, while training learned navigation/perception models on millions of real-world miles. Serve emphasizes Level-4 autonomy with reduced teleoperation; Starship competes on global scale (2,000+ units). Coco relies more heavily on human teleoperation/remote oversight than Serve's autonomy positioning.

one_liner: Recyclables are lost to landfills at MRFs; RecycleOS uses deep-learning vision to identify 60+ recyclable classes in real time and drives robotic arms to pick/sort.
notes: AI-native recycling robotics: learned vision perception is core to product, driving robotic pick/sort at MRFs (claims 2-3x recovery, 99%+ uptime). In-scope because AI-trained variable-item manipulation/perception enables a capability conventional sorting conveyors cannot do. Founded 2018 by JD (Jagadeesh) Ambati (CEO) and Pratik Khadloya; ~3.5 years R&D, began commercializing H2 2021. HQ: 48820 Kato Road, Suite 500B, Fremont, CA 94538 (some India/Ahmedabad R&D presence per Tracxn). Series A $16.1M (Sep 2022) led by Translink Capital with NEC Orchestrating Future Fund, BGV, Sierra Ventures, Morado Ventures, Xplorer Capital; prior seed via BGV. Total funding ~$21M is approximate (seed + Series A); exact seed undisclosed. Team size ~39 (2023, getLatka/Tracxn). Secondary L4_hardware noted because they deploy robotic arm systems. Sources: everestlabs.ai, GlobeNewswire 2022-09-28 funding release, Recycling Today, Robotics 24/7, Crunchbase, Tracxn, Sierra Ventures profile. [AI_intensity: Perception uses a learned policy: deep-learning AI models trained on a proprietary 5B+ object dataset identify 60+ recyclable classes at 95%+ accuracy in 10-15ms. Manipulation/pick likely relies partly on classical planning, so full learned-policy manipulation is borderline; the learned perception clears the AI-intensity bar.]
business_model: Sells RecycleOS, an AI vision + robotics platform for Materials Recovery Facilities (MRFs), primarily via a "Robot-as-a-Service" model: MRF operators pay for AI-driven robotic sorting cells (3D depth cameras + robotic arms identifying 60-200+ recyclable classes) with consumables, repairs, and replacements bundled in. Buyers are recycling plant operators justifying spend by labor savings (cited ~3-6 month payback) plus higher material recovery and lower landfill fees; also sells RecycleOS analytics/tracking software.
customers: Named customers include Sims Municipal Recycling (Sunset Park, Brooklyn - cited largest single customer at 300k+ tons/yr), Republic Services, Pioneer Recycling Services, KW Plastics, Alameda County Industries, RethinkWaste, City of Sunnyvale, and Sojitz (Japan), plus two of the 10 largest U.S. waste companies.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$4.6M revenue FY2023, third-party/unverified per getlatka; reported 5x YoY revenue growth; total funding ~$35.8M through a 2025 Series B)
technical_differentiation: Vs AMP Robotics and Glacier, EverestLabs emphasizes a software-first "RecycleOS" data/analytics layer plus 3D depth-sensing cameras claiming identification of up to 200 items per frame in ~12ms across 60+ classes, and a RaaS model bundling consumables/maintenance. AMP is larger and more vertically integrated (owns/operates sorting facilities); Glacier targets smaller-footprint robots. EverestLabs differentiates on per-item analytics/tracking and breadth of material classification rather than facility ownership.

one_liner: Tackles low US recycling rates with low-cost robotic arms that use computer-vision/deep-learning to identify 30+ material types and sort recyclables at material recovery facilities.
notes: Co-founded 2019 by Rebecca Hu (Hu-Thrams) and Areeb Malik; female co-led. Amazon Climate Pledge Fund-backed. $16M Series A (Apr 2025) led by Ecosystem Integrity Fund, with AlleyCorp, NEA, Amazon Climate Pledge Fund, Cox Exponential, Alumni Ventures, Elysium, One Small Planet, Overlap Holdings, Overture, VSC Ventures, Working Capital Fund. Total funding ~$30.2M (incl. prior ~$7.7M seed). Robots sort ~45-50 items/min, identify 30-70+ material types, have sorted 90M+ items; deployed in SF, LA, Chicago, Detroit, Phoenix, Seattle (Recology King County MRF). HQ San Francisco (assignment noted Oakland; sources consistently say SF). In_scope=true: AI-native variable-item manipulation where each item differs and learned perception is essential. Operates under endwaste.io domain. [AI_intensity: Perception is a learned policy: deep-learning computer-vision models trained on a large proprietary recyclables dataset classify 30-70+ material types. The pick/manipulation policy appears partly classical (engineered pick-and-place of identified items on a conveyor), so AI-intensity is borderline but the core perception task is learned.]
business_model: Sells AI-vision robotic sorting units to Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs), priced ~half of conventional sorting automation, with most fees deferred/put-at-risk and tied to hitting recovery/purity success metrics (performance-based contracts; ROI typically under a year). A second revenue line is "DataStream," a subscription data product selling waste-stream insights to MRFs, consumer-brand companies, and government agencies.
customers: Recology (King County MRF, Seattle), Waste Connections (Penn Waste facility), and Amazon (commercial packaging-recyclability pilot, also an investor). Brand partner Colgate (custom toothpaste-tube identification model). Live units span 10+ states including LA, Chicago, Detroit, Phoenix, and San Francisco.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public ARR/revenue figure disclosed; ~$24M+ total funding raised incl. $16M Series A in April 2025)
technical_differentiation: Competes vs AMP Robotics (formerly AMP / now AMP Sortation) and Greyparrot/Recycleye in AI recycling sorting. Differentiation: a low-cost, compact robotic arm (~3 ft of conveyor, sub-day install, ~half the cost of incumbents) bundled with deep-learning vision identifying 30+ material types, monetized via a performance-deferred pricing model plus the DataStream analytics layer, rather than selling large capital-intensive optical sorters.

one_liner: Autonomous "AI Science Factory" labs where AI models design experiments, run them on robotic platforms (arms, liquid handlers, analytics), learn from results, and choose next experiments across life/chemical/materials science.
notes: Cambridge, MA company built in Flagship Pioneering's labs; out of stealth March 2025 with $200M seed. $350M Series A (incl. $115M Oct 2025 NVIDIA-backed extension) brought total to $550M at >$1.3B valuation; reportedly later in talks at ~$8.5B valuation (unconfirmed). In-scope: AI-native self-driving lab — the closed-loop generative/active-learning experiment-design system is core; the robotic lab platform would not function as an autonomous discovery engine without it. AI-intensity is learned experiment planning rather than low-level learned manipulation. Team size ~200-230 (third-party data Nov 2025), undisclosed officially. [AI_intensity: Learned experiment planning / active learning (generative AI + iterative model-driven hypothesis generation) drives the self-driving lab. Perception/manipulation use commercial lab-automation hardware rather than a low-level learned manipulation policy; the AI-native element is the closed-loop experiment-selection policy.]
business_model: Vertically integrated "AI Science Factory": Lila sells access to its autonomous-science platform (AI scientific agents plus robotic wet labs) to enterprises via project-based discovery programs and enterprise platform/subscription access, with R&D-as-a-service contracts in life sciences, materials, energy and semiconductors. It also positions to generate value from proprietary IP/discoveries made on the platform, and is building commercial AI Science Factories (Cambridge, Boston, SF, London) to sell automated experimental capacity.
customers: Undisclosed. As of early 2026 the company says it is "welcoming its first cohort of customers" in strategic scientific domains (drug development, energy, semiconductors, materials) but has not named them publicly. Flagship Pioneering (founder/investor) and Nvidia are backers/partners rather than disclosed paying customers.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$45.8M ARR, June 2025, GetLatka third-party estimate - unverified)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates by owning the full closed-loop stack -- AI experiment design plus physically integrated robotic labs (arms, liquid handlers, analytics) -- across multiple science verticals, versus narrower peers. Closest named competitors: Future House (nonprofit, mostly in-silico AI scientists, less wet-lab integration) and Periodic Labs (AI-for-physical-science discovery); Lila competes on capital scale ($550M raised, >$1.3B valuation, Nvidia backing) and breadth of autonomous physical labs.

one_liner: Builds AI scientists and autonomous robotic labs that run physical materials-science experiments, iterating conjecture-test-learn loops to accelerate discovery of new materials such as superconductors.
team_size: ~20-30 (20+ scientists at launch; exact headcount undisclosed)
notes: Founded 2025 by Liam Fedus (ex-OpenAI VP Research / ChatGPT co-creator) and Ekin Dogus Cubuk (ex-Google Brain/DeepMind materials lead, GNoME). Out of stealth Sep 30, 2025 with record ~$300M seed led by a16z; other backers NVIDIA, DST, Accel, Jeff Bezos, Eric Schmidt, Jeff Dean, Elad Gil. HQ ~2261 Market St, San Francisco. AI-native self-driving lab: learned models plan/drive physical materials experiments and generate physical-world training data. In scope as AI-native autonomous-lab/scientific-discovery platform (L6 6h_other lab automation); robots present but AI intensity is in learned planning/active learning, not learned manipulation. Some aggregators cite higher cumulative figures (~$605M) but the confirmed disclosed round is the $300M seed. Team size exact headcount undisclosed. [AI_intensity: Planning is the learned core: AI models (reasoning + active learning) plan and drive experiments; learned-planning/active-learning intensity rather than learned low-level robot manipulation, which uses lab automation/classical control.]
business_model: B2B research-as-a-service: Periodic offers access to its AI scientist models plus its autonomous robotic materials/physics labs to firms doing physical R&D (custom AI + experimental-data generation), sold via custom project-scoped contracts. Longer term it aims to capture direct economics or licensing from the materials it discovers (e.g., high-temperature superconductors).
customers: Undisclosed by name. Third-party reports indicate early paying customers in semiconductor, space, and defense sectors doing physical R&D, but no specific organizations are publicly named.
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$6.6M ARR, unverified third-party estimate per getlatka, 2025)
technical_differentiation: Vs Lila Sciences (closest competitor, also building AI-run autonomous labs): Periodic focuses specifically on the physical sciences (materials, solid-state physics, superconductors) and pursues fuller end-to-end automation of the ideation-experiment-learn loop, whereas Lila spans materials, life science, and chemistry with more human-supervised "AI Science Factories." Founder pedigree (ChatGPT co-creator Liam Fedus, ex-DeepMind Ekin Cubuk) and $300M seed backing are key edges, but differentiation is largely execution/capital-driven within a crowded autonomous-lab field.

also_known_as: RAD, RAD Security, AITX (parent), ROAMEO, ROSA, RIO, SARA
one_liner: Autonomous AI security robots (ROAMEO patrol vehicle, ROSA, RIO stationary units) using onboard edge AI with LiDAR/radar to detect intruders, weapons and anomalies, cutting guard costs.
total_funding_usd: undisclosed (publicly traded via parent AITX, OTC: AITX; equity financing agreements)
notes: RAD is a wholly owned subsidiary of Artificial Intelligence Technology Solutions, Inc. (OTC: AITX), founded 2016, led by founder/CEO Steve Reinharz; HQ at the RAD Excellence Center (REX), 10800 Galaxie Ave, Ferndale, MI. Products: ROAMEO (autonomous wheeled patrol robot, Gen 4 ~730kg, marketed as cutting guard costs ~80%), ROSA (fixed edge-AI deterrence), RIO (security trailer), AVA (access control), SARA (agentic AI orchestration), RADCAM. Funding is via public equity (AITX) not VC rounds; June 2025 $30M equity financing agreement with GHS Investments; FY2025 (ended Feb 2025) revenue $6.1M, FY2026 projected $12-18M. In scope: category is AI-native autonomous security ground robots whose value depends on learned threat/anomaly perception, not a traditional non-AI product. Borderline on strict learned-policy bar for navigation — included with note; core perception is learned. [AI_intensity: Core perception is learned (deep-learning CV for threat/weapon/intruder/anomaly detection; ML that learns 'normal' baseline for an environment over time). Navigation/planning appears largely classical (LiDAR/radar/IMU mapping and route-following) — unclear whether a learned policy drives navigation; perception is the clear learned component.]
business_model: Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS): RAD leases its autonomous security units (ROAMEO patrol vehicle, ROSA trailer, RIO/RIO stationary towers) on recurring monthly subscriptions, typically with a 12-month minimum contract, pitched as 35-80% cheaper than manned guarding. The buyers are enterprise/commercial security and facilities teams; RAD is a wholly owned subsidiary of publicly traded AITX (OTC).
customers: Customers are largely disclosed only by descriptor rather than name: a Fortune 50 client (100+ RIO orders), a Fortune 500 client running a pilot scalable to 60 units, and a "major healthcare network" placing multiple ROAMEO orders. AITX cites a pipeline of 35+ Fortune 500 companies but does not publicly name specific paying organizations.
revenue_status: early-revenue ($6.13M revenue FY2025 ended Feb 28 2025, ~275% of FY2024; RMR run-rate surpassing ~$1M, targeting ~$2M; ROAMEO contracts alone projected at ~$2.5M ARR)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a vertically integrated, fully owned hardware-to-cloud stack (onboard edge AI with LiDAR/radar, SARA orchestration, RADSOC monitoring) sold on a low-cost RaaS subscription, versus Knightscope (KSCP), its closest publicly traded autonomous-security-robot peer, and Axon (a partner) in deterrence. Differentiation is execution/cost-driven rather than a defensible AI moat; both RAD and Knightscope remain sub-scale and unprofitable.

one_liner: Builds AI-powered autonomous sidewalk robots for last-mile food and goods delivery, using foundation-model-driven navigation trained on billions of real-world image-LiDAR samples.
notes: Public company (Nasdaq: SERV); uplisted/IPO'd April 18, 2024 at $4.00/share ($40M raise). Originated 2017 as Postmates X (Postmates' robotics division); spun out as independent Serve Robotics in 2021 with backing from Neo and Uber. HQ Redwood City, CA. Total funding ~$247M across 7 rounds; most recent capital raise ~$86M post-IPO offering Jan 2025. Acquired Vayu Robotics (Aug 18, 2025) to integrate robotics foundation models / simulation data engine into its autonomy stack for interpretable, foundation-model-driven sidewalk navigation; logs ~1M miles/month (~170B image-LiDAR samples). Serves 2,500+ restaurants across five cities; expanding into healthcare robots. In scope: AI-native autonomous sidewalk delivery robot relying on learned navigation/perception policies — distinct from a delivery van; not a road AV. Team ~121 at end of 2024, grown to ~291 (Oct 2025) and reportedly up to ~370 FTE. [AI_intensity: Navigation uses a learned policy: post-Vayu acquisition (2025) Serve adopts AI foundation-model-driven autonomy for sidewalk navigation, continuously training robot-learning models on ~170B image-LiDAR samples/month. Perception and navigation are learned, not purely classical. Meets the AI-intensity bar.]
business_model: Serve earns revenue two ways: "fleet services" (per-delivery fees from delivery-platform partners, chiefly Uber Eats, which has a signed agreement to use up to 2,000 Serve sidewalk robots), and "software/branding services" including a multi-year AI/autonomy software and advertising-revenue-sharing arrangement (e.g., its Magna/Wiland-style branding and the software-as-a-service licensing of its autonomy stack). It monetizes robots as a delivery-as-a-service fleet plus recurring software fees.
customers: Named partners include Uber Eats (anchor platform customer, agreement for up to 2,000 robots), Shake Shack (via Uber Eats in Los Angeles and Atlanta), and 7-Eleven; investors/partners include NVIDIA and Magna. Operates across ~44 cities in 14 states.
revenue_status: early-revenue ($3.0M revenue Q1 2026, +578% YoY; ~$26M revenue guidance FY2026; ~half now recurring)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a foundation-model-driven, vision-first Level-4 autonomy stack co-developed with NVIDIA (Jetson Orin compute, Isaac sim) and deep Uber Eats platform integration, versus sidewalk-robot peers Starship Technologies (lower-cost, mapping/sensor-fusion fleet, mostly campus/closed-environment) and Coco/Cartken (Cartken leans more teleop-assisted). Serve emphasizes higher autonomy and a public-market capital advantage.

one_liner: Robotics-as-a-service autonomous restroom-cleaning robot that navigates buildings (doors, elevators) and sprays/wipes/vacuums toilets and surfaces using manipulation policies taught via VR teleoperation demonstrations.
notes: Founded 2018 by Michael Levy (CEO) and Eugene Zasoba (CTO); accelerators HAX (2018) and Y Combinator (W20). HQ New York, NY. Product: BCR (Bathroom Cleaning Robot) on RaaS model, flat monthly fee ($1,500-$4,500). Robot autonomously navigates commercial buildings, opens doors, rides elevators, and performs touchless cleaning of toilets/urinals/floors/surfaces (spray, wipe, vacuum, dry). Manipulation policies trained via VR teleoperation where operators clean virtually in a headset to teach the AI = imitation learning from human demonstrations, which makes it AI-native and in_scope=true. Funding figures vary by source (Crunchbase ~$21.1M total; PitchBook/Tracxn cite a ~$13.7M Series A-II in Feb 2025). Investors include Y Combinator, SOSV/HAX, Soma Capital, Pioneer, Mana Ventures, Gaingels, C2 Ventures, Cathexis, AG Collective. Team small (<10 reported, may understate). Last-round lead not disclosed in available sources. Sources: getsomatic.com, Crunchbase (crunchbase.com/organization/somatic-bf50), TechCrunch (2020 VR-training coverage), CleanLink, New Atlas, PitchBook, Tracxn. [AI_intensity: Manipulation uses a learned policy: cleaning/wiping/spraying actions are trained via imitation learning from human VR teleoperation demonstrations. Navigation (door opening, elevator riding, building traversal) likely combines classical autonomy with the learned manipulation stack.]
business_model: Robotics-as-a-Service: Somatic deploys its Bathroom Cleaning Robot (BCR) into commercial buildings under a subscription, reportedly ~$1,000/month per robot with no upfront fee and no long-term contract, where the robot lives onsite and cleans restrooms ~40 hours/week. Setup/training of each site is done remotely via VR teleoperation by Somatic staff.
customers: Largely undisclosed; press reports state Somatic has a handful of commercial deployments including an unnamed FAANG company office, with target verticals being airports, casinos, offices, and large commercial restroom facilities. No specific named paying customers are publicly confirmed.
revenue_status: early-revenue (no public revenue/ARR figure disclosed; has raised ~$21.2M total funding across 8 rounds, latest a $13.7M Series A-II in Feb 2025 per Tracxn/PitchBook)
technical_differentiation: Unlike closest commercial cleaning-robot players Avidbots (Neo) and Brain Corp (BrainOS), which only autonomously scrub open floors, Somatic targets the harder dexterous task of fully cleaning restrooms (spray/wipe toilets, sinks, surfaces, wetvac floors) while navigating doors and elevators, using a manipulator-arm robot trained via VR teleoperation. Differentiation is the full-restroom dexterity and building navigation versus floor-only competitors.

one_liner: Spinout of CUHK's Robotics Institute building the TR1 dual-form floor-washing robot that uses multi-sensor fusion and a reinforcement-learning policy to navigate tight spaces.
notes: CUHK Robotics Institute spinout; team includes robotics PhDs, IEEE Fellows, and veterans from DJI, Roborock, XREAL, eufy. Unveiled dual-form transformable TR1 floor-washing/handheld cleaning robot at IFA 2025 (Sept 5-9, Hall 25 Booth 260). Robot-form navigation explicitly uses multi-sensor fusion + Reinforcement Learning; handheld form learns from human behavior; cloud RLHF + OTA enable "Self-Evolving Intelligence." Kickstarter launch planned April 21, 2026. Consumer/prosumer cleaning robot — cleaning robots exist without AI, but this product's learned RL navigation and RLHF adaptation are central (AI-native learned policy), so in_scope=true per the AI-intensity bar; borderline given consumer category. Founded 2022. HQ spans Hong Kong (CUHK) and Shenzhen; listed HK. Funding not publicly disclosed (no Crunchbase round data found). Company site (xlean.ai) confirms team-from-DJI/XREAL/Roborock; founding year and funding not on site. Sources: PR Newswire IFA 2025 release; Yahoo Finance; xlean.ai/pages/about-us. [AI_intensity: Navigation uses a learned reinforcement-learning policy (RL) for moving through tight spaces during deep/spot cleaning; the handheld mode learns from human behavior via RLHF and self-evolving OTA updates. RL navigation policy meets the AI-intensity bar.]
business_model: Consumer hardware: sells the xLean TR1 dual-form (autonomous robot + handheld) floor-washing robot direct-to-consumer to households. One-time unit sales at ~$899-$999 Kickstarter pledge prices (down from a $1,699 MSRP), with plans to ship via crowdfunding pre-orders and eventual retail/DTC e-commerce.
customers: Individual consumers (homeowners); no enterprise customers. ~1,100+ Kickstarter backers and a 300-person early-tester program are the only named buyer base; specific organizations are undisclosed (consumer product).
revenue_status: early-revenue (~$1.14M raised on Kickstarter from 1,131 backers as of the active campaign; product shipping est. Sept-Oct 2026 — no recurring/ARR figure)
technical_differentiation: Differentiates via a "dual-form" transformable design (one-second switch between autonomous robot and cordless handheld mop) plus RLHF "self-evolving" cleaning policy and single-pass high-negative-pressure solid+liquid pickup, versus round vacuum-mop competitors Roborock and Anker's eufy, which offer fixed-form autonomous-only robots without a handheld mode.
